text: The *Guangyi Ji* [Tang Dynasty] Compiled by Dai Fu I wish to observe the relationship between Heaven and humanity, the signs of change, the origins of good and evil. Even the sage does not know, and even the gods cannot predict. When one disturbs the primordial vital energy and disrupts the five elements, the sage reveals strange powers and chaotic spirits, and through rites, music, punishment, and administration, clarifies the sage's path to correct and guide behavior. Thus, Xǔ Shì's interpretation of celestial phenomena is meant to show people. In ancient texts, the character “示” is similar to the modern character “不,” and some scholars do not understand its true meaning, saying, “The Master did not speak of these things,” which completely undermines established wisdom, not the essence of observing celestial phenomena for teaching. The Great Potter distributes vital energy without being confined to one place. Praying for blessings transforms into a yellow bear, Pēng Shēng into a large pig, Cháng Hóng into blue, Shū Nǚ into a spring, Níu Āi into a tiger, Huáng Mǔ into a turtle, a noble person into a monkey or crane, a common person into small insects or sand, a woman from Wǔdū transforms into a man, a man from Chéngdū transforms into a woman, a silkworm named Zhōu É dies in a tomb for ten years but lives again, a weak man named Léi Diè dies in the market for six days but revives, the soul of a Shu emperor is called the cuckoo, the daughter of the Flame Emperor is called Jingwei, the vast and intricate transformations are beyond measure. In ancient times, the legendary figure Qingwū inspected tombs, Bái Zé investigated spirits and demons, Shùn commanded Kuí to harmonize with the spirits, Tāng questioned Gé about strange matters, sounds were heard in the wall of Lu, forms were inscribed on the bronze vessels of Xia, jade tablets and stone records, five diagrams and nine keys, and scholars have varied interpretations. Thus, Emperor Wen of Han summoned Jia Yi to discuss matters concerning ghosts and spirits, sitting up until midnight.

Scholars who specialize in recording strange or supernatural events, such as Liu Xin's *Lie Xian* (Treatise on Immortals), Ge Hong's *Shen Xian* (Treatise on Immortals), Wang Zhen's *Shi Yi* (Collected Oddities), Dongfang Shuo's *Shen Yi* (Treatise on Divine Phenomena), Zhang Maixian's *Bo Wu* (Treatise on Natural History), Guo Zihuang's *Dong Ming* (Treatise on the Mysteries of the Unseen), and Hou Junsu's *Ji Sheng* (Treatise on Sages), among others, all contain profound and mysterious content. Gu Jun's *Zhen Gao* (Divine Revelations), Zhou's *Min Tong* (Treatise on Communication with the Spirit World), and texts such as *Yi Yuan* (Collected Oddities), *Sou Shen* (Treatise on Divine Beings), the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), *You Ming* (Treatise on the Underworld), *Xiang Yang Qi Jiu* (Treatise on the Elders of Xiangyang), *Chu Guo Xian Xian* (Treatise on the Sages of Chu), *Feng Su Suo Tong* (Treatise on Customs and Traditions), *Qi Shi Suo Ji* (Treatise on Significant Events), *Wu Xing* (Treatise on Wu), *Yang Xian* (Treatise on Yang), *Nan Yue* (Treatise on Nan), and *Xi Jing* (Treatise on Xi), all cite both ancient and modern sources, with a grand and sweeping style. Pei Songzhi, Sheng Hongzhi, and Lu Daozhan, among other scholars, have contributed to these works, and their discussions are vast and endless.

In the current dynasty, there are works such as Yan Gong's "The Biography of the Four Elders of Liang," Tang Lin's "The Records of the Afterlife," Wang Du's "The Record of the Ancient Mirror," Kong Shenyen's "The Chronicles of the Supernatural," and Zhao Ziqin's "The Record of Fate." Additionally, there are stories passed down by figures like Li Yucheng and Zhang Xiaojue. Among these, Dai Junfu of Tao County is the most profound in his understanding of the mysterious. He is a descendant of An Dao and follows in the footsteps of Ruosi. Mo was a high-ranking official in the Jin dynasty, and Kui was a hermit in Wu. The family has maintained its literary elegance and reputation. During the early years of the Zhide era, when the world began to fall into chaos, Kuang and I passed the same imperial examination together. Dai Junfu started as a proofreader in the imperial library and ended his career as a local government official in Raozhou. He was fifty-seven years old at the time of his death and had a collection of twenty volumes of writings. This work, spanning twenty volumes and using one thousand sheets of paper, amounts to more than one hundred thousand words. Although his life was cut short, his resonant voice can still serve the spirits. His two sons, Yue and Yong, expressed their father's wishes and wept, asking their father's friend Kuang to write a preface for the work.

Xu Fu Xu Fu, courtesy name Junfang, was a man of unknown origin. During the reign of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, many wrongfully dead people lay on the roads in the region of Dayuan. Birds frequently carried grass and covered the faces of the dead, causing them to revive instantly. Officials reported this to Emperor Qin Shi Huang, who then sent envoys with the grass to consult Mr. Guiguzi of the northern suburb. Mr. Guiguzi said, "This is the immortal grass of Zuzhou in the East Sea, which grows in jade fields and is also known as the herb that nourishes the spirit. Its leaves resemble gugu (a type of aquatic plant), it does not grow in clusters, and one plant can revive a thousand people." Emperor Qin Shi Huang believed that it could be found and sent Xu Fu and 3,000 young men and women each, in large ships, to search for Zuzhou. They did not return, and their fate is unknown later. Until Shen Xi attained the Dao, the Yellow Emperor and Laozi sent Xu Fu as an envoy, riding a white tiger chariot, while Lord Duoshi Simasheng rode a dragon chariot, and Attendant Bao Yanzhi rode a white deer chariot, all coming to welcome Shen Xi and then leaving. Thus, later generations knew that Xu Fu had attained the Dao. In the early Tang Dynasty during the Kaiyuan period, there was a scholar suffering from half of his body turning black and withered. The imperial physician Zhang Shangrong and others could not diagnose it. The scholar gathered his family and said, "With such a condition, how long can it last? Hearing that there are immortals in the great sea, one should seek the elixir of immortality to cure this illness." His family tried to stop him but failed. He then took his servants and provisions to the coast of the great sea near Dengzhou. He found an empty boat and loaded his belongings, setting sail with the wind.

After traveling for more than ten days, we approached a solitary island. On the island, there were hundreds of people, as if in the posture of court attendance. In a moment, we reached the shore. By the shore, a woman was washing medicine. I asked who they all were, and the woman pointed and said, "The one sitting on the central bed with white hair is Mr. Xu." I then asked who Mr. Xu was, and the woman replied, "Do you know Xu Fu of the Qin Emperor's time?" I said, "I do." "This is he." Soon after, everyone dispersed, and I went ashore to pay my respects, detailing the entire story and seeking his medical advice. Mr. Xu said, "Your illness, if you meet me, will be cured." Initially, he fed me with delicious food, and all the utensils were strangely small. I felt it was insufficient, and Mr. Xu said, "If you can finish this, it will be another meal, but I fear you won't be able to." I ate it continuously, like several bowls of food, until full. Then I drank, and it was also served in a small vessel, which made me drunk. The next day, he gave me black pills to eat. After eating them, I passed several liters of black liquid, and my illness was cured. I asked to stay and serve him, but Mr. Xu said, "You have a position and status, so it is not appropriate for you to stay. I will send you back with the east wind; do not worry about the long journey back." He also gave me a bag of yellow medicine, saying, "This medicine is good at treating all diseases. If you encounter someone with an illness, you can give it to them in a small measure." I returned to Dengzhou after several days and presented the medicine to the emperor. At the time, Emperor Xuanzong ordered those with illnesses to take it, and they all recovered.

**Pupu Master** Pupu Master, whose origin is unknown. He claims his surname is Pupu and his given name is also Pupu, but no one knows where he comes from. He has lived in Huangtu Mountain, Le'an County, Guangzhou for over thirty years, engaging in deep contemplation and taking apricot kernel pills. His clothing and diet are like those of ordinary people, and he makes a living by selling medicine. In the third year of the Yuan period (likely a mistake), the former magistrate of Wudi County, Wang Tao, lived in Huangtu Mountain. Pupu Master visited him. Wang Tao ordered his son, Bian, to treat him well. Pupu Master then taught Bian the art of making apricot kernel pills. At this time, Bian's brother-in-law, Wu Mingsi, was a Biejia in Guangzhou, and Bian was staying with him. Shortly after, Pupu Master rode the clouds and passed through. Thousands of people and officials witnessed this event. Bian looked up and said, "Master, you haven't finished teaching me the art of making pills, how can you leave me behind?" By this time, Pupu Master had already passed through fifteen times. No one could understand this phenomenon. When Bian spoke, the onlookers were shocked, and someone reported this to Governor Li Xiuguang. Li summoned Wu Mingsi and asked him, "Your nephew has been associating with a sorcerer, you should take action against him."

The uncle then ordered the official to go and summon the Daoist. The official arrived at the Daoist's residence, and the Daoist soon followed, reporting the situation in detail. The Daoist said, "I am a Daoist. I do not wish to meet officials." The official replied, "If he shows respect, one should then transform him. If one acts rashly and loses propriety, one should use authority to restrain him, making his heart submit to the Dao, is that not acceptable?" The Daoist said, "Good." They then went to the Xiu Guang office. Xiu Guang sat down to receive them and scolded, "If you are a Daoist, you should have left immediately. Now that you have gone and returned, you are an omen of evil." The Daoist said, "Ma Gu, Cai Jing, Wang Fangping, Kong Shen, and the Two Mao, among others, came to ask me questions. I had not finished explaining to them, so I stayed behind, not for any other reason." Xiu Guang became even angrier and shouted at his attendants to seize the Daoist. Dragons and tigers appeared beside him, and the Daoist rode them away. They flew over a hundred feet, and dark clouds gathered around them. Soon, thunder and lightning struck, breaking more than ten court locust trees and shaking and damaging the government buildings. All spectators fled in panic. Xiu Guang fled in fear and lost his headband.

The official seized the headgear, led his wife and children barefoot out of the office, and then moved the residence. Xiu Guang reported the situation to the emperor, and Emperor Xuanzong issued an edict to rename Le'an County as Xianju County, establishing the Xiantang Temple at the place where the Master lived, renaming Huangtu Village as Xiantang Village, and supervising the construction by the county magistrate Yan Zhenghui. Wang Bian was appointed as the abbot of the temple, also as a Censor-in-Attendant, and bestowed on him the title of Tongzhen Master. Due to taking apricot elixir, Wang Bian remained youthful, reaching the age of sixty-six, yet his appearance was that of someone in their forties, with strength commensurate with his age. Later, a woman named Xie Ziran from Houshou State ascended to the heavens during the day. When Xie Ziran was learning the Dao, immortals frequently descended, including one surnamed Cui, who was also named Cui, and one surnamed Du, who was also named Du, and similarly for others, making their names similar to those of the Master Pupu.

Perhaps the immortals have descended into the human world, not wishing to be known by name among the common people? "Later, a traveler was passing through the outskirts of Yiyang when, as sunset approached, he was unable to reach the village ahead. Suddenly, he saw a thatched cottage by the roadside and went to seek lodging. Inside the room, there was only an old man. The traveler asked the reason for his visit. He replied, 'The sky is overcast, and the days are short; it has become dark, and I wish to stay for the night.' The old man said, 'Staying overnight is not a problem, but there is no food.' After a while, the traveler, who was very hungry, received a few pills of medicine from the old man. Eating them made him full, and after dawn, he left. On his return, he suddenly saw the old man riding a five-colored cloud, traversing the ground for dozens of feet. The traveler hastily bowed, watching it gradually becoming distant. Upon reaching Anlu, he told many people about it. The county official thought it was misleading the people and detained and interrogated him. The traveler said, 'I actually saw immortals.' However, he had no way to clear himself, so he spoke to the empty air, saying, 'Why did you appear, why am I now facing unpredictable punishment?' After finishing speaking, a five-colored cloud came from the north, and the old man was sitting in the cloud. Only then was the traveler released. The county official bowed twice and asked about the old man's surname and given name. The old man replied, 'I am a rustic person, what surname and given name do I have?' The state official drew a picture and reported it to the emperor, who ordered the establishment of a temple for Mr. Pupu at the site of the thatched hut. Now it exists."

○张李二公 During the early Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong (713-756 CE), there were two gentlemen named Zhang and Li who shared the same aspirations and associated with each other, studying Dao (the Way) at Mount Tai. After a long time, Li, due to his royal lineage, desired officialdom and bid farewell to return home. Zhang said, "Everyone has their own aspirations; serving as an official is his lord's will, why should one feel ashamed?" By the end of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, Li had risen to the position of Grand Judge (大理丞) and, during the An Lushan Rebellion, fled with his family from the Wu Pass to take refuge in Xiangyang. Soon after, he was sent on a mission to Yangzhou, where he encountered Zhang on the road. Zhang was dressed in tattered clothes and appeared as if lost, and Li felt pity for him, asking to share the same lodging. Zhang replied, "My host has a good livelihood." He invited Li to go with him. Upon arrival, they found a grand and magnificent courtyard, with splendid attendants, resembling a noble person. Li was greatly surprised and asked, "How could it be like this?" Zhang warned him not to speak, as he might be laughed at. After they had eaten a sumptuous meal, Zhang commanded five female performers, all holding their own instruments. Among them was a zither player who very much resembled Li's wife. Li looked at her intently several times. Zhang asked why, and Li pointed to the zither player, saying, "She looks like my wife, could I not be attached to her?" Zhang laughed and said, "There are similar people in the world."

When the gathering was about to disperse, Zhang called out to the woman holding the zither, tied a quince to her belt, and then sent her away. He asked Li, "How much money do you want to fulfill your wish?" Li replied, "If I get three hundred thousand coins, I will handle my own affairs." Zhang had an old hat and told Li, "You can take this to the medicine shop and ask at Wang's old house. Zhang San ordered to take this to get three hundred thousand coins. He will give it to you." They then parted ways. The next day, Li went to the door, but the pavilion and hall were overgrown and neglected, the door had been locked for a long time, and there were no signs of human activity. He then asked the neighbors about Zhang San. The neighbors said, "This used to be Liu Daoxuan's house, and no one has lived there for over ten years." Li was surprised and pondered for a long time before taking the hat to Wang's house to ask for money. Wang told him to send the hat to his family and ask, "Is this indeed Zhang San's old hat?" His daughter replied, "The green thread he previously tied is still there." Li asked who Zhang was, and Wang said, "He was a customer who came fifty years ago for lingzhi fungus. Now there are over two thousand coins in the medicine shop." Li took the money and returned, but he sought Zhang again and never found him. Later, he returned to Xiangyang and searched for the quince on his wife's belt, and indeed found it. He asked about the reason, and she said, "Last night I dreamed that five or six people chased me, saying that Zhang the deity had called me to play the zither. When we parted, he tied a quince to my belt." It was then known that Zhang had become a deity.

During the Tianbao period of the Tang dynasty, there was a man named Liu Qingzhen, who, along with twenty of his disciples, was making tea in Shouzhou. Each person brought a load of tea as goods, and they encountered bandits in Chenliu. Someone guided them, telling them to go to Wei Prefecture. Liu Qingzhen and his companions went there again and met an old monk who led them to Wutai Mountain. Liu Qingzhen and his companions feared the labor and hardship, and since the Wutai Temple was still far away, they invited Liu Qingzhen and his companions to return to a hermitage for the night. Liu Qingzhen and his companions privately discussed and suspected that the old monk was the Bodhisattva Manjushri. They followed the monk back. After walking several miles, they finally arrived at the hermitage, where the halls and buildings were solemn and clean, and all were filled with reverence and solemnity. The monk gave a sermon, greatly opening the door of convenience, and Liu Qingzhen and his companions resolved to become monks, following him as their disciple.

After accumulating over twenty years, the old monk suddenly told Qingzhen and others, "A great demon is about to arise, and you will surely suffer from it. You should prepare beforehand; if not, it will ruin your religious practices." He then ordered Qingzhen and others to kneel for a long time, and the monk contained water and sprayed it around, reciting secret mantras. Qingzhen and others all turned into stone, understanding clearly but not moving. In a moment, several dozen officials and soldiers from Dai Prefecture came to the platform to make some arrests. They arrived at Qingzhen's residence but only saw wild grass and stones, so they all left. Later in the evening, the old monk came again and sprayed water on Qingzhen and others, who became human again. Qingzhen and others realized their divine nature, knowing they had met a Bodhisattva, and all strove to progress. After more than a month, the monk said, "Now another great demon is about to arise, and they will certainly search for you. What can be done about it? I want to send you far away. Will you all go?" Qingzhen and others agreed. The monk ordered everyone to close their eyes and warned them, "First, do not peek; it will ruin everything. Just feel when you reach the ground. If you reach the mountains, you will be safe."

Seeing a large tree, one should seek shelter together. If the tree produces medicine, it should also be nurtured." They each gave a pill and said, "Eating this will no longer make you hungry, but you should only think of the sacred path, which is the bridge to the other world." After finishing speaking, they performed a bow, and after the bow, they closed their eyes. Slowly ascending, they were in the void for about half a day, and their feet eventually touched the ground, and they saw a great forest. They met a woodcutter, who asked about the place's name, and it was Lushan. After traveling more than ten miles, they saw a large vine tree, with a circumference of about five or six people, its green shade blocking the sun. Qingzhen and others were happy and said, "This must be the strange tree the master spoke of." They each cut grass and sat down. A few days later, the tree produced white mushrooms, fresh and lustrous, constantly fluttering. The group said to each other, "This is the miraculous medicine the master spoke of." They collected and shared them to eat.

In the midst of them, one person tricked the others into finishing their food first. The companions were all angry and scolded him, saying, "You have violated the teachings of our master." However, since it had already happened, they could not physically attack him. After a long while, he suddenly disappeared. Looking up, they saw him calmly sitting at the top of a tree. Qingzhen and the others said, "You were able to ascend because you swallowed a potion." The person did not come down. After seven days, his entire body was covered in green hair. Suddenly, a crane flew above him, and he addressed the nineteen people, saying, "I have indeed wronged you, but now I have attained enlightenment. I will leave you to meet the emperor in the heavens above. You should each strive to achieve the highest truth." Qingzhen and the others tried to invite him down from the tree to say goodbye, but the immortal paid no heed and ascended into the clouds, eventually disappearing. Qingzhen and the others lost the potion and dispersed back into the human world. This story was told by Zhang Lun from Zhongshan, who heard it from Qingzhen and others.

A Man from Machang Village A man from a village in Machang County, Xunyang Prefecture, had a pig that was eating his crops. He became angry and prepared to shoot it with a bow and arrow. The next day, the pig returned, and the man shot it, hitting it. The pig ran several miles and entered the main gate of a grand building. Inside, he saw a magnificent hall with a room adorned with luxurious furnishings. An elderly man with a white beard and a walking stick was seated, accompanied by a young boy in green clothes. The man asked the elderly man how he had come to be there. He replied, "The pig was eating my crops, so I shot it, and it followed me here." The elderly man said, "Is it not too much to take someone else's ox by trampling their field?" He then ordered a young boy to serve the man wine. They walked forward several steps to the main hall, where they saw a group of immortals. The immortals wore feathered robes and black headdresses, some playing dice, others playing chess, and some drinking. The young boy went to the drinking area and instructed, "The Master has ordered that this man be given a cup of wine." After drinking, the man felt no hunger. They then went to another place where there were several beds, each occupied by someone holding a book, as if listening to a lecture. After a while, they returned to the main hall. The Master scolded the gatekeeper, saying, "Why did you open the door, allowing the pig to come and go without knowing?" He then told the man, "This is not a real pig; you should leave." He ordered the young boy to escort the man out. The man asked who the elderly man was, and the boy replied, "He is known as the River Master, appointed by Heaven to teach the *I Ching* to the immortals." The man asked, "Who are you?" The boy answered, "I am Wang Bi. Since I received the *I Ching*, it has been nearly five hundred years, and I have not yet fully grasped its profound meanings, so I have been punished to guard the gate." After the man left, the boy kicked a large stone to block the door, and the man was never seen again.

In the year of Guangde of the Tang dynasty (764 CE), a bandit named Yuan Chao from Linhai County attacked Yongjia. His ship encountered a storm and was carried eastward for thousands of miles. From afar, they saw a mountain lush with greenery, and it had a city wall that shone with five colors. They turned the ship to anchor. Upon entering a temple, they saw glass tiles and tortoiseshell walls. Inside the hallways, there was no one present. The room contained over twenty small animals, and all the objects were made of gold, with no other materials. There were also bedding and mats that were very bright and ornate, mostly made of fine brocade from Sichuan. There was also a golden city, with piles of broken gold that were too numerous to count.

The bandits, seeing no one, began to take things. Suddenly, a woman emerged from the golden city. She was about six feet tall, dressed in brocade, and wore a purple gauze skirt. She said to the bandits, "Are you not part of the Yuan Chao faction? How did you get here? These objects belong to you? How dare you take them!" She continued, "I saw a dog before, but you call this a dog? No, it's a dragon. The things you are taking, I truly do not mind, but I fear the dragons will be angry and pull your boat forward, leading to your death in an instant. You should return them quickly." The bandits prostrated themselves and returned the items to their original places. They then asked where they were, and the woman replied, "This is the place where the compassionate immortal of Mirror Lake Mountain practices Dao. You have no reason to join Yuan Chao's bandits; within ten days, you will face great calamity. You should be very cautious." The bandits then begged for a favorable wind to take them back to the coast. The woman turned around and gave orders, and soon the wind rose. The bandits bowed and bid farewell. They set sail and reached the coast in a few days. However, the ship ran aground on the sandbank and could not proceed. They were killed by the imperial troops, but only six or seven women survived. In the eastern part of Zhejiang, the deputy military officer Xie Quan received a female servant named Qu Ye, who personally recounted the story.

### 石巨 Shí Jù was a Hu person who lived in Yōuzhōu. He had a fondness for alchemy and elixirs. During the middle of the Dàlì era, he fell ill for over a hundred days, becoming emaciated but his spirit remained undiminished. Suddenly, he told his son, "There is a fortune-teller at the Héqiáo bridge. You can temporarily visit to ask him questions." The son returned and said, "There was no fortune-teller, only an elderly woman." Shí Jù replied, "That is exactly the one to summon." The son invited her to their house. Shí Jù lay on a paper screen in front of the hall, and the old woman directly went to where Shí Jù was, speaking very quietly and confidentially. The son listened from outside but could not hear what they were saying. After a long time, the old woman left. Several days later, in the morning, white cranes descended from the sky, passing through the paper screen where Shí Jù lay, entering his room. They sang and called for a mealtime, then suddenly rose into the air, transforming into a single white crane and flying away. The son went to see, but no longer saw Shí Jù, and he followed the crane away. They arrived at a large mound on the east side of the city, where they saw dozens of white cranes flying together into the sky, gradually disappearing. Chángshǐ Lǐ Huáxiān summoned his son and asked about the incident, and the son answered as he had seen it. Lǐ Huáxiān did not believe him and said to the son, "This is a superstitious or false story. Your father must have become a deity. Our territory is suffering from drought. I will bring rain, and if it does not rain, I will kill you." The son returned home, burned incense, and offered prayers. Lǐ Huáxiān sent Jīn Cānjūn with wine and meat offerings to Shí Jù's residence to offer a sacrifice. On that day, it rained heavily, satisfying both near and far. Lǐ Huáxiān, seeing the miraculous and effective results, built a temple at Shí Jù's residence and worshipped there regularly during festivals.

There was an elderly man named Wang, who often sold medicine in the Western Capital and had been seen over many generations. A man named Li, who served as a granary official, lived in Shengye Lane. Knowing that Wang was a magician, Li always held him in high regard and treated him with extra respect. Therefore, Wang frequently relied on the Li family for over ten years. Later, Li asked to accompany Wang into the mountains, and Wang agreed, so they set out with several servants and attendants, riding horses together. They traveled for over a hundred miles, passing through high and steep peaks, climbing vines and trees, and ascending several miles, reaching a place not trodden by human feet. Wang said, "Traveling with you, I still fear that we might not reach the realm of immortals. Since it is not a place for servants and attendants, they should all be sent away." Li followed his advice, and together they reached the peak, where the fields were flat, herb gardens and stone springs adorned the landscape, and beautiful scenery was arranged in order. Soon, they arrived at the entrance of the forest, and several Daoists came to inquire about Wang, knowing that he had invited a guest, so they waited for them. Li followed Wang to their dwelling, a humble thatch and bamboo hut, which was graceful and inviting. Inside, there were dozens of students who saw Li and each came to ask about his relatives. Some remained silent, while others looked sorrowful, saying, "The master is not here. It is best to stay a little longer. We will prepare simple vegetarian meals, no different from the world below." They set food for Li.

After several days, a colorful cloud covered the ground, and three white cranes descended from the clouds. The scholars then appeared, as if to greet and wait. Soon after, someone said, "The master has arrived." They saw an old man with white beard and hair, like a crane, coming from the clouds. Wang Lao led Li to bow and pay respects on the side of the road. The master asked Wang Lao, "Why did you bring someone else here?" The scholars bowed and paid respects, then each went to their room, and Li entered a room as well. It was quite hot, so Li went out to find a spring to bathe in. After walking over a hundred steps, he reached a stone spring and saw dozens of white cranes descending from the rocky mountains. They came to the stone, arranged in rows, and soon began to play music, with clear and bright sounds, unlike anything found in the human world. Li crouched down to listen to the wonderful sounds, and after the music ended, the cranes flew away. Li returned and told the master about the incident. The master asked, "Did you not offend the immortal officials?" Li replied, "Not dare." The master said to Li, "You have official duties and should not stay here; you can come only after your duties are completed." Then he ordered Wang Lao to send Li out, saying, "The mountain needs two oxen; you can deliver them to the vine." Li bought the oxen and delivered them, and then there was no more visible path.

○ Li Xianren In Luoyang, there was a woman named Gao Wuniang, who was beautiful. She remarried Li Xianren, who was a celestial being who had been demoted to Earth. Li Xianren, known as the celestial being, lived in Luoyang with Gao Wuniang and practiced alchemy, which he taught to her. By the end of the Yuan Dynasty, they had been married for several years. One night, after the fifth drum (a traditional Chinese time signal), Gao Wuniang heard her name called from the air. She got up and went outside. After speaking with Li Xianren, he returned and said to Gao Wuniang, "I am a celestial being. I was sent to Earth for a minor offense. My time here is over, and I must return. Although we have been together for many years, it is hard to say goodbye. After I leave, you should continue practicing alchemy to support yourself, but be careful not to share these practices with others. Do not perform alchemical practices widely for others, as it will not only harm you but also bring misfortune to others." After Li Xianren flew away, Gao Wuniang initially followed his advice. However, she later sold a lot of silver, which was reported by the local authorities. Li Qi, a local official in Henan, heard about this and reported it to the high-ranking officials. Within a year, both Li Xianren and Gao Wuniang died, and people believed it was divine retribution.

A Hermit of Mount Heng A hermit of Mount Heng, whose name is unknown, often sold medicine and stayed at the temple on Mount Heng. Sometimes he went without eating for four or five days, which surprised the monks. He again sold medicine to the monks, and when the monks saw that he did not eat, they knew he was a strange person and treated him very respectfully. Just as musicians brought a beautiful girl to the temple, the monks wanted to take her, but her parents demanded five hundred thousand coins, and no one dared to proceed. The hermit heard about the girl's marriage and invited the monks to see her. He was pleased and wanted to take her, still carrying two bars of gold, each weighing two hundred taels. He said to the girl's father, "This gold is worth seven hundred strings of cash, but I won't count it." After giving the gold, he said. The music teacher was then serving as an official and suddenly sent them off. The hermit showed them his residence, saying, "It is about forty miles from here, but when you reach the mountain, you will know." The girl's parents, after finishing their business, remembered their daughter and went to visit her. They saw a grand red gate, knocked on the door, and the hermit and the girl came out to welcome them. At first, they ate one meal, and then they were no longer hungry. They stayed for five or six days and did not think about food. When the parents were about to return, the hermit gave them five bars of gold wrapped in colorful cloth as a gift. He said to the parents, "This place is deep and secluded, no longer inhabited by people, so there is no need to seek it again." Later, the parents returned, but they only saw mountain grass, no longer inhabited by people, and then they realized it was a cave of immortals.

○Pan Zunshi Sage Master Pan Zunshi, a Taoist priest of Mount Song, was named Fa Zheng and was indeed a high-ranking sage. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong in the Tang Dynasty (713-756 CE), he told his disciple Master Sima Lianshi, "Tao Hongjing is the Lord of Mount Song, and it has been a hundred years since then. Recently, he requested a replacement from the Heavenly Emperor, who instructed him to nominate someone he knew to take his place. Tao Hongjing nominated me. The records have been finalized, and I cannot stay in the mortal world for long." A few days later, he underwent the transformation of a sage into an immortal and departed. Later, in the West of Songyang Temple in Dengfeng County, there was a dragon spring. A local resident named Zhang Tan washed his undergarments at the spring when suddenly he was taken by an unseen force. He traveled several miles and arrived at a grand mansion, where dragons were present at the entrance. After entering more than ten steps, he saw a large hall where Master Fa Zheng was sitting, holding a red ink brush to write. He asked Zhang Tan, "Are you from the side of the temple? Do you recognize me?" Zhang Tan replied, "I do recognize you; you are Master Pan Zunshi." Master Fa Zheng asked Zhang Tan, "Why did you defile the abode of the dragons?" Zhang Tan bowed deeply and apologized. Then, Master Fa Zheng asked, "Do you know Master Sima the Taoist?" Zhang Tan replied, "Yes, I do." Master Fa Zheng said, "Now I will let you return." He then took a white feather fan from the table and said to Zhang Tan, "Please deliver this fan to Master Sima the Taoist. Why didn't you come to see me and why did you linger in the pleasures of the world?" Someone escorted Zhang Tan out of the water, and he saw Master Fa Zheng's body lying on the shore. Zhang Tan felt a deep aversion and suddenly woke up, as if from a dream, and he came back to life. When Master Sima the Taoist saw the fan, he wept and said, "This is the fan my master usually held. When he passed away, it was placed in his coffin. Now you have brought it, proving that my master is still alive and not a mere illusion." He then delved deeper into the mountains and passed away a few years later.

**A Woman from the Qin Dynasty** During the Tang dynasty, the governor of the state of Dai, fearing the rise of false or evil events due to the presence of many guest monks on Wutai Mountain, expelled those without proper authority. The monks, fearing expulsion, temporarily hid in the mountains and valleys. One such monk, named Fahuang, deeply entered the Yanmen Mountains and found a secluded ravine with a stone cave large enough for people to enter and exit. He carried a lot of dry rations, wanting to live in this mountain, so he followed the cave in. After hundreds of steps, it gradually widened, reaching a flat area. He crossed a stream and reached the other bank, where the sun and moon were very bright. After walking another two li, he reached a thatched house. Inside, there was a woman wearing clothes made of grass leaves, with a beautiful appearance. Seeing the monk, she was startled and asked, "Who are you?" The monk replied, "I am a human." The woman laughed and said, "Is there anyone with such a body?" The monk said, "I serve the Buddha, and the Buddha requires one to renounce the physical body, hence this." She then asked what the Buddha is, and the monk explained thoroughly. They looked at each other and laughed, saying, "The words are very reasonable." She then asked about the main principles, and the monk explained the *Diamond Sutra*. She praised it several times. The monk then asked, "What world is this?" The woman said, "I am from the Qin dynasty, following Meng Tian in building the Great Wall. Meng Tian often used women, and we could not bear the hardship, so we fled here. Initially, we ate grass roots and thus survived. Since then, I do not know how many years have passed, and I no longer return to the human world." She then kept the monk, feeding him with grass roots, which were bitter and hard to eat. The monk stayed there for over forty days, temporarily left to find food in the human world. When he reached the state of Dai, prepared food and went again, but became lost and did not know where he was.

A Woman Named He Ernang In Guangzhou, there was a woman named He Ernang who made shoes for a living. At the age of twenty, she lived with her mother. She had never practiced Daoist techniques, but suddenly told her mother, "Living here is boring; I want to travel." The next day, she flew away. She ascended to the Luofu Mountain Temple. The mountain monk asked her why she had come, and she replied, "I wish to serve a monk." From then on, she permanently resided there. Initially, she did not eat or drink, but she would gather wild fruits to supplement the vegetarian meals for the temple community. It was unknown where she obtained these fruits. To the north of Luofu Mountain is Xunzhou, which is four hundred li from Nanhai. There is a bayberry tree at the Xunzhou mountain temple, with a girth of several dozen zhi. He Ernang would often pick its fruits and return to the temple with them. Later, a monk from the Xunzhou mountain temple came to Luofu Mountain and said, "On a certain day in a certain month, a fairy came to pick bayberries." When verified, it was indeed the day when He Ernang had picked the bayberries. Thus, people far and near knew that she had attained immortality. Later, she no longer stayed at the temple but would come every ten days or so. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713-756 CE), an imperial decree was issued to send a court official to Guangzhou to seek out He Ernang. He was found and brought with the official to the capital. On the way, the court official was attracted to her beauty and wanted to court her but did not say anything. Suddenly, she said, "If the court official has such thoughts, I cannot stay here." After speaking, she leaped into the air and disappeared, and no one knew where she went. Afterward, she disappeared completely and never returned to the human world.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, in the year of Kaiyuan, there was a female Daoist named Bian Dongxuan from Zaoqiang County, Jizhou. She had practiced Daoism and taken elixirs for forty years and was eighty-four years old. Suddenly, an old man appeared, carrying a bowl of soup, and came to see Bian Dongxuan. He said, "I am an immortal from the Three Mountains. Because you have attained the Dao, I have come to take you. This soup is made of jade essence powder, which is highly valued by immortals. Many who have recently attained the Dao have consumed it. You should eat it without doubt, and in seven days, you will surely achieve immortality." After eating the soup, the old man said, "I will go first now, and you will follow later." With that, he disappeared. The next day, Bian Dongxuan suddenly felt her body become lighter, and her teeth and hair were completely changed. She told her disciples, "The Upper Purity has summoned me. I will not be long in coming. I think of you all and hope you do not grieve. Continue to practice my Dao, and do not be attached to worldly affairs, for they are like a wooden coffin for the soul to scatter!" On the seventh day, the disciples went to inquire about her well-being early in the morning. They saw purple clouds gathering and condensing, filling the courtyard. They also heard voices in the air and dared not enter, so they all stopped outside the door. Soon, the door opened, and Bian Dongxuan rose into the air on a purple cloud, standing more than a hundred feet above the ground. She bid farewell to her disciples and fellow practitioners. At that time, Governor Yuan Fu and several thousand officials and commoners were all looking at her from afar, paying their respects. Soon, the sun rose, and the purple vapor transformed into a five-colored cloud. Bian Dongxuan slowly ascended into the sky and disappeared after a while.

Zhang Lianqiao, a female Taoist nun from Huangmei County, was eight or nine years old. She often carried a bottle to draw water. One day, she suddenly saw a lotus flower in the well, like a small plate, gradually emerging from the well mouth. When she went to take it, it would shrink; if she didn't take it, it would emerge again, like this several times. She then entered the well. Her family was surprised that she did not return for a long time, so they went to look for her and saw Zhang Lianqiao standing on the water of the well. When she came out, she suddenly developed a laughing illness. They asked her why, and she said, "There was someone behind me touching my armpit with their hand, causing unbearable itchiness." Her parents believed it was caused by ghosts or demons and secretly moved her to her maternal uncle's family at midnight, and she stopped laughing. After a while, she returned to her home, saying, "I am hungry and want to eat." She ate several dou of rice every day, even at night, and they placed pickled vegetables and dishes by her sleeping place, and she would eat them as soon as she woke up, like this for six or seven days. Then she began to smell the food, and from then on, she no longer ate. Sometimes during festivals, they would give her three or four dates. Her parents then ordered her to become a Taoist nun.

At the age of eighteen, Lianqiao was sitting alone in a temple during the day when she saw two coins fall from the sky. She got up to pick them up. A neighbor's wife pushed down a fence and also tried to pick them up. Lianqiao stood on the coins and gave her three pills of yellow medicine, quickly taking them. The woman grabbed one pill and ran away. As a result, both of them swallowed two pills, and shortly after, they both died. Lianqiao woke up shortly after and felt strong and clear-minded, twice as much as usual. The woman who swallowed one pill revived after a day and ate and drank as usual. By the end of the Tianbao era, Lianqiao was still in the temple, suddenly missing her parents, as if she had a purpose to go somewhere. The common people and local officials all saw a colorful cloud surrounding a treasure carriage descending from the sky. People thought Lianqiao had left and rushed to see it. Lianqiao initially felt nothing, and the cloud also dissipated. An interpreter said, "Because there were many people, it did not leave." Lianqiao is still here, with her sides joined together, her body withered and emaciated, and she no longer eats.

A Taoist Priest Named Fu Shentong A Taoist priest named Fu Shentong lived in Shu Prefecture. He was orphaned and poor in his youth, and he always herded cattle for others to sustain himself. At the place where Shentong herded cattle, he often saw a Taoist priest coming and going, and he therefore paid respect and became acquainted with him. After several years, the Taoist priest asked Shentong, "Can you be my disciple?" Shentong replied, "Very willing." The priest then led Shentong into the water and said to him, "When I enter, you should follow me. Do not be afraid." After entering, the priest led Shentong to his dwelling place, which was clean and neat. There were medicine bags and alchemical furnaces, and under the bed were all great elixirs. The priest then had Shentong watch the fire and taught him the art of turning base metals into gold and silver. After three years, Shentong was already in his twenties and missed the human world. When the Taoist priest was not present, Shentong stole the elixirs and stored them separately in one place. When the priest returned and asked where the elixirs were, Shentong claimed not to see them.

The Taoist sighed and said, "I wish to impart the essence of Tao to you, but you are like this now, how could I teach you? I have thoroughly mastered all methods, but they are of no use for longevity." He led me to another path and sent me away, then left. Tongshen was very pleased, traversing rugged caves, sustaining himself with medicine, and after more than seventy days, he finally returned to the human world. Later, he grew weary of worldly affairs and thought of the Taoist. He heard that the Taoist came and went at the Kaiyuan Temple in Shu Prefecture, so he requested ordination and was registered there. He tried this way dozens of times, but never met the Taoist. Tongshen secretly gave a hundred pounds of gold to the servant in his room, instructing him to report if the Taoist came. After receiving the gold, the servant frequently reported, but Tongshen never saw the Taoist. The governor of Shu Prefecture reported to Emperor Xuanzong that Tongshen understood the alchemical arts, and when the emperor tested him, it was all verified. He would always use a clay pot to boil mercury, and when the emperor requested, he would add a small amount of medicine, and it would instantly transform. The emperor sought to obtain the method, but it was interrupted by the An Lushan Rebellion.

○Zheng Xiangru Zheng Xian was skilled in poetry and fond of alcohol, with a very carefree nature. Emperor Xuanzong admired his open-mindedness and wanted to appoint him to a government office. However, because he was not diligent in official duties, a special academy (Guangwen Academy) was established, and Zheng Qian was appointed as a doctor (likely a scholar-official). His reputation was very well-known, and his residence was frequented by talented and distinguished individuals. V. Z., a man from Cangzhou who had passed the imperial examination and come to the capital, heard of Zheng Qian's reputation and visited him due to their shared surname. Zheng Qian treated him as his younger brother or nephew, but did not show much respect to him due to his old-fashioned appearance. A few days later, V. Z. visited again, and Zheng Qian sat alone with him. Zheng Qian asked about his skills and talents, and V. Z. responded with a smile, saying, "Uncle, you do not know me, and should treat me as an ordinary person. However, people are not easy to understand, and since you asked, I will speak my mind. If I were in Confucius's school, I would rank in one of the four categories and be superior to Yan Hui and Zizhan. If you were in Confucius's school, you would not be ranked in the four categories. I do not meet my time and am only responding to the usual routine. I should only remain silent and obscure myself. However, the country will change its era name by the 30th year of Kaiyuan, and 15 years later, there will be a disaster." Zheng Xiangru's prediction about the future of the country is both intriguing and ominous, reflecting the complex and often unpredictable nature of historical events.

When the world reached this state, military conflicts arose. A traitorous minister usurped the throne at this time. Shu Ying was appointed to a false office and listed among the officials in the court, still being burdened by it, wishing to maintain the loyalty of a minister, and thus avoiding it. After this, the common people continued to suffer. Xiang Ru passed the imperial examination this year, and after five selections, he was appointed as the magistrate of Xin'an in Quzhou. By the third year, he died in Quzhou. His official salary was like this, and it could not be forced or obtained. That year, he indeed passed the imperial examination. Xiang Ru bid farewell to Qian and returned to his hometown. By the time of the selection, he saw Qian in the capital. The Ministry of Personnel appointed him as the magistrate of Xin'an, and Xiang Ru showed a happy expression. He then bid farewell to Qian and took up his post. At the end of the first year, he asked the official in charge of the examination in Quzhou, "How is Governor Zheng?" The official replied, "Very good." He asked about his governance, and the official said, "Like the ancients." At the end of the second year, he was examined again, and the official said, "No problem." At the end of the third year, he was asked again, and the official said, "Xiang Ru fell ill suddenly and died." Qian was greatly surprised and then thought about his words. In the fifteenth year of Tianbao, An Lushan rebelled, sent troops to enter the capital, and seized all officials and sent them to Luoyang. Qian was then a Zuo Shi Lang (a literary official) and was demoted to the position of Water Department Langzhong. When the capital was recaptured, he was exiled to the position of Sihu in Quzhou and died in office, just as Xiang Ru had said.

In Duzhou, there are two Vajra statues at Kaiyuan Temple. The world calls them divine, and birds and sparrows dare not approach. Those who pray for illness are repeatedly validated, and those who come and go show respect. During the Yuan dynasty, a local official hosted a banquet on the temple's gatehouse. Everyone said that the Vajra statues were there and could not be disrespected. One person said, "They are just statues, what can they do!" and put wine and meat into their mouths. Soon after, the clouds darkened and lightning flashed, and there was both wind and thunder. The wine and meat flew about, and the crowd was in danger and fear. Only the person who defiled the statues was dragged out of the building dozens of feet and struck dead.

#### Chang'an County Condemned Prisoner During the Tang Dynasty, in Chang'an County, a condemned prisoner, after spending forty or more days in prison, continuously recited the *Diamond Sutra*. On the day of his execution, his shackles fell off. The shackles then emitted light, stretching for dozens of zhang, illuminating the entire county. The county magistrate reported this to Emperor Xuanzong, who then pardoned the prisoner's crime. #### Lu During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Lu who was living temporarily in Huazhou. One day, while sitting idly in his office, he saw two officials in yellow robes enter the door. Lu asked who they were, and they replied, "We are village officials, carrying a summons to bring you." Lu was greatly startled and asked, "Why are you bringing me?" He then asked to see the summons. The seal on the document read "Weixian," and upon opening it, the writing was incorrect and did not resemble human writing. Surprised, he questioned the officials, who said, "We were following orders to bring you, but we do not know why." Soon, he saw a horse already prepared at the steps, and had no choice but to mount the horse and leave. Looking back, he saw his body sitting on the bed, which filled him with dread. In the haste of the moment, he did not know it was dead, and also saw the horse exit without going through the door, walking on the walls. He was filled with shock and wept, realizing he was destined to die and wishing he could say goodbye to his mother and sisters. After traveling about ten miles, he arrived at a magnificent city. He asked what city this was, and the official said, "It is a kingdom, where you are to be brought to your place of service." After entering the city, the official wanted to take Lu to the king, passing through a courtyard. Lu asked what courtyard this was, and the official said, "It is the office of the Grand Censor." Then, Lu asked what the name of the Grand Censor was, and the official said, "His name is Li, with a certain surname." Lu was surprised and delighted, and told the official, "This is my cousin. Have the official introduce me."

After a short while, he emerged, and they were very pleased to see each other. They spoke in detail about the past and invited each other to sit and talk. The doctor said, "Your recitation has brought great merit and virtue. It is because the *Diamond Sutra* is the essence of the sacred teachings, a profound and incomprehensible virtue." When Lu first entered the courtyard, he saw dozens of people, all of whom were of high social status. Later, most of them were found in a net, some without clothes, others with their heads uncovered. Lu asked, "Who are all these people?" They replied, "They are people of high status from the mortal world. Those in the net are there because of heavy sins. If you can give a single teaching, all who see it will ascend to heaven." So, they ordered a high seat to be brought, and Lu was asked to sit on it and recite the *Diamond Sutra*. Some people in the net began to emerge, and by the time the recitation was halfway through, they all rose to the ground, some wearing wide-sleeved robes, others riding in carriages and controlling the clouds. After the recitation ended, all had passed away. When Lu returned to the court, the king called him a teacher and showed him great respect. The king said, "Your virtue is truly incomprehensible, and the calculation is endless." He praised the power of recitation and soon ordered the attendant to send Lu back. Upon arriving home, Lu saw his family crying with disheveled hair, with his body lying on the ground. His heart was deeply moved. Suddenly, a maid came from the courtyard into the hall. The attendant ordered her to follow up the steps. As she approached, her spirit and soul suddenly returned to her body, and thus she came back to life.

○ Chen Libin Chen Libin, a native of Kuaiji, excelled in the imperial examination at the age of twenty, demonstrating great skill in writing and composing poetry that was included in the anthology *Jinmen Ji*. He was appointed as a magistrate in Changcheng. As a young man, he recited the *Diamond Sutra* and often received its assistance in times of difficulty. During the year Yuanzhong, Chen Libin was traveling from Kuaiji to Dongyang. Coincidentally, it had been raining for a long time, and the river water was overflowing. He set sail with twenty or so boats, riding the wind and hoisting the sail. Soon, the sky grew dark, and the wind became stronger. They reached the boundary stone cave, where the water was blocked and surged downward, with waves crashing against the boats, making it impossible to anchor. The twenty or so boats ahead of him all failed at the cave entrance. The boatman was frightened, and Chen Libin hurriedly recited the *Diamond Sutra*. Reaching the place of the surging water, suddenly a creature like a red dragon appeared, supporting the boat, allowing him to ascend. Those who discussed it attributed the success to the recitation of the sutra. ○ Wang Hong Wang Hong, as a young man, engaged in fishing and hunting. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, he once released a hawk to chase a rabbit, entered a hole, and followed it, finding a scroll of the *Diamond Sutra*. From then on, he stopped hunting.

Tian Shi, a military officer of Yizhou, had a fondness for hunting and always kept hawks and dogs. During the early Tianbao era of the Tang dynasty, while he was in Yizhou, he released an eagle, which found a scroll of a book on a thorny bush in a forest. He took it and read it, discovering it to be the Diamond Sutra. From then on, he made a vow to recite it, and over several years, he had recited it more than two thousand times, yet he did not stop hunting. Later, he contracted an illness and suddenly died after several days. He was taken to the underworld, where he saw various birds and animals surrounding several acres, demanding retribution from him. Soon after, he was brought before the king, who asked, "Why so many sins?" Tian Shi had no way to answer. The king ordered the officials to investigate, and ten of his companions were brought in. They arrived at the office of the officials, where the official ordered them to open their mouths. A pill was thrown into each of their mouths, and they instantly turned into fiery beings, covering their entire bodies in flames. In a moment, they turned to ashes and then returned to their human form, repeating this process six or seven times. When it came to Tian Shi, after three pills, there was no sign of fire. The official found this strange. Tian Shi was then brought back to the king, who was fully informed of the truth. The king asked, "What good deeds did you do in life?" Tian Shi replied, "I initially engaged in hunting." The king asked again, and Tian Shi said, "During my lifetime, I found the Diamond Sutra on a thorny bush in Yizhou and recited it over two thousand times." The king said, "Exactly this eradicates all sins." He ordered the attendants to check Tian Shi's record of good deeds, and they reported back as said. The king then ordered Tian Shi to recite the sutra. After just three sheets, Tian Shi looked back and saw that the birds and animals in the courtyard were no longer there. After finishing the recitation, the king praised him, saying, "Reciting it two thousand times extends your life by fifteen years." Thus, he was allowed to return.

○ Li Weiyan Li Weiyan, magistrate of Jiande County, practiced the *Diamond Sutra* from a young age. At the end of the Tianbao era of the Tang dynasty, Li Weiyan served as a military secretary in Yuyao, and upon completing his term, he returned northward, passing through Wuzhang Store. At that time, the embankment of the Shangyu River had collapsed, and the water had dried up. It was the middle of the night, dark and gloomy, and all around was deserted. This road had long been plagued by bandits. Li Weiyan’s boat carried hundreds of bolts of Wu silk, and fearing that it might be taken by thieves, he took a sword and went to the front of the boat to chant the sutra. After the third watch, he saw two torches on the embankment approaching from afar. Li Weiyan suspected they were villagers coming to protect him. The fire moved a hundred steps away from the boat, then turned back. He found this somewhat strange. He chanted the sutra even more loudly, and secretly thought to himself, “Could this not be the power of the *Diamond Sutra*?” At that time, the water in the embankment had dried up, while the water outside was full. Li Weiyan then mentally resolved that if the embankment broke, it would be helped by the water. Halfway through the night, he suddenly heard the sound of flowing water at the bow of the boat. He exclaimed, “The embankment is several zhang wide—how could it possibly break?” After a long while, he gradually felt the boat rising. By morning, the river had filled up. Opposite the boat, there was a hole several chi in size, and thus he realized the power of chanting the *Diamond Sutra*. Li Weiyan’s younger brother, Li Weiyu, who was then serving as the *biejia* (assistant governor) of Qianzhou, saw that his elder brother had benefited from chanting the sutra, and so followed his example. Later, when he was sailing out of a gorge, the water was swift and the oar broke, and the boat was about to capsize. He then forcefully chanted the sutra, and suddenly saw an oar floating downstream. He thus succeeded in crossing. His other relatives also often chanted the *Diamond Sutra*. During the An Lushan Rebellion, one of them hid among the wild grass. As the bandits were approaching, he thought he needed a shoe to run away. Soon after, something fell on his back. He looked in surprise and saw that it was a new shoe.

Stranger Sun Ming Stranger Sun Ming was a man from Yangwu, Zhengzhou, during the Tang Dynasty. He was born into a poor and lowly family and worked as a tenant farmer for the Lu family. He was skilled in reciting the *Diamond Sutra* and would recite it twenty times a day for twenty years. From the moment he began reciting the sutra, he abstained from meat and blood. Later, while reciting the sutra, he suddenly saw two officials come to fetch him. Sun Ming thought they were county officials and went to the county. He traveled about five or six li and arrived at a government office. The gatekeeper said, "The king has already gone out on patrol." The officials then locked Sun Ming in an empty room. The room was spacious, covering about fifty or sixty rooms, and was shaded by trees. After seven days, the king finally arrived. The officials led Sun Ming into the palace, where the king asked, "What good fortune do you have?" Sun Ming replied, "I have been reciting the *Diamond Sutra* for twenty years." The king said, "This is great good fortune!" He then turned to those around him and said, "Yesterday, I received a document from the *Vihara* (monastery) that discussed how diligent and earnest Sun Ming was in his recitation. I have requested an extension of his life by twenty years." Thus, Sun Ming realized that cultivating the Way is beyond comprehension. The extension of twenty years was to repay his merit. The officials were then ordered to return him to his home. His family had already buried him, but his spirit returned to his body, though his family did not know. A hunter happened to pass by the burial place and heard the sound of crying out. He informed Sun Ming's family, and thus Sun Ming was saved. By the end of the Tianbao era, Sun Ming had been alive for six or seven years and was very well.

The Three-Blade Master During the reign of Emperor Suzong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man known as the Three-Blade Master, whose common surname was Zhang and given name was Baiying. In the early 760s, he was a warrior from Shouzhou. He was extremely filial, and when his father was living in Yingzhou, he stole a government horse to visit him. Upon reaching Huaiyin, he was caught by the guards. The governor, Cui Zhao, ordered him to be beheaded outside the city. The executioner, known for his skill with the blade, attempted to cut him twice, but initially, he was not injured. The executioner then switched to a sharper blade and used all his strength to chop, but he remained unharmed. The executioner was surprised and said, "When I try to cut him, my hand feels weak, I don't know why." He quickly reported this to the governor. Cui Zhao asked for the reason, and Zhang Baiying replied, "When I was fifteen, I abstained from meat and blood for ten years and recited the *Diamond Sutra* for more than ten years. Since the An Lushan Rebellion, I have been in the army and no longer recited. Yesterday, because of my unexpected crime, I only focused on reciting the *Diamond Sutra*." Cui Zhao sighed and let him go. Zhang Baiying then shaved his head and became a monk. He wore a large iron bell while begging for food and prepared meals for a thousand people, which he could do in one day. People called him "San Dao Shi," believing he was like the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha.

Song the Magistrate During the Tang Dynasty, Song, a magistrate of Fang Prefecture, had held the *Diamond Sutra* since his youth. Upon taking office, he temporarily stayed at the residence of the former official, who was known to have been haunted. Every night, he recited the sutra without fail. Suddenly, he saw a woman standing outside the door. After a while, Song asked, "Are you not a ghost?" She replied, "Yes." He then said, "The principles of the spiritual and physical worlds are different. You should not be able to see me. Is it not because you have a grievance or injustice?" The woman wept bitterly and said, "Yes." She explained, "I was the wife of the previous official. His younger brother forced me, and I resisted him, so I was killed. They wrapped my body in felt and threw it into the northwest corner of the courtyard toilet. Unable to bear the filth and accumulation, many people who come here want to report this. Ordinary people are timid and will be frightened if they see my form. Therefore, my anger remains unexpressed, and evil deeds are suddenly heard. You, as an official, practice recitation as a virtue. You should also greatly protect all sentient beings. Considering my unjust suffering, how could you not pity me?" Song replied, "I am now a low-ranking official. I cannot act alone. Tomorrow, I will definitely report this to the prefect." The ghost then departed. When morning came, he reported it. They dug up the ground and the toilet but did not find the body.

During the reign of Emperor Song, a woman appeared to him again, asking, "Why didn't you get it?" He replied, "It is only one inch to the northwest; you will find it tomorrow to complete the favor." Following his instructions, he found it. Inside the felt, there were only bones left, which he then washed and moved to another place. That evening, the woman returned to thank him, saying happily, "The protection you have given me is too deep, difficult to repay. Even in darkness, there is some connection. You have two sons; the older one is difficult to raise, but the younger one will certainly have descendants and achieve honor and position." She also mentioned that Song had frequently changed his official positions and salaries. She added, "I am greatly ashamed before the governor, not knowing how to repay you." Song reported everything to the governor, who asked what his next position would be. That evening, the woman returned and conveyed the governor's message, saying, "You will change positions in one month, but it will not be to your liking; you will be promoted to the position of Vice Magistrate of Guangzhou." Song confirmed that all these events came true. Initially, Song asked, "Since you were killed by someone, why don't you seek revenge?" She replied, "The person who killed me is still in office, and it is not his time to die." This is why she did not seek revenge.

**Liu Hongjian** Liu Hongjian was a descendant of the family of the Grand Censor Zhan. During the early part of the Tang Dynasty, he encountered chaos and fled south. There was a monk who advised him to recite the *Diamond Sutra*. Liu Hongjian followed this advice and recited the sutra daily. In the year Shangyuan (760), he was a guest in Shouchun. One day, he went out and suddenly saw two officials who said, "We are here to summon you under the authority of the Grand Commandant." Liu Hongjian replied, "I have never known the Grand Commandant; why do you summon me?" He wanted to resist but the officials suddenly dragged him forward. Liu Hongjian asked to put on a robe, but the officials would not allow it. They dragged him for a short while, and suddenly they crossed the Huai River and arrived at a village. Soon after, they handed him a large, old-fashioned robe and a belt and made him put them on. They laughed and said, "Just a big, old robe." They then headed north, and the road became increasingly obstructed. Eventually, they reached a large city and entered a grand government building. Suddenly, they saw the monk who had previously advised him to recite the sutra emerge from the office. The monk's attendant recognized Liu Hongjian and quickly informed the monk, saying, "Liu Sixteenth, why have you been summoned?" The monk said, "My disciple has no trouble."

In a moment, the official led Liu Hongjian into the hall. Behind the desk, there was a five-colored pagoda, about three to four feet tall, spinning and rotating. Before he could interrogate, the monk had already entered, and the pagoda transformed into a handsome man in his thirties. The man said he was the Vice-Chancellor, descended the steps to greet the monk, and asked, "Why did you come again?" The monk replied, "Liu Hongjian is my disciple. He holds the *Diamond Sutra* and has great spiritual power. His fate has not yet ended; he should be released." The official said, "If he holds the *Diamond Sutra*, I would like to hear it." He then ordered Liu Hongjian to recite while kneeling. Liu Hongjian finished reciting two pages, but suddenly forgot. In the west hall, there was someone holding a golden hook dragon-head banner, with blue letters written with the *Diamond Sutra*, spread before Liu Hongjian. The official ordered him to clearly recite the sutra until the end. No one was seen, only the quiet and empty hall remained. Therefore, he went out the door, only seeing the pursuing official. Suddenly, a thing shaped like two suns appeared, coming to strike Liu Hongjian. Frightened, he ran. Suddenly, he saw water by the roadside. Liu Hongjian wanted to stop and drink it, but the pursuing official said, "This is human fat. After settling, the top part is clear, but the bottom part is all leftover skin and rotten meat. If you drink it, you cannot return." Soon, he arrived at the house and saw a skeleton lying on the bed. His heart was quite sad. A ghost pushed him from behind, darkly as if entering a room, and he came back to life. The ghost got money and then left.

○ Zhang Xiyou Zhang Jiayou from Guangling was appointed as the Governor of Mingzhou at the beginning of the Baoying era during the Tang Dynasty. He fell ill and died. His body was carried back home and buried outside the south gate of Guangling. In the Yongtai era, one of his old friends, a man named Lao, was walking to the south of the city and sat under a pagoda. Suddenly, he saw Zhang Xiyou riding a white horse coming from the south. Zhang Xiyou dismounted and comforted Lao as if they were still alive, but he did not remove his hat and spoke while looking down. Lao asked about his sins and blessings in the afterlife. Zhang Xiyou replied, "Sins and blessings are clearly evident, and nothing is not obtained according to one's actions. But I have always held the *Diamond Sutra*, so now I am free from burdens. I will also go somewhere else in a few months. You return and tell my brother to recite the *Diamond Sutra* a thousand times. Why did you put my incense burner with all these bad things? You also have two scrolls of scripture; please recite them to increase your own blessings." After finishing his words, he bid farewell and left. Lao was dazed and regained consciousness after a long time. ○ Wei Xun Wei Xun, the son of Wei Shangde, a Left Attendant, held the *Diamond Sutra*. During the Shengong era, he was appointed as the Grand General of the Gate Guards. At the time, there was a man named Cai Ce in the capital who suddenly died but revived after several days. He said that when he first arrived in the afterlife, the officials were surprised that they could not find him. They were about to punish the messengers. The messengers said, "General Wei Xun is protected by good spirits due to his holding of the *Diamond Sutra*; we cannot find him." They immediately sent another messenger to search again, and soon, the report was the same. The official in the afterlife said, "Let us stop the search for now." Upon hearing this, Wei Xun redoubled his efforts in his practice of the *Diamond Sutra*.

○Du Sine Du Sine of Tongchi County in Luxi Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, through the power of holding the *Diamond Sutra*, was cured of his illness. Every time he held the sutra, he would see divine light. ○Abbot of Longxing Temple During a grand vegetarian feast at Longxing Temple in Yuanzhou during the Tang Dynasty, the abbot, who had been a monk for many years and was thus considered a wise elder, sat below the seat of the chief monk. There was a young monk who arrived later from outside, and there was no seat available except one below the abbot's seat. The young monk wanted to sit down, but the abbot scolded him immediately. This happened several times, and the young monk, fearing he would miss the meal, eventually took the seat. The abbot became very angry, leaned against the pillar and sat down, intending to slap the young monk. Just as he was about to raise his hand, his large sleeve was pressed by the pillar, preventing him from striking. He folded his hands in shock. The young monk felt ashamed and discouraged, and left the gathering without eating, returning to his room. The assembly discussed that this might be due to the young monk's moral virtue. The abbot then went with the other monks to pay respects to the young monk. The young monk, feeling uneasy, said, "I have no spiritual practice, I dare not accept the respect of a great master." He hesitantly walked away. The abbot then asked him about his life's work, and the young monk replied, "For twenty years, I have only held the *Diamond Sutra*." Everyone praised him, saying it was the power of the *Diamond Sutra* protecting him. They then burned incense and bowed at the pillar, reciting an incantation: "If this is the power of the *Diamond Sutra*, return this robe." The young monk's robe then came out with a wave of his hand.

Tchen Zhe of Lin'an Tchen Zhe, a resident of Lin'an during the Tang Dynasty, lived in Yuhang and practiced with utmost precision and consistency, holding the *Diamond Sutra*. In the early years of the Guangde era, bandits from Wuchang, led by Zhu Tan, raided Yuhang. Tchen Zhe, being wealthy, planned to move his property to avoid the situation. Soon after the bandits arrived, Tchen Zhe mistook them for imperial soldiers and asked how close they were. The bandits, enraged, shouted, "What kind of old dog are you, daring to insult us!" and began to stab him with their swords. Each time they struck, a five-colored circular light, several feet in diameter, appeared to protect Tchen Zhe's body. The swords could not harm him, and the bandits were astonished, believing him to be a sage. None of them felt ashamed and regretful, and they left him alone and departed.

Monk Daoxian In the Tang dynasty, Daoxian, a monk of the Holy Virtue Temple, had the lay surname Yuan. He served as the abbot of the Dàyún Temple in Jiangzhou and was respected by his fellow monks. At that time, Governor Yuan of Jiangzhou wished to commission seven paintings of Guanyin Bodhisattva. Given Daoxian's disciplined practice, he was entrusted with the task. Daoxian instructed the painters to observe vegetarianism and maintain purity, and all the colors were prepared using milk-juice incense instead of glue, ensuring utmost cleanliness. Governor Yuan deeply praised him for this. Upon completion of the paintings, Daoxian traveled to Yuni to cut and prepare timber for the construction of a Wenshu Hall. As he was about to return with the timber, he suddenly fell into the river. The current was swift and turbulent, and his fellow monks could not rescue him. At the moment of falling into the water, Daoxian thought of Guanyin Bodhisattva and saw an unusual light at the bottom of the water. After a while, he saw the seven bodhisattvas he had painted standing on both sides. They told him, "Just recite 'Namo' to the bodhisattvas." His belongings remained as if in daylight, and he was aware that he was still in the water, fearing for his life. He then thought, "Recite Amitabha Buddha." Upon reciting Amitabha Buddha, the seven bodhisattvas did not yet hold his feet. As he approached the surface, his clothes were not polluted, and he accompanied by the timber, traveled over forty miles. Daoxian passed away in the early Tiansbao era. Now, the seven bodhisattvas at Dàyún Temple in Jiangzhou are still present, along with the story of the painting falling into the water.

Cheng Gui, during the early years of the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, was a low-ranking official in Changsha. He was tasked with transporting bridge timbers from Henan to Yangzhou. However, he encountered numerous difficulties due to adverse weather conditions, resulting in the loss of a significant portion of the shipment. The officials in Yangzhou accused Cheng Gui of stealing and selling the timbers, and they subjected the messengers to severe torture. Unable to bear the pain, the messengers falsely claimed that the timbers had been used. The officials in Yangzhou then sent a notice to the governor of Tan Prefecture, where Ban Jingqian, a strict and meticulous official, was in charge. A subordinate of Ban Jingqian, Yang Jinning, took advantage of the situation and helped frame Cheng Gui. Ban Jingqian sent Yang Jinning to arrest Cheng Gui. Upon arriving in Yangzhou, Yang Jinning placed Cheng Gui in a small fetter and transported him by land to Ningjiang. When they reached the boat, Cheng Gui was chained and attached to the ship's beam, with all sides nailed shut, leaving only small holes for food and water. Cheng Gui believed that if he reached Tan Prefecture, he would surely die. From Yangzhou, he began to meditate on the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara who saves from suffering. He ate once a day, sometimes not eating at all, and only drank water while fasting. After more than ten days, they arrived at Chuzhou. At dusk, he recited earnestly, and his fetters and chains suddenly opened.

Gua was emaciated and free from worldly attachments. Waiting until deep into the night when the boatmen were all asleep, he removed the nails and pulled something out from the back of the boat. He then reached the roof of Yang Jin's hall and shouted, "Yang Jin, what can you do to me?" Yang Jin was startled awake and asked how Gua had managed to get there. Gua replied, "I intend to be buried in the belly of a fish, how can you succeed with me!" Determined, he jumped into the water. Initially, he reached the bottom of the pond. Shortly after, he encountered a floating piece of wood with a vertical branch. Gua rode and held onto the wood, eventually reaching the water's surface. In the darkness of midnight, he looked around in confusion. The wood sank to the bottom of the pond and then rose again. With sincere thoughts and prayers to Guanyin, he suddenly turned over and followed the floating wood. Knowing they had reached the shore, he hid among the reeds. There were many fierce beasts by the river, but they looked back and forth without harming each other. By morning, he reached a nearby village. The villagers dressed Gua and sent him to Chuzhou. The officials of the state praised him and provided him with donkeys, horses, and food. Gua then went to the capital and petitioned the Censorate.
