Initially, Yang Jin, having lost Gua, disbanded immediately. As a result, Yang Jin also became a monk.

A Note on Wang Qi Wang Qi was a man from Taiyuan during the Tang Dynasty. He lived in Yingyang and abstained from eating meat and blood from a young age. In the early Dali era, he served as a household affairs officer in Quzhou. He had a nature inclined to always recite the *Guanyin Sutra*. From his youth to adulthood, he suffered from serious illnesses many times, but in all cases of recitation, there has been no failure to recover. During recitation, there must be strange and bizarre appearances to come and disturb, but because of Wang Qi's upright heart, they cannot interfere. Initially, when Wang Qi was nine years old, he suffered from an illness for five or six days and became unable to speak. Suddenly, he heard someone calling his name outside the door, saying, "I have come to take you." So he followed along and walked about fifty li, arriving at a government office. The official in charge was greatly surprised and said, "Why did you bring this little child here? He should be sent back immediately!" A bystander said, "Whenever someone is summoned, they should not be allowed to leave; they should be made to serve, and then they can go." The official said, "There is a dog that should die." He ordered Wang Qi to fetch the dog. Wang Qi complained that being young and small, he could not travel alone, so the official ordered him to go with the messenger. On the way, the messenger gave a pill to Wang Qi, shaped like a ball, and ordered him to knock on the dog's house door. When the dog came out, he threw the pill at it, and the dog swallowed the pill and died immediately. The official said, "Once the task is completed, you may return."

After encountering the illness again, he suddenly felt that there were eighty-two people inside his four limbs, each with eyes, nose, mouth, and eyebrows, guarding their respective positions. Those inside his arms and legs, coming and going, attacked his flesh. Whenever they reached the wrist joints, there was always a collision, causing unbearable pain and suffocation. Qi asked, "Do you want to kill me?" They replied, "We are here to treat your illness. How could we kill you?" Qi said, "If you treat my illness, I will provide a grand feast for you." The ghosts were greatly delighted and called for meat. The next day, Qi prepared a meal for them. After eating, they all left, and Qi's illness also healed. Qi had a clean knife, about a foot long, which he held whenever he recited. When suffering from a contagious disease, he always placed the knife by his bedside for self-protection. Later, when his illness worsened, he struggled to get up in the dark and recited the name of Guanyin Bodhisattva. The darkness suddenly became as bright as daylight, and he saw the blade of the knife pointing upwards. A monk came and sat beside Qi, asking him what kind of knife it was. Qi said, "It is a demon-killing knife." The monk then vanished. Soon, an iron hammer fell from the sky and struck the knife. After striking the knife over two hundred times, the hammer was completely shattered, but the knife remained undamaged. He then saw a large iron pot, capable of holding over two hundred stones, inverted. Two large men were holding a pestle nearby.

Qiqi asked, "Do you recognize this?" Qiqi replied, "I do not." Someone said, "This is the Iron Chain Hell." Qiqi said, "It is precisely this hell that restrains demons." After speaking, everything disappeared. Qiqi then saw beds carrying precious food, about a hundred beds, exiting through the door. He also saw hundreds of people, all dressed in splendid attire, lined up in the house. He saw his deceased father holding a knife, angrily saying, "There is no place for you to stay." The people dispersed at once, and soon Qiqi recovered from his illness. During the reign of Emperor Suzong of Tang, in Jingzhou, Qiqi suffered from a severe illness again and thought of Guanyin in his heart. He saw hundreds of ghosts arriving by boat. They came from afar and were hungry, seeking food from Qiqi. Qiqi ordered his family members to prepare food and distribute it in the courtyard, and the demons sat in rows. Two demons jumped out of Qiqi's mouth, sat down, and ate until finished. Initially, they said, "It is not enough." Qiqi said, "Don't you need clothes?" The demons replied, "Exactly so." Qiqi then ordered his family members to make dozens of pairs of paper clothes and red and green shirts, which were burned in the courtyard. The demons put on the clothes and dispersed, and Qiqi's illness also soon recovered. During the reign of Emperor Daizong of Tang, Qiqi suffered from a severe illness again and, under the lamp, cleared his mind and recited the *Prajnaparamita Sutra*.

A sudden sound like a bird flying arose from the body, rising upward, and the mouth remained open and could not be closed. Reflecting that this must be a demonic manifestation causing distress, Wang Qi increased his calmness and focus. Soon, everything returned to normal. He then saw a swollen corpse in the bed, a snake as large as a jar, and various ghosts, many of whom were known to have died before, disturbing and tormenting him. Wang Qi closed his eyes and recited scriptures twenty-four times with sincere devotion, and the phenomenon suddenly became silent and disappeared. After thirty-nine recitations, he became exhausted and fell asleep, recovering the next day. His wife, Lady Li, had once encountered a disease or epidemic. Wang Qi, by the light of a lamp, recited the *Heart Sutra* with sincere devotion, reciting a few sentences. Suddenly, he saw three heads under the lamp, the middle one being the maid of Lady Li who had recently died. He heard Lady Li making a sighing sound and supporting herself to sit up. Lady Li stared with wide eyes and could not speak, only pointing to things around her, as if seeing something. Wang Qi ordered his servant to cut whatever Lady Li pointed at with a long knife. After a long time, she woke up and said, "Is it Wang Sanlang?" referring to him as her brother. Wang asked what she was pointing at, and Lady Li said, "I saw a person in the window with a nose several feet long, and two objects in front of the bed that looked like camels. I also saw red curtains and screens on the roof, all cut and broken by the servant's knife, disappearing suddenly." Wang then recited the scriptures forty-nine times, and Lady Li soon recovered. ○ Zhang Yushi

During the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, Zhang San, a high-ranking official serving as a judge, was sent on a mission to Anhui to investigate and resolve cases. As he was about to cross the Huai River, a man in a yellow robe ran up from behind, claiming to have an urgent matter and asking to be allowed to board the boat. When he finally arrived, he said, "I just want to hitch a ride across the Huai River." The boatman wanted to drive him away and scolded, "Why did you stop the judge?" Zhang San said, "Do not strike him." He then rebuked the boatman, saying, "It's not a big deal to carry one commoner across the river." He personally gave the man some leftover food, and the man was deeply ashamed. After crossing the river, they parted ways, and soon the man appeared at the next inn. Zhang San thought he had come to request something and was annoyed, telling him, "I just helped you cross the river; why did you come back? Go away quickly." The man said, "I am not a human being. I want to discuss something with the judge, but it's not something those around me can hear." He then dismissed those around him and said, "I have been commanded to take you. You will drown in the Huai River. Just now, I received a meal, and I will not forget your kindness. I can only stay for one day." Zhang San asked to return to his room to make some arrangements. The ghost said, "I cannot stay for more than one day. I am a messenger in the underworld, but my duties are similar to those of local officials in the human world." Zhang San wanted to ask for help, but the ghost said, "Humans and ghosts have different paths; there should be no close contact. You cannot escape this fate." Zhang San bowed respectfully, and the ghost said, "If you can recite the *Continued Life Sutra* a thousand times in one day, you will be granted an extension of your life." After saying this, the ghost left. At the door, he turned back and asked, "Do you know the *Continued Life Sutra*?" Zhang San initially did not understand. The ghost said, "It is the *Diamond Sutra*."

A person said, "It is already too late today, how can one possibly complete a thousand scrolls of scripture?" The ghost replied, "If a person can manage it, it can be done." The person then called out to the innkeeper and other commoners, and together they managed to complete the task. By the next evening, they had finished reciting the thousand scrolls. The ghost returned and said, "The judge has been exempted, and you will need to temporarily visit the underworld." Everyone saw a yellow-clad official accompanying the person as they left. Upon meeting the underworld king, the person reported that they had recited the *Continuation of Life Sutra* a thousand times, which was sufficient to extend their life. The record stated, "It matches what was recited." The underworld king then joined his palms and said, "If so, you should especially gain another ten years of life." The person was then released and returned to life. As they reached the gate, the pursuing official said, "Due to delaying the judge, you have now encountered punishment." The person showed their body, saying, "I am a poor scholar, staying in a temporary inn, and am afraid I cannot afford it." The ghost replied, "Only two hundred coins."

The speaker said, "If it is paper money, I should offer five hundred strings of coins." The ghost replied, "I am deeply grateful for your kindness, but my virtue is meager; how could I accept such a large sum of money? Two hundred strings of coins would be sufficient." The speaker said, "Now I am a ghost as well, returning to an inn at night, and it is not easy to obtain." The ghost said, "The judge should only think of his wife returning, and I will take care of it." The speaker then thought only of his wife returning, and the ghost said, "It has been received." Shortly after, the ghost returned and said, "The wife wants to give it, but the grandmother refuses." The ghost then instructed the speaker to think only of the grandmother, and shortly after, said, "It has been obtained." The speaker then fell into a deep pit, and thus was revived. He requested leave to return home and told the entire story. His wife said, "That night, I dreamed that you had died and requested two hundred strings of coins for paper money, intending to buy and make it. The grandmother said, 'Dreams are not to be trusted!' That night, the grandmother had another dream." As a result, the person died ten years later.

Li Si was skilled in the Thousand-Armed, Thousand-Eyed Mantra. There was a person suffering from a ghostly ailment, specifically malaria. Li Si chanted the mantra, and the ghost appeared, saying to the person, "I originally intended to severely torment and humiliate you, but I fear Li Fourteen, so I dare not return." Li Fourteen is Li Si. Li Si's family was from Dongjun, and he was traveling as a guest in Henan Province. His sister contracted an illness and died, but after several days, she revived and told the story: "At first, several people led me into a tomb, and then there were dozens more who intended to humiliate and abuse me. One of them suddenly said, 'This is Li Fourteen's sister. What do you intend to do now? Li Fourteen has already returned and will soon arrive at the house. He is a virtuous person. If they hear that we have taken their sister, he will surely torment and humiliate us with divine mantras. It would be better to send her back early.' They then all escorted the girl back to her home. After the girl revived, Li Si also arrived at her home."

### The Recluse Li Qia Once upon a time, there was a hermit named Li Qia. He traveled from the capital to the imperial city. On his way, he reached Bashi, where he encountered an official holding a summons, who said, "We are here to arrest Li Qia." Li Qia looked at the summons and saw that the writing was disordered and unreadable. He said to the official, "The writing on the summons is so messy." The official replied, "This is a summons from Yama, the king of hell." Upon hearing this, Li Qia wept and asked the official to let him return temporarily to bid farewell to his family. The official agreed and accompanied him back to his home. They passed through the market, and Li Qia saw various foodstuffs in the shops. The official looked at them for a long time, and Li Qia asked, "Do you wish to eat something?" The official replied, "Yes." Li Qia then gave the official a thousand coins and let him choose whatever he desired. The official bought only one dish. After eating, the official was very pleased. He said to Li Qia, "Now you should quickly copy the *Gokuraku-kyo* (Sutra of the Golden Light), and perhaps you will be spared." Li Qia returned home, finished copying the sutra, bid farewell to his family, and left with the official. They traveled for dozens of miles and arrived at a city with tall and imposing walls. Li Qia asked the official, "What is this city?" The official replied, "During the rebellion led by An Lushan, the authorities were afraid that the rebels would escape, so they built this city to block them." Li Qia then asked, "Who is the lord of this city?" The official answered, "It is Wuyuan Chang." Li Qia had known the city's lord before and requested to be introduced to him. Wuyuan Chang summoned Li Qia, and they met with great joy and sorrow. Soon after, tens of thousands of soldiers and horses passed through the city. Wuyuan Chang kept Li Qia seated and went out to greet them. After a long while, he returned, and Li Qia asked about the soldiers. Wuyuan Chang said, "They are escorting Yama to the Great An Guo Temple in the Western Capital." Upon arriving at the temple, Li Qia ascended a hundred-foot high platform. Yama began to review the records, saying, "This person has just copied the *Gokuraku-kyo* and thus has extended his lifespan, so he should not die yet." Wuyuan Chang admired this greatly and ordered Li Qia to be sent back. Thus, Li Qia was spared and returned to life.

Wáng Yǐ, who had been reciting the *Ruyi Lun Zou* (如意轮咒) since his youth, was traveling with three companions to the North River during the early years of the Yuan dynasty. A boatman asked to carry Wáng Yǐ and his companions, saying that the fare was not important because it was for his own use. Wáng Yǐ initially did not want to go, telling his companions, "He is offering such a low price, which must be a trap. Could he not be harboring ill intentions?" The boatman replied, "The money we pay will only be used for alcohol and meat, and it’s just to make sure our luggage isn’t delayed." His companions believed him and boarded the boat. They then bought wine and drank together, with the boatman repeatedly offering the wine to Wáng Yǐ. Wáng Yǐ often heard a voice from the air saying, "Do not drink." His heart grew even more terrified. Because of this, he began to suspect something was amiss. Even if he put the wine in his mouth, he secretly spat it out, and thus he alone remained sober. By night, holding a candle, his companions were all deeply snoring. Wáng Yǐ worried about something improper and sat silently, reciting the *Ruyi Lun Zou* to protect himself.

Suddenly, a boatman appeared, wielding a large axe with a blade about five or six inches long, entering from the water tank. He beheaded two slaves and then beheaded two companions. It was then his turn for Wang Yi, who bent over to receive death. The candle suddenly went out, and Wang Yi was struck three times with the axe. Behind him was a door that had been nailed shut for a long time. Suddenly, two people appeared from the door, carrying Wang Yi and throwing him into the water. The water was deep, so they threw him onto the shore. Blood covered his body, but he did not feel much pain. After walking more than ten miles, he arrived at a thatched cottage, where he shouted, "I was robbed by bandits." The people in the house took him into the room, thinking he was refusing to let them in. When they reported to the county, officials brought him to the site of the robbery. They saw that the bank was dozens of feet high, and finally realized the power of the divine spell. A few days later, the bandits were captured in Bianzhou. When asked how it happened, they said, "The candlelight suddenly dimmed, and Wang Yi disappeared; we don't know where he went." One of his wounds was deep but did not break the bone. Soon after, he recovered completely. This was the power of the *Ruyi Luan* spell.

○钳耳含光 Zhi County Magistrate Qian'er Hanguang's wife, Lady Lu, had died half a year ago. After completing his term of office, Hanguang returned home and lived in Zhi Mountain Temple. One day, he climbed to a high point on a large mound during his leisure time and suddenly saw Lady Lu beside the mound. They met and were filled with sorrow and joy. Hanguang asked about her death, and she pointed north and said, "The place where I live is there." She invited Hanguang to go with her. They entered the city, which was as grand and magnificent as the human world. Along the way, they came to a courtyard. Inside, there were several dozen rooms, and Lady Lu lived in the third one. Their relationship remained the same as before, and their clothes and belongings were also unchanged. Later, as the sun set, Lady Lu told Hanguang, "The underworld is strict. You should return for now. Come back tomorrow with our children; I have something to tell you. Don't come again tomorrow." The next day, Hanguang went back. Lady Lu saw him and was surprised, saying, "I warned you not to come, yet here you are again!" Soon after, a red-robed official arrived with dozens of attendants and entered the courtyard. Lady Lu instructed Hanguang to hide under the bed, hanging a curtain from the floor to shield him. She warned him not to look, fearing that he might violate the rules. Soon, she heard someone calling for Lady Lu Si Niang outside. Lady Lu went out to meet them. Hanguang was initially very frightened but later peeked out and saw twenty-eight women in red robes, each being ordered to untie their hair and tie it into pairs, then thrown into a cauldron. The sounds of their cries of pain could be heard for miles, and the fire was extinguished before they left. Lady Lu returned to the room, and Hanguang saw her, holding her hand on the bed. After a long time, he became unconscious.

After waking up, Hanguang asked, "Throughout my life, I ate vegetarian food, recited scriptures, and chanted the Buddha's name. Why did I suffer this torment?" The monk replied, "When I was about to die, a monk came to visit and instructed me to write the *Golden Light Sutra*. At the time, I agreed. Due to my illness, I acted hastily and forgot my last instructions. Therefore, I suffered the retribution for lying and endured this severe punishment. The reason I wanted to see my sons was to write the *Golden Light Sutra*. Now that you have seen it, there is no need to trouble your sons." Hanguang returned home and told his sons about the matter in detail, weeping all night. By morning, when he went to look, he could no longer see the vision of hell, but only wild grass remained. He then sold his family property and obtained five hundred thousand strings of cash. Including the prefect and below, each provided assistance, totaling two thousand strings of cash. He then ordered his eldest son to go to Wutai Mountain to write scriptures. Upon arriving at the mountain, the son traversed all the platforms but did not settle in one place. Soon after, he climbed up the platform again, halfway up the mountain path, where he encountered an old monk. The monk said to him, "Writing scriptures to save your mother, why have you delayed so much? Leave the money at the temple and quickly return to write the *Diamond Sutra*." After speaking, the monk disappeared. His son knew it was the Bodhisattva Manjushri and left the money, returning home. Until the scriptures were completed, he climbed the mound and again saw the hell realm. Therefore, he entered directly. Encountering a closed door, he knocked on it. The person inside asked, "Who is it?" Clamping his ears, Hanguang replied, "It is I." After a long time, a woman emerged and said, "The noble family’s command to thank you. The power of writing scriptures has already allowed me to be reborn in the human realm. Please take great care." Hanguang then asked, "Madam, why do you still reside here?" She replied, "Our crimes are quite similar, so we remain here."

○席豫 In the early years of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, Xiyu, as an inspector, was sent to review the Hexi region. On his way, two stations away from Hexi, he had a meal and requested a sheep's liver, but it could not be obtained, so he beat the head of the station's staff. When the liver was brought, he saw it moving in the dish, and he frowned for a long time, ordering it to be taken away. He then took a piece of silk and cast a Buddha statue for the sheep. Half a day later, Xiyu suddenly died. Following the officials, he met a king who said, "Killing has its way; why did you take its liver while it was alive, and how could you alone bear it?" Xiyu replied, "At first, I wanted the liver, but when I saw it moving, I really didn't dare to eat it." After he finished speaking, a small Buddha descended from the clouds, and the king performed a respectful bow. The Buddha said, "As Xiyu has stated." The king then said to the sheep, "He did not eat your liver; what do you wish now?" Soon, Xiyu was released and returned. ○苏颋 In the Tang Dynasty, Shu Ting, a high-ranking official, was told by a fortune-teller when he was young that he would become a high-ranking official and reach the rank of Second Class. Later, he reached the rank of Third Class. When he fell seriously ill, he called a shaman to see him, and the shaman said, "Your life is over, and you cannot recover." Shu Ting then discussed the fortune-teller's words, and the shaman said, "At first, it was indeed so, but because you killed two people during your time as the governor of Guilin, these two people are now appealing to the underworld. The authorities have reduced your lifespan by two years, which is why you did not reach the rank of Second Class." Shu Ting had previously served in Guizhou, where two officials accused the county magistrate, and he ordered the killing of the officials. He then sighed for a long time and died.

Zhang Zong, the magistrate of Jinjiang County in Quanzhou during the Tang Dynasty, loved eating raw fish. Suddenly, he fell ill and died, but his chest was still warm. Seven days later, he revived and said that a yellow-robed official had informed him: "The king summons you." Zhang followed the official and soon saw the king. The king asked the official, "I summoned Zhang Zong, why did you bring him here? He should be sent away quickly." An official standing beside the king said to the king, "This man loves eating raw fish; he can temporarily be punished to become a fish." The king ordered Zhang to transform into a fish and then said, "He will return to his original form." The official led Zhang to the riverbank and pushed him into the water. Zhang transformed into a small fish, about an inch long. Day and night, he grew, and by the seventh day, he was over two feet long. Suddenly, he saw fishermen coming to the river to set their nets. Fearing deeply, he unconsciously entered the net and was captured by the fishermen. They placed him under the grass in the boat. Soon, they heard that the governor of Jinjiang had sent someone to catch fish for raw fish dishes. The fishermen initially gave them a small fish, but they still received a beating. They searched the net again and found a carp under the grass, which they carried back to the governor's house to the front hall. There, they saw the governor's wife combing her hair in front of a mirror, exposing one arm. In the kitchen, the person cutting the fish used a knife to slice the fish. Zhang initially did not feel pain, but only felt the coldness of the iron. Soon, his head was cut off, and he returned to life. At the time, the imperial censor Li E, who had been demoted to the position of magistrate of Jinjiang, was dining on raw fish at the governor's house. Hearing that Zhang had revived, he hurriedly went to see him. After entering, Zhang welcomed him with his hand and told Li E, "Did you eat enough raw fish?" Li E asked how he knew, and Zhang explained the entire story. Only then did they realize that the fish they ate was Zhang himself.

Du Siang, as a young man, once crossed the Yellow River at Puzheng, where the river was very swift. At the time, many people were boarding the boat, and the boatman had already untied the ropes. On the shore, there was an old man who called out, "Mr. Du, please come down temporarily." His words were very urgent. Du went reluctantly to see him, and they spoke for a long time. The boatman waited for Du but he did not arrive, so he left Du on the shore and then departed. Du looked back as the boat left, feeling very resentful. That day, the wind was strong and the waves were rough. Suddenly, Du saw dozens of hands grabbing onto the boat and sinking, and all the passengers died, but only Du survived. The old man said to Du, "Your career is extremely noble, so I came to save you." And then he disappeared. Du later rose through the ranks to become a high official.

#### Huangfu Family During the Tang Dynasty, Pei Zunqing, a high-ranking official known as Sheshi, was the son of Huangfu, a woman from the Huangfu family. When she was young, she often recited scriptures, and in her scripture case, there was a small coral tree. Later, a small dragon bone suddenly appeared standing beside the tree, which contemporaries believed was an auspicious sign for the Pei family. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Pei Zunqing eventually became a prime minister. #### Assistant Historian of Jurong County An assistant historian of Jurong County could eat up to tens of pounds of fish paste but never felt full. The county magistrate, hearing of his ability, brought out a hundred pounds of fish paste. The official ate all the fish paste quickly, then felt short of breath, and after a while, vomited something shaped like the sole of a straw shoe. The county magistrate ordered it to be washed out and placed it in the container of fish paste, which turned into water. He repeatedly asked physicians and mystics, but none could identify it. The magistrate then ordered a minor official to take it to Yangzhou to sell it, hoping someone would recognize it. He instructed the official: "If anyone buys it, just set a high price and see how much money." The person arrived in Yangzhou after a few days, and a foreigner wanted to buy it. The price started at a thousand and kept rising, eventually reaching three hundred strings of cash. The foreigner returned it without any negotiation, and people told the foreigner: "This is an item from the county magistrate of Jurong. You must buy it, and I will accompany you." The foreigner then accompanied him back to Jurong. The county magistrate asked what it was, and the foreigner said: "This is the essence of fish that dissolves, and it can also dissolve blockages in the human abdomen. If someone is sick, use a piece as big as the tip of a finger, tie it with a string, and place it where the illness is. The blockage will dissolve. My country's crown prince had this illness, and his father sought a cure, rewarding the healer with a thousand gold pieces. If you see it for sale, you will gain great profit." The magistrate finally sold half of it to him.

○ Wu Shengzhi In the late years of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang dynasty, Wu Shengzhi of Taiyuan served as the military official of Xuanzhou and was in charge of affairs in Jingjiang. One day, he suddenly saw Leigong, the Thunder God, treading on faint clouds, chasing a small yellow snake that coiled around the river shoal. A man from Jingjiang, amused, threw a stone at the snake, which struck it, producing a clear metallic sound. Leigong then flew away. Someone was sent to look for it and found a copper sword, on which was inscribed in seal script: “The third sword of Xu Jingyang for slaying the dragon.” ○ The Sword That Breaks Mountains In recent times, a scholar was plowing his fields when he unearthed a sword. He polished and cleaned it, then went to the marketplace. A foreigner wished to buy it, first offering a thousand coins, then gradually increasing the price to a hundred thousand. The scholar refused. The foreigner then went to his home and, unable to let go of the sword, raised the price to a million. After the deal was made, the next day the foreigner brought the money to take the sword. On a night of fine moonlight, the scholar and his wife held the sword together and laughed, saying, “What is this thing that it is now worth so much?” In the courtyard was a stone used for pounding cloth. He pointed the sword at it, and the stone split in two. By dawn, the foreigner arrived with the money, took the sword, and upon inspecting it, sighed, saying, “The sword’s light has vanished entirely—how could this be?” He no longer wished to buy it. The scholar questioned him, and the foreigner replied, “This is the ‘Break Mountain Sword,’ which can be used only once. I intended to wield it to break the Treasure Mountain. Now its light has vanished entirely—I suspect it was touched by something.” The scholar and his wife regretted their actions and told the foreigner what had happened. The foreigner then bought it for ten thousand coins and left.

○Con Qi Con Qi was a vice censor. Once, he committed a crime and was imprisoned in the imperial prison, facing execution. One night, he was filled with anxiety and sat down to take a nap, when he suddenly dreamed of his mother's lower body. Con Qi became even more fearful, and his fear was evident on his face. His peers asked him about the dream, and he told them, saying it was the most ominous dream he had ever had. At that time, there was a skilled interpreter who congratulated him, saying, "You will be spared!" When asked how he knew, the interpreter replied, "Your mother's lower body is your way of life. Seeing the way of life again, how auspicious can it be! That is why I congratulate you." The next day, Vice Minister of Personnel Xue Ji reported that the execution was unjust, and Con Qi was indeed spared. Later, Con Qi rose to the position of chancellor. ○Emperor Xuanzong Emperor Xuanzong once dreamed of falling from the hall, and a filial son helped him up. On another day, he asked Gao Lishi about the dream, and Gao Lishi said, "The filial son in white clothes is Wei Jiansu." The emperor deeply agreed. A few days later, Wei Jiansu was promoted from the position of Vice Minister of Personnel to chancellor.

**Lü Yin** Lü Yin once dreamed during the day that he was being pursued by the underworld, and soon after, he saw a judge. The judge said, "This person has very high achievements, and should not be used." Lü Yin then raised his voice and said, "My mother is old, and my children are young, there is no one to take care of the family." He appealed very urgently. The judge ordered to bring him before the king, and soon the attendants reported to the king, "This person has already found a replacement." When asked who the replacement was, they replied, "It is Kuai Shi." The king said, "Kuai Shi is a distinguished scholar, and the position is suitable for him." Lü Yin was then released. At the time, Lü Yin was staying with his brother-in-law Gu Kuang. After waking up, he told Gu Kuang about the dream. Several dozen days later, Kuai Shi was appointed as the magistrate of Wu County, and he was very well. Gu Kuang often teased Lü Yin, regarding it as a source of amusement. After a month, Kuai Shi resigned and built a residence in the Jishan area of Wu. Suddenly, a messenger rushed in and said, "Ding Shi Yu relayed a message, ordering you to meet the Third Young Master." Kuai Shi said, "I have never heard of Ding Shi Yu, who is he?" The messenger replied, "It is Xian Zhi." Kuai Shi said, "Xian Zhi died in Yuhang, why is he called Shi Yu?" The messenger said, "He is a Shi Yu in the underworld." Kuai Shi was displeased and said, "A Shi Yu in the underworld, what does it mean to relay a message to a living person?" The messenger said, "Also ordered to follow him, not just to relay a message. The records are already set, it is difficult to change." Kuai Shi requested to see Ding Shi Yu, saying, "I should not die, please find a substitute for me." The messenger left and came back, saying, "The Shi Yu did not agree, urged to hurry and prepare." Because of an illness, Kuai Shi died after several days.

Zhuo Shi** **Zhuo Shi, an Assistant Editor, during the middle of the Dali era, suffered a severe epidemic. He was unconscious for forty days. One day, he suddenly dreamed that a yellow-robed female Taoist nun appeared at his place and said to him, "You have an official position and salary, originally you were not supposed to die." She then called out, "Fan Zheng, bring the medicine." Suddenly, he saw a child holding a glass bottle and a large bowl filled with medicine. All who drank it recovered easily. By morning, Xu Shuji ordered the medicine to be sent. Zhuo Shi had been ill for a long time and did not open his eyes at first. When he saw the child and the bowl of medicine, he recognized them as what he had seen in his dream the previous night. He called the child "Fan Zheng" and asked him, and indeed, it was true. His illness then healed.

薛义，河东人，曾在秘省担任校书郎。他的妹夫崔秘是桐庐的县尉。薛义和他的姨母韦氏是崔秘家的客人。过了很久，薛义得了疟疾，病程持续了几个月，时好时坏，几乎丧命。韦氏非常担忧，夜里梦见一位神人，穿着白色的衣服和单衣。韦氏合掌致敬，请求神人治愈薛义的病。神人说：“如果长期不治，就会变成严重的疟疾，那时就无法治愈了。”于是神人给了韦氏两张符咒和一个咒语。咒语是：“疟疾啊疟疾，四山之神，让我来缚住你。六丁使者，五道将军，收你的精气，摄你的神鬼。快快离去，不要碰到这个人。急急如律令。”每当薛义发病时，韦氏就念诵这个咒语，并手持符咒，薛义的病就痊愈了。当时，韦氏的一个七岁女儿也得了疟疾，她看到一个像黑狗一样有粗毛的东西。神人说：“这就是害你女儿的病源，可以赶紧抓住并杀死它，你的女儿就会痊愈。如果不这样做，你家的两个小丫鬟也会得疟疾。”韦氏在梦中杀死了那只狗。醒来后，她把咒语告诉了薛义，薛义诚心持咒，病也痊愈了。韦氏的女儿也痊愈了，一切都如神人所说。

○ Zhao Jiao An Lushan used the pretext of attacking the king's side to blame the Yang family, listing their faults in a memorial, and then sent the memorial to the eastern capital. Those who discussed it found the memorial's wording unfavorable to the Yang family, making it difficult to convey, and they feared that in the future An Lushan might be punished. Therefore, they sent the chief clerk of the Dali Temple, Zhao Jiao, to deliver the memorial to the capital. Emperor Xuanzong was displeased upon reading it and simply issued an edict saying, "Zhao Jiao should return." Zhao Jiao left the Secretariat and met Yang Guozhong, asking, "Is it not tiring to send this memorial? I am relieved that it does not accuse anyone, but if there were any harsh words, should it still be sent?" Yang Guozhong ordered him to leave quickly. Zhao Jiao returned to the Xikou Inn, feeling very anxious, and sat on a rope bed in the hall, suddenly as if in a dream. Suddenly, he felt the rope bed several feet above the ground, looking up, he saw a man standing in armor in the middle, shouting at more than twenty people to beat him. Though he was dragged, another person in a short cap and purple robe came and said, "This is not Jiang Qing; it is not appropriate to kill him." Zhao Jiao was then released. After several days, Zhao Jiao returned to Luoyang. The rebels soon arrived, and Zhao Jiao, along with several others, was captured and bound. Zhao Jiao was tall and had a dignified bearing, standing at the head of the line. He went to see the rebel general Tian Qianzhen. Tian Qianzhen stood in armor, exactly the same as the figure Zhao Jiao had seen in his hallucination. The rebel shouted at Zhao Jiao, saying, "What kind of lowly person are you, daring to defy the imperial army!" He ordered his attendants to beat him to death. The attendants just arrived, and Yan Zang hurriedly came from the hall and said, "This is not Jiang Qing; it is not appropriate to punish him." Tian Qianzhen asked his name, and Zhao Jiao replied, "My surname is Zhao." He was then released. Next came Jiang, who was subsequently killed.

Li Shao Yun, a man from Longxi, was the son-in-law of Lu Ruoxu from Fanyang. He was unrestrained and carefree, enjoying drinking and gathering for parties. One night, his wife had a dream in which she saw Li Shao Yun and several others, mixed with courtesans, all stripped and bound with long ropes, driven away together, and crying as they looked back at their wives before being separated. She woke up in tears, her pillow and bedding soaked, and told him about the dream. Li Shao Yun also had the same dream, and they found it very significant. Because of this, he became deeply fearful and abstained from meat and blood, holding the *Diamond Sutra* and frequently inviting monks for meals. For three years, there was nothing wrong. Later, when the dream did not come true, he gradually became lax and negligent. During a gathering, his friends pressured him to drink and eat roasted meat, and since he was usually unrestrained, he indulged in drinking and feasting as before. The following year on the third day of the third month, he sailed on the Qujiang River with Li Meng, Pei Shinan, Liang Bao, and ten others. They selected famous courtesans from Chang'an and held large gatherings with singing and dancing. When the wine was at its height, the boat capsized, and everyone drowned.

○ Li Shuxiao The Censor-General Li Shuji, together with his brother Zhong Qu, both passed the imperial examinations and were promoted to the rank of Jinshi, and they were famous in contemporary times. In the early years of the Dali era, Li Shuji died. After more than a year, his brother-in-law and Zhong Yun slept together, and suddenly dreamed of Li Shuji. He saw him as he was, unchanged. When speaking of Zhong Yun, his voice and appearance were filled with sorrow, saying, "The principle of separation between the living and the dead is absolute, and there is no way for us to meet. We will only be reunited after a hundred years. I have a poem, which you can recite to my elder brother." The poem reads: "Suddenly, we part without a set time, deep sorrow and regret remain. Although Chang'an is not far, there is no way to send messages." After several years, Zhong Yun also died. ○ Lu Yanxu Lu Yanxu, the head of the granary in Xu Prefecture, lived in a place where a sudden summer rain came, filling the pit with water, which drained away quickly. Lu Yanxu sent someone to look, and he saw an ancient tomb below, inside of which was a clay coffin. There was a woman in her twenties, white and pure, with fingernails several inches long, and over ten gold hairpins in her hair. The inscription said, "She was a person from the Qin dynasty, a thousand years later, it will be opened by Lu Yanxu, it is the will of fate. Closing it is auspicious, opening it is inauspicious." There was also a single precious mirror, the back of which had gold flowers, which looked like golden wheels when held up to the sun. Lu Yanxu took the hairpins, mirror, and other items, and then closed the tomb. In the evening, he dreamed of a woman who said, "Why did you take my toys?" with a look of anger. After a year, Lu Yanxu died.

○豆卢荣 In the early years of the Kaiyuan era, Doulu Rong served as the deputy governor of Wenzhou and died. His wife was the daughter of Princess Jinhe. Princess Jinhe had previously married into the kingdom of Biye, which was part of the same political entity. When the king of Biye died, she returned. Doulu Rong then served as an assistant in Wenzhou, and Princess Jinhe accompanied him for several years. In the early years of the Baoying era, the mountain bandit Yuan Chao attacked and captured Taizhou. One night, Princess Jinhe's daughter dreamed of a man with long hair and bleeding, who told her, "Wenzhou is about to fall into chaos. You should leave quickly. Otherwise, you will surely suffer a disaster." Upon waking, she told her mother about the dream. Princess Jinhe said, "Dreams are often confused and unreliable. How can they be trusted?" Shortly after, she fell asleep again and dreamed of Doulu Rong, who said, "The man with long hair is your father, and he is now a general in the underworld. The forces in Zhe Dong will fail, and he wants you to leave with your family. You should follow and obey, and do not be attached to material possessions." The daughter then told Princess Jinhe about this dream. At that time, rice in Jiang Dong was expensive, but rice in Wenzhou was cheap. Princess Jinhe had someone buy thousands of bolts of Wu silk, so they were reluctant to leave. On another day, the daughter dreamed of her father, who said, "The kingdom of Zhe Dong has been captured by Yuan Chao. Your mother did not leave early enough, and she will surely suffer hardships." He spoke with tears in his eyes. Princess Jinhe then moved to Guazhou. When Guazhou was captured, she escaped with her life, just as her dreams had foretold.

### The Commander of Fugou The Commander of Fugou, named Jie, lost his surname. He died in the second year of the Dali era (767 CE). After half a year, his wife dreamed of meeting Jie. She asked him about his merits and demerits in the underworld. Jie replied, "In my lifetime, I was a scholar, but I fell into frivolity. I either defamed poetry and prose or mocked and criticized people. Now, I am punished by being sent two snakes and three centipedes every day, which enter and exit through my seven orifices, causing me immense suffering that I cannot bear. According to the law, I must suffer this punishment for 360 days. Once the punishment ends, I will be reborn. Recently, I was stripped by Yama, and my old undergarments were in disarray, making me a laughingstock. Please make me a new undergarment." The wife said, "There is nothing to make." Jie replied, "The magistrate of Wannian, Gai Youxuan, brought two bolts of silk. How can you say there is nothing?" He also requested that an image be made and the *Lotus Sutra* be copied. The wife agreed to both requests, and then Jie left. ### Wang Fangping Wang Fangping of Taiyuan was known for his utmost filial piety. His father fell gravely ill, and Fangping served him medicine and nourishment, wearing no belt for over a month. Later, while tending to his father's illness, he grew extremely tired and fell asleep beside his father's bed. He dreamed of two ghosts conversing, planning to enter his father's stomach. One ghost asked, "Why do you want to enter?" The other replied, "Wait until he eats thin porridge, then we can enter." After they made this agreement, Fangping woke up in a fright. He made a hole in a bowl, placed his finger under it, and put a small bottle underneath. He waited until his father drank, then removed his finger. The porridge entered the bottle, was covered, and boiled in a pot for a hundred times. Upon inspection, the bottle was full of meat. His father's illness was cured, and people believed it was due to Fangping's pure filial piety.

○阎陟 When Yan Zhi was young, his father served as the Prefectural Governor of Mizhou, and Yan Zhi accompanied him on his post. Once during the day, he slept and suddenly dreamed of a woman aged fifteen or sixteen, with a beautiful appearance and charming looks, who came to meet him. Like this for several months, whenever he slept, he dreamed of her. On another day, he dreamed of the woman coming to say goodbye, her voice and appearance filled with sorrow. She said, "I was the daughter of a former Prefectural Governor. My body is buried in the southeast corner of the city. You did not regard me as insignificant or lowly, and offered comfort in bed. My brother will come tomorrow to take my body away. This is a final farewell for all eternity. How could I not feel regret. Today I have a hundred thousand coins to give to you, to express my deep affection." After speaking, she ordered a maid to deliver the money under his bed, and then left. When Yan Zhi woke up, he looked under the bed and indeed found a hundred thousand paper coins. ○李进士 There was a scholar named Li, but his name has been forgotten. Once he dreamed of several people coming to chase him, and they went to a city. Upon entering, there was a hall, and the rooms were grand and spacious. At first, no one was seen. Li directly ascended to the hall and sat at the corner of the bed. Suddenly a person came with a stick and hit him, shouting, "What kind of new ghost are you, daring to sit on the king's bed!" Li immediately walked out. After a while, a voice was heard from inside the door saying, "The king is coming." He then saw a purple-clad person ascend to sit. The attendants led him in. The king asked why he had stolen money from his sister-in-law, but he did not understand at first. The king said, "You sold a horse to him and should have received twenty-seven thousand coins, but you took thirty thousand, isn't that theft!" After a while, a red-clad person arrived and apologized on behalf of Li, saying, "This person still has a life to live, it is not right to keep him here, just send the money back." The king set a deadline of fifteen days, if the matter is not resolved, he will be summoned again.

After Li woke up, he thought the dream was a ridiculous thing, and the logic behind it was not credible. About ten days later, a man who sharpened mirrors came to his house and performed a good divination. Li's family asked him to divinate, and it proved to be accurate. Eventually, he told Li. Li personally went to see him and asked, "What kind of lowly person are you, deceiving the common people?" The mirror-sharpening man angrily replied, "Selling horses and stealing funds, the king's order to settle accounts. Now the deadline is approaching, and not a single coin is returned. The king will pursue you. How dare you insult a national hero?" Li was surprised and amazed to hear that this was a dream, and he bowed and thanked him. He asked how he knew this, and the mirror-sharpening man said, "The mysterious figure in red clothing who saved you is your great-grandfather. You are being pursued again, so I am reporting to you." Li said, "My brother-in-law is dead, and there is no one to whom to return the money." The mirror-sharpening man said, "Just give to the poor and beggars, and distribute it among the temples, saying it is for the deceased brother-in-law, and that will suffice." As he said, he distributed the money, and they did not pursue it either.

Emperor Gaozong was about to perform a ritual to enshrine Mount Tai, but there had been prolonged rain, and the emperor doubted it. He sent someone to inquire of the Taoist priest Li Bo of Mount Hua, to report to the Jade Emperor in the Capital of Jade. Li Bo is the father of Li Chunfeng. He then sent the Chief Councillor Liu Rengui to Mount Hua to ask Li Bo about the ritual of enshrinement. Li Bo said, "Wait until I ask the Lord of Mount Tai." He then ordered him to be summoned. After a long time, the Lord of Mount Tai arrived, bowing in the courtyard with great respect. Li Bo said, "The Emperor of Tang wants to perform the ritual of enshrinement, what should we do?" The Lord of Mount Tai replied, "It is appropriate to perform the ritual. After sixty years, it should be performed again." Li Bo bowed and left. At that time, Liu Rengui stood beside Li Bo and saw the Lord of Mount Tai, looking at him repeatedly. Li Bo then called him back and said, "This is the Prime Minister of Tang, he does not recognize the Lord of Mount Tai, it is not appropriate to be surprised." Afterwards, he told Liu Rengui, "The Lord of Mount Tai was slightly surprised that the Prime Minister did not bow, he ordered his attendants to record this person's name, fearing it might tarnish his virtue, so he called him back to deal with it." Liu Rengui was very nervous for a long time. Li Bo said, "It has been dealt with, there should be no problem now." Afterwards, the emperor performed the ritual of enshrinement.

During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, Di Renjie served as a censor. The temples of gods and spirits in the Jiang and Ling regions were almost entirely burned down. When he reached the region of Duanzhou, there was a barbarian god. Di Renjie wanted to burn it, and anyone who entered the temple died immediately. Di Renjie offered a reward of a hundred thousand coins to anyone who would burn it. At that time, two people came forward to accept the reward. Di Renjie asked, "What is needed for the round trip?" They said, "We wish to receive an imperial decree." Di Renjie gave them the decree. They took it to the temple, entered, and said, "There is an imperial decree." They opened the decree and read it aloud. The god did not move, and they burned it down. Later, when Di Renjie returned to Bianzhou, he met a ghost who said, "Your Excellency, there is a barbarian god who says his temple was burned and he wants revenge." Di Renjie asked, "What happened in the end?" The ghost said, "Your Excellency is about to become a high official, and there are over twenty ghosts and spirits following you; what can they do?" After a long time, the god returned to Lingnan.

Wang Wanche During the reign of Empress Wu, many palace women died within a month, with over a hundred already deceased. Empress Wu then summoned Wang Wanche, a spirit exorcist, to inspect the palace. Wang Wanche reported, "The Heavenly Emperor is displeased with Your Majesty's long rule over the empire, causing the spirits to be unhappy, which has led to this situation." Empress Wu asked, "What can be done?" Wang Wanche replied, "I can pacify it." He then laid out a mat in front of the hall, held a knife, spat water, and recited incantations in four directions. After a while, he said, "The emperor has arrived." Wang Wanche then questioned the emperor in the open court, saying, "Heaven's will has its ups and downs, and the course of time has its rises and falls. In the past, the emperor assisted Your Majesty in ruling the empire, promoting the teachings of Jiang Yuan and Wenmu, and was thus revered and all nations followed. This is the will of heaven, not human affairs. Your Majesty's spirit is in heaven, separated by the boundary between the visible and invisible realms. How could You not recognize the opportunity and cause such severe harm to living people?" The emperor then spoke from the air, saying, "It is not my intention; this is because Empress Wang has had her grievances redressed. It is not just the harem; it will be detrimental to your ruler." Empress Wu and those around her clearly heard this. Empress Wu remained silent, her expression changed, and she ordered the mat to be removed. The following year, the Five Princes supported the restoration of Emperor Zhongzong, moved Empress Wu to the Shangyang Palace, and she died in seclusion.

The Wife of the Magistrate of Zhaozhou The magistrate of Zhaozhou, Lu, was traveling to his new post after his marriage. His wife was very beautiful. After several years, he was dismissed from his post and returned to the capital. On the fifth day of the fifth month, his wife wanted to go to the market to buy items to prolong her life and present them to her parents-in-law. As her carriage approached the door, she suddenly suffered from a severe chest pain and died after a meal. The magistrate cried out in grief and hurried to see Zheng Jian, the Right Counselor, knocking on the door urgently. Zheng Jian was surprised and said, "This is a time when the sun is at its zenith, and someone is knocking on the gate with intensity. There must be an emergency." He then rushed out. The magistrate bowed twice and told him about the incident. Zheng Jian said, "This is the work of the Three Lord of Mount Tai." He then wrote three talismans and gave them to the magistrate, saying, "When you return home, burn the first talisman quickly. If she does not revive after walking ten miles, burn the second one. If she still does not revive, burn the third one. If she dies suddenly, she will come back to life; if she does not, she will truly die." The magistrate returned home and burned the three talismans as instructed. His wife revived and was able to speak after a while. She said, "I was taken by a carriage to the top of Mount Tai, where there were separate palaces. I saw a young man who said he was the Three Lord. He ordered ten or more maids to take me to a private room and prepare me for some purpose."

The third son was in the hall, playing *shuanglu* with a young man, waiting for her makeup and combing to be finished, and was about to hold a banquet. The maids ordered her to hurry with her makeup, and she was still crying because she was attached to her old friends. After a while, someone knocked on the door and said, "It is the magistrate of the upper district. He just received instructions from the governor to ask the third son why he took the wife from the Lu family and should send her back immediately." The third son angrily said, "Taking someone else's wife, what business is it of the governor!" He scolded the magistrate and ordered him to leave. They exchanged harsh words, and their words were very harsh. After a short while, someone else knocked on the door and said, "It is the direct symbol messenger. The governor ordered to take the woman from the Lu family." The one playing the game tried to persuade him, but he did not listen. The one playing the game said, "Not only will it harm you, but it will also bring disaster to me." He still did not listen. Soon a strong wind blew black clouds from the top of the cliff. The two messengers shouted, "The Great One Direct Symbol is about to arrive." The third son showed a look of fear. The wind suddenly lifted the house, more than a hundred feet high, and released it. People and things were crushed, but only the woman from the Lu family was saved. The three messengers sent her back, and when they arrived at the hall, they saw her lying on the bed, looking very sad and resentful. She was pushed into a form and thus revived.

During the Jingyun period, Li, the county magistrate of Hedong, had a wife named Wang, who was renowned for her beauty in the Sanfu region. One morning, Li left early for the government office and had not yet returned. Wang had just finished dressing and was sitting idly, burning incense. Suddenly, several palace attendants appeared, driving a small ox-drawn carriage that descended from the clouds to the courtyard. Wang was startled and asked what they were doing there. They replied, "The Lord of Mount Hua has sent us to come and fetch you." She could not refuse their invitation. In a hurry, she was about to leave, and she told her family, "I wish I could see Li, the county magistrate, one last time." She shed tears and walked away, dying at the side of the steps. Soon after, colorful clouds lifted the carriage into the sky, gradually disappearing. When Li returned from the prefecture, he did not see his wife. He embraced her body, weeping and wailing, and died and revived several times. A short while later, someone came to the door, claiming they could revive Wang. Li bowed respectfully and requested their protection and assistance.

The person sat on the bed, searching for red ink to write a talisman. When the red ink did not arrive, he wrote a black talisman and sent it away. After a short while, another talisman was sent. He smiled and told Li, "Do not worry, you will soon be saved." Soon after, Wang regained consciousness. Li bowed and thanked him many times, offering gifts with all his might. The person laughed and said, "To rescue and comfort in times of disaster, why need material things?" Then he left and was not seen again. Wang, upon regaining her senses, said, "At first, I arrived at Mount Hua and met Wang, who was very pleased. He arranged a feast at the summit and enjoyed a banquet with several of his disciples. After the feast, we were about to continue our intimate conversation. Just as we were drinking, suddenly a man arrived on a black cloud, saying, 'The Supreme Deity Taiyi has summoned Lady Wang.' The god remained calm and asked to wait until the feast was over. Soon after, another man arrived on a red cloud, greatly angered and said, 'Taiyi asks why Mount Hua has taken a living woman. If you do not send her back quickly, there will be severe punishment!' The god was greatly alarmed and immediately sent her back home."

At the beginning of the Kaiyuan era, three guards returned from the capital to Qingzhou. Upon reaching the temple of Mount Hua, they saw a servant girl dressed in green, whose attire was old and shabby. She approached them and said, "The lady wishes to see you." She then led them onward. They encountered a woman in her late teens, whose appearance was pale and weary. She said, "I am not human; I am the third wife of the son of the Lord of Mount Hua. My husband is extremely wicked. My home is in the region of Bohai, and I have not received any letters for three years. This is why I am especially despised by the Lord's son. I heard that you are far away, and I wish to entrust this letter to you. If you can deliver it, my father will reward you handsomely." She then handed over the letter. The guards were also devout, and one of them asked, "Where in the region of Bohai should I deliver the letter?" The woman replied, "By the second tree by the sea pool, just knock on it, and someone will come out to receive it." After saying this, she bid farewell and left. When they reached the region of Bohai, they followed her instructions and delivered the letter. Suddenly, a red gate appeared under the tree, and someone came out to receive the letter.

A man handed him a letter and entered. Soon after, he emerged and said, “The Great King invites the guest.” He followed for more than a hundred steps, then entered a gate, where a man clad in red robes stood, over ten feet tall, attended by thousands or hundreds of female attendants on either side. After they sat down, the red-robed man said, “Three years without a letter from a woman.” The Great King, upon reading the letter, flew into a rage and exclaimed, “How dare you servants!” He then issued an order to summon the left and right guards. In a moment, they arrived—all over ten feet tall, with large heads and noses, and grotesque appearances. He commanded the mobilization of fifty thousand troops, to be ready by the fifteenth day to march westward and attack Mount Hua, giving no orders that would not lead to victory. The two men received the instructions and left, then said to the three guards, “There is nothing to report upward.” He ordered his attendants to take two bolts of silk to give to the messenger. The three guards were not pleased and resented the small amount of silk. As they parted, the red-robed man said, “Two bolts of silk can be sold for twenty thousand guan; be careful not to sell it cheaply to others.” After the three guards had left, they wished to verify the matter and returned to Huayin.

On the fifteenth day, after dusk, a black vapor like a canopy was seen from afar in the east. Gradually moving westward, thunder roared and lightning flashed, heard for a hundred li. In a moment, a great wind on Mount Hua uprooted trees, blowing clouds from the west, the cloud formations grew more imposing. Until it reached Mount Hua. Thunder and fire roared and scorched, turning the entire mountain red, after a long time it finally ceased. By dawn, the mountain was scorched black. The three guards then went to the capital to sell silk. Buyers heard the price of two million, and none failed to mock and be terrified, thinking them mad. Several days later, a man on a white horse came to buy, paying two million without hesitation, the money had already been locked in the western market. The three guards then asked what he intended to use it for. The man said, "Now, the god of Wei River is marrying off his daughter, and I am using this as a gift. The best silk in the world comes from the North Sea region. I was about to send someone to buy it, but I heard you selling North Sea silk, so I came myself." The three guards obtained the money. After several months, they finished trading and returned east to Qingtu.

Upon arriving at Huayin, they once again saw the blue-clad woman from before, who said, "Madam has come to thank for the favor." They then saw a covered cow cart descending from the mountain, with more than ten attendants on both sides. Upon arriving, the woman descended from the cart, and she was the same maiden from before. Her appearance and attire were radiant, her eyes flowing with clear glances, to the point where she was hard to recognize. Seeing the three guards, she bowed and said, "I received your great favor and reported far to my parents. Since the chaos of war, the feeling of gratitude has been very deep, but I feel ashamed to have nothing to repay. However, the third young master because you delivered the letter, transferred his anger onto you. Now he will send five hundred soldiers to wait at Tongguan. If you go, you will surely be harmed. You may return to the capital for now. Not long after, the imperial carriage will travel east, and ghosts and deities will fear the drum chariot. If you sit on the drum chariot, there will be no worry." After she finished speaking, she disappeared. The three guards were greatly afraid and immediately returned to the capital. After several dozen days, when Emperor Xuanzong traveled to Luoyang, they gave money to the drummer and followed the drum chariot out of the pass, and thus they were free from worry.

刘可大，在天宝年间通过了进士考试，来到了京城。离开东都洛阳后，他遇到了一个少年，那少年看起来像是贵公子，穿着华丽奢侈，手持弹弓行走，随从非常壮观。起初，刘可大与这位少年相处了几天，然后一起前往华阴，少年说：“我的庄园在县东。”并邀请刘可大一同前往。他们跟随少年来到庄园，只见建筑宏伟壮丽。少年将刘可大请入大厅，随后进入室内很长时间。刘可大偷偷从中间的门缝向外窥视，看到一位贵人在内厅处理事务，院子里有许多囚徒，他们经常受到拷打，发出凄惨的声音。刘可大怀疑这不是人间之地，感到害怕想要离开。起初，少年准备进入内厅时，对刘可大说：“小心不要四处乱看，恐怕会给你带来麻烦。”等到出来后，少年说：“刚才已经咨询并告知了，你为何违背约定？但因为这个原因，我不能再隐瞒了。”

My father is the god of Mount Hua, an old acquaintance of yours. In the end, it will be beneficial, so there is no need to fear." Shortly after, food was served, and he instructed the attendant, "Take separate food from the human world for Liu Xiucai." When the food arrived, they each ate to their fill, and wine was also served to enjoy together, and nothing was left undone. Liu Xiucai requested a thorough check of his own records, asking what progress should be made and how things would be this year. The yellow-robed official checked the records. After a while, the official said, "Mr. Liu will pass the imperial examination next year and will hold seven official positions." Liu Xiucai insistedently requested a prediction for this year, and the official said, "This year, you will only be a county magistrate." Both of them were sorry about this, but Liu Xiucai insistedently requested the higher prediction. The official revised the prediction again, and after the official left, Liu Xiucai repeatedly expressed his concern, lamenting the reduced position. Liu Xiucai feared that the gods and spirits would not believe him, so he insistedently requested the higher prediction again. In the end, he lost his position because of this. The following year, he departed and passed the imperial examination in the capital. After several years, he was appointed as the county magistrate of Xingyang and died.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the magistrate of Luoyang, Yang Rang, was once out on an official errand when he saw a fortune-teller sitting calmly under the shade of a槐 (槐) tree. He passed by, but the fortune-teller remained seated, unperturbed. A low-ranking official, Wu Bo, ordered the fortune-teller to stand up and move aside, but the fortune-teller did not budge. Yang Rang then had his hands tied and brought the fortune-teller to the main hall, intending to beat him. He personally interrogated the fortune-teller, who raised his head and said, "You are a county magistrate for only two days. Why are you accusing me?" Yang Rang asked what he meant, and the fortune-teller replied, "In two days, you will die." Yang Rang was greatly startled and asked how the fortune-teller knew this. The fortune-teller explained what he had seen. Yang Rang's entire household was filled with fear. They told the fortune-teller, "If you can predict this, you must be able to avert it. How can we avoid this fate?" Yang Rang bowed twice and pleaded for release. The fortune-teller said, "I will use your knowledge and experience to protect you. Whether you can be saved or not, it is uncertain." The fortune-teller then led Yang Rang into the eastern courtyard, where he ordered Yang Rang to have his hair loose, barefoot, and stand with his back to the wall. The fortune-teller himself sat at the table and wrote a talisman. After midnight, the fortune-teller smiled and said to Yang Rang, "Tonight, you will be fortunate enough to escape. Tomorrow, prepare thirty sheets of paper to make money, and make many cakes and pies along with a pot of wine. Go outside the gate of the prison, into the mulberry grove, and wait for someone who passes by to give them the food and drink. If the person wears a black robe with the right sleeve exposed, that is the messenger who will call you. If he stays and eats the food, you will have no worries. Otherwise, it will truly be difficult to save you."

The person should change their clothes and stay in a small room to wait, using polite language to apologize and inquire about the other party's desires. This is all the advice I have." Rang followed the advice. As the sun set in the west, the wine and food were almost finished, but the black-furred person did not arrive, causing Rang to be deeply worried. Soon, the black-furred person arrived, inviting Rang to join them, and was pleased to bring many gifts. Rang then bowed and greeted respectfully, and the person said, "You came to my place several times yesterday and did not see me again. I suspected you were staying in the east courtyard, protected by good spirits, so I did not dare to intrude. Now, the underworld is repeatedly summoning you, what should we do?" Rang bowed twice and asked for help many times, burning paper money to provide for the black-furred person's journey. The ghost said, "I am grateful for your great favor. Tomorrow, I will come with officials from the underworld to discuss the matter, so prepare a lavish meal." After saying this, the ghost disappeared. The next day, Rang prepared a feast, offering the finest of land and sea. As evening approached, the messenger and his followers of several dozen people arrived together, enjoying a banquet that was unusually grand and enjoyable. They said to each other, "How can we not be concerned about the matter of the local official (杨长官)?" After a while, they told Rang, "Your neighbor 杨锡 also has talent, but now he is being taken by the king to make gold. When you hear the fifth watch drum sound, you should wait outside 杨锡's door. If you hear crying, you will be saved." Rang followed the advice and went. He saw the ghost on the tree, wanting to go to 杨锡's house, but was bitten by a dog and could not proceed. Soon, he entered through a gap in the wall, hesitating and hearing crying, and Rang was ultimately saved. ○李湜

During the Kaiyuan period, Li Shi of Zhao Prefecture visited the Hua Mountain Temple. Passing by the courtyard of the Three Ladies, he suddenly saw that the deities were living people, and they invited him into a luxurious tent, where they experienced utmost joy and harmony. The three ladies alternately engaged in merriment, speaking until the end before departing. As they were about to part, they told Li Shi, "Every year from the seventh to the twelfth of the seventh month, the mountain god ascends to heaven. At that time, we will welcome you, and you should not refuse or decline. Today's meeting is also during this period, hence the joy." For the next seven years, Li Shi would faint on those days, and his family would watch over him for three days before he regained consciousness. He described his experiences: "The tent was ethereal, with precious mats and fine silk cushions. They waved moon fans to cool the heat and draped silk robes to release fragrance. Their jade pendants made a cool sound, and the fragrant breeze flowed continuously. When I arrived, they all smiled with star-like dimples, and their faces were adorned with flowers, delicate and beautiful. They told stories of separation and shed tears, and discussed new joys, becoming emotionally close. All three ladies were there. Li Shi's talent was remarkable, and he was particularly valued, and each enjoyed their joy. When they returned home, they all felt sad and wept, extending the moment and cherishing the farewell." After regaining consciousness, Li Shi's appearance became fluid and permeated, and he suffered illness for ten days or so before recovering. A magician saw Li Shi and said, "You have evil spirits." He wrote a talisman, and after that, although they met, they could not get close. Two of the ladies, one surnamed Wang and the other surnamed Du, cursed, saying, "Extremely immoral, why wear a talisman?" The young lady surnamed Xiao had a deep bond of gratitude and righteousness, wept and looked at Li Shi, and warned him, "Do not speak for three years, for speaking will not only harm you but also harm me." Li Shi asked about his future position, and they said, "You should pass the imperial examination and end as a small county magistrate." All of these predictions came true.
**Wang Ji (Liang Dynasty)**

Wang Ji was the nephew of Wang Xuan, a high-ranking official. During the year 758, he was residing temporarily in Kuaiji. One day, his servant fell ill and died, but after several days, the servant came back to life, saying that he had been summoned by officials in the afterlife. The officials asked, "Whose servant are you?" The servant replied, detailing his master's identity. The officials then said, "We are now summoning your master to serve as a General of the Five Paths." They gave the servant some form of assistance, allowing him to return to the living world. Along the way, the servant saw many banners and guards, and when he asked what they were for, he was told, "They are welcoming General Wang." Soon after, Wang Ji died. On the day of his death, people saw a procession of chariots and horses, with countless guards, all there to welcome him. When asked why, they explained that it was to welcome Wang Ji. ○ Zhang Guo's Daughter During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the daughter of Zhang Guo, the Military Governor of Yizhou, died at the age of fifteen. Unable to bear parting with her, the family temporarily buried her beneath the eastern pavilion. Later, Zhang Guo was transferred to the position of Governor of Zhengzhou, and because the distance was too great to transport her remains, they left her there. Soon after, Liu Yi succeeded Zhang Guo. One day, Liu Yi's son was staying in the pavilion and, as dusk fell, he saw a beautiful woman approaching from outside. Suspecting that she might be a woman seeking to elope, Liu Yi approached her, and they became acquainted. They decided to stay together and spend the night. The woman was charming and graceful in her manner, and Liu Yi cherished her deeply. She would come to him every evening and leave at dawn. After several months, she suddenly told Liu Yi, "I was once the daughter of the former Military Governor Zhang, and unfortunately, I died young. I have been buried in this pavilion. Fate has decreed that I should live again, and I wish to be with you. In three days, you will see me buried. Dig slowly and wait for my breath, but be careful not to startle me." She pointed to the place where she had been buried and then left. On the appointed day, Liu Yi was overjoyed. He went there alone with one of his servants at night, dug deeply for several feet, and found a lacquered coffin.

徐 opened the coffin and found the woman's complexion fresh and radiant, her limbs warm and supple, her clothes and makeup intact and undamaged. He gently lifted her and placed her on the bed, where she had a faint breath through her nose. Shortly after, she had breath in her mouth. He gave her a thin gruel, which she could swallow in small amounts. By dawn, she had revived, gradually regaining the ability to speak and sit up. After a few days, she began to fear that her parents would find out, so she used an excuse of studying books, making it inconvenient to leave her room, and often sent someone with food and drink to her chamber. Her father, Yi, suspected that his son was different, and while he was outside seeing off a guest, he secretly looked into his son's room and saw the woman there. He asked her about the situation, and she fully explained, revealing that the coffin was still under the bed. Yi and his wife were moved with sorrow and joy, saying, "This was a meeting of souls due to profound feelings, why not tell us earlier?" They then hid her in the hall. The son did not see the woman and was very surprised. Yi then said to him, "This was a unique and extraordinary meeting, unparalleled in a thousand years, telling me would not harm anything, why go to such lengths to hide it?" He then sent someone to Zhengzhou to fully report to Guo, requesting marriage. The parents were moved with sorrow and joy, and they set a date for the wedding, which she became a good wife. Later, she gave birth to several children.

Chapter on Ye Jingneng In the early years of the Kaiyuan era, Emperor Xuanzong, because the empress had no son, ordered the Taoist Ye Jingneng to submit a petition to the Jade Emperor of the Heavenly Capital, asking if the empress had a son. After a long time, the reply came down. It stated: "No son." The handwriting was very clear. Chapter on the Village Chief of Yingyang The Village Chief of Yingyang, whose name is not recorded, once returned to the village drunk, stopping at a temple dedicated to a young woman. He tied his horse and lay down at the temple gate. After a while, he began to wake up, turning his head but unable to get up. He heard someone knocking on the temple gate, the sound being very loud. Soon, he heard a voice inside asking, "Who is it?" The response was, "The official ordered someone to go and make it rain." The temple resident replied, "The whole family went to the mountain temple as guests, and now there is no one left." The official said, "Just take the one lying at the gate." The temple resident said, "This is a passerby; how can he be used?" Despite their struggle, they could not avoid it, so they called out to the man to get up. They then took him to a place where everything was shrouded in misty clouds. There was an animal like a camel, and the official carried him onto the camel's back and gave him a bottle. The official warned him, "Just hold the bottle straight, do not let it tip over." The animal then started moving. The water in the bottle fell in droplets. At that time, it had been a long drought, and looking down, he saw the place where the people lived, fearing the rain would be insufficient, so he tipped the bottle. The rainmaking was finished, and the official let him return. When he reached the temple gate, he saw his own body in the water, and then went forward and came back to life. He rode his horse home. Because of tipping the bottle, the house was flooded, and everyone in the house died. He went mad from then on and died after several months.

The Noble Consort Huai In the early years of the Kaiyuan era, the Noble Consort Huai was favored by Emperor Xuanzong and bore Prince Qingsong. After her death, she was buried in Chang'an. By the twenty-eighth year, bandits planned to open her tomb. They constructed a fake grave over a hundred steps from the actual burial site, making it appear as if a burial was taking place. They secretly dug a tunnel from the fake grave to the real tomb. Upon opening the coffin, the Consort's face was as if she were still alive, and her limbs could still bend and stretch. The bandits acted with impunity, insulting her, cutting off her wrist to take a gold bracelet, and severing her tongue to prevent her from communicating in dreams. They placed her body on its side and lit candles in the dark, taking all the treasures inside the tomb, too numerous to count, and moved them to the fake grave. In the city, they used a soft carriage to carry an empty coffin, and at dusk, they stayed overnight at the tomb, placing the various items in the spirit car and funeral car, then covering and returning. Before the reburial, Prince Qingsong dreamed of the Consort with her hair loose and naked, weeping as she came to him, saying, "The bandits opened my tomb and further insulted me. My lonely soul is wronged beyond words. But I will see them fail at the Chunming Gate." She then described the situation in detail before disappearing. The prince, always filial, was suddenly awakened in tears. The next morning, he reported this to the emperor, who then summoned the Prefect of Jingzhao and the County Magistrate of Wannian, ordering them to apprehend the bandits urgently. When the bandits returned to the city with the treasures, intending to enter the Chunming Gate, the gatekeeper stopped them. Upon searching the carriage, they found all the valuables. All the bandits were captured, and under interrogation, they confessed. Dozens of people, all sons of nobles and high officials lacking moral standards, were arrested. The prince requested to personally execute the five ringleaders, and the emperor agreed. The hearts and livers of the bandits were removed, cooked, and offered as sacrifices. The rest were beaten to death outside the Jingzhao Gate. The Noble Consort was reburied, and the prince mourned her in his heart for three years.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, Zhang Jiayou served as the governor of Xiangzhou. The governor's residence had a reputation for being haunted, and as soon as Zhang Jiayou arrived, there were ghostly disturbances in his household. The disturbances were extremely disruptive, but Zhang Jiayou was not afraid. In the western courtyard, the small hall and other food items were overturned. Zhang Jiayou went to investigate and saw a woman. He asked her, "Who are you, spirit?" The woman replied, "I am the daughter of the late Zhou dynasty general, the former governor of Xiangzhou, Duke Wei Chi. There is a great injustice in my family, and I wish to see the governor to present my case." Zhang Jiayou said, "I will attend to you respectfully." Shortly after, she arrived. She was dignified and imposing, with a look of nobility, and chose to show respect to Zhang Jiayou. He invited her to sit. He asked her, "Living as a virtuous person, dying as a bright spirit, why do you cause fear and disturbance at night, frightening the children, thus causing this state to be called an unlucky one? Why, being upright, have you come to this?" She said, "In the past, the Zhou dynasty came to an end, and Yang Jian usurped the throne. As a subject of the Zhou, how could I bear to see the state fall? Therefore, I wanted to preserve my loyalty and lead the way in upholding righteousness, hoping to restore order and preserve the legacy of our founding emperor."
