A loyal minister of the Zhou dynasty, Wei Xiaokuan, was unable to act upon righteousness when he heard it and instead fell under the control of Yang Jian, who later became Emperor Wen of the Sui dynasty. With the strength of one state, he faced the overwhelming forces of the entire world. His sincere loyalty seemed to reach the heavens, yet no help came from all corners of the earth. Eventually, he lost his position and his entire family was massacred. More than sixty members of his family lay in the hall, their bones accumulating over time, and their sorrow grew deeper. It was impossible to change their fate, and those who knew of their plight were too afraid to speak out, leaving no one to lodge a complaint on their behalf. Your Excellency, please bestow your attention upon us. If the remains of the fallen could be spared and the spirits of the dead could be summoned and honored, then even on the day of their death, it would feel like living years. Master Jia You agreed to this request. On another day, he had the accumulated bones exhumed and buried with proper rites behind the hall. He then turned the hall into a temple, where prayers and offerings were made regularly. Master Jia You had a daughter who was about eight or nine years old. When the family wanted to ask for something, they would have her speak on behalf of the spirit, and the spirit would always respond. When the spirit wanted to convey a message to Master Jia You, it would also have the little girl come forward, which had become a regular occurrence. Later, when Master Jia You’s family traveled, the spirit would always send unseen soldiers to escort them out of the territory. Upon their return, the soldiers would report that they had escorted them to a certain place, no further than the Yellow River Bridge to the west.

○ Wang Fazhi In Tonglu, there was a young woman named Wang Fazhi who, from a young age, served the deity known as Langzi Shen. During the Dali era, she suddenly heard the deity speak in a deep, adult voice. Her father asked, "Are these not words of a sage or wise person?" The deity replied, "Yes. My surname is Teng, my given name is Chuan Yin, and I originally come from Jingzhao Wan Nian, residing in the district of Chongxian Fang. I have a connection with Fazhi." The deity engaged in conversation, demonstrating profound understanding of the principles of things, and was greatly esteemed by officials in various regions. The county magistrate of Tonglu, Zheng Feng, a man of curiosity, often summoned Wang Fazhi to his residence and instructed her to invite the deity known as Teng Shier Lang. After a long while, the deity finally arrived. The deity's conversation was imbued with the demeanor of a scholar, and Zheng Feng listened without tiring. Whenever he encountered poets and writers, the deity would discuss the classics, recite poetry, and converse joyfully all day. Once, a visiting monk came to Wang Fazhi seeking alms, and the deity conversed with him, presenting a poem: "I stand out without seeking fame to become a monk, always harboring a small aspiration in the blue sky. Today, my heroic spirit soars, but who can sit for long on the precious lotus flower?"

He once composed a poem to give to someone, saying: "Throughout my life, my talent has been insufficient, Yet in establishing my character, faithfulness has been more than enough. I myself lament having no great calamity, Gentlemen, do not treat me as distant." On the twenty-fifth day of the second month in the sixth year, Dai Fu gathered with Xu Huang, the Left Guard Military Officer, Cui Xiang, magistrate of Longquan, Li Congxun, deputy magistrate of Danyang County, and local residents Han Wei and Su Xiu, at the residence of Feng. When Fa Zhi arrived, the host summoned Teng Chuan Yin. After a long delay, Teng finally came. He exchanged words with Xu and the others for hundreds of lines, then addressed the assembled scholars, requesting that each recite a poem. After the recitations, the group requested his own poetry, and he spontaneously recited two poems, saying: "At the river mouth, the tide comes in, vast and spreading, A lotus boat sways and drifts, gathering flowers is hard. My springheart unfulfilled, I return in vain, I shall wait until the tide stills to fold and look again." He said, "Do not mock me, gentlemen." He then recited another: "Suddenly, a cloud flies over the lake, Unaware, my clothes are wet with rain on the boat. I plucked the lotus, yet I completely forgot it, In vain, I used a lotus leaf to cover my head on the way home." He remarked himself, "This poem is also somewhat hesitant." He then instructed Fa Zhi's younger brother to exchange words with Feng for hundreds of lines, and then departed.

○郭知运 During the Kaiyuan period, Governor Guo Zhiyun of Liangzhou went on an inspection tour. He died suddenly, one hundred miles away from the city, at an inn. His soul then came out, instructing the innkeeper to lock the room and not open it, and returned to the governor's residence, without the entourage knowing. He stayed in the residence for over forty days, handling both public and private affairs. Afterward, he sent someone to the inn to fetch his own funeral. Upon arrival, he inspected the funeral preparations himself. After the funeral rites were completed, he bid farewell to his family, threw himself into the coffin, and then disappeared. ○汝阴人 A man from Ruyin named Xu lost his father at a young age. He was fair-skinned and had a graceful demeanor. He loved fine clothes and good horses, and enjoyed leisurely outings without limit. He often took a yellow dog with him, hunting in wild ravines, and would rest under a large tree when tired. The tree was over a hundred feet tall and had a girth of several dozen feet, with high branches extending to the side, casting shade over several acres. Looking up at the branches, he saw a colorful bag hanging, thinking it might have been left behind, he took it back home. The knot could not be untied, and he found it very unusual and precious, placing it in his headwear box. As evening approached, the bag transformed into a woman, who presented a name card and said, "Lady Wang orders me to convey her message." After presenting her name, she left. Soon, a strange fragrance filled the room, and gradually, the sound of chariots and horses could be heard. Xu went out to see and saw a line of torches, with a young man riding a white horse, accompanied by more than ten horsemen, coming straight to him. The young man said, "My younger sister, a simple woman, secretly admires your noble virtue and wishes to entrust a good match to a gentleman like you. What do you think?" Xu, impressed by the young man's spirit, did not refuse strongly. The young man immediately ordered his attendants to clean and prepare a separate room. Shortly after, the woman's carriage arrived, and light and fragrance filled the road.

A dozen maids, all of them beautiful, rode horses and carried screens to escort the lady down from her carriage. They invited her into a separate room, which was fully furnished with curtains, beds, and mats, causing the family to be greatly surprised, as they could see everything. The young man urged Xu to bathe and put on new clothes, and the maids helped him into the women's quarters. The lady, aged sixteen or seventeen, was unparalleled in beauty, wearing a green robe with flowing sleeves, adorned with dazzling and intricate jewelry. She descended the stairs to pay respects, and they ascended the hall together. The young man then left. The room was decorated with a cloud-mother screen, a green silk curtain, and deer-patterned brocade screens surrounding the four walls. A grand feast was set out, featuring a variety of exotic fruits that were sweet, delicious, fresh, and fragrant, not of this world. The dining ware included seven-lobed shells, nine-branched plates, red shell cups, and lotus leaf bowls, all made of gold with intricate designs, embellished with precious stones. There was also a jade vessel containing wine from the Carthage region, which was fragrant and strong. Candles with connected wicks were placed on the table, all on purple jade plates, as bright as day. Xu, usually light and unrestrained, boasted about the surroundings, and was very pleased. Once seated, Xu asked, "I am a humble and uneducated person, living in a modest and cramped dwelling. It is not fitting for you to show such deep regard. Joy and excitement intermingle, and I do not know what to do."

The woman replied, "My father is the Southern General of Zhongle. Despite your humble status, he wishes for you to rely on a noble person. I serve and refine myself personally, and I am fortunate to have a good meeting with you. My joy and sincere wish are deep." She then asked, "What is the current position of the Southern General?" He replied, "He is in charge of a separate department of the Grand Master, like the ancient Four Garrison Generals." When the wine was consumed, he sighed, "What a wonderful night this is, to see such a good person." Her speech was clear and charming, something never heard or seen before. She taught him to play the zither with the tunes "Flying Wild Geese" and "Parting Cranes," singing while bending her neck, and poured wine for him. Her clear and mournful sound, graceful and unrestrained demeanor, could hardly control herself. He could not bear his emotions and quickly embraced her. She gave a subtle glance and smile, saying, "Having been criticized by poets for their emotional response, and also laughed at by guests for their emotional response, what to do?" She looked around and ordered the banquet to be cleared, removing the candles and going to the tent. They freely enjoyed each other's company, with her plump and delicate body, soft and smooth skin like candy. The next day, she called all family members, performing the formal rites of marriage, and gave generous gifts. Three days later, the young man returned and said, "My father is deeply grateful and ashamed, wishing to meet, sending someone to welcome." They went together, arriving at the previous hunting place, where there was no longer a large tree.

Upon seeing the red doors and white walls, it was as if in today's large official residences, where soldiers lined up on both sides and all greeted and bowed. The young man led her in, and she saw the Lord of the Manor wearing a flat-topped hat and a red silk robe, sitting on a high throne in the main hall, with spears arranged and banners set up in the courtyard. Xu bowed to pay her respects, and the Lord of the Manor stood up, gesturing for her to ascend the steps. He asked with labor and concern, "The young girl lost her support at a young age, fortunately, she was able to rely on your high wisdom. Infinite gratitude and joy. But this is also a predestined spiritual connection, not by the most sincere mutual feelings, how could it be possible?" Xu thanked him, and he then led her inside. The hallways were deep and profound, with circular corridors and winding pavilions, all connected and continuous. In the central hall, a grand gathering was in full swing, the feast lively and joyful. He then ordered music to be set up, the music complex and varied, the tunes novel and unique. Dozens of singing girls, all beautiful and elegant. After the performance ended, he gave them a generous gift of gold and silk, and provided them with servants and horses, and her family was then well-provided for. He also built a house for her in the neighborhood, all extremely luxurious. The young lady was skilled in the art of cultivating life through the principles of the Dao, and Xu's physical strength and vitality were much better than usual, indicating that she was a true spiritual being. When she returned occasionally, she was always accompanied by the young lady, and the Lord of the Manor always gave her generous gifts. For several decades, she had five children, but her beauty remained intact. After Xu died, she then took her children and left, and no one knew where they went.

In the second year of the Dali era, Li Zuoshi, the County Magistrate of Shanyin, fell ill and remained sick for several days. After partially recovering, he traveled from Kuaiji to Longqiu, where he met his cousin, Zongren Shu, who had become the magistrate. Li Zuoshi stayed in the magistrate's hall for several days. One evening, he sat down with his guest Li Ju, lighting a lamp. Suddenly, he saw twenty people dressed in red and purple, all holding military weapons, rushing to pay homage in the courtyard. Li Zuoshi asked who they were, and they replied, "We are ghost soldiers. The Great King has appointed you as a judge and has specially sent us to welcome and wait upon you to serve as your attendant." Li Zuoshi said, "I am already in mourning, and such behavior is not proper. How did the King know about me?" They replied, "It was County Magistrate Dou Kan of Wuyi who recommended you." Li Zuoshi said, "Dou Kan does not know me, why was I recommended?" They replied, "The royal decree has already been issued; it is difficult to refuse."

In a moment, Kan arrived, paying formal respects, and behaved with a reserved and dignified demeanor, like an ordinary person. He sat and told Zhao Shi, "The king seeks a son-in-law, and orders to choose from the highest-ranking families. The reason for proposing this is also due to karma or destiny." Zhao Shi refused firmly but could not succeed. Shortly after, the king's daughter also arrived, accompanied by a sweet and fragrant scent, and a grand procession of carriages and cavalry. Zhao Shi descended the steps to greet and bow, seeing the daughter's appearance, attire, and grace, and was quite pleased. She said to Zhao Shi, "Who among people does not die? As rare as you are, it is not appropriate to refuse repeatedly, which will anger the king." Zhao Shi knew he could not escape in the end. After a long time, the king's daughter and Kan left, leaving behind more than two hundred attendants. They served as officials. The next day, Shu and his younger brother Zao came together to Zhao Shi. Zhao Shi told them the whole story, saying, "I will certainly not survive, so I ask for one meal." Shu prepared a lavish feast. Zhao Shi ate pheasant soup, suddenly said he did not see the bowl, scolded the attendants, "Why did you take away the soup?" and fell onto the food table, then died. His wife Zheng was in Kuaiji, and on the evening when the funeral boat arrived, a maid suddenly spoke as if Zhao Shi's spirit, saying, "The king's daughter has already married elsewhere, but send me to return my wife." She spoke with great sorrow.

Wang Guangben, during the Kaiyuan period, served as the Deputy Governor of Luozhou. In the spring, the Prefect sent Guangben to tour the counties. After several days, his wife, Lady Li, died suddenly. Upon returning, he regretted not being present to provide medical care, believing her death to be unjust. He wept incessantly, his sorrow touching those around him. After more than ten days, when all his children had wept, Guangben wept again, uttering over a hundred sobs. Suddenly, he saw Lady Li emerge from behind the curtain, dressed in her most beautiful and elaborate attire, even more splendid than usual. Guangben stopped weeping and asked her about her death. Lady Li said, "I have not yet departed; I am still in this hall. Hearing your intense weeping, I in the netherworld am even more deeply moved. It is said, 'Excessive grief by the living disturbs the peace of the dead.' Indeed, this is true. From now on, I do not wish for you to grieve so, as it burdens the realm of the dead." She then entrusted her family with instructions: to ordain her daughter as a nun and to free her maidservant to become a free person, all matters being handled with propriety. She stayed for a meal's time, then said to Guangben, "The ways of the living and the dead are vastly different; it is not fitting for me to stay long, and this only deepens my regret." After speaking, she entered the hall and vanished. While others heard only her words, only Guangben saw her.

Cui Minke, from Boling, was known for his straightforward and honest nature, and he was fearless of gods and spirits. When he was ten years old, he died suddenly. He remained dead for eighteen years before being revived. He said that he had been wrongfully summoned, and he struggled to clear himself, eventually being released after more than a year. The king told Cui Minke, "You should return. However, your house has fallen into disrepair, what should you do?" Cui Minke persistently prayed to return. The king said, "You should be reborn again and receive doubled official salary." Cui Minke refused. The king found it hard to argue against him and lingered for a long time. Cui Minke stated the case of injustice, and the king, unwillingly, sent someone to the Western Country to seek the medicine of rebirth. It took several years to return. The medicine reached his bones, and all became flesh, except for the soles of his feet, which did not regenerate, leaving the bones exposed. Later, the family frequently dreamed that Cui Minke said, "I am alive." They then opened the coffin. Initially, there were signs of life, and he was nursed for over a month before fully recovering. In the afterlife, Cui Minke examined his own fate and was destined to become a prefect of ten districts. He then repeatedly sought out dangerous positions, disrespecting gods and spirits, but eventually remained unharmed.

Afterward, serving as the governor of Xuzhou, none dared to occupy the main hall, according to tradition, "it was the former hall of Xiang Yu." When Mǐnshé arrived at the state, he immediately ordered the cleaning. After serving for several days, suddenly heard a loud shout in the air, saying, "I am the Western Chu Overlord, how dare Cui Mǐnshé take my former residence!" Mǐnshé calmly said, "How contemptible Xiang Yu is! He failed to compete with Han Gaozu for the empire. Now he even competes with Cui Mǐnshé over a ruined house! Moreover, the king died at the U River, his head traveled ten thousand miles. Even if there is any lingering spirit, what is there to fear!" And then became silent. The hall thus became peaceful. Later, serving as the governor of Huazhou. Near the Hall of Mount Hua, early in the night, someone heard shouting in the temple. Upon looking, the courtyard torches were very bright, and hundreds of soldiers were lined up. Received an order saying, "To meet the bride with the third son." It was also said, "Cui the magistrate is in the state, do not dare to cause sudden storms and heavy rains." All replied, "We dare not." After they left, then nothing was seen.

During the early years of the Yonghui era, Zhang Cong served as the magistrate of Nanyang. While sleeping in his chamber, he heard a moaning sound coming from the bamboo in front of his steps. When he looked, he saw nothing. This happened several nights in a row, which puzzled him, so he prayed, saying, "If there are any spirits here, please speak to me." That night, a person suddenly emerged from the bamboo, appearing very disheveled. He stepped forward and said, "During Zhu Càn's rebellion, I was killed in battle by Zhu Càn. My body is right in front of your chamber. One eye was damaged by the bamboo root, and I cannot bear the pain. Because of your kindness and wisdom, I dare to report this to you. I hope you can move my remains, and I will never forget your great kindness." The magistrate asked, "Why didn't you tell me earlier?" and agreed to help. The next day, he prepared a coffin and had the ground dug up, and indeed, a body was found with a bamboo root piercing its left eye. He then dressed it in proper attire and reburied it outside the city. Later, the magistrate flogged an old man from the village, and the old man's family planned to take revenge, intending to kill the magistrate when he went out at night. Soon after, a fire broke out in the city, spreading to more than ten households. The magistrate was about to go out to inspect the situation when he saw the ghost from before blocking his horse, saying, "What strange plans do you have, Magistrate, going out at night?" The magistrate asked who he was, and the ghost replied, "I am the one who offended you before." The magistrate then returned inside. The next day, he arrested the old man's family, and upon questioning, all the evidence confirmed their involvement, so he pursued the matter further. At night, he held a memorial service at the tomb and carved an inscription on a stone in front of it, which read, "He sacrificed himself for the nation's difficulties, dying without forgetting loyalty. His noble and steadfast soul, truly a hero among ghosts."

Yang Yuanying, during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, served as the Grand Master of Ceremonies. Twenty years later, during the Kaiyuan era, he had passed away. His son, while visiting a knife-sharpener in the Yecheng坊, recognized his father's sword in the grave and was surprised by it. He asked the knife-sharpener, "How did you get this sword?" The knife-sharpener replied, "A noble person with the appearance and clothing of a dignitary brought it for repair and said he would come to pick it up the next day at noon." The son suspected that his father had given the sword to the knife-sharpener and wondered if someone had opened his father's grave. On the appointed day, he and his brother went to the knife-sharpener's home to wait. When the time came, the person who came to retrieve the sword was indeed his father, riding a white horse, dressed as he had been in life, accompanied by five or six people. The brothers bowed to him on the side of the road, and they wept for a long time. Yang Yuanying dismounted, took the sword, and led his sons to a secluded place to discuss family matters. He then asked, "Is your mother still at home?" They replied, "She has been buried with him for fifteen years." Yang Yuanying said, "I didn't know that at first." He sighed repeatedly. He told his son, "I have official duties to attend to and cannot stay long. Tomorrow, you can come here again, and I will give you some money to help you." As agreed, the next day, Yang Yuanying arrived, and they received three hundred thousand coins. He warned them, "You must spend this money within a few days." After saying this, he bid farewell and left. The sons followed him, weeping. Yang Yuanying then said to his son, "You cannot resolve this matter. The paths of the living and the dead are different. How can there be a father and son relationship lasting a hundred years?" After saying this, he bid farewell again and left. The son followed him until they reached the Shangdong Gate, where Yang Yuanying rode into the Maoshan mountains and disappeared after a few dozen steps. A few days later, all the items in the market were gone. Three days after that, all the people in the market received paper money.

A servant of County Magistrate Sun Mian in Quwo During the reign of County Magistrate Sun Mian in Quwo, a servant who was six years old had never spoken. Later, when Sun Mian's mother was sitting on the steps, the servant suddenly stared intently. The mother found this strange and asked him, and the servant then smiled and said, "Do you remember, Mother, when you were a child in your 'total angle' time, you wore a yellow skirt and a white short jacket, and raised a wild cat? Do you still remember?" The mother also remembered. The servant said, "At that time, the wild cat was me. After it escaped, it hid in a tile groove and heard your crying. By evening, it came down and entered the east garden, where there was an ancient tomb. The wild cat lived hidden there for two years. Later, it was killed by hunters and followed the usual procedure to meet King Yama. King Yama said, 'You have no other sins, you will become a human.' He was sent to be born in Haizhou as a beggar's child. Throughout his life, he suffered from hunger and cold, dying at twenty. He then saw King Yama again, who said, 'I will make you a servant in a noble household. Although the name of the servant is not good, it is far from being afraid.' Thus, he came to be here. Now, I have been through three lives, and you are still alive, still well and blessed, isn't that strange?"

#### Zhang Shilan and Wang Jingchao Zhang Shilan was skilled in divining tombs, and his disciple Wang Jingchao inherited his profession. During the Yuan Dynasty, Tang Yaocen died in Zhengzhou, and Zhang Shilan sent Wang Jingchao to determine the burial site. After the burial, the Tang family's livestock began to speak, cursing, "What kind of insects and dogs, burying us in such a place?" The family, fearing the strange occurrences, quickly moved the tomb, and the supernatural phenomena ceased. #### Tomb of a Slave Official In Zan County, there is a tomb of a slave official from the Eastern Han Dynasty. Initially, villagers cultivated the land near the tomb, and every autumn harvest, the fields close to the tomb often suffered poor yields. After several years, they grew tired of it. Later, they began to watch the tomb at night and saw four large geese emerging from the tomb, eating the grain. When they chased the geese, they would retreat. The villagers, who had long heard that the tomb of the slave official contained treasures, decided to open it together. Upon entering the front chamber, they saw geese flapping their wings and attacking people. The robbers hit them back with sticks, but the geese did not move anymore; they were actually bronze geese. Gradually, they entered the outer hall and found two treasure swords, along with many other unidentified objects. Next, they entered the main chamber, where the water was deep, and a man in purple robes stood at the door. He fought with the robbers. The robbers rushed forward to attack him, but he ran out among them and entered the county, shouting loudly, "The robbers are looting my tomb!" The gatekeeper asked, "Where is your tomb?" He replied, "It is exactly the tomb of the slave official." The county magistrate ordered the village head to chase the robbers, and they were all captured. At the end of the Yuan Dynasty, the governor of Mingzhou submitted more than thirty cases.

Thunder Battle** At the end of the Tang Dynasty during Emperor Xuanzong's reign, in Leizhou, there was a battle between thunder gods and a whale. The whale emerged from the water, and dozens of thunder gods were seen moving up and down in the air, some setting fire, others cursing and striking. The battle lasted for seven days before ending. People living by the sea went to watch, but it was unknown who won, but they saw the sea water turn bright red. **Zhang Xumi** During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong in the middle period of the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Xumi, a grain storage official from Quanjiao, Chuzhou, was sent by the county to deliver animals to the state. The mountain road was dangerous, and in Huainan, there were many benevolent halls and wells used to shelter travelers. At dusk, it rained heavily, and Zhang Xumi and the monk Zini entered a benevolent hall together. Zhang Xumi drove the donkey driver Wang Lao to collect the donkeys in the rain. Soon after, he heard a sound from the clouds falling to the ground and suddenly saw nine village girls supporting a cart. One of the girls was Wang Lao's daughter, Aotui, who had died half a year ago and was also in the cart. She saw her father and expressed her joy and sorrow, asking about family matters without leaving anything out. Her companions urged her to leave. Initially, as the cart rose, clouds gathered and thunder sounded, revealing that it was a thunder cart.

#### P. Ximin In the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named P. Ximin, whose family lived in the Eastern Capital. On a hot summer night, dozens of his brothers gathered in the hall. Suddenly, a heavy rain began, accompanied by thunder and lightning, and a dark and gloomy atmosphere. Something fell into the courtyard, making a rustling sound. They lit a fire to see what it was, and it turned out to be a woman. She was wearing a yellow silk skirt and a cloth blouse, but she did not understand their language, so they regarded her as a heavenly maiden. After five or six years, she learned to speak Chinese. When asked about her hometown, she did not know. She only said, "In my hometown, they eat polished rice, but they have no bowls or utensils, and they use willow boxes to store rice and eat it." They still did not know which country she came from. It was said that at first, in her own country, at night, she went out, and was taken by lightning, and soon fell into P. Ximin's courtyard. #### M. Jingxian In the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named M. Jingxian, who had a younger brother named A Si. A Si was stubborn, foolish, and unrestrained, and M. Jingxian often scolded him. However, his mother added love and concern, and bent to explain. One day, A Si responded loudly, and thunderclouds suddenly arrived, dragging M. Jingxian into the clouds. There were officials, with dozens of attendants, who rebuked and questioned M. Jingxian. He answered, "Because the younger brother was not well-behaved, there was a lack of support, so I scolded him. The mother ordered me to release him, not to curse her." The officials did not understand his words, and found a green-clad person who jumped down from the air to answer for M. Jingxian. The green-clad person said, "If you let him go, when he gets home, he can write a defense, nail it to the east wall, and I will retrieve it." Then, they pushed M. Jingxian into the pond in front of the house. He came out of the water, completely unharmed. He sought paper to write a defense, nailed it to the east wall, and indeed, the wind came and the defense disappeared.

A Man Named Hulei During the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Ouyang Hulei, whose original name was Shao, and he was from Guiyang. He was strong and vigorous, brave in battle, and had once served as a county commander, earning him a reputation. He was appointed as the Longshi of Leizhou and resided in the western part of the city, in front of a large pond that used to emit clouds and mist. Those who lived there often died. When Shao arrived, he lived there without hesitation. He had someone measure the depth of the water and dug a separate large ravine, deep and wide, similar to the pond. After it was completed, he diverted the water. Then clouds arose, the sky and earth became dark and gloomy, and lightning and thunder came in great force, with flames touching the ground. Shao led more than twenty of his followers, carrying bows, arrows, and shields, and fought against the thunder god. Their clothes were burned and rolled up, and their bodies were wounded and decayed, but they did not stop. From morning until evening, the lightning and thunder dispersed, the pond also dried up, and they found a snake in the middle. The snake was shaped like a silkworm, about four or five feet long, without a head or eyes, and could not be injured by cutting or stabbing. It wriggled and crawled. They prepared a large pot and fried it in oil, but it also did not die. They melted iron and finally burned it. Then they pounded it into powder and took it all. People in the south began to call Shao 'Hulei.'

A Tale of Cheng Bi At the end of the Sui Dynasty, there was an adept residing in Mount Taibai, refining cinnabar. Having completed the Great Return Elixir, he attained immortality and lived in the mountains for several decades. There was a man named Cheng Bi who served as his attendant. The adept lived with him for over ten years but did not reveal the secret of the elixir to him. Later, Cheng Bi left due to a family misfortune. The adept said to him, "You have followed me for so long, and now you face another sorrow. I have nothing to leave you, but I will give you ten pills of elixir. One pill can transform ten pounds of red copper into gold, which will be sufficient for your funeral expenses." Cheng Bi returned home and, as instructed, transformed copper into gold to cover the costs. After the funeral was completed, Cheng Bi developed strange intentions and returned to the mountains to see the adept again. He asked for the elixir, but the adept refused. Cheng Bi then took a knife and seized him by force. When he failed to obtain the elixir, he cut off the adept's hands. When that did not work, he amputated his feet. The adept remained calm and unflustered. Cheng Bi became increasingly angry and eventually beheaded the adept. Upon examining the adept's body, he found a red bag tied behind his elbow, which contained the elixir. Cheng Bi was delighted and took the elixir down the mountain. Suddenly, he heard someone calling his name. He turned around and saw the adept standing before him. Cheng Bi was greatly shocked and said, "I did not expect you to return to life. You lack the virtue to receive the elixir, and the spirits will surely punish you. You will end up like me."

A Man Named Bi and the Tang Gold Bi, a man who was not seen by others, obtained much cinnabar and transformed it into gold. The golden color was slightly reddish, superior to ordinary gold, and could be taken as a tonic. His family became prosperous, and as a result, he was reported by others, who alleged that he had engaged in deceit. He was captured, and Bi claimed that he could turn gold, stating that there was no other reason. Emperor Taizong of Tang asked him about it, summoned him, and ordered him to make gold. When the gold was made, the emperor was pleased and granted him the rank of a fifth-rank official. He then ordered Bi to make gold, saying that it would stop only when all the copper in the world was used up. Bi made gold, totaling several tens of thousands of pounds, until the cinnabar ran out. This gold was called Tang Gold, which became more refined with each refinement, and was highly valued. When Bi ran out of cinnabar and asked to leave, Emperor Taizong ordered him to list the formula. Bi truly did not know the formula and pleaded his case, but the emperor thought he was lying and threatened him with soldiers. Bi still claimed that he knew, and the soldiers cut off his hand. When he still did not speak, they amputated his middle finger. In his desperation, Bi described the beginning and end of the process, but the emperor did not believe him and had him beheaded. The Tang Gold then circulated widely. Later, a Brahmin came, called the special treasure. The emperor examined it thoroughly in the treasury, and the Brahmin pointed to the gold and a large rug, saying, "These are the only two treasures." The emperor asked, "What is special about the rug, and why is it called a treasure?" The Brahmin ordered the rug to be spread on the ground, wetted with water, and all the water flowed away, leaving the rug completely dry. Until now, foreign countries have spread the story of Bi's gold, considering it a treasure.

○青泥珠 During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a foreign country presented the jawbone of the deity Vairocana (毗娄博义天王), the tongue of a pratyekabuddha, and a blue mud pearl (青泥珠). Empress Wu Zetian displayed the jawbone and tongue to the people, possibly to demonstrate their value or to assert her authority. The jawbone was as large as a trivet, and the tongue was green, as large as a cow's tongue. The pearl was the size of a thumb and slightly blue. Later, she gave the pearl to the monks of Ximing Temple, placing it in the forehead of a statue of Vairocana. A foreigner came to listen to a lecture at the temple and kept his eyes fixed on the pearl, showing great interest. After observing the pearl for over ten days, he finally admitted his desire to buy it. The monk initially asked for a thousand strings of cash, then gradually increased the price to ten thousand strings, but the foreigner refused to pay. Eventually, the price was set at ten thousand strings of cash, and the monk sold the pearl to the foreigner. The foreigner swallowed the pearl and hid it in his leg, then returned to his home country. Later, the monk heard about this and reported it to Empress Wu Zetian, who ordered the search for the foreigner. After several days, the foreigner was found. The messenger asked where the pearl was, and the foreigner admitted that he had swallowed it. The messenger threatened to cut open his abdomen, and the foreigner reluctantly removed the pearl from his leg. Empress Wu Zetian summoned the foreigner and asked why he had bought the pearl at such a high price. The foreigner explained that in his Western country, there was a body of water called Qingni Pond, which contained many pearls and treasures, but the mud was too deep to access them easily. If the pearl were thrown into the pond, the mud would turn into water, making the treasures accessible. Empress Wu Zetian kept the pearl, and it remained in her possession until the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.

A Pearl of an Inch in Diameter In recent times, a Persian merchant arrived at an inn in Fufeng and saw a square stone outside the owner's door. He lingered there for several days. The innkeeper asked him why he was staying, and the merchant replied, "I want to use the stone to pound silk." He then offered two thousand coins to buy the stone. The innkeeper, pleased with the money, gave him the stone. The merchant loaded the stone onto his carriage and, in front of the crowd, broke it open to reveal a pearl an inch in diameter. He cut open his armpit and hid the pearl inside, then returned to his homeland. On the ship, he sailed across the sea for over ten days when the ship suddenly began to sink. The sailors knew that the sea deity was seeking a treasure and searched everywhere for it. Finding no treasure to offer, they intended to drown the merchant. Fearing for his life, the merchant cut open his armpit and retrieved the pearl. The sailors cursed, saying, "If you seek this pearl, you will surely receive it." The sea deity then emerged with one hand, large and covered in hair, and took the pearl away.

A Precious Pearl Behind the Yue Temple in Xianyang, there was the crown of Emperor Wu of Zhou. On the crown was a pearl, as large as a rare plum, which had never been considered a treasure through the ages. During the reign of Empress Wu, a scholar passed by the temple, saw the pearl, and playfully took it. It was extremely hot, so he went to the temple gate to change his clothes, wrapped the pearl in a cloth, placed it under the feet of the statue of Vajrapani, and then forgot to retrieve it. The next day, he went to Yangzhou to collect a debt. On the way, he stayed overnight in an inn in Chenliu. At night, he heard about the foreigners competing in displaying their treasures, so he pulled up his clothes and followed to see, and then mentioned the pearl on the crown. The foreigners were greatly surprised and said, "We have long known that China has this treasure, and we were just about to go and seek it." The scholar said, "I have already lost it." The foreigners sighed in regret and told him, "If you can come here, we will reward you with gold and silk. Now, how much debt do you owe in Yangzhou?" The scholar replied, "Five hundred thousand." The foreigners then gave him five hundred thousand and asked him to return and retrieve the pearl. The scholar went to the feet of the statue of Vajrapani, and the pearl was still there, so he took it back and showed it to the foreigners. The foreigners were overjoyed, and they drank and feasted for more than ten days before finally seeking to purchase it.

Therefore, the scholar was asked, "What price do you seek?" The scholar insisted on a thousand strings of coins, and the leader laughed and said, "How could this pearl be disrespected?" They collectively set the price at fifty thousand strings of coins, and the group of foreigners pooled their money to buy it. Then, the leader invited the scholar to go to the sea together to observe the pearl's value. The scholar accompanied the leader to the eastern sea, where the leader boiled clarified butter in a silver pot and placed the pearl in a golden bottle, re-boiling it in the butter. After only seven days, two old men and hundreds of their followers arrived with treasures, seeking to redeem the pearl, but the leader refused to give it. After several more days, they brought more treasures piled up like a mountain, claiming to want to redeem the pearl, but the leader still refused. After more than thirty days, the people dispersed. Then, two beautiful dragon women, pure white and graceful, entered the pearl bottle, and the pearl and the dragon women combined into an ointment. The scholar asked, "Who are those who sought to redeem the pearl?" The leader replied, "This pearl is a great treasure, and it should be guarded by two dragon women. The many dragons cherished their daughters, so they came to redeem the pearl with various treasures. I seek to transcend the world; would I care about the wealth of this world?" The leader then applied the ointment to his feet, walked on water, and left the boat. The foreigners each said, "We all bought this pearl; why do you monopolize the benefit? You have already gone; where will we go?" The leader ordered them to apply the boiled clarified butter to their boats, and they would receive favorable winds to return home, and all went as he said. In the end, the leader disappeared, and no one knew where he went.

During the early Tang dynasty, when the capital was recaptured, the grain supply was insufficient. Censorial Commissioner Kang Yunjian, serving as the financial administrator for the Jianghuai region, levied one-fifth of the merchants and common people in the Jianghuai region to supplement the current needs. Nanchang, a major city in the Jianghuai region, was where Commissioner Li Weiyan, the magistrate and recorder, was responsible for this task. A monk requested to contribute a million units and took a small bottle from under his armpit, about the size of a clenched fist. When asked what it contained, he evasively did not answer. Li Weiyan, unable to refuse his request, pretended to be surprised and said, "How did you obtain such an object, Master? If you must sell it, I will not haggle over the price." A Persian merchant saw this and bought it at the agreed price and left. When the merchant arrived in Yangzhou, Governor Deng Jingshan learned about the matter and asked the merchant about it. The merchant replied, "The bottle contains *zǐ mò jié* (紫末羯), a substance that, when possessed, is protected by spirits and cannot be burned by fire or drowned by water. It has great value, surpassing even pearls and other precious goods." The merchant then contributed another ten thousand units and was pleased to part with his wealth without regret. Inside the bottle were twelve pearls.

### The Fruit of the Hēlìlè Gao Xianzhi, a prominent Tang dynasty general, attacked the Abbasid Caliphate and obtained a fruit called the Hēlìlè, which was about five or six inches long. When he first placed it in his stomach, he immediately felt abdominal pain and had a violent diarrhea for more than ten times. Initially, he thought the Hēlìlè was causing harm and wanted to discard it. He then asked an Arab elder for advice. The elder said, "This fruit, when worn by a person, can eliminate all diseases; those with diarrhea are expelling harmful substances." Gao Xianzhi treasured the fruit greatly. At the end of the Tianbao era, he was executed, and thus the fruit's whereabouts were lost. ### The General of Lín Huái During the Shangyuan period, the generals of Lín Huái and others held a night banquet, roasting pigs and sheep, and the aroma was abundant. Suddenly, a huge hand entered through the window and asked for a piece of meat. The guests all refused to give it. The hand asked repeatedly several times, but still, they did not give it. They then secretly tied a rope to make a noose and placed it in the hole. Pretending to say, "Here is the meat," the hand entered again. They then tied the rope around its arm and pulled very hard, but it could not be removed. Just before dawn, the hand suddenly snapped, and they looked at it, and it was a branch of a willow tree. They held it to find a tree and nearly reached the river, where it broke into pieces, often with blood.

○ Qi Han During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong in the Tang Dynasty, Qi Han, the Inspector of Henan and the Prefect of Bianzhou, proposed to open an eighteen-mile canal to reach the Qing River and level the dangers of the Yangtze River. The canal was dug by the counties along its route. Cui Yan Yi, the County Magistrate of Zhenyuan in Bozhou, supervised the digging of the canal and excavated several thousand steps, where they discovered a dragon hall. Initially, they thought it was an ancient tomb, but it appeared as if it had been newly constructed and was clean. Upon inspection, they found a five-colored dragon longer than a standard measure at the base of the northern wall. There were also five or six carp, each about a foot long, beside the dragon's head. Additionally, there were two-headed turtles, each one foot and two inches long, with eyes nine-tenths the size of ordinary turtles. Cui Yan Yi reported this to Wu Yuan Chang, the river commissioner, who then informed Qi Han. Qi Han ordered the dragon to be moved into the Yangtze River and the turtles to be brought to Bianzhou. Cui Yan Yi transported the dragon and the fish over two hundred miles to the Yangtze River. Upon reaching the riverbank, millions of white fish leaped towards the dragon, causing the water to boil. The dragon entered the Yangtze, spewed water, and was obscured by clouds and mist, disappearing from sight. Initially, when they were about to move the dragon, the censor Yuan Xi plucked one of its whiskers. Wu Yuan Chang arranged for a net to be sent to Song to transport the turtles. They encountered a pond, and the large turtle repeatedly extended its neck towards the water. The netters felt pity for it and temporarily released it into the water. The water was several feet wide but no more than five inches deep, and the large turtle disappeared. They drained the water to search for it but could not find it, only managing to bring back a small turtle.

During his leisure time, Su Ting, who initially held the position of magistrate in Wucheng, once went on a boat trip along the Xiaoxi River with his colleagues. After drinking to excess, they recited poetry and eventually arrived at Daoji Temple. In front of the temple lies the deepest part of the Xiaoxi River, which is so deep that its depth cannot be measured. There are dangerous creatures like dragons and serpents in the water, which have long been a problem for people. Su Ting, still drunk, decided to walk back from Lu Bridge. As he walked, the bridge suddenly collapsed, and he fell into the water, sinking to the bottom of the deep pool. However, a figure in the water helped him out and slowly brought him to the surface. Thus, Su Ting managed to cross the water safely.

### 斗蛟 In the late Tianbao era of the Tang dynasty, a cow in She Prefecture fought with a dragon. Initially, the dragon in the water had killed many people and animals. The cow, while drinking, was surrounded by the dragon and directly entered the bottom of the pool. They then began to fight. After several days, the cow emerged, and the pool water turned red, leading people to believe the dragon had died. ### 巴人 The people of Ba enjoyed cutting down trees in groups. At the beginning of the Yuan dynasty, more than a hundred people from Ba traveled through the mountains from Baozhong to the White Emperor Temple. There were over a hundred cypress trees in front of the temple, each with a circumference of several zhang. The group of Ba people were delighted and said, "This is a sign from heaven!" They stopped to cut down the trees. After felling more than twenty trees, an old man wearing a hat and holding a walking stick approached them and said to the Ba people, "These are divine trees. Why are you cutting them down?" The Ba people initially did not stop their work. The old man said, "I am the White Emperor. Stop cutting the ones that have already fallen. Please leave the ones that haven't fallen. There is no need to continue." The Ba people did not stop. The old man said, "If you do not stop, you will all die, and it will be of no benefit." They still did not stop. The old man then climbed the mountain and called out, "Spotted ones!" Suddenly, several tigers appeared one after another and almost completely devoured the Ba people, with only five or six escaping. The god said to them, "Because of your good intentions, I will not let you be killed. You should leave quickly." The fallen trees were still standing at the end of the Tianbao era. An imperial edict was issued to repair the inner palace, and Yang Guozhong sent someone to the mountain to issue an imperial decree to take the trees, to make boards for use. The god eventually gave them permission.

A Tale of Fèi Zhong The people of Fèi Prefecture, a region known for its fierce tigers, were of a distinct ethnic group. The entire clan bore the surname Fèi, and due to the prevalence of tiger attacks, they lived in elevated structures to avoid them. During the Yuan period, Di Guangsi served as the prefect of Fèi Prefecture, and his grandson Bo Wang was born in the government residence. Bo Wang's wet nurse's husband, Fèi Zhong, was a brave and skilled archer. Once, while carrying rice back home from the prefecture, he encountered an obstacle on the mountain path and realized it had grown late. He still had about thirty miles to go. Fèi Zhong was afraid he would not escape, so he used the blade he carried to cut several bundles of wood, struck a stone to get fire, and burned them to defend himself. Soon, he heard the sound of tigers, which shook the forest. Fèi Zhong covered the rice bag with his headscarf and tied it with his belt, standing under the firelight and climbing a tall tree. Shortly after, four tigers arrived together. They saw the rice bag and the big tiger approached cautiously. Realizing it was not a human, the tigers looked at each other in silence. The second tiger led two cubs away, leaving the big tiger alone at the fire. Suddenly, it shed its skin, revealing an old man sleeping with his hand under his head. Fèi Zhong, who was usually strong and agile, thought little of it and slowly climbed down the tree, choking the old man's throat and holding a knife to his neck. The old man begged for his life, and Fèi Zhong tied his hands and questioned him. The old man said, "I am the old man from the north village, punished to be a tiger."

The Celestial Authority has issued a decree that people are to be eaten according to the calendar. Tonight, the meal is to be had by Fei Zhong, so they waited for him. Just arrived and coincidentally met the rice bag, the mood was very depressed and dissatisfied, leaving this and waiting for him to return. Unintentionally caught by you. If you don't believe it, you can check the calendar on my waist, you will know. Fei Zhong looked at the calendar, asked, "How to save me?" The old man replied, "If there is someone with the same name, they can also substitute. If the matter is discovered later, I will bear the punishment, no more than ten days of hunger." Fei Zhong said, "Now there is Fei Zhong from the southern village, can he substitute for me?" The old man agreed. Fei Zhong first held the old man's skin up to the tree top, then descended to release the old man. The old man said, "You just tightly bind his body to the tree, if I enter the skin, I will not recognize him. If I hear a roar and fall to the ground, I will definitely be eaten. This is how things are, not breaking the agreement." Fei Zhong bid farewell, climbed the tree, and threw the skin back. The old man got the skin, entered from behind, regained his original form, roared several times, and then left. Fei Zhong returned home. Several days later, Fei Zhong from the southern village was encountered while farming and eaten.

A Woman Married to a Tiger During the early Tang Dynasty, a tiger took a woman from a human household as his wife and lived with her in a secluded mountain retreat. For two years, the woman was unaware of the tiger's true nature. One day, two guests arrived with wine and began to drink together in the room. The tiger warned his wife, saying, "These guests are somewhat unusual; be careful not to peek." Soon, they all fell asleep drunk. The woman went to look and discovered that they were all tigers. She was greatly shocked but dared not speak. After a long time, the tiger transformed back into a human form and asked his wife, "Did you not peek?" The woman replied, "At first, I didn't dare leave this place." Later, the tiger suddenly said he missed his home and wished to return to visit. After ten days, he took wine and meat with his wife and traveled together. Gradually, they arrived at the wife's home, where they encountered deep water. The woman crossed first, and the tiger was about to lift his clothes. The woman playfully remarked, "How can there be a tiger's tail coming out from behind you?" The tiger was greatly embarrassed and did not cross the water, galloping away without returning.

The Hu of Jizhou were engaged in hunting and trapping as their profession. At the end of the Tang Kaiyuan era, they chased a deer deep into the mountains. The deer, in panic, ran into a room where a Taoist priest was sitting, wearing red clothing and leaning against a table. Seeing the Hu people, the Taoist priest was startled and asked them why they had come. The Hu people stated their names in detail, saying, "We were just chasing a deer and unconsciously ventured deep inside, so we apologize for any disturbance." The Taoist priest told the Hu people, "I am the king of tigers. The Heavenly Emperor has ordered me to manage the food for all the tigers. Every animal has its designated counterpart, and none is wronged. Just now, I heard your names, and you are destined to be my food." On the table, there was a red pen, a cup, and a ledger. The Taoist priest opened the ledger and showed it to the Hu people. The Hu people feared greatly for a long time and firmly requested to be released. The Taoist priest said, "I do not mind releasing you, but it is the will of heaven. What can I do about it? If I release you, I will lose one of my meals. Since we have met, I must find a way to save you."

After a long time, the Taoist said, "Tomorrow, make a straw man, dress it in your clothes, and bring three pecks of pig's blood and one bolt of silk with you. Perhaps you will be spared." The man hesitated and did not leave. He saw a group of tigers coming to pay homage. The Taoist arranged their food, and they then dispersed. The man bowed twice and returned. The next day, he brought the items to the place. The Taoist smiled and said, "You are a good person because you are trustworthy." He then ordered the man to stand the straw man in the courtyard and place the pig's blood beside it. Then he ordered the man to climb the tree to look down at it from a height of over ten zhang, saying, "This is enough. You can tie your body to the tree with silk; otherwise, you might fall and get hurt." The Taoist then returned to his room and transformed into a tiger. He came out of the courtyard and looked up at the man, roaring several times, and jumped towards the tree, realizing the man could not be caught. He then grabbed the straw man and threw it high into the air, went to eat the pig's blood until it was finished, and returned to his room, becoming the Taoist again. He told the man, "Come down quickly." The man came down, bowed twice, and the Taoist marked his name with a red pen, thus sparing him from danger. ○Biyishi At the end of the Kaiyuan era, Chuzhou was plagued by ferocious tigers. Traps were set, but they were never caught. One moonlit night, someone climbed a tree to watch. He saw a伥ghost like a seven or eight-year-old child, naked and light-footed, completely green, and came to trigger the trap. When it passed, the person climbed down the tree to fix it. Soon, a tiger came straight and was caught in the trap and died. After a while, the child walked back crying and entered the tiger's mouth. When morning came and they looked, there was a green stone as big as an egg in the tiger's throat.

The Mountain Demon and the Striped Tiger The *shānxiāo* is a creature found in the southern regions of China. The male *shānxiāo* has one leg and walks backward, with hands and feet branching into three parts. The female *shānxiāo* likes to apply rouge and powder. They make their nests in the hollows of large trees, which are furnished with wooden screens and curtains, and they have an abundance of food. Travelers in the mountains of southern China often carry yellow grease, rouge, and money with them. The male *shānxiāo* is called "mountain lord" and demands money, while the female *shānxiāo* is called "mountain maiden" and demands rouge and powder. Those who give to them can be protected by them. During the Tianbao period of the Tang Dynasty, a northern traveler was journeying through the mountains of southern China. At night, he was afraid of tigers and wanted to sleep in a tree. Suddenly, he encountered a female *shānxiāo*. The traveler usually carried light goods and, upon seeing the *shānxiāo*, got down from the tree and bowed twice, calling out, "Mountain maiden." From afar in the tree, the *shānxiāo* asked, "What goods do you have?" The traveler gave her the rouge and powder. She was very pleased and told him, "You can sleep safely without worry." The traveler slept at the base of the tree. In the middle of the night, two tigers approached his place. The *shānxiāo* got down from the tree and put her hand on the tiger's head, saying, "Stripes, my guest is here, you should leave quickly." The two tigers then left. The next day, the traveler bid farewell to the *shānxiāo*, thanking her very respectfully. Some of the *shānxiāo* are difficult to understand. Every year, they help people farm. People provide land and seeds, and the rest of the work is done by the *shānxiāo*. When the grain is ripe, they call people to divide it equally. The *shānxiāo* are straightforward in nature and share with people, not taking more than their share. People也不敢 take more, for those who take more will encounter natural epidemics.

#### Liu Jian At the end of the Tianbao era, Liu Jian was a judge in Lingnan. While traveling through the mountains, he suddenly encountered a mountain demon, which he called an evil ghost. The mountain demon became angry and said, "Judge Liu, I am merely playing around; what harm have I done to you that you would curse me like this?" It then stood on a branch of a tree below and called for a group of tigers. Soon, a tiger arrived and was ordered to capture Judge Liu. Liu was greatly frightened and fled on horseback. In a short while, he was seized by the tiger and sat at its feet. The mountain demon laughed and said, "Judge Liu, are you going to curse me again?" Liu bowed twice and begged for his life. Slowly, the mountain demon said, "You may go." The tiger then released Liu. Liu was terrified almost to death and was carried back home, where he fell ill for several days before recovering. Liu often told others about this incident. #### Qin Zili Qin Zili, a man from Zhangpu, was enlisted as a soldier at the end of the Tianbao era. He followed the army to Annan and fought against the Tibetans for ten years without returning. Qin's wife, Lin, was coerced by her parents into losing her resolve and was about to remarry a man from the same county, Chen. On the night of their wedding, Qin returned. His parents explained the entire process of his wife's remarriage. Qin was filled with uncontrollable anger upon hearing this. His wife's home was about ten miles from his own. At the time, he had just defeated the Tibetans and obtained a sharp sword. That night, he took the sword and went to see Lin. After walking eight or nine miles, a heavy rainstorm began, and the sky grew dark, making it impossible to proceed or retreat. Suddenly, a lightning flash illuminated the path, revealing a large tree on the side of the road. There was a side hole, and Qin temporarily took shelter inside. There were three tiger cubs already in the hole, which Qin killed all of them. After some time, a large tiger brought something into the hole and then left. Qin heard someone groaning and approached cautiously, touching the person, who turned out to be a woman.

Self-Discipline asked her who she was, and the woman replied: "I am a woman of the Lin family, previously married to Diligent Self-Discipline as his wife. He has not yet returned from serving in the military. My parents were unreasonable and forced me to remarry, and I am to marry tonight. I miss the old days and cannot see him again; my anger and resentment cannot be dispelled. So I held a scarf and hanged myself in the mulberry grove behind the house, and was taken by a tiger. Fortunately, I met you, and I am still unharmed. If you can save me, there will be a reward later." Self-Discipline said to her: "I am Self-Discipline. I returned in the morning to the house, and my parents told me you are married. Therefore, I drew my sword and came to visit. How unexpectedly I met you here!" They held each other and wept. After a while, a tiger arrived, roaring loudly at first, then bowing its body into a hole. Self-Discipline swung his sword at it, severing the tiger's waist. Fearing there might be another tiger, he did not dare to come out. Soon after, the moon became bright, and indeed another tiger arrived, seeing its mate dead and roaring even louder. It then bowed its body into the hole again and was killed once more by Self-Discipline. He carried his wife back home, and now she is still unharmed.

At the end of the Tianbao era, there was a child in Xuanchou who lived near the mountains. Every night, he would see a ghost leading a tiger to chase him, and this had happened several times already. The child told his parents, "If the ghost leads the tiger here, I will surely die. People say that 'if one is eaten by a tiger, the ghost becomes a伥 (伥鬼).' If I die, I will surely become a伥鬼. If the tiger follows my instructions, I will lead it to the village. Traps should be set in strategic locations to catch it." Several days later, the child was indeed killed by the tiger. After some time, he appeared in a dream to his father, saying, "I have become a伥鬼. Tomorrow, I will lead the tiger here. A trap should be quickly built on the western side." The father then worked with the villagers to build a trap. On the day the trap was completed, they indeed caught the tiger.

The Flute Player At the end of the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, An Lushan rose in rebellion, and Tongguan fell to the rebels. The people of the capital city fled in disarray, like birds scattering. Among the disciples of the imperial music and dance troupe was a flute player who also fled to the valleys of Mount Zhongnan. There was a Buddhist monastery there, and he took refuge there. On a clear night with bright moonlight, grieving over the chaos and full of thoughts, he took up his flute and played. The clear, piercing sound of the flute echoed through the valleys. Suddenly, a figure with a tiger's head and human body appeared, wearing a white short-sleeved robe. The flute player was terrified and stepped down from the veranda, staring in shock. The tiger-headed figure said, "How beautiful is this flute! Play it again." The flute player then played several more tunes in succession. After the music ended, for a long time, the figure suddenly fell asleep, laughing and snoring loudly. The flute player, fearing he might be discovered, quickly got up and ran out, climbing a tall tree. The branches and leaves were dense enough to hide a person. When the figure woke up and did not see the flute player, he sighed deeply and said, "I didn't eat him early enough, and he escaped." He then stood up and let out a long, mournful cry. In a short while, more than ten tigers came, looking like they were paying homage. The tiger-headed figure said, "There was a child playing the flute who took advantage of my sleep and ran away; we should divide and search for him in all directions." After speaking, they dispersed. After the fifth watch of the night, they returned, all speaking like humans, "We searched for about four or five miles but did not find him." When the moon set and the light slanted, they suddenly saw a shadow on the tall tree. The tiger looked at it and laughed, "I thought you were like lightning, disappearing instantly, but you are still here." He then led the tigers to grab him. They could not reach him, and the figure with the tiger's head tried to jump again, but failed to reach him. They then dispersed. A short while later, it became dawn, and people began to gather. The flute player was able to return with them.

A Fisherman Named Zhang Yuzhou During the Jianzhong era of the Tang Dynasty, in the north of Beihai County, Qingzhou, there was the Sea-Viewing Terrace of Qin Shi Huang. On the side of the terrace was a small lake called Jìn Pō. By the lake lived a fisherman named Zhang Yuzhou, who built a thatched hermitage and lived there. One night, a tiger suddenly burst into the hermitage. Just as Zhang Yuzhou was sleeping, he woke up just before dawn, sensing someone's presence. At first, he did not know it was a tiger. Only when it was light could he see it. Zhang Yuzhou was filled with fear and lay still, not daring to move. The tiger slowly touched Zhang Yuzhou with its foot. Zhang Yuzhou suspected there was a reason and got up and sat. The tiger lifted its left front paw to show Zhang Yuzhou. Zhang Yuzhou saw a thorn on its paw, about five or six inches long, and removed it. The tiger then jumped out of the hermitage, bowing in a submissive manner, and rubbed its body against Zhang Yuzhou. For a long time, it looked back and left. At midnight, he suddenly heard a large object fall in front of the hermitage. Zhang Yuzhou went out and saw a wild boar, very fat, weighing nearly three hundred pounds. The boar, seeing Zhang Yuzhou, rubbed its body against him again. For a long time, it left. From then on, every night it would bring something, either a boar or a deer. The villagers thought it was a demon and reported it to the county. Zhang Yuzhou explained the whole story. The county sent officials to follow and observe. At two hours past midnight, another deer was brought. The county then released him from blame. Zhang Yuzhou performed a hundred and one Buddhist rituals for the tiger. That night, the tiger brought a piece of silk. One day, the hermitage was suddenly torn down by the tiger. It seemed the tiger did not want Zhang Yuzhou to stay there. Zhang Yuzhou understood and moved to a new place. From then on, the tiger did not come back.

Wang Tai, a hero from Hailing, was walking in the wilderness with about fifteen companions when they suddenly encountered a tiger blocking their path. His companions said, "We are fifteen or sixteen people; we won't all die. Each should take off one piece of clothing to test it." When it came to Wang Tai's turn, the tiger roared and circled around his clothes several times. In Hailing, there are many tigers, and travelers all carry large sticks. Wang Tai picked up a stick, stripped off his clothes, and stood alone, telling the other fourteen, "You should go quickly." Once he was sure they were far away, he approached the tiger with his stick, striking it in the ear, which caused the tiger to fall unconscious. After a short while, the tiger recovered and ran away. Terrified, Wang Tai could not return to the original path and had to navigate through the grass. After walking for about ten miles, he found a divine temple and took refuge under the beams for the night. That night, the moon was bright, and after dark, he heard a tiger moving in the grass. Soon, the tiger appeared in the temple courtyard, jumped, and transformed into a man, wearing beautiful clothes and having a handsome appearance.

Someone in the hall asked, "Why are you so exhausted tonight?" The spirit replied, "I suddenly encountered someone who was unexpectedly strong and brave, and he hit me with a sturdy stick. I became extremely exhausted and nearly died." After speaking, he entered the wooden figure on the seat. Suddenly, he raised his head and saw Tai, who asked, "Who is this guest?" Tai, fearing for his life, fell to the ground and fully recounted the beginning and end of the events. The spirit said, "You were destined to be eaten by me. Only after ten or more days can you die. I took your life early, so I hit you with a stick. Now that we have met, it is right to protect you. In a few days, you should bring a pig, and coat it with your own blood." He pointed to the large tree in the courtyard and said, "Can tie it here. Quickly climb the tree, and you will be saved." Tai followed the instructions. The spirit came out of the hall and transformed into a tiger, jumped powerfully, but was too high to reach. It then bent down to eat the pig, and after eating, it returned to the hall and resumed its human form. Tai climbed down the tree, bowed twice, and then returned. After that, there were no more troubles.

#### Source Text in Original Language ○荆州人 荆州有人山行，忽遇伥鬼。以虎皮冒己，因化为虎。受伥鬼指挥。凡三四年，搏食人畜及诸野兽，不可胜数。身虽虎而心不愿，无如之何。后伥引虎经一寺门过，因遽走入寺库，伏库僧床下。道人惊恐，以白有德者。时有禅师能伏诸横兽，因至虎所，顿锡问：“弟子何所求耶为欲食人为厌兽身？”虎弭耳流涕。禅师手巾系颈，牵还本房，恒以众生食及他味哺之。半年毛落，变人形，具说始事。二年不敢离寺。后暂出门，忽复遇伥，以虎皮冒己，遽走入寺。皮及其腰下，遂复成虎，笃志诵经，岁余方变。自尔不敢出寺门，竟至死。 #### Source Text in Pinyin ○ Jīngzhōu rén Jīngzhōu yǒu rén shān xíng, hū yù cháng guǐ. Yǐ hǔ pí mào jǐ, yīn huà wéi hǔ. Shòu cháng guǐ zhǐ huī. Fán sān sì nián, bō shí rén chù jí zhū yě shòu, bù kě shèng shǔ. Shēn suī hǔ ér xīn bù yuàn, wú rú zhī hé. Hòu cháng yǐn hǔ jīng yī sì mén guò, yīn jù zǒu rù sì kù, fú kù sēng chuáng xià. Dào rén jīng kǒng, yǐ bái yǒu dé zhě. Shí yǒu chán shī néng fú zhū héng shòu, yīn zhì hǔ suǒ, dùn xī wèn: “Dì zǐ hé suǒ qiú yē wèi yù shí rén wéi yàn shòu shēn?” Hǔ mǐ ěr liú tì. Chán shī shǒu jīn xì jǐng, qiān huán běn fáng, héng yǐ zhòng shēng shí jí tā wèi bǔ zhī. Bàn nián máo luò, biàn rén xíng, jù shuō shǐ shì. Èr nián bù gǎn lí sì. Hòu zàn chū mén, hū fù yù cháng, yǐ hǔ pí mào jǐ, jù zǒu rù sì. Pí jí qí yāo xià, suí fù chéng hǔ, dǔ zhì sòng jīng, suì yú fāng biàn. Zì ěr bù gǎn chū sì mén, jìng zhì sǐ. #### Source Text in English People from Jingzhou A person from Jingzhou was hiking in the mountains when he suddenly encountered a伥 ghost. The ghost covered him with a tiger skin, and he transformed into a tiger. He was commanded by the伥 ghost. For three to four years, he preyed on humans, livestock, and various wild animals, too numerous to count. Although his body was a tiger, his heart did not wish to do so, and there was nothing to be done about it. Later, the伥 ghost led the tiger past a temple gate, and the tiger quickly entered the temple treasury, hiding under the bed of a temple monk. The monk was terrified and reported this to a virtuous person. At the time, there was a Zen master who could subdue various ferocious beasts, so he went to where the tiger was, stopped, and asked with a staff, "What do you seek? To eat humans or to be disgusted with your tiger form?" The tiger lowered its ears and shed tears. The Zen master tied a handkerchief around its neck, led it back to its original room, and continuously fed it with food for all beings and other flavors. After half a year, the fur fell off, and he transformed back into human form, telling the entire story. For two years, he dared not leave the temple. Later, he briefly went out, and suddenly met the伥 ghost again. The ghost covered him with a tiger skin, and he quickly entered the temple. The skin reached down to his waist, and thus he transformed back into a tiger. He devoted himself to reciting scriptures, and after more than a year, he transformed back into a human. From then on, he dared not leave the temple gate, until he died. #### Translation in English People from Jingzhou A person from Jingzhou was hiking in the mountains when he suddenly encountered a伥 ghost. Dong Lao In the mountains and valleys of Xinzhou, there was an elderly man named Liu, who held the position of a priest in white robes. A person brought over two hundred geese to Liu for release, and Liu constantly watched and cared for them. After several months, every day the geese were taken by a tiger, resulting in the loss of more than thirty geese. The villagers were troubled by this. They set traps all over the place of release, and since then, the tiger did not return. A few days later, an old man with a large head and long hair suddenly appeared before Liu and asked, "Why are there fewer geese?" Liu replied, "They have been taken by the tiger." The old man then asked, "Why don't you catch the tiger?" Liu answered, "We have already set traps, and the tiger does not return." The old man said, "This is because the tiger is being guided by the ghost of a伥. If you first control the伥, you will be able to catch the tiger." Liu asked, "What method can be used to capture it?" The old man said, "This ghost likes sour things. You can use plum and white plum, as well as bayberry, to set up along important paths. If the伥 eats them, it will not be able to see anything, and the tiger can then be captured." After speaking, the old man disappeared. That night, the villagers followed his advice and set up the plums and bayberries along the important paths. After the fourth drum strike, they heard the tiger fall into the trap. Since then, the problem has been solved.

A woman from Li Prefecture, whose son's wife went to a mountain garden to pick vegetables, was taken by a tiger and lived with it for twelve years before returning. She recounted that she entered a cave in the deep mountains, originally thinking she would be eaten. However, she lived with four tigers for a long time. The tiger that took her was the oldest. The other tigers would bring deer meat back to feed her and sometimes hold water in their mouths and spit it out into hers. Whenever she tried to leave, the tiger would get angry and drive her back into the cave. This went on for six or seven years. After a few more years, the other tigers gradually disappeared, and only the old one remained. The old tiger had a woman of her own and never stayed outside. One day, the old tiger suddenly did not return at night. The woman was puzzled but dared not go out. The next day, she slowly ventured out, walking for several dozen steps before no longer seeing the tiger. She then walked for about five or six miles, hearing the sound of woodcutting in the mountains. She went straight to them. The woodcutters thought she was a ghost and threw gravel and stones at her. The woman loudly explained her situation, and they asked her one by one. She said she was the new wife of a certain family. Some of the woodcutters recognized her as the woman who had been taken by the tiger. They then believed her and took off her clothes, bringing her back. Coincidentally, her husband had died, and her parents-in-law took pity on her and raised her. The woman was simple-minded and lacked energy, and she was often teased by passersby. Liu Quanbai personally saw the woman and heard her story.

A Man from Songyang A man from Songyang entered the mountain to gather firewood and, as evening approached, was chased by two tigers. He quickly climbed a tree. The tree was not very tall, and the two tigers leaped at it repeatedly but could not reach him. Suddenly, they spoke to each other, saying, "If we get Zhu Dushi, we should definitely succeed." They left one tiger to guard and the other went away. Soon another tiger, slender and skilled at seizing, appeared. At that time, the moon was bright, and everything was clearly visible. The smaller tiger frequently seized his clothes, but the man still had his axe at his waist. He waited for the tiger to grab again and then struck it with the axe, cutting off its front paw. The tiger roared loudly, and both tigers fled together. By morning, the man was able to return. When he met villagers, he told them what had happened. The villagers said, "There is Zhu Dushi to the east of the county. Go and see him; could this be him?" Several people went to inquire, and Zhu Dushi replied, "Last night I briefly went out and got injured. Now I am lying down." It was then confirmed that Zhu Dushi had transformed into a tiger. They reported this to the county magistrate, who ordered the officials to bring knives and surround the place, setting it on fire. Zhu Dushi suddenly rose up, transforming into a tiger, and burst out, disappearing without a trace.

### The Tiger Pities Humans A general named Li from Fengxiang Prefecture was taken by a tiger, who sat on top of him. Li repeatedly cried out, "Great King, please spare my life!" The tiger lowered its ears in what seemed to be a gesture of pleasure. After a while, the tiger carried Li for over ten miles and deposited him into a cave. Several young tigers saw the person and jumped with joy. The tiger looked down from the cave entrance for a long time before leaving. Later, the tigers entered the cave and consistently shared their prey with Li. Over ten days, the young tigers grew as large as dogs and played and nursed. The tiger then carried the third young one. Li feared that if the tiger left, he would be abandoned and die in the cave, so he held onto the tiger and said, "Great King, do you not want to take me too?" The tiger lowered its tail, and Li held onto it and managed to escape the cave. Li then said, "May you have already protected me, but will you not take me to my home?" The tiger carried Li to the place where he was taken and then parted ways. Every three days, the tiger would come to Li's house, as if to visit. After twenty days, the tiger came five or six times, causing the villagers to fear. The next time the tiger came, Li said, "Great King, you have been very kind, but the villagers are afraid. Please do not come again." After a month, the tiger came once more, and after that, it never returned.

**范端** Fan Duan, the village headman of Fuling, was diligent and capable in his duties, serving in local government roles. After a long time, he transformed into a tiger. The villagers treated him as if he were a tiger and reported this to the county official, saying, "He constantly leads wild tigers into the village and steals livestock." The county magistrate replied, "This is an accusation made out of ill will. How could there be such a thing?" He then summoned Fan Duan for questioning, and Fan Duan answered as the magistrate had suggested. After some time, a tiger entered the granary at night and stole meat. When dawn came, the tiger was trapped and unable to escape. The villagers formed a circle around the granary, and the tiger injured several people before escaping. The elders spoke up again, and the county magistrate strictly interrogated Fan Duan about the cause. Fan Duan then fully confessed, saying, "I often thought about eating raw meat, but I couldn't get it myself. One night, I actually went to the neighbor's pigpen and stole a pig. I found it delicious, and so I began to want to eat people, but I was only troubled by the lack of company."

Every night, Duan would search in all directions, encountering two tigers that followed him. Whatever he found, they would share it together, and he also did not know that his body was changing.
