Observing his actions, it seemed as if he were drunk. The county magistrate reasoned with him and sent him away. That night, he left and returned after several days, his clothes remaining the same. At home for three or four days, after dusk, wild tigers would come to the outskirts of the village and roar. The villagers were frightened and wanted to kill him. His mother advised him to leave, and Duan cried, bid farewell to his mother, and left. After several days, someone saw three tigers, one of which had a boot on its left hind leg. Duan's mother searched the mountains and valleys everywhere and found him again. The mother wept, the two tigers walked away, and the one with the boot remained alone. She approached it, and the tiger lay down and closed its eyes. She then removed the boot, and it was still a human foot. The mother held it and wept, and after a long time, she left. After that, the villagers often saw him, sometimes calling him "Fan Lize." The two tigers ran away in fright, and one tiger looked back, seeming to be sad. From then on, no one knew where he went.

Shi Jingya, who initially served as a village head but did not like it, turned to Confucianism and became a scholar. He went to a town to buy clothes and reached a stream. On the south bank of the stream, there was a Taoist priest wearing red clothes, with two green-robed attendants standing beside him. The Taoist priest said, "Tomorrow at noon, I will eat the scholar Shi Jingya as food. You can tell him to remove his weapons to prevent any injury." The two attendants replied, "Understood." Shi Jingya saw the Taoist priest, but the priest did not see Shi Jingya. Shi Jingya was shocked by this prophecy and stayed for several nights. Suddenly, soldiers came and asked Shi Jingya, "Are you carrying weapons?" Shi Jingya, remembering the Taoist priest's warning, removed his sword and spearhead and hid them in his chest. The soldiers took the weapons. Shi Jingya hesitated and was repeatedly asked to leave by the innkeeper. Shi Jingya, unable to avoid leaving, hid the spearhead in a bamboo tube and continued his journey. When he reached a crossroads, he saw a tiger blocking the road, ready to attack him. Shi Jingya then stabbed the tiger with his spear, hitting it right in the heart and killing it. The two attendants examined the dead tiger and sang with joy.

○凉州人牛 During the Tianbao era, a household in Liangzhou gave birth to a cow that was strong and large. As it grew, it could not be restrained and thus became wild. Many other cows followed it, and they always formed a herd in the west of the city for dozens of miles, which people could not control. Later, the cows gradually became more aggressive, reaching hundreds in number. The villagers could no longer bear the trouble, and the governor planned how to deal with them. Just then, a fierce beast was offered by a Western barbarian. It looked like a large dog but was a deep blue color. The governor asked the barbarian, "What is the purpose of this offering?" The barbarian replied, "It can fight and kill fierce beasts." The governor told him about the wild cows, saying, "If there is a reward, I will capture them for you." They agreed to a reward of three hundred thousand coins. The barbarian then held the beast, chanted a spell, and spoke to it as if in conversation. The beast then charged forward, kicking up dust that darkened the field, and soon returned. The villagers went to look and found a mound of earth, but they did not know what kind of beast it was. At first, they watched the fight and saw the beast as large as a Shu horse. After the fight, the cow had its neck broken and died. The barbarian cut open the cow's belly, took out its five internal organs, and fed them to the beast in a bowl. The beast gradually ate them all and returned to its original size.

The County Magistrate Wei Yourou of Jian'an County had a servant who managed his horses. This servant was in his early twenties when he died of illness. One of Wei Yourou's guests, who was skilled in reciting spells, suddenly dreamed of the deceased servant, who said, "I am unfortunate to have died, but I still owe you, sir, forty-five thousand. The authority in the underworld has decreed that I must become an animal to repay this debt. I asked to be reborn as a horse with an unusual appearance, and this has already been decided." The following year, a white horse with black eyes was born, resembling the servant in appearance. After several years, the horse was worth over a hundred thousand, and Wei Yourou deeply lamented that the servant's words had not come true. Soon after, Pei Kuan became the Inspector and appointed Wei Yourou as his deputy. When Pei Kuan saw the white horse, he wanted to buy it. He asked for its price, and Wei Yourou only requested thirty thousand, which Pei Kuan accepted. Wei Yourou then said, "This servant still owes me fifteen thousand, and he should come again." A few days later, Pei Kuan told Wei Yourou, "The horse is indeed a fine one, but the price you charged earlier was too low." He then paid another fifteen thousand to Wei Yourou, and the matter was ultimately verified.

○ Yao Jia There was a family from Wu Xing named Yao, who during the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty was exiled to the southern frontier. The man kept two dogs, who would accompany him in the south as well. His household servant, Fu Zi, and his son Xiao Nu were both strong and brave, and they conspired to kill their master, then take the entire family back north. Yao lived in a remote area, with no neighbors nearby. Suddenly, Fu Zi spoke to his master, saying, “Your family originally comes from the north, but now you have fled to the southern wilderness, having wandered ten thousand miles. Something ominous has occurred. I shall arrange for your funeral rites and take you back north. Recently, I have already felt weak and exhausted. I fear that after your sudden death, the rest of the weak and young will not be able to return your bones to your homeland. I humbly request that you consider this.” Yao understood his intent and said, “Are you trying to kill me?” The servant replied, “Exactly as you have been thinking.” Yao asked him to wait until the next morning. When the time came, Fu Zi and his son prepared a meal and urged Yao to eat heartily. They presented a cup of wine with sobs, but Yao’s heart was already in a state of panic and he could not eat at first. Instead, he merely fed the two dogs with food. As the servant entered to take the cup, Yao gently held the two dogs and said, “I have raised you for many years, but now the servants are killing me—do you know this?” From then on, the two dogs refused to eat and looked at their master, wailing in sorrow. Shortly after, Fu Zi arrived, and one of the dogs bit into his throat and killed him. The other dog hurried into the kitchen, bit the throat of the young servant and killed him, then bit the wife of Fu Zi and killed her as well. Thus, Yao was saved from death.

During the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, Cui Huitong, the son-in-law of a high-ranking official from Gaodu, had a slave named Wan Di. Wan Di was extremely violent in nature and cruel, willing to kill. One of the master's female dogs, named Huangnu, went missing for several days. Just as the master was about to send Wan Di on a task, Huangnu suddenly moved back and forth before the master, biting Wan Di. Others tried to stop her but could not. After a long time, she retreated. When called, she disappeared. The master's family found this strange. Wan Di first said, 'A few days ago, I really cooked this dog, but I don't know why it came to this.' Initially, they did not believe him. Wan Di then said, 'I saw where its head was buried, to take as proof.' Thus, it was known that it was the spirit of the wronged dog.

○杨氏 In the residence of the Yang family in Chang'an, there was always a green-clad woman whose origin was unknown. Whenever she entered the main hall, she would directly approach the daughters, saying, "The gods have destined me to be with your daughters." The daughters were all terrified and tried to avoid her, but if she persisted, she would speak in disrespectful terms. Her behavior was extremely vulgar, sometimes walking naked, forcing others to cover their eyes. She would then go outside and flirt with men, engaging in violent and filthy acts, and could never be captured. One day, she took all the daughters' clothes from their bags and exposed them in the courtyard. The daughters could no longer bear their anger and cursed her loudly. She then spoke out in great detail about her shameful deeds, revealing every secret. This continued for more than ten days. The family called upon a sorcerer, who tried to exorcise her with talismans, but she would return immediately after he left, and no one could stop her. They then moved their family away to avoid her. Coincidentally, a relative of the Yang family arrived from afar and heard the story. This person was always brave, so he was sent to stay alone in the house. At night, he lit a lamp and went to bed, and the woman indeed came. He pretended to keep her for the night, secretly getting up and hiding her green shoes. When he could not find her, she left in a disheveled state. He took the shoes and saw that they were sheep hooves. He searched for her with a plan and found her in a temple east of the house, where he saw a green sheep that had been born with no hooves, struggling to walk. He bought and killed the sheep, and the haunting ceased. ○陈正观 Chen Zhengguan of Yenxian was extremely skilled in cutting and preparing lamb heads. During the Tianbao era, someone visited Zhengguan, and Zhengguan provided them with a meal. As he was cutting a lamb head, he first stabbed it with a knife, piercing its brain. Zhengguan then washed his hands briefly. The head made several bleating sounds like a sheep, and Zhengguan suddenly felt a chill in his heart, and he died within a few days.

During the early years of the Kaiyuan era, Cui Rirong served as the governor of Ruzhou. The house he moved into had a history of bad luck, and no one had lived there before him. Upon arriving, Cui Rirong repaired and cleaned the house, living there without doubt. One night, while sitting alone in the hall with a bright candle, several dozen people in black clothes entered through the door and gathered at the foot of his seat. Some of them were lame, and others were blind. Cui Rirong asked, "What kind of ghosts are you all? Why have you come here to frighten people?" The lame one among them explained, "We are all pigs with sins, released by temples and known as long-lived pigs. We have never been happy with this life of filth and impurity, but we cannot die. We want to tell people about our plight, but they are always afraid. Now that you are in charge of the region, we come to entrust the transformation of our bodies to you." Cui Rirong told them, "If that is the case, it is not difficult." All of them bowed and thanked before leaving. The next day, the officials came to see Cui Rirong, and they were amazed that he was unharmed. After the morning meeting, Cui Rirong ordered the judicial officer to give the pigs names. When the pigs arrived, some were lame and others were blind, just as they had seen before. They were amazed for a long time. Cui Rirong then ordered the pigs to be killed and their meat sold, the proceeds used to create Buddhist images, and their bones collected and buried. Later, the ghosts returned to thank him, all appearing as young men. They said, "Without your help, we would still be suffering for ten years. We have no way to repay you, but now we have a pair of valuable swords, worth a thousand gold pieces each, which can ward off evil and eliminate misfortune." They placed the swords on the bed and bowed again before leaving. Cui Rirong asked, "What position will I hold?" They replied, "You will be the governor of Taiyuan in two days." He then asked, "Will I become a chancellor?" They remained silent without answering.

○ Li Ce Li Ce, during the Kaiyuan period, served as the magistrate of a certain county. In his office, a bird three feet tall, without feathers, and flesh-colored and completely red, entered his residence. Li Ce considered this an omen of ill fortune and ordered soldiers to strike it. The soldiers struck with axes, but the blades entered the wood while the bird remained unharmed. Li Ce was greatly troubled by this. He then tried to fry the bird in a pot of oil, covering it with something, and after several days, when he opened it, the bird flew away with the rising steam. Later, the bird returned, and Li Ce ordered a rope to be tied around its neck and it was attached to a large stone and sunk in the river. After a month, the bird returned again, and the broken rope was still around its neck. Li Ce then took a large log, hollowed it out, placed the bird inside, covered both ends with iron, and sank it in the river. From then on, the bird did not appear again. During the Tianbao period, Li Ce was transferred to another position, and the house remained ominous. Within a few days of taking up his new duties, small people, a few inches tall, appeared in the house in numbers of four or five hundred, filling Li Ce's official quarters. Li Ce struck one of them with a stick, and it fell dead. Upon inspection, they were all human beings. The next night, the small people gathered in groups, weeping, and there was a cart carrying a coffin, with people wearing mourning clothes, performing sacrifices, and offering condolences. They proceeded to bury someone at the base of the western steps, and in the morning, they departed. Li Ce then dug up the burial site and found a red, hairless mouse. He then ordered workers to search for holes and dig them out, finding hundreds of mice. The strange occurrences finally ceased. Li Ce's household was also very safe.

During the early years of the Tianbao era, the area around Handan County was often plagued by a nightmare-inducing ghost. This ghost would visit various villages and linger for several days before departing, and it had become a common occurrence. One night, three cavalrymen stopped at a village to rest. An elderly woman told them, "You are welcome to stay, but be wary of the nightmare ghost. When guests arrive, it will surely torment you, so you should take precautions. Although it cannot harm you, it can still cause minor confusion and dizziness." The cavalrymen initially did not fear the ghost and decided to stay. After the second watch of the night, the two cavalrymen who were sleeping soundly on the front bed began to feel the effects. One of them suddenly awoke and saw a creature enter from outside. The creature appeared like a rat, black and furry. It was wearing a green robe and held a tablet about five or six inches long. It bowed to the sleeping cavalrymen and then left. The cavalryman who awoke immediately fell into a nightmare. The ghost then tormented the other two cavalrymen, and finally, the one who had woken up. He reached out and grabbed the ghost's foot, but the ghost remained motionless, yet its body was cold as ice. The three cavalrymen found it easy to hold onto the ghost. By dawn, the villagers gathered and questioned the ghost. At first, the ghost did not speak. The cavalryman angrily said, "You still won't speak! I will fry you in a pot of oil!" He then ordered the villagers to prepare a pot of oil. The ghost then spoke, "I am a thousand-year-old rat. If I can torment three thousand people, I will transform into a weasel. However, I have never actually harmed anyone. If you can release me, I will leave this place a thousand miles away." The cavalryman then released the ghost, and the strange phenomenon ceased. The Grand Censor, who once served as the magistrate of Handan, personally witnessed this event. His younger brother, Chang, recounted it.

○毕杭 In the late Tianbao era, Bi Hang, the Imperial Censor-in-Chief, served as the Prefect of Hui Prefecture. He was captured by the rebels led by An Lushan and soon planned to defect but had not yet acted. A few days later, in the courtyard, he suddenly saw hundreds of tiny people, each about five to six inches tall, playing as if nothing was unusual. The family killed them. The next day, the tiny people appeared again, all dressed in white mourning clothes and crying, carrying the dead on funeral carts and with funeral implements, as if they were scholars preparing for a funeral. They then made a grave in the courtyard, buried the dead, and went into a hole in the south wall. The family was very surprised. They opened the grave and found a dead rat. They made hot water and poured it in, after a while they dug and found hundreds of dead rats. After more than ten days, Bi Hang failed in his plan, and the entire family was killed. ○崔怀嶷 Cui Huaiyi had hundreds of rats in his house. The rats walked on two legs and made "gua-gua" sounds in the courtyard. The family, both young and old, all came out to watch, and the house suddenly collapsed. His grandson, Wang Wen, told me himself. Recently, there was a man who raised a daughter, who was more than ten years old, and suddenly she disappeared. For a year, there was no trace of her. In the family's house, they often heard the sound of a child crying underground. They dug, initially finding a small hole, which gradually became deeper and wider, about ten feet long and wide. They saw the daughter sitting in a pit, holding a child, with a bald rat as big as a wine jar beside her. The daughter saw her family but did not recognize them. The parents then realized she had been bewitched by the rat. They beat the rat and killed it. The daughter then cried, saying, "He is my husband, why did someone kill him!" The family also killed the child. The daughter then cried continuously, and before they could treat her, she died.

In the region of Langzhou, the Muyao people often cut reeds by the riverbank. One day, a large elephant suddenly appeared and rolled the Muyao onto its back. The elephant traveled over a hundred miles and entered a deep swamp. In the swamp, there was a sick elephant lying down and breathing heavily, suffering greatly from pain. When the elephant got down to the ground, the Muyao understood its intention. The elephant had a bamboo splinter in its foot. The Muyao used a waist rope to tie the bamboo splinter and pulled it out, removing about five or six liters of pus and blood. The young elephant used its trunk to pick up mugwort and wanted to plug the wound. The Muyao picked the mugwort and rubbed it thoroughly, plugging the wound until all the mugwort was used up. After a while, the sick elephant could stand up, walking and standing in different directions, and then lying down again. The elephant looked back at the young elephant and pointed at the mountain with its trunk, making a sound like "yo-yo." The young elephant then left. Soon, a tusk arrived. The sick elephant saw the tusk and roared loudly, as if it disliked it. The young elephant took the tusk away. Soon after, it brought another large tusk. The Muyao called the elephant "General," saying it was hungry. The elephant went to break off several branches of chestnuts and ate them, then it was full. It then sent the people and tusks back. The elephant traveled fifty miles, suddenly turned back, and the people initially did not understand its intention. They returned to retrieve the knife it had left behind. After retrieving the knife, they sent them back to the original place. The elephant bumped heads with the people, wagging its ears left and right, and after a while, it left.

A Tooth of Tremendous Size A tooth of extraordinary size was transported to Hongzhou. A foreign merchant sought to buy it and kept raising the price, eventually reaching forty million strings of cash. Later, he went to another shop, where he quickly covered the tooth with a straw mat. Another merchant asked, "What kind of treasure is this, and why are you avoiding it?" The owner removed the mat and said, "It's just one big tooth." The other merchant saw the color of the tooth and was moved, privately telling the owner that he would pay a million strings of cash and offered ten thousand to the owner as an intermediary. They pretended to leave, but soon arrived carrying money. The original merchant contested it, saying, "The one who originally wanted to buy the tooth is me! You, an elder, are interfering in the market, which violates public law. If the owner asks for a thousand or ten thousand strings of cash, do I not have it?" They argued back and forth, and ended up fighting each other. The officials reported to the county, which then reported to the prefecture. The prefect questioned the reason, and the merchant initially refused to admit that the tooth was a treasure. The prefect said, "This tooth will be presented to the emperor. If you don't speak up, it won't matter in the end." Stubbornly refusing, the merchant finally said, "There are two dragons inside the tooth, standing and jumping. They can be cut into strips. In our country, this is valued as a commodity, worth tens of millions. Whoever gets it will become a great merchant." Hongzhou then presented the tooth, its owner, and the two merchants to the court. Empress Wu ordered the tooth to be cut open, and indeed, two dragon-shaped strips were found. She said to the tooth's owner, "You look poor and humble, you cannot accept too much money or goods." She granted an edict to Langzhou, giving him fifty thousand strings of cash each year, to be replenished when used up, until the end of his life.

A Hunter from Annam The people of Annam make a living through hunting, often attaching poison to their arrowheads to ensure that any birds or beasts they shoot are killed instantly. During the Yuan Dynasty, one such hunter ventured into the deep mountains and took a brief nap under a tree. Suddenly, he was startled awake by something touching him. He opened his eyes to see a white elephant, much larger than any other he had ever seen. The people of the South call such elephants "Generals," and the hunter bowed and prayed to it. The elephant used its trunk to lift him onto its back and then took his bow, arrows, and poison container, handing them back to him. Thus, they galloped for over a hundred miles, entering a deep valley and reaching a flat rock. Looking ahead for about ten miles, he saw that both cliffs were covered with large trees, their circumferences like those of giant houses, densely covering the sky. When the elephant reached a platform, it was frightened and, while walking, it kept looking around. After traveling six or seven miles, it went to lean against a big tree and used its trunk to brush the hunter upward. The hunter understood its intention and took the bow and arrows, climbing the tree. The elephant looked up at the tree from below, and the hunter could climb over twenty zhang. When he wanted to stop, the elephant's trunk pointed straight up, as if guiding him to go higher. The hunter climbed directly to sixty zhang, and the elephant looked and then left. The hunter spent the night in the tree. By morning, he saw two bright lights on the flat rock. After a while, he saw a huge beast, over ten zhang tall, with pitch-black fur. Soon it became clear, and the white elephant from yesterday, leading over a hundred other elephants, came along the mountain, kneeling in front of the huge beast. The huge beast devoured two elephants, and after eating, they each went their own way.

A person then conceived an idea, wanting to make it shoot. He applied medicine to the tip of the arrow and shot it with great effort, repeatedly hitting two arrows. The beast looked at the arrow and roared in struggle, its sound shaking the forest. The person also shouted loudly to attract the beast. The beast came looking for the person, who clung to a tree. Happening to find it opening its mouth, he shot it in the mouth. The beast roared and threw itself, and after a long time, it finally died. Suddenly, a large elephant came from the flat rock, taking one step and looking once, arriving at the beast's place. Examining that it was already dead, the elephant touched it with its head, looked up to the sky, and roared loudly. In a short while, several hundred elephants gathered like clouds and roared, their sound reaching dozens of miles. The large elephant came to the tree's place, bent its knee, and bowed twice, then beckoned the person with its trunk. The person descended from the tree and climbed onto its back. The elephant carried the person forward, and the other elephants followed. They found a place where trees were planted like a ridge. The large elephant lifted logs with its trunk, and all the elephants lifted them, finishing by late in the day. The elephant's trunk was like a sieve, and soon after, they found the place where it slept last. The elephant put the person down on the ground, bowed twice, and left. The person returned and reported to the protector. The protector sent an envoy to follow him, obtaining several tens of thousands of tusks, which made tusks in the region beyond the mountains cheap. The envoy arrived at the flat rock place, where only the bones of the large beast remained. The protector took a section of bone, and ten people carried it to. The bone had a hole that allowed people to pass through.

The Son of the Governor of Jizhou During the Tang Dynasty, a son of the Governor of Jizhou, whose name has been forgotten, had his father send him to the capital to request a change of position. On his way, before he had even left the borders, he saw a noble household with a large number of guests and attendants. Among them was a woman of beautiful appearance, and the son was pleased and asked her about herself. The household was greatly surprised, and an old maid angrily said, "Who are you? How dare you act so recklessly! I am the daughter of the Longshu of Youzhou, and my husband has recently passed away, returning to the capital. You are not an official of a state or county, why are you asking such questions?" The son then claimed, "My father is currently serving as the Governor of Jizhou, and I wish to seek a marriage alliance." The household was initially very alarmed but gradually agreed. A few days later, they secretly married, but on the way back, they returned. The governor and his wife deeply missed their son and no longer questioned him, but the new wife answered rationally, and they did not suspect anything. When she came, there were many people and horses, and the entire family was filled with joy. After more than thirty days, one night, the new wife's horse suddenly trampled, and the old maid sent several maids to check, and the door was slammed shut. By morning, the governor's family went to the son's room and found no servants or maids, and in the stable, there were no horses. They were greatly suspicious and reported to the governor. The governor and his wife then went to the front of the room and called out to the son, but he did not respond. They ordered someone to break the window and door open, and a large white wolf rushed out, and the son had almost completely eaten.

○ Villagers of Zhengping County At the end of the Yongtai era during the Tang Dynasty, an elderly villager in Zhengping County, Jizhou, suffered from illness for several months. Afterward, he went without food for more than ten days, and at night he would disappear, leaving no one to know where he went. One evening, a villager who had gone to the fields to gather mulberry leaves was chased by a male wolf, and in panic, he climbed a tree. The tree was not very tall, but the wolf stood and bit his clothes. The villager was in danger, and he struck the wolf with a mulberry axe, hitting it right on the forehead. The wolf suddenly lay down and after a while, it left. The villager only descended from the tree at dawn, then followed the wolf's tracks to the old man's house. He entered the hall and called his son, telling him the whole story. The son noticed the axe marks on his father's forehead and, fearing it might harm others again, strangled it to death, turning it into an old wolf. He then went to the county office to report the matter, and the county officials did not punish him. ○ Another In the same year, in another village in Jizhou, there was a boy about twenty years old. After falling ill, he lost his spirit and transformed into a wolf. He secretly ate many children in the village, and those who lost their children did not know the reason, but they could not find him. The boy often worked for others, and one day, he passed by the house of a child who had been lost. The father of the lost child called out his name, saying, "Come to my house to work, and I will prepare a feast for you." The boy then laughed and said, "Who am I? Should I work for you? A man should not lack variety in his diet!" The father of the lost child found his words bold and questioned him. The boy replied, "Heaven has made me eat people. Yesterday, I ate a small child, about five or six years old, and the meat was very delicious." The father of the lost child saw blood and animal odor in his mouth and then beat him to death, turning him back into a wolf and killing him.

#### The Son of Zheng In recent times, there was a man surnamed Zheng who temporarily resided at the Zhongxuan Temple in Wu. On a leisure day, he climbed to the top of a tower and suddenly saw a beautiful woman. He formed a friendly relationship with her. The woman did not refuse or fear him, and from then on, she often came to his bedroom. Zheng began to hate his original wife and did not live with her, staying at ease for several months while the woman was always present. Later, his original wife invited a virtuous nun to come to the bedroom to recite scriptures, and the woman no longer came. Zheng became very angry, saying, "Why did you call this witch to our home, causing my family to be troubled!" When the nun returned to the temple, the woman came again. The nun came and went several times. Later, Zheng constantly scolded his wife, telling her not to use this nun. The wife knew this would work and kept the nun in the bedroom, reciting scriptures day and night. The woman suddenly said to Zheng, "I wanted to form a friendly relationship with you, but due to the presence of the nun, I was forced to leave. I am only the female fox spirit named Ernang from the tower." She spoke these words and disappeared, and she was never seen again. #### Wei Yuanzhong (Note: The second part of the text is incomplete and does not provide enough context for translation. If more information is available, please provide it for a complete translation.)

Wei Yuanzhong, originally named Zhenzai, was inherently strong, upright, and had the ability to discern matters. When he was not yet prominent, he was poor and had only one servant. While cooking in the kitchen, he went out to fetch water and returned to find an old monkey watching the fire. The servant was surprised and told him about it. Yuanzhong calmly said, "The monkey is pitiful that I have no one to help me, so it is watching the fire for me. That is very good!" Another time, when Yuanzhong called out to his servant, the dog called back for him instead. He said, "This is a filial dog, it can help me with my work." Once, when he was sitting alone, a group of mice stood before him with their paws raised. He said, "The mice are hungry, they are asking me for food." He then gave them food. One night, an owl called at the end of his house. His family wanted to shoot it, but Yuanzhong stopped them, saying, "The owl cannot see anything during the day, so it flies at night. This is a creature nurtured by heaven and earth, we should not force it to go south to Yue or north to Hu. Where else could it go?" After this, no strange occurrences happened anymore. Yuanzhong held high-ranking positions, including Taiguan, Shizhong, Zhongshuling, and Puyue. After the death of Empress Wu Zetian, Emperor Zhongzong was in mourning. An edict was issued appointing Yuanzhong as the acting prime minister, and all officials were to follow his orders for three days. He lived to be over eighty years old before passing away. When Yuanzhong was not yet prominent, he often visited Zhang Jingcang. Zhang treated him very coldly, and when Yuanzhong asked him about his future prospects, Zhang did not answer. Yuanzhong became very angry and said, "I traveled a thousand miles to see you, not just to visit. I expected you to give me some guidance, but you treat me with cold words. How can you not fulfill my earnest expectations! However, my fortune is determined by heaven, what does it have to do with you!" He then waved his sleeves and left. Zhang Jingcang quickly stopped him and said, "Your destiny is in your anger, you will one day become a prime minister." As predicted, Yuanzhong indeed rose to the position of prime minister.

The Son of Wei Xuheng The Minister of Revenue, Wei Xuheng, had a son who often sat alone in the hall during the day. Suddenly, he heard a sound from the eaves and looked up to see a cow-headed demon, just as depicted in the hell images, peering down at him from above. Wei cowered in fear and dared not move. After a moment, the demon climbed the steps and went straight to the bed, standing over him. It did this several times before finally leaving. Wei's son, unable to bear the fear, tried to drive it away, throwing a pillow at it, but missed. He then opened the door and rushed forward to chase it. Wei called out, but the demon only circled around an empty well. As he pressed it more urgently, he threw it into the well. The creature then sat on the well, and Wei looked up to see it transform into a monkey. After a long time, the family arrived, and the monkey disappeared. They saw footprints of someone running and struggling around the well, and then found Wei in the well. They lowered him down with a rope, but he was dazed and unable to speak. He could only speak after three days and died after a month.

Zhang Jian, a native of Chengdu but from Wuxian, was demoted from his position as an official in Luxi during the Yuan period. Unable to secure a new appointment through the official channels, he returned to Shu. While traveling through Bazhi, it grew dark. Urging his horse forward, he suddenly saw a man emerge from a mountain path on the left side of the road. The man bowed and asked, "My lord has heard that you have nowhere to stay tonight and wishes to invite you. Please follow me." Zhang Jian asked, "Who is your lord? Is he not the prefect who has invited me?" The man replied, "No, he is the Marquis of Bazhi." Zhang Jian followed him, and after walking about a hundred steps along the mountain path, they saw a high red gate, many people, and armored soldiers, indicating a high level of security and status. After another several dozen steps, they arrived at the place. A messenger stopped Zhang Jian at the gate and said, "Please wait here while I inform my lord. You should wait." After a while, the messenger came out and led Zhang Jian inside. Zhang Jian entered and saw a man standing on the dais, wearing a coarse leather cloak, with an extremely unusual appearance. He was surrounded by attendants on both sides, dressed in silk and brocade, adorned with jade and pearls. Zhang Jian hurried forward and bowed. After bowing, the man bowed back and gestured for Zhang Jian to ascend the stairs. He said, "I am the Marquis of Bazhi, and I have lived here for several decades. I just learned that you have nowhere to stay tonight, so I have invited you. Please stay a little while to enjoy ourselves." Zhang Jian bowed again to express his gratitude.

Afterward, he commanded to open the feast and set up wine. The items used were all luxurious and precious. He then ordered his attendants to invite the Six Heroes, the White-Browed Marquis, and the River Lord, and also invited the Five Leopards General, the Great Lake Marquis, and the Black Hill Colonel. He issued an order saying, "Today we have honored guests, and I wish to enjoy a full feast, so I command them to be invited." The messenger replied "Yes" and left, and they arrived after some time. In front were six men all in black clothes, with an awe-inspiring appearance, known as the Six Heroes. The Marquis of Bashu stood up to bow, and the Six Heroes also bowed. Another man in a brocade robe and white cap, with a fearsome appearance, was the White-Browed Marquis. The Marquis of Bashu bowed again, and the White-Browed Marquis also bowed. Another man in blue, with a robust and imposing figure, was the River Lord. The Marquis of Bashu bowed once more, and the River Lord also bowed. Another man in spotted clothing, similar to the White-Browed Marquis but slightly smaller, was the Five Leopards General. The Marquis of Bashu bowed again, and the Five Leopards General also bowed. Another man in brown, with a three-cornered head, was the Great Lake Marquis. The Marquis of Bashu greeted him. Another man in black, resembling the River Lord, was the Black Hill Colonel. The Marquis of Bashu also greeted him. Then they were invited to sit. The Marquis of Bashu sat facing south, while the Five Leopards General faced north. The Six Heroes, the White-Browed Marquis, and the River Lord sat on the east side, and the Five Leopards General, the Great Lake Marquis, and the Black Hill Colonel sat on the west side. After they sat, wine was served, and music was commanded. Dozens of beautiful women sang and danced, and the music of strings and flutes played, reaching the utmost in their excellence.

The White Tiger Lord, after drinking heavily, turned to Zhan and said, "I will have a meal tonight. Can you provide me with a full meal?" Zhan replied, "I do not know what you are about to eat. I hope you will instruct me." The White Tiger Lord said, "Your body can satisfy my hunger. Why value other flavors?" Zhan, fearing the crude comment, retreated in fear. The Lord of Bashu said, "There is no such logic. How can there be disrespect to a noble guest at a banquet?" The White Tiger Lord laughed and said, "My words were just a joke. How could it be like that? It certainly isn't." After some time, someone reported that the Scholar of the Mystic Void was at the door, wishing to report something. Upon hearing this, the Lord of Bashu invited him to sit and asked, "Why have you come?" The scholar replied, "I am a skilled diviner. I know that you will have great worries. Therefore, I have come to inform you." The Lord of Bashu asked, "What is your concern?" The scholar said, "Someone at the banquet will plot against you. If you do not eliminate them now, they will surely cause harm in the future. I hope you will consider my words carefully." The Lord of Bashu said, "Our banquet is just beginning. Where is there anything strange?" The Lord of Bashu then ordered the scholar to be killed. The scholar said, "If you follow my advice, all will be safe."

If you do not heed my words, then both I and you will die. What should we do? Even if there is regret, can it be undone? The nobleman of Bashan then killed the diviner and placed him on the hall. It was nearly midnight, and everyone was drunk and lying on their beds; Cshan was also dozing lightly. As dawn approached, he suddenly woke up with a start. He saw himself lying in a large stone niche, where embroidered curtains were set up, and valuable items like pearls, jade, and ivory were arranged around. There was a large ape, resembling a human, lying drunk on the ground, which was presumably the nobleman of Bashan. He also saw a huge bear lying in front, which was presumably the General of the Six Heroes. Another white-topped tiger was lying in front as well, known as the White-Fronted Lord. There was also a wolf, known as the Lord of Canglang, and a leopard, known as the General of the Five Leopards. A giant deer and a fox were also lying in front, which were presumably the Lord of Juling and the Colonel of Xuanqiu, respectively. All of them appeared to be in a deep sleep, as if intoxicated. There was also a turtle with an unusual appearance, lying dead in front of the niche, which was the diviner Mr. Dongxuan whom they had killed. Cshan, upon seeing this, was greatly shocked and immediately fled through the mountain path, galloping back to inform the villagers. The villagers gathered in the hundreds and armed themselves with bows and arrows, entering the mountain to reach the place. Afterward, the ape suddenly woke up and said, "Not listening to the words of Mr. Dongxuan, today indeed has come to this." They then surrounded the niche and killed all the animals inside. The items displayed were all valuable and beautiful, and they reported the matter to the prefect. Prior to this, people who first held pearls and silk, who passed by this place, all disappeared without a trace. Since then, the problem has been eliminated.

During the reign of Emperor Taizong, he bestowed a beautiful woman upon Prince Changsun Wuji, a high-ranking official, as a token of his special favor. Suddenly, she encountered a fox spirit, which called itself Wang Ba, a fox spirit eight feet tall, always present with the woman. Whenever the woman saw Prince Changsun, she would hold a long knife and slash. Emperor Taizong heard of this and summoned various exorcists several times, but they could not drive the fox away. Later, an exorcist said: "The military officer Cui from Xiangzhou can cure this disease." When Cui was about to arrive in the capital, the fox fled. When Cui arrived, an imperial edict was issued to go to Prince Changsun's residence. At that time, Emperor Taizong also visited the residence. Cui set up a table and wrote a spell, with Emperor Taizong and Prince Changsun standing behind him. Soon, dozens of spirits representing the twelve earthly branches and other household spirits, some tall and some short, with strange appearances, all appeared in the courtyard. Cui rebuked them: "You are the spirits of the noble family, with important duties. Why did you allow the fox to enter the house?" The spirits replied: "It is a celestial fox, beyond our power to control, not because we were bribed." Cui ordered them to capture the fox.

After a short while, they returned, each wearing swords and arrows, and said, "We have just fought fiercely and been wounded, but ultimately could not capture it." After speaking, they dispersed. Cui then wrote and flew a talisman, and the sky and earth suddenly turned dark. The emperor and Wuji, fearing this, entered the room. Soon, they heard the sound of troops in the void. In a moment, they saw five people, each several zhang tall, coming to Cui's place. They lined up to show respect, and Cui then descended the steps and slightly bent his knee. Soon, he called the emperor and Wuji out to bow in the courtyard, while the gods stood and watched. Cui said, "The general's house has a seductive fox spirit. Dare I trouble you to fetch it?" The gods respectfully agreed and then each dispersed. The emperor asked what gods they were, and Cui replied, "They are the gods of the Five Great Mountains." Again, they heard the sound of troops, and a fox was seen bound and fell to the ground. Wuji, unable to contain his anger, then struck the fox with a long sword. The fox initially did not show fear, and Cui said, "This one has already communicated with the divine; striking it would be futile, it would only bring trouble." Then he declared, "To act wickedly and privately, punished by the divine, decided to punish with five blows." The fox then begged for its life, and Cui took a peach branch and struck it, causing blood to flow all over the ground. Wuji did not find it satisfying, but regretted the few blows. Cui said, "Five blows are equivalent to five hundred in the human world, which is no small punishment. Since these creatures are used by the Heavenly Court, killing them is not permissible, but they are ordered to never return to the general's house." The fox then flew away, and the beauty's illness was then cured.

○ Monk Fuli During the Yonghui period of the Tang dynasty, a man from Taiyuan claimed to be the Buddha Maitreya. Those who paid him homage saw his form extend to the heavens, but after a while it gradually shrank until it was only five or six feet tall, his body appearing like a red lotus flower among leaves. He said to the people, “Do you know that the Buddha has three bodies? The largest is the *dharma body*.” The entire city paid him reverence. Monk Fuli, well-versed in Buddhist doctrine, sighed and said, “After the Age of the True Dharma comes the Age of the Image Dharma; beyond the Image Dharma, there is still the Age of the Final Dharma. In the Final Dharma, the Dharma itself ceases to exist. The Age of the Image Dharma has already lasted for several thousand years. Only when the teachings of Shakyamuni have come to an end will the great disaster begin. After the great disaster, Maitreya will leave Tushita Heaven and descend to Jambudvipa. Now, the teachings of Shakyamuni have not yet been lost—so why has Maitreya descended so soon?” Because of this, he performed worship with sincerity, as if in the presence of Maitreya. Suddenly, he saw that beneath his feet was an old fox; the banners, flowers, and parasols were all merely paper money from tombs. Fuli struck his palm in dismay and said, “Is this how Maitreya appears?” He described the situation exactly as it was, then ran away, and the fox’s feet could not catch him.

Shangguan Yi During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang in the period of Linde, Shangguan Yi served as the governor of Jizhou. He had a son who was around twenty years old, who often stood alone outside the gate at dawn. One day, a girl who was about thirteen or fourteen years old, with extraordinary beauty, passed by the door. The son was pleased with her and began to flirt with her, seeking intimacy. He asked her where she lived and intended to visit her. The girl said, "My family background is difficult, and you are the son of a state official. We both do not want others to know about us. But if you have the heart, find a way to come to me." The son invited her, expecting to meet every day. Initially, she firmly refused, but the son intended to keep her, and gradually, she agreed. After dusk, he waited for her, and as expected, she arrived. From then on, she came every night. After several days, an old servant girl peered through the window and realized it was a demon. She told Shangguan Yi, who tried all sorts of methods to stop her, but could not control her. The demon came more frequently and did not leave day or night.

Whenever the child was about to eat, the demon would snatch away the bowls and cups. This demon was already full, so the child could not eat. Wing often prepared food himself, cutting it to give to the child, but by the time it reached the child, the demon had already taken it away. Wing, being quite intelligent, devised a plan to secretly prepare a poisonous substance. In late autumn, when the oil and sesame seeds were freshly harvested, Wing ordered two batches to be cooked, placing poison in one of them. He first gave the good batch to everyone in the family, and finally to the child, but the demon took it away again. Then he gave the batch with the poison to the child, and the demon also took it away. After giving several portions, the demon suddenly transformed into an old fox, writhing and collapsing. It was then captured. Immediately, Wing ordered it to be burned, and the whole family celebrated. On this day after dusk, they heard the cries of several people from afar. Soon they drew near, and then entered behind the hall. All of them cried out in injustice, wailing bitterly. Among them was an old man, whose cries often said, "If you grieve for the old fox, why then did you kill it unjustly?" For several days, they came to the house morning and evening, and often they saw people dressed in mourning attire. Wing was deeply troubled by it. Later they became less frequent, and after a long time, they finally stopped. There was also no harm.

Great Ancestor Monk During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, there was a woman who claimed to be the Holy Bodhisattva, stating that wherever a person's heart was, she could know it. The Empress summoned her into the palace, and everything she said came true, so the palace respectfully attended to her. After several months, she was regarded as a true Bodhisattva. Later, Great Ancestor Monk entered the palace, and the Empress asked, "Have you seen the Holy Bodhisattva?" The monk replied, "The Bodhisattva is where? I wish to see her." The Empress ordered that they be allowed to meet. The monk had a majestic and distant aura, and after a long time, Great Ancestor said, "You are good at observing the mind; try to observe where my mind is." She answered, "Your mind is in the bell at the top of the stupa's wheel." He asked her again, and she said, "In the Tushita Heaven, listening to teachings in Maitreya's palace." On the third question, she said, "In the Neither Perception nor Non-perception Heaven." All of her answers were as he said, and the Empress was delighted. Great Ancestor then placed his mind in the realm of the Four Fruits of Arhatship, and she could no longer know it. The monk rebuked her, saying, "My mind has just been placed in the realm of Arhatship, and you already do not know. If I place it in the realms of Bodhisattvas and Buddhas, how could you possibly know!" The woman was at a loss for words, transformed into a female fox, descended the steps, and ran away, not knowing where to go.

Yang Baocheng, in the early years of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, served as a high-ranking official in the capital region. One day, a man named Wu Nanhe came to his door and announced himself as such. Yang Baocheng met with him. Wu Nanhe was in his thirties, tall at seven feet, and had a very handsome appearance. He led Wu Nanhe to sit down. Wu Nanhe was unmatched in eloquence and argument, and Yang Baocheng could not keep up with the conversation. After a while, Wu Nanhe asked to have the attendants leave so they could have a secret conversation. He said, "I heard that your daughter is named Lingshu, and I wish to serve under your family." Yang Baocheng was very surprised and said, "A woman should marry through a matchmaker, and we only met by chance. How can you be so forward?" Wu Nanhe became very angry and called Yang Baocheng an old slave: "I want your daughter, and how dare you refuse me!" He used many disrespectful words. Yang Baocheng did not know what to do. Wu Nanhe took off his clothes and went inside, until he reached the daughter's room, where he sat in a partition made of paper. After a while, he came out with the daughter. The daughter said, "Now I am marrying Wu Nanhe. Why are you scolding me?" Yang Baocheng realized that Wu Nanhe was a fox spirit. He ordered over ten family members to attack him, but they were instead dealt with, many having mud smeared on their ears. Yang Baocheng asked for leave for over twenty days. The emperor asked, "Why don't I see Yang Baocheng?" Everyone said his family was troubled by a fox spirit. The emperor ordered over ten Taoist priests to come to his house, but all had mud smeared on their ears and were bound, and none could be subdued. Yang Baocheng felt ashamed. When he was granted leave, he moved the whole family to the countryside and built a house for Wu Nanhe on the estate. Family members secretly cursed, but they were all dealt with, so no one dared to speak.

During his leisure time, with nothing to do, Bercheng went to the fields to watch people harvesting wheat and rested under a tree. Suddenly, a Taoist appeared, looking very thin and emaciated, and came to Bercheng's place to ask for water. Bercheng prepared food for him. After eating, the Taoist asked, "Why are you sad?" Bercheng was afraid of Nanhe and whispered the matter to him. The Taoist laughed and said, "I am a heavenly immortal, and I am here on the emperor's orders to capture these four or five individuals." He then asked for paper and ink. Yang Bercheng sent a little servant to fetch them, but he still feared that they might know something, so he warned the servant not to make noise. When the paper and ink arrived, the Taoist wrote three characters, which looked like ancient seal script, and told the little servant to take it to Nanhe and say, "Your master has summoned you." The servant took the note into the room and saw Nanhe playing with a servant girl. Nanhe saw the note and crawled forward, reaching the tree. The Taoist shouted, "You old fox dare to take human form!" And he transformed into a fox, suffering from severe scabies. The Taoist said, "These are under the command of Heaven, so I cannot kill them, but because of you, I cannot let them go unpunished." He hit him with a small stick a hundred times, and blood flowed all over the ground. Bercheng offered him precious gifts, but the Taoist refused. He drove the fox forward, and Bercheng followed behind. They walked for more than a hundred steps, reaching the edge of the willow forest, where the fox slowly ascended into the sky and eventually disappeared. Bercheng was very happy, and the whole family celebrated. His daughter woke up after a short nap and exclaimed, "I was originally in the city, how did I end up here?" Everyone realized that she had been bewitched by the fox, and her spirit was still in a state of sleep.

In the early Tang Dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (713-741), there was a man named Liu Jia from Pengcheng who was about to take office in a county in the Hebei region. On his way, he stayed overnight at a mountain inn. During the night, people saw that Liu Jia's wife was very beautiful. Someone said, "There are spirits here that love to steal beautiful women. Many people who have come here before have been taken by these spirits. You should be cautious." Liu Jia and his family encouraged each other to stay awake and surrounded his wife, still applying flour to her body and head. After five o'clock, Liu Jia was happy and said, "What the spirits do happens at night. Now it is almost dawn, what can they do to us?" He then took a brief nap. Soon after, his wife disappeared. Liu Jia hired villagers with money and asked them to search for her, following the trail of flour. Initially, the fox spirit came out through the window hole and gradually went over the eastern wall. There was an old tomb with a large mulberry tree on top, and a small hole underneath. The face of the wife entered inside. They dug up the area. After digging about a zhang (about 3 meters), they found a large tree hole like a house. Inside, there was an old fox sitting on a jade table, with ten or so beautiful women standing in two rows, holding musical instruments. These women were the ones the fox spirit had previously stolen from other households. There were also hundreds of small foxes around. They killed all of them.

### 李参军 唐兖州李参军被任命并前往上任，途中在新郑的一家客栈遇到了一位正在阅读《汉书》的老人。李参军与老人交谈起来，话题逐渐涉及到了婚姻。老人询问李参军是否已经结婚，李参军回答说还没有。老人说：“您出身名门，应当选择一个好人家的女儿作为配偶。现在听说陶贞益担任此州的都督，如果他强迫自己的女儿嫁给您，您又该如何拒绝呢？陶家和李家结亲，这在人们听来会非常惊讶，我虽然卑微，但私下里为您的婚姻感到羞愧。这里离此地只有几里路，有一位萧公，他是吏部璿的族人，家世也很显赫。他有几个女儿，容貌美丽非凡。”李参军听后非常喜欢，于是请求老人向萧家介绍自己，老人答应了。不久，老人回来，说萧公非常高兴，以礼待客。李参军与仆人一同前往。到达后，萧家的府邸整洁肃穆，房屋宏伟壮观，高大的槐树和修长的竹子绵延不断，构成了一幅绝世美景。起初，两位黄门官手持金器，邀请李参军入座。稍后，萧公出来，穿着紫色的蜀地长袍，手持鸠杖，两侧有两位随从扶持，须发飘逸，目光炯炯，一举一动都显得庄重而优雅。

Li Wang showed his respect and thanked repeatedly. The old man said, "An old man like me who has retired from public life for a long time, who would have thought that a gentleman would take such a roundabout route to visit." He invited Li into the main hall. The fine clothing and ornaments were dimly lit, rarely seen in the world. Soon, he served a sumptuous meal with dishes from both sea and land, many of which were unnamed. After the meal, a drinking party was held, and the old man then said, "Li, the military officer, had previously intended to discuss marriage, and I have already received permission." The old man then narrated several sentences, deeply imbued with the manners of a scholar-official. He wrote a letter to the county official, requesting a diviner to choose a date. Shortly after, the diviner arrived and said, "The divination is favorable, exactly tonight." The old man wrote another letter to the county official, borrowing head flowers, hairpins, silk, and laborers, and soon they all arrived. That night, a county official also came to serve as a matchmaker. The joyous events were no different from those in the world. Upon entering the blue tent, the woman was exceptionally beautiful, and Li was even more delighted. Until morning, the old man said, "Li, you have a scheduled appointment, so you cannot stay long." He immediately sent a woman to accompany him, with five ornate carts, thirty servants and horses, and other fine clothing and ornaments too numerous to count. Those who saw them thought they were like princesses or royal consorts, all envied them greatly.

After two years, Li Zhi Ren was sent on a mission to Luoyang and left his wife behind in the house. The maidservants were all enchanting and seductive, bewitching the men who came and went, often passing by. On another day, the military officer Wang Yong led a dog out to hunt. The maidservants of Li's household were greatly frightened by the dog and ran inside the house. Wang had long suspected their enchanting ways, and that day his heart was moved. He directly led the dog into the house. The whole family blocked the main door, not daring to breathe. The dog also restrained itself and barked. The wife of Li shouted loudly from the door, saying, "The maidservants were just bitten by the dog, and they are still afraid. Why did Wang Yong lead the dog into the house of his colleague, not considering it the place of Li, the military officer?" Wang thought it was a fox and decided to break the window to release the dog, which then bit and killed the group of foxes. Only the wife's body remained human, but her tail did not change. Wang went to inform Zhen Yi, who went to verify the evidence. Seeing the dead foxes, he sighed and lamented for a long time. At that time, it was cold, so they buried them in one place. After several days, the Magistrate Xiao arrived. Upon entering the door, he cried out, and everyone was greatly startled. After several days, he came to Tao to report, his words were indeed true, and his appearance and attire were noble. Tao treated him with great respect and therefore arrested Wang Yong and put him in prison.

King firmly believed it was a fox and took a previous dog and made it bite Xiao. At the time, Xiao and Tao were eating together, and when the dog arrived, Xiao led the dog's head to his knee, stroked it with his hand, and then gave it food. The dog had no intention to attack or bite. Several days later, Li returned, cried for several days, suddenly went mad, and bit King, causing him to swell all over. Xiao said to Li, "The servants all said that the dead were all wild foxes, how painful! I wanted to open the grave immediately, fearing that Li would be deceived and not believe me. Now we should open and inspect it to expose deceit and falsehood." He ordered to open and inspect it, and they found that all the dead were human forms. Li became even more sad and wept. Zhen increased the severity of You's punishment, imprisoning and interrogating him. You privately said, "I had already ordered to hold ten thousand, to take a dog that bites foxes from the Eastern Capital, which takes about ten to twenty days." Zhen further added a hundred thousand in public funds. The dog arrived, and the person who brought the dog paid respects to Xiao and discussed the matter. Tao stood waiting in the main hall. Xiao entered the office, looking gloomy and acting in a state of distress, different from usual. Soon, the dog came in from outside. Xiao transformed into an old fox, walked down the steps a few steps, and was bitten to death by the dog. Zhen ordered to examine the dead, and they were all wild foxes. Thus, You escaped this difficulty.

A County Magistrate of Qianyang During the Tang dynasty, there was a county magistrate of Qianyang whose name is unknown. While in office, he suddenly said, "I wish to become a monk." He recited scriptures with sincerity and earnestness. After a month, a colorful cloud appeared over his house, and he saw a Bodhisattva seated on a lion, calling out to him to marvel, saying, "Your resolve is great, you will attain a high fruit, you should be firm and protect yourself, do not let yourself be defeated." Then the Bodhisattva flew away. The magistrate then entered a state of meditation, closed the door, and did not eat for six or seven days. His family was worried and fearful, fearing that his persistence would harm his life. Coincidentally, a Taoist priest named Gongyuan from Sichuan was on his way to the capital, passing through Long County. The magistrate's son asked him about the reason. Gongyuan smiled and said, "This is a heavenly fox, it is also easy to deal with." He then wrote several talismans and said that the magistrate would be cured. The son threw the talisman into the well, and the magistrate opened the door, seeing his father in a state of starvation. He forced his father to swallow the talisman. Suddenly, the magistrate became clear and awake, and no longer talked about practicing Buddhism. Several years later, after resigning from office and returning home, the magistrate's family usually lived in the suburbs, with flat land stretching for a thousand miles. On a leisure day, the magistrate leaned on a cane and went out, seeing a noble person coming from the south in the mulberry grove from afar, with more than ten horsemen before and behind, looking like a king. The magistrate went inside to avoid him. The horsemen soon arrived at the door, announcing, "Liu Cheng comes to visit the magistrate." The magistrate was greatly surprised and said, "I never met him, why did he come to see me?" When they met, Liu Cheng ascended the hall and sat, saying to the magistrate, "I am granted marriage by grace, how dare I not obey the command!" Initially, the magistrate had a daughter who was ten years old, and by now she was sixteen. The magistrate said, "I never met him, how could there be a marriage?" Liu Cheng said, "If you do not agree to my marriage, the matter is also easy to resolve."

The fox stood with its right mouth wide open, causing the house to shake momentarily, the wells and latrines to mix, and everything to be tossed about. The official had no choice but to agree. The wedding was set for the next day, and they sent gifts to consummate the union. After the wedding, the fox often stayed at the official's house, receiving very generous gifts and abundant resources, which the family did not resent. One day, the official's son traveled to the capital to meet Master Gongyuan. Gongyuan said, "This fox used to be incapable, but now it has mastered talismans, which I cannot match. What shall we do?" The official's son earnestly requested a meeting. Gongyuan then proposed to go and soon arrived at the fox's residence, setting up an altar outside the official's house, about ten steps away. The fox walked with a staff to the altar, cursing, "Old Taoist!" and saying, "Why do you come and go without any fear or restraint!" Gongyuan completed his ritual and requested a battle. The fox sat at the official's door, while Gongyuan sat on the altar. Gongyuan struck the fox with an object, causing it to fall to the ground. After a long time, the fox got up and also struck Gongyuan with an object, causing him to fall as well. They fought back and forth dozens of times. Gongyuan suddenly told his disciple, "If he strikes me dead, you should mourn greatly, and I will use divine methods to bind him." When he struck, Gongyuan fell to the ground, and his disciple wailed loudly. The fox was pleased and did not prepare for the attack. Gongyuan then sent a spirit to strike the fox, who was greatly frightened, admitted his exhaustion, and transformed into an old fox. After Gongyuan got up, he struck the fox with a seating mat, wrapped it in a large bag, and rode a relay horse back to the capital. Emperor Xuanzong saw the fox and found it amusing. Gongyuan reported, "This is a celestial fox, and it cannot be killed. It should be exiled to the eastern frontier." He wrote a talisman and exiled the fox to Silla. The fox held the talisman and flew away. Today, there is a deity named Liu Cheng in Silla, whom scholars revere and serve.

Li Yuangong, a high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty, had an exceptionally beautiful granddaughter named Cui. At the age of fifteen or sixteen, she suddenly fell ill with a supernatural ailment. After some time, a fox appeared in the form of a young man and introduced himself as Hu Lang. Despite repeated attempts by sorcerers to exorcise the spirit, it could not be removed. Li Yuangong's son, who was well-educated and intelligent, often asked the fox, "Does Hu Lang also study?" The fox then engaged in discussions on various topics, answering all questions without reservation. Over time, the boy became quite fond and amused by the fox's knowledge and company. After a while, the fox told Cui, "One cannot live without learning." He then brought an elderly man to teach Cui about the classics and histories. After three years, Cui became fairly well-versed in the main principles of various schools of thought. The fox then brought another person to teach her writing, and after a year, she became known for her skilled writing. The fox then said, "Why wouldn't a woman learn music and play instruments like the konghou and pipa? These are merely ordinary forms of music; it would be better to learn the qin." He then brought another person, who claimed to be skilled at playing the qin. This person said his name was Hu and that he was a scholar during the Sui dynasty from Yangzhai County.

He taught all the tunes, fully mastering their subtleties, along with many other famous pieces, too numerous to recount. He said, "I am also skilled in *Guangling San*, which I have often seen played by Ji Zhongzhan (Ji Kang), but he does not permit it to be taught to others." He was especially skilled in *Wuyeti* and passed on its subtleties. The lady asked, "Why hasn't Hu Lang brought his wife home?" The fox was very happy and bowed in gratitude, saying, "I have also long harbored this thought, but I was hesitant because I considered myself insignificant." That day, he visited everyone in the family, and the joy was complete. The lady asked, "Where is Hu Lang's house if he wants to bring his wife home?" The fox replied, "There are two large bamboo poles in front of my house." At the time, the lady's family had a bamboo garden, and she followed the fox to find the two large bamboo poles with a small hole between them, suspecting it to be the fox's lair. She poured water into the hole. First, she caught several dozen foxes and other animals, and finally, an old fox wearing a green robe emerged from the hole. It was the robe he usually wore. The family was delighted and said, "Hu Lang has appeared." They killed the old fox, and the strange occurrences ceased.

During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a Daoist practitioner named Jiao who practiced Daoism and gathered many disciples. A woman wearing yellow skirts introduced herself as Ah Hu and came to learn Daoist techniques from Jiao. After three years, she had mastered all of Jiao's techniques. She firmly declined to stay, and Jiao tried hard to keep her. Ah Hu said, "I am a wild fox, and I originally learned the art. Now that I have no more techniques to learn, it is not right for me to stay." Jiao then attempted to use his techniques to detain her, but Ah Hu responded to everything appropriately, and Jiao could not match her. Jiao set up an altar on the summit of Mount Song and invoked Laozi, stating, "Although I am not talented, I am a disciple of Daoism. The evil fox has humiliated me, and I fear that the true path will be destroyed." His words were sincere and earnest. Suddenly, smoke emerged from the four corners of the altar, soon forming a purple cloud that rose tens of feet high. In the cloud, Laozi appeared standing. Jiao then worshiped and stated to the cloud, "The true method has been learned by the evil fox; we should seek another method to subdue her." Laozi then performed a ritual in the cloud, and a divine general in the cloud cut the fox's waist with a sword. Jiao was greatly joyful. Laozi suddenly descended from the cloud and transformed into a woman wearing yellow skirts, then departed.

○Li Shi During the Tang Dynasty, during the Kaiyuan period, there was a Li girl who lost her father at a young age and returned to live with her maternal uncle. At the age of twelve, a fox spirit wanted to befriend her. Although the fox spirit did not appear in human form, it engaged in extensive conversation with her. After several months, the younger fox spirit returned, and its voice was slightly different. The family laughed and said, "This is another wild fox." The fox spirit also laughed and said, "How do you know that the previous one was the older brother (Shi Shu Xiong), and I am the younger one? Recently, I wanted to win over a girl from the Wei family and make her a red silk half-sleeve robe, but my older brother unjustly stole it, preventing me from achieving my goal. I have always wanted to avenge myself. Now, I have come here." The Li girl apologized and asked for a way to resolve the situation. The fox spirit said, "Tomorrow is the day when the older brother will be enfeebled. He will definitely come here. You can ward off his influence by having the girl pinch the first joint of her ring finger." After saying this, the fox spirit left. The older fox spirit arrived, and the girl was eating at the time. She followed the younger fox spirit's instructions and pinched the first joint of her ring finger. The older fox spirit threw several pills, about the size of a Bodhi seed, into the girl's bowl of food. The fox spirit missed repeatedly, exclaiming in surprise, "Only by entering Mount Song to study Daoism can I achieve this." An elderly woman holding the pills was afraid and threw them away. When asked why, she said, "The wild fox flattered me."

The fox spoke rudely: "What sort of old woman, would anyone use such people!" After the fox left, a young fox came and said, "How did things turn out? Was what I said true?" The family all apologized. The fox replied, "In about ten days, my brother will come again. Be cautious. This person has already communicated with the heavenly bureaucracy. The magic and protective charms are of no use; only I can control him. When he is about to arrive, I will come again." On the day in question, the young fox came again. It gave a bundle of medicine resembling pine flowers to the woman and said, "My brother will definitely come tomorrow. In the morning, you can carry the woman in a carriage, go northeast, and if there are armored cavalry chasing, you should place the medicine behind the carriage, and you will be safe." The Li family followed the fox's instructions the next morning. After traveling for several miles, a large number of armored cavalry were chasing. Just as they were about to catch up, they placed the medicine behind the carriage, and the chasers saw the medicine, stopped, and dared not proceed. That evening, the young fox came again and laughed, saying, "Did you benefit from my help? There is another method that will ensure permanent protection. I will not come again." The Li family bowed repeatedly and asked earnestly, and the fox instructed, "Take a branch from the east, write the names of people from Qizhou County on a board with red ink, and use the talisman to secure the main gate and the middle gate. This will ensure that there will be no more strange occurrences." The fox did not come again. The woman was still young and had not yet married. After several years, she was eventually lost.

A Story of Magistrate Wei During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, a man came to visit Magistrate Wei, introducing himself as Cui, a military officer, and requesting to marry into his family. Wei was shocked and realized that the man was a fox spirit, but he still treated him with courtesy and sent him away. Soon after, the fox spirit appeared in the inner chamber, introducing himself as the son-in-law, causing Wei's daughter to weep and speak incoherently. Wei repeatedly invited magicians to deal with the spirit, but the fox became increasingly disrespectful and could not be driven away. Hearing that there was a Taoist priest on Mount Emei who could exorcise evil spirits, Wei requested a transfer to be the magistrate of Shu, hoping to use the priest's skills to ward off the spirit. Once they arrived, the priest set up an altar to exorcise the spirit. After a short time, the fox spirit appeared at the altar, seized the priest, and hung him from a large tree, binding him. Wei came to the courtyard and asked, “Why is the revered master here?” The fox replied, “I dared to perform a forbidden ritual and just happened to bind him.” From then on, Wei willingly served the fox's daughter, no longer harboring any hope. His family said to him, “If you become the son-in-law, you can give 2,000 strings of coins as a dowry.” Cui ordered Wei to spread out a mat under the eaves of the hall and string together coins. The coins were strung from the eaves up and down, with a group of maids stringing them, exactly making 2,000 strings. After a long time, the marriage was finally agreed upon, and Wei was ordered to take leave to send gifts and gather his relatives. When they arrived, the carriages and horses were splendid, and the attendants were elegant, more than thirty in number. Upon reaching Wei, they gave fifty bolts of various colored fabrics, fifty bolts of red silk, and other items to the same extent. Wei then gave his daughter in marriage. After a year, his son fell ill, and his parents asked Cui, who replied, “Uncle Eight’s little sister, who is now grown up, has been ordered by her uncle to marry into a noble family. The reason for his illness is that the little sister has entered the room.”
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

During the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, the county magistrate of Dongguang, Xie Hunzhi, was known for governing with severity, cruelty, and violence in Henan. Xie Hunzhi once conducted a large hunt to the east of the county, killing many foxes and wolves. That winter, two people went to the imperial court to accuse Xie Hunzhi of killing their father and brother, and of causing widespread disorder through theft. The Chancellor, Zhang Jiuling, ordered the censor, Zhang Xiao, to investigate the case, and also to detain the accusers along with him. Zhang Xiao, who had always been friendly with Xie Hunzhi, first drafted a preliminary account of the case and instructed Xie Hunzhi to handle it himself. Xie Hunzhi questioned local officials, but they all said, "We do not recognize this person." Xie Hunzhi considered the accusations to be fraudulent and prepared to prove them false in court. As Zhang Xiao was about to arrive in Cangzhou, he first detained Xie Hunzhi in prison. Xie Hunzhi ordered his officials to prepare the office for the envoy, waiting for Zhang Xiao's arrival.

A village head passed by the temple gate, and outside the gate, there was a wooden room securely locked and guarded. He heard a voice coming from beneath the Buddha statue. The lock was so tight that no one could enter. The village head cautiously approached to listen. He heard the voice saying, "The county magistrate has done wrong, killing my father and brothers. Now my two younger brothers have come to the court to appeal our grievance. People are coming to take them away. May the great deity protect us and help us to get justice." After a while, he saw a filial son emerge from a hidden place. The village head suspected that he was not a human and went forward to search for him. The man saw the village head, became frightened, and entered the temple. He disappeared after reaching the area behind the temple. The village head returned and told this to Hun. Hun was astonished for a long time and said, "I killed many foxes and wolves at the beginning of spring. Could it be this?" By dawn, he arrived and brought the plaintiff out. The people of the county did not recognize him. The plaintiff spoke angrily, and his arguments were unassailable. Hun did not know the reason for this. A knowledgeable person advised him to get a hunting dog. The hunting dog arrived, saw the plaintiff, and immediately attacked. It jumped directly onto the roof and transformed into two foxes and disappeared.

Wáng Bāo of Wu Commandery in the Tang dynasty, as a young man, served the Taoist master Yè Jìngnéng. Later, he abandoned his Taoist studies and became a student at the Imperial Academy. After several years of study, a woman came to lodge at his place, and Wáng Bāo formed a close relationship with her, their affection being very deep. When Yè Jìngnéng was in the capital, Wáng Bāo went to visit him. Yè Jìngnéng asked, "How can you have the smell of a wild fox?" Wáng Bāo firmly replied that he had none, but Yè Jìngnéng said, "You do have it." Wáng Bāo then explained the beginning and end of how he had obtained the woman. Yè Jìngnéng said, "This is precisely this old wild fox." Before parting, Yè Jìngnéng wrote a talisman and gave it to Wáng Bāo, instructing him to hold it in his mouth and warning him, "When you return home, you can spit it out. She will come here on her own, and I will send her away. Do not worry." Wáng Bāo returned home and followed Yè Jìngnéng's instructions. The woman got the talisman, transformed into an old fox, and ran away while holding the talisman in her mouth. She went to Yè Jìngnéng's place and bowed to thank him. Yè Jìngnéng said, "I will spare your life, but you should not return to the Wang family again." From this point on, their relationship ended.

In the Tang dynasty, in the Sīgōnglǐ district of Luoyang, there was a low-ranking official named Tang Sanjun. He was upright in character and well-disciplined, and selective in his social interactions. One day, two visitors, Zhao Ménfú and Kang Sān, presented their visiting cards to seek an audience. Tang Sanjun did not immediately receive them and asked about their purpose. Zhao Ménfú said, "We only seek a light meal." Tang Sanjun had his attendants inform them that he was not available. However, the two men entered the main hall directly. Zhao Ménfú said, "How can you say you are not here, just for a meal?" Tang Sanjun explained that the gatekeeper had not reported their arrival. He led them out to the outer hall and had his family provide them with food. Privately, he warned his servant to place a sword in a dish, ready to stab the visitors if they entered. When the visitors arrived, Tang Sanjun drew his sword to stab Zhao Ménfú but missed. He then struck Kang Sān, hitting him, but Kang Sān still managed to leap into the pond in front of the courtyard. Zhao Ménfú cursed, saying, "Although we are both foxes, I am a thousand-year-old, and a thousand-year-old fox is surnamed Zhao or Zhang; a five-hundred-year-old fox is surnamed Bai or Kang.
