How can you be so unrighteous? If you kill me, Kang Sān, I will surely seek retribution from you, and I will not let the son of the Kang family die in vain." Tang Sanjun deeply thanked him and ordered his servant to summon Kang Sān. Zhao Ménfú went to the pond and called out to Kang Sān, who responded, "Yes." However, when they searched, they could only find Kang Sān's nose remaining. After Zhao Ménfú left, Tang Sanjun sprinkled peach blossom water around the house and hung talismans and incantations. From then on, the visitors did not return, and Tang Sanjun believed that the measures were effective.

After some time, the cherries in the garden ripened. One leisurely day, the Tang couple went to inspect the garden and suddenly saw Zhao Menfu on a cherry tree, picking and eating the cherries. The Tang wife was startled and exclaimed, "Zhao Menfu, how dare you come here again?" Zhao Menfu smiled and replied, "You deceived me with the peach, so I just came to pick and eat these cherries. Will you eat them too?" He then threw several cherries to the Tang couple. The Tang family became even more afraid and widely summoned monks to establish an altar and chant spells. Zhao Menfu did not come for several days. The monks chanted with great earnestness, hoping it would be effective and claiming it as their own achievement. One day, after the evening cleared up, the monks sat in front of the pillars and suddenly saw a multicolored cloud come from the west, directly arriving at the front of the Tang family's hall. In the cloud was a Buddha with a dignified appearance, who asked the monks, "Are you here to exorcise the wild fox for the Tang family?" The monks prostrated themselves. The Tang family, both old and young, worshipped with great reverence, gladly seeing the true Buddha, and bowed and invited the Buddha to descend. After a long while, the Buddha descended and sat on the altar, and the family served with great diligence. The Buddha said to the monk, "You are practicing the Way, please understand. Why must you eat vegetables for a long time? Can you eat meat for the sake of the Dharma? But ask if your heart can remain steadfast. Even if you eat meat, it will not cause harm." Then the Buddha ordered the Tang family to buy meat, set the food, and gave it to the monk and the family, who all ate. After the meal, they suddenly saw Zhao Menfu on the altar, and the whole family sighed with regret, feeling misled. Zhao Menfu laughed and said, "Do not bother to dislike me, I will not come again." From then on, he did not come again.

**严谏** During the Tang Dynasty, the magistrate of Luoyang, Yan Jian, had a maternal uncle who passed away. Yan Jian went to pay his respects. Ten days later, the family had removed their mourning clothes. Yan Jian summoned the family and asked about the situation. They replied, "The deceased forbade it." He then described the deceased's words and actions as if they were still alive. Yan Jian suspected it was a wild fox spirit and intended to deal with it. Later, he went to his uncle's house, where the spirit became angry and instructed the family members: "Do not allow the nephew of the high-ranking official to come again, as it is of no benefit to our family affairs and only leads to mutual suspicion." The spirit also told Yan Jian, "Nephew, your official duties seem busy; you should not come so frequently." Yan Jian then suddenly brought gray eagles, double falcons, black eagles, and hunting dogs, along with over a hundred workers, all armed with weapons, and surrounded the house several times before entering the spirit hall. Suddenly, he saw a wild fox with red fur walking upside down on the roof. He tried to shoot it but missed. Soon after, the fox opened the door and leaped out, disappearing without a trace. Yan Jian was extremely puzzled by the strange occurrence.

○ Wei Censorial Officer A censorial officer from Runzhou during the Tang dynasty was known for his hidden virtue, which even his brothers did not recognize. His brothers considered him unintelligent and looked down on him. Later, he suddenly told his brothers, "Wealth and property should be acquired through the right way, not through force." His brothers were surprised by his words and asked, "How have you grown so wise?" He replied, "There is a great treasure in the Kunming Pool, and we can take it together." His brothers accompanied him to the pool. Upon arriving at the pool, he dipped his hand into the water, and the water dried up, revealing a large amount of gold and treasures. He told his brothers, "You can take them." As his brothers delved deeper, they could not find any. He then said, "These treasures can be seen but cannot be taken, because there is a fixed destiny or fate." His brothers admired him and asked, "How did you learn this profound method?" He smiled but did not answer. After a while, he said, "Next year, I will receive an official position, and I will not worry about poverty." He was selected and appointed as a censorial officer in Runzhou, and he then traveled east to take up his post. On his way, he passed through Kaifeng County, where the county magistrate's mother was suffering from a fox spirit. Previous practitioners of magic and medicine had failed to cure her.

A Taoist, skilled in seeing ghosts, told the magistrate, "Recently, I have seen strange beings entering the county. If we can get this person, the illness of the respected lady will surely be cured." The magistrate sent someone to wait for him. A few days later, the Taoist reported, "He has arrived at the inn in this county. It is advisable to pay a personal visit." The magistrate went to see the Taoist and formally invited him with courtesy. The Taoist smiled and said, "Is this Taoist speaking for you? But because of the respected lady, to humble oneself before others is also pitiable. Fortunately, I have met you, and her illness will surely be cured. Tomorrow, from the county bridge to your residence, stop briefly so the people can see him. I will go there to send him away. In the meantime, you should return home, clean, and burn incense in anticipation." The magistrate followed all his instructions. The next day, when the Taoist arrived at the residence, he asked about the illness of the respected lady and sprinkled water from a willow branch on her body. Soon, an old white wild fox descended from the bed, walked slowly to the county bridge, and then disappeared. The magistrate offered gifts, but the Taoist refused them all. After a year in office, the Taoist told his wife, "Next month I will die. After my death, you should marry the state's judge, and you will have three sons." All of this happened as he had said.

### The Woman of the Yang Family In the Tang Dynasty, there was a woman from the Yang family whose two daughters were married into a family of non-Han Chinese origin. The younger son of this family was favored by his mother-in-law. The elder son told his servant, "The younger son is nothing but a wild fox. My mother-in-law spares him but not me." The servant reported this to the mother. She asked, "How do you know this?" The servant replied, "It would be best to hang a magpie's head above the door. If the younger son comes, have his wife call out to him asking for cooked meat, and repeat this several times. He will surely leave." The Yang family followed this advice, and the younger son indeed departed. Thus, people today still say, "Calling out for cooked meat wards off fox spirits," and it is said to be quite effective. ### Xue Jiong In the Tang Dynasty, Xue Jiong from Hedong, along with ten of his followers, spent several nights in the Eastern Capital engaging in intimate relations with a courtesan. They each gave her ten thousand coins as a gift. On one night at midnight, the courtesan requested to leave, but Xue Jiong kept her until dawn. The woman became agitated and tried to leave multiple times, carrying the money with her. Xue Jiong ordered the doorkeeper not to let her out, and the doorkeeper did not open the door. The woman, holding the money, went to check and found herself at the water drain, where she transformed into a wild fox and left through the drain, leaving the money behind. ### Xin Tefou In the Tang Dynasty, after the death of Xin Tefou's mother, there were always spirits speaking from her spirit table, just as they had been before her death. The family treated her with the same respect as if she were still alive. Xin Tefou's cousin, a magician, heard of this and came to observe. He secretly performed rituals behind Xin Tefou's house. Upon entering, he saw a hairless female wild fox and killed it, thus ending the phenomenon.

The People of Dai Prefecture** During the Tang Dynasty, there was a woman in Dai Prefecture whose brother was stationed far away and not present. Her mother and daughter lived alone. Suddenly, a Bodhisattva arrived riding on clouds, saying to the mother, "Your home is very good, I wish to reside here. Please repair it quickly, and I will come again soon." The villagers rushed to the house. After the repairs were completed, the Bodhisattva descended on a cloud of five colors to the room. The villagers offered her much worship. The Bodhisattva then ordered everyone not to speak about it, fearing that people from all directions who had faith would keep coming. The villagers thus warned each other not to mention the matter. The Bodhisattva secretly had intercourse with the daughter, and she became pregnant. After a year, her brother returned. The Bodhisattva said, "I do not wish to see men," and ordered the mother to drive her away. The daughter could not reach her, so she spent all her wealth to seek a Taoist priest. After a long time, a Taoist priest performed a ritual and secretly looked at the Bodhisattva, discovering that she was an old fox. He then entered with a knife and killed her. **Feng Jie** During the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Feng Jie who suffered from a fox demon illness. Later, his father found a magician who treated Feng Jie's illness. The demon suddenly wept and said to Feng Jie, "I originally intended to stay together until the end, but now I am being forced by the magician and cannot remain." She cried for many days before finally giving Feng Jie a set of clothes, saying, "Take good care of them, they are a token of my long-lasting remembrance." Feng Jie initially hid the clothes from his family, rolling them up and keeping them in a book. After he recovered, he went to the capital to take the imperial examinations and did not open the book to look at the clothes. After passing the exams, he finally opened the book, and it was just paper.

○贺兰进明 During the Tang Dynasty, Helan Jinming was married to a fox. Every time the season came, the fox wife would visit the capital house, introducing herself and inquiring about well-being, and bringing gifts and greetings. Some family members who saw her found her extremely beautiful in appearance. On the fifth day of the fifth month, everyone in the household, from the highest to the lowest, had their lives prolonged. The family considered this an omen and burned the objects. The fox wife wept bitterly, saying, "These are all real things, why did you burn them?" The objects obtained afterward were used up. Later, a family member sought a gold-flowered mirror and went into a house to steal a mirror, hanging it around her neck, climbing the wall, and was killed by the homeowner. After that, the strange occurrences ceased. ○崔昌 During the Tang Dynasty, Cui Chang was studying in the eastern capital. A child with a distinctive appearance came and stayed in the courtyard. After some time, the child gradually climbed the stairs and sat by Cui Chang's bed. Cui Chang did not pay attention to it and used her hand to roll up his books. Cui Chang asked slowly, "Who are you, and what do you want?" The child replied, "I originally loved reading and admired your learning." Cui Chang did not reject it and often asked about literary meanings, which were very reasonable. After several months, at dusk, the child suddenly brought an old man who was drunk to Cui Chang's place. The child temporarily left, and the old man, drunk, vomited human nails and hair. Cui Chang found this very disgusting. Cui Chang had a sharp sword that he usually carried and cut off the old man's head, revealing it to be an old fox. Soon after, the child returned, saying angrily, "Why did you act so rudely, killing my father? I could kill you too, but I am doing so out of old favor." The child yelled at him and left. After that, the strange occurrences ceased.

Long Sun Jia Long Sun Jia, the county magistrate of Zhongbu in Fangzhou during the Tang Dynasty, was a devout follower of Buddhism and Taoism. One day during his fasting period, the entire family saw the Bodhisattva Manjushri riding a colorful cloud descending from the edge of the sun. Soon after, the Bodhisattva arrived at the eaves of the fasting place and remained motionless. The whole family showed deep respect and earnest worship, and after a long time, the Bodhisattva descended. The family continued to offer sacrifices and worship for several days. However, Long Sun Jia's son was skeptical and went to the capital to find a Taoist priest to set up a magical barrier, which resulted in the killing of a fox. Long Sun Jia's family offered a horse and fifty thousand coins as compensation. After several more days, another Bodhisattva appeared, and the family continued to show respect. The son again invited a Taoist priest to perform the same magical barrier. After more than ten days, the Bodhisattva asked the Taoist priest, "What is your magical ability?" The priest replied, "I have nothing left." The Bodhisattva then said, "I will give you a beating." The Bodhisattva asked the priest, "Have you read the Taoist scriptures? Do you know about Hugangzi the fox spirit?" The priest answered, "Yes, I know him." The Bodhisattva said, "Hugangzi the fox spirit is me. I have achieved immortality and have existed for three ten-thousand years. As a Taoist priest, you should practice purity and detachment. Why do you kill living beings? My descendants were killed by you, so how can you live?" The Bodhisattva then beat the Taoist priest a hundred times and told Long Sun Jia, "My descendants were misbehaving and causing trouble, but I am ashamed to say anything. I will ensure that you never suffer misfortune or disaster as a reward." The Bodhisattva then told the Taoist priest, "Return the horse and money to him immediately." After saying this, the Bodhisattva flew away.

In the Suiyang Commandery of the Tang Dynasty, by the tomb of the King of Song, there lived an old fox. Every time the official duties were performed, all the dogs in the town went to pay homage to it. The fox would sit on the tomb, and the dogs would line up below it. In the Eastern Capital, there was an elderly man named Wang who had two dogs capable of catching ghosts. These dogs had killed many ghosts in the past. The people of Song collectively hired these dogs to catch the fox. The elderly man took the dogs to the scene, but the dogs went straight to the other dogs and lay down without moving, greatly disappointing the expectations of the people of Song. Nowadays, people who don't understand the situation often joke about it, saying, "Get the wild fox dogs of Suiyang."

Duan Ai, the son of Liu Quanbai's wet nurse, was a young man who enjoyed setting nets at night to trap wild pigs and foxes. Liu Quanbai's estate was located in Qishan, and one evening, Duan Ai set a net several miles west of the estate and lay in wait within it. In the darkness, he heard the sound of something moving and saw a creature lying on the ground, peering at the net. The creature then stood up and transformed into a woman dressed in a red skirt. She walked past the net and approached Duan Ai's carriage, where she suddenly caught and ate a mouse. Duan Ai shouted at her, and the woman hurriedly entered the net. He struck her with a stick, killing her, but her human form did not change. Duan Ai was filled with doubt and fear, wondering if she might be a human. He decided to sink the net into a water-filled hemp pool. That night, he discussed the matter with his parents. The next morning, the entire family wanted to secretly escape, but Duan Ai whispered, "Is there a woman who eats raw mice? This must be a fox!" He returned to the hemp pool and found the woman alive. He then chopped her with a large axe from behind his waist, and she turned into an old fox. Duan Ai was overjoyed and brought the fox back to the village. An old monk, seeing that the fox was still alive, advised Duan Ai to keep it alive, saying, "There is a charm bead in the fox's mouth. If you can obtain it, it will be greatly valued." Duan Ai tied the fox's four legs with a rope and placed a large cage over it. After a few days, the fox was able to eat. The monk used a narrow-necked bottle, buried it in the ground with the mouth level with the surface, and placed two pieces of cooked pork inside. The fox loved the roasted meat but could not reach it, so it kept its mouth in the bottle, waiting for the meat to cool. The monk repeated this process, and after the fox drooled for a long time, the bottle and the meat were full. The fox then spat out the pearl and died. The pearl was shaped like a chess piece, perfectly round and clean. Duan Ai's mother wore it, and it greatly increased in value.

Wang An, having married into the Cui family, followed his father-in-law Shi Tong to Jiangxia during the Tianbao period of the Tang dynasty. Shi Tong was the governor of Mian Prefecture. Wang An was bewitched by a fox and did not want to cross the river. He cried out madly and always wanted to jump into the water. His wife and relatives were terrified and tied Wang An to the bedpost. When the boat was halfway across the river, he suddenly laughed. Upon reaching the shore, he was very happy and said, "I thought the young ladies would not follow me across the river, but now they are on the city wall, so there is nothing to worry about!" Upon taking office, Shi Tong immediately sought out a sorcerer. His attendants mentioned a local man who could shoot foxes, and Shi Tong invited him over. He had the entire hall prepared with beds and mats and placed Wang An in the northwest corner of the house. Dozens of family members took turns guarding him. Shi Tong himself set up a separate bed outside the hall, holding a bow and arrows to wait for the fox. After three nights, he suddenly said, "Have everyone gotten a good sleep? I have already hit the fox; I will take it tomorrow."

The People Thought Him Mad, Yet Did Not Believe It. When dawn came, they saw blood on the window, and following the trail of blood, they entered a large pit, where beneath the grass they found a female fox, mortally wounded with an arrow. An’s wife burned the fox to ashes and consumed them entirely. From then on, he recovered fully. Later, he became the county magistrate of Yuanwu, and while in his office hall, he suddenly saw an old fox servant who bowed twice before him. The fox said, “This is your grandmother, the great lady. In the past, your lady was wrongly killed by the Cui family. Her parents-in-law have never ceased to remember her, and now they wish to give their daughter once more in marriage to Wang郎, hence I have come to convey this intention and to choose a propitious day for the union.” An was greatly frightened and agreed to offer a generous reward, spending lavishly to prepare for it. He immediately took out more than ten bolts of silk and brocade and burned them in the main street. The old fox then said to his wife, “How many fine men are there in the world? Why should we take an old man from the Wang family as a son-in-law?” Having spoken these words, he vanished. ○ Sun Zengsheng In the Tang dynasty, there was a Daoist priest named Sun Zengsheng, who originally made a living by raising hawks. Later, when he let his hawk into a cave, he saw dozens of foxes reading books. One old fox sat in the center, passing down the teachings one after another. Sun Zengsheng boldly entered and took the book and returned. The next day, more than ten people came to his door bearing gold and silk, requesting its return. Sun Zengsheng refused to give it back. They said, “You have obtained this, but you cannot understand how to use it. If you write a copy and return it to us, we will teach you the oral instructions.” Sun Zengsheng ultimately learned the method and became a famous practitioner of Daoist arts. The foxes had originally made a pact with Sun Zengsheng that the knowledge must not be shown to others, and if this were violated, the violator would surely meet a violent death. At the end of the Tianbao era, Emperor Xuanzong insisted on obtaining the secret, but Sun Zengsheng refused to give it up, and in the end, he was executed.

○Wáng Xuān Wáng Xuān, the governor of Sòng Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, was very handsome when he was young and was charmed by a female fox. Those who saw her, whether adults or children, found her appearance graceful and beautiful. They would bow and show respect, and she would call herself a new bride, responding with reason and intelligence. As a result, people enjoyed seeing her. Every Dragon Boat Festival and other holidays, she would send gifts, saying, "New bride for a certain young master or young lady, to extend life." People laughed at her, but she gave many gifts. Later, when Wáng Xuān held a high-ranking position, the fox no longer appeared, as his high status prevented her from causing mischief. (From Volume 451 of *Taiping Guangji*) ○Cóng Pǔ Cóng Pǔ, the magistrate of Chángchéng during the Dàlì period of the Tang Dynasty, once said that when he was young, he and his companions hunted wild foxes at night for several nights without success. One night during the full moon, they tried again. They saw a ghost wearing a hat and riding a fox, singing the *Dúpánzǐ*. When they reached the trap, the fox tried to enter it, but the ghost touched the fox's cheek and made it return. This happened several times. On another night, Cóng Pǔ set up the trap again and waited for the fox. The ghost rode the fox again, with two small ghosts leading the way, coming and going extensively. They caught nothing and stopped. Another person said that when he was young, he was hunting at the edge of a cliff and suddenly saw an old man supporting himself with a cane, standing under a tree above him. The old man asked from above, "Who are the people on the tree?" The young people were very frightened, and his brother angrily shouted, "Old wild fox, how dare you do this!" He jumped down from the tree to chase the fox, which then transformed and ran away.

Li Nian, the Magistrate of Dongping, was newly appointed and traveled from the capital to his post. One night, he stopped in an old city. There was an old acquaintance in the shop who sold trapezia as his profession, and his wife, surnamed Zheng, was beautiful. Li Nian looked at her and was pleased, so he stayed in their house. He lingered for several days, then exchanged fifteen thousand coins to marry the woman. Upon arriving in Dongping, he treated her with great favor. She was gentle, charming, and flirtatious, excelling in all women's work and especially skilled in music. They stayed in Dongping for three years and had one son. Later, Li Nian went to the capital on a mission to collect rents and returned with Zheng. When they reached the old city, they held a grand gathering with the locals, lasting more than ten days. Li Nian urged her to leave several times, but Zheng stubbornly claimed illness and refused to go. Li Nian felt sorry for her and allowed her to stay. After another ten days, unable to delay further, Li Nian had to leave due to circumstances. As they approached the city gate, Zheng suddenly claimed stomach pain, dismounted, and ran away like the wind. Li Nian and his servants chased after her, but they could not catch up, and she entered the old city. She turned into a village near the Yi River, rested briefly, and Li Nian, unable to abandon her, followed her again. As they were about to catch up, she entered a small cave, and Li Nian called out to her, but there was no response. Li Nian was deeply saddened and wept. As evening fell, the villagers blocked the cave entrance with grass and returned to their inn for the night. The next morning, Li Nian went back to call out to her, but saw nothing, so he lit a fire to smoke her out.

After a long time, the villagers dug deep into the ground and found a dead female fox in her den, her clothes shed like a snake's skin, and silk socks on her feet. Li sighed for a long time, then buried her and returned to the inn. He took a hunting dog to bite the fox's child, who showed no fear, and then took the child to the capital to be raised by a relative. After completing his duties, he returned to the capital and married into the Xiao family. Xiao often called Li the "wild fox husband," and Li had no response at first. One evening, Li and Xiao returned to their room together and discussed the matter again. Suddenly, they heard someone in the hall, and Li asked, "Who came at night?" The response was, "Don't you recognize Zheng Si Niang?" Li, who was deeply attached to Zheng Si Niang, became excited upon hearing her voice and asked, "Are you a ghost or a human?" She replied, "I am a ghost." She wanted to approach him but could not. Zheng Si Niang then said to Li, "Humans and spirits are different, and your honorable wife repeatedly insults me. The child you gave birth to is entrusted to others, who say it was born from a fox and do not provide clothing and food. Do you not think about this? You should take care of the child early to avoid regrets in the netherworld. If your wife continues to insult me and does not take care of the child, it will become a problem for you." After speaking, she disappeared, and Xiao no longer dared to mention the matter. By the end of the Tang Dynasty's Tianbao era, the child was over ten years old and was very well.

During the Tianbao period of the Tang dynasty, Li Chang served as the state granary officer in Jizhou, temporarily managing household affairs. It was believed that this office was inherently ominous, and if a spirit known as "little Confucius" appeared in the office, the holder of the household affairs office would die. This belief was widely known and believed across the empire. Chang took up the temporary assignment and immediately occupied this office. After more than ten days, a child who was more than ten years old went to the toilet. Suddenly, a woman in white holding the child's head tried to take him to the wall. People rescued him, and he was saved. The woman suddenly disappeared. Chang angrily cursed, and from the air, a tile hit Chang's hand. Chang's cousin, who was a military officer in the same state, came to Chang's place that day. He said, "This is just a fox spirit." Qiuwo was known for its eagles and hunting dogs, and we should bring them here. Soon after, a pile of feces was thrown into Cui's cup. A few days later, the dogs arrived, and Chang went on a big hunt, capturing several cunning foxes. He hung them on the eaves.

At night, a voice called out from the eaves, "Li Sishi," saying, "This is the fox spirit causing trouble. Why did you wrongfully kill my child? I wish to invite you to a drink. Prepare wine for me tomorrow." Chang replied, "I just have some wine. Come by in the morning." The next day, when the wine was ready, the fox spirit arrived. It was invisible, but its voice could be heard. Chang drank with it. When it came to the fox, the wine disappeared in an instant. The fox drank several dou in total, while Chang only drank two sheng. Suddenly, it said, "I am drunk today, and fear I might lose proper etiquette. You may stop, Sishi. The fox spirit is no longer a concern. Tomorrow, I will send a method to dispel the evil." The next day, as Chang was about to enter the office, he suddenly heard from the eaves, "Receive the method." Soon after, a piece of paper fell. Chang opened it and found a talisman. He lit a lamp on the table and wrote a spell behind it. The ritual was very thorough. Chang followed the instructions, and the supernatural trouble ceased.

The Position of Governor of Xinzhou During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, the position of Governor of Xinzhou was a long-vacant one, and many of the previous holders died in office. A high-ranking military official was sent to try the position. Upon arriving, he slept alone in the hall at night. After the second watch, he saw a black object outside the eaves, which was shaped like a large ship, with its eyes several zhang apart. The governor asked, "Who are you?" The creature replied, "I am a great snake." The governor ordered it to change its appearance and speak with him, and the snake transformed into a human form and came to the hall. The governor then asked, "Why did you kill people?" The snake said, "I had no intention to kill; the guests were frightened to death by their own fear." The governor then asked, "If you had no intention to kill, why did you appear so often?" The snake said, "I have been wronged and trapped, and I need the magistrate to help me." The governor asked, "What have you been wronged by?" The snake said, "When I was young, I once entered an ancient tomb, and since then, my body has grown larger, and I cannot get out. Foxes, rabbits, and other animals sometimes enter the tomb, and I can eat them. Now I am stuck in the earth, unable to die, so I am asking for help from the magistrate." The governor asked, "If so, should we dig you out? How?" The snake said, "I have spread out for more than ten li. If you dig, the city will collapse. There is a village called Wang to the east of the city, and a tree called Jiu to the west of the village. The magistrate can set up a ritual purification, and someone can dig a hole two zhang deep in the tree. Inside, there is an iron box. Open the box and look inside, and I will be able to escape." After finishing speaking, he left. The next morning, they dug as instructed and found the box. They returned to the hall and opened it, and a blue dragon flew out of the box and killed the snake, splitting it in half. The snake was finally killed, and the strange occurrences ceased.

The County Magistrate of Yudun The county magistrate of Yudun, in Poyang, would die shortly after taking office, and no one dared to take the position after that, leaving the residence abandoned. During the early years of the Tang Dynasty, a poor scholar came to take the post. Upon arrival, the officials requested that he stay in a separate office. The magistrate then ordered the old residence to be repaired, clearing away thorny grass and weeds, and completing the walls and buildings. He stayed alone in the hall, setting up candles at night to watch. After the second watch, a white bag-like object, about the size of a three-dou container, leaped toward the bed and directly jumped onto the table. The magistrate showed no fear, gently touching it with his hand, and found it was indeed a leather bag filled with water. He then said, "Move the lamp straight to the southwest corner." As soon as he finished speaking, the lamp was already in the southwest corner. He then said, "You can massage me." The bag turned and lay on his body, and he felt very comfortable. He then joked, "Can you make my bed float in the air?" Soon, the bed was already floating in the air. Whatever he said, it happened as he wished. Just before dawn, it leaped away. The magistrate followed it and found it had vanished by the pond outside the residence. The next day, he looked at the spot where it had disappeared and saw a hole, barely as big as an ant's burrow. When they dug it, it was about a zhang long and the hole widened, with a circumference of more than three chi, and its depth was immeasurable. The magistrate then ordered the preparation of many cauldrons, firewood, and charcoal, drawing all the water from the pond to make soup and pouring it into the hole. About a hundred dou of water were poured in, and a thunderous sound came from the hole, causing the ground to shake. After another hundred dou, it became silent. They then dug together with all their strength and, after several zhang, found a large snake, over a hundred chi long, with countless smaller ones beside it, all lying dead in the hole. The magistrate took the largest one, dried it for preservation, and distributed it among the county officials. Afterward, everything returned to peace and safety.

**Zhang Qishi** Zhang Qishi, he himself said, was a young boy when he accompanied Duke Ying Li Ji across the sea. They encountered a storm, lasting more than ten days, and they did not know how many miles they had traveled. The wind remained calm and unchanging, and suddenly they saw two black objects with heads resembling snakes, as large as giant ships, their length not fully visible. Shortly after, they arrived at the ship, and all wrapped their heads around the ship and pushed it horizontally, as fast as the wind. The sailors were terrified, not knowing how to resist, and had already been divided into what would be eaten. They only recited the Buddha's name, praying for a quick death. After a long time, they arrived at a mountain, where ships were broken and piled up. Each one thought to themselves, "Those people were all eaten by these creatures." Shortly after, the wind was very strong. Looking behind the ship, they saw three more snakes, which also chased and arrived, seeming to fight over food. The two snakes released the ship, turned, and fought the three snakes on the sand, each twisting and writhing on the isolated island. The sailors then took advantage of the wind and raised the sails, thus escaping the danger. After several days, they arrived at another mountain, saw smoke and fire from afar, and thought it was a human settlement. They lowered the sails and landed on the shore, walking with two people. The gate was very large, so they approached and knocked on it. There was a person several zhang tall, all over covered with white hair, who came out and saw the two people and ate them. One person quickly ran to the ship. Just as they got on the ship, before it could be opened, the white-haired person ran over and pulled the anchor rope. The people on the ship each grabbed bows and knives and shot at him. Repeatedly swinging several blades, only then was he released. About a mile from the shore, there were already dozens of them on the shore, pointing their fingers and shouting loudly. Then, for five or six days, they sailed with the wind, saw an island from afar, anchored the boat, and asked someone. They said it was the boundary of Qingyuan County, belonging to Nanhai.

A Wise Person at the Zhi Xiang Temple In the Zhi Xiang Temple of Chang'an, there was a wise person who, since he was over ten years old, had been practicing Daoism in the Western Meditation Hall. In the hall's shrine, there was always a snake beneath the seat. When the wise person first began his practice, the snake was as thick as an arm. After more than forty years, the snake had grown to the size of a pillar in the hall. Despite being in close proximity, the people and the snake did not harm each other. One night during the Yuan period, the wise person went to the shrine to pay homage. There was no lamp in the hall, yet it was filled with bright, dazzling light, which greatly surprised him. He found a pearl of one inch in diameter at the place where the snake emerged. He took the pearl to the market and offered it for a high price, hoping that someone knowledgeable would recognize its value. After several days, a foreigner came to the market and offered one million units of currency for it. The wise person said, "This pearl glows in the dark; it should be priceless. Why is the price so low?" The foreigner replied, "Pearls from oysters are valuable, but this is a snake pearl, which can be worth up to a thousand strings of coins." The wise person admired and respected the foreigner's knowledge and sold the pearl.

○ Li Qiwu The governor of Henan, Li Qiwu, was demoted during the Tianbao era to serve as the magistrate of Jingling. It was a local superstition that if a white smoke appeared on the southern tower of the city and the governor did not act upon it, he would die; scholars regarded this as a common omen. When Li Qiwu was demoted, he felt deeply resentful, and suddenly white smoke appeared in the tower. He angrily declared, “I fear not death—what can the gods do to me?” He sent men to investigate the source of the smoke, and they reported, “The white smoke is entirely made of white insects—it may be a great snake.” Li Qiwu ordered the excavation, and as the hole widened, a large snake was found inside, its body as big as a large jar. He ordered several shi of oil to be heated in a great cauldron, and when it boiled, they used it to burn the snake. At first, the snake roared like thunder, shaking the city walls, and it took a full day before it finally died. Then he had the ditch filled and blocked to prevent it from reappearing. Li Qiwu experienced no other strange occurrences. ○ Yan Tingzhi Yan Tingzhi became the governor of Weizhou, and upon first assuming his post, he stood in the hall of governance. A small ?snake entered through the door and reached the table, placing its head upon it. Yan Tingzhi did not immediately understand what was happening, and quickly took up his wooden tablet, pressing the snake’s head to the ground and standing upright in contemplation. Soon after, the snake transformed into a talisman. Yan Tingzhi thought this must be the work of a magician, but after searching in vain, he gave up.

### 天宝樵人 In the middle of the Tianbao era, a woodcutter went into the mountains and fell asleep drunk. He was swallowed by a snake. When he woke up slightly, he felt strange and experienced body movements. He opened his eyes but could not see anything, and he realized that he had been swallowed by something. He used a woodcutter's knife to cut his belly and managed to get out. He felt dizzy and confused, and after a long time, he finally understood what had happened. Half of his body's skin had peeled off, resembling a white snake. ### 张镐 The city of Hongzhou, established by Ma Yuan, had not been repaired since its founding, and it was said that those who repaired it would die. During the Yongtai era, the military governor Zhang Gao repaired it without hesitation. Suddenly, in a corner of the northwest, they encountered a large pit, in which they saw two snakes, one white and one black, with heads like oxen, shaped like large jars, over sixty feet long, coiling in the pit, with numerous small snakes. They immediately reported to Zhang Gao, who ordered them to drive the snakes out. They bound the snakes' heads with bamboo strips and pulled them out. The snakes initially did not open their eyes and followed as they were pulled out. There were many small snakes, and some soldiers injured several of them, but they were still as large as drinking bowls. The two snakes followed each other into the release pond under Xu Ru's pavilion, where the water was several zhang deep, and the turtles all came out onto the shore and were caught by people. The fish also puffed their gills out of the water and died shortly after. Seven days later, Zhang Gao died. The deputy magistrate Zheng Cong, the magistrate of Nanchang Ma Jiao, and their two sons died one after another.

A Hunter from Haizhou People from Haizhou made hunting their occupation. Once, while hunting deer in the mountains near the East Sea, they suddenly saw a snake, black in color, as large as connected mountains, nearly ten zhang long, with eyes like the sun, coming from the sea. Seeing the snake, the hunter was filled with fear, knowing he could not escape death, and thus relied on reciting Buddha's name. The snake arrived at the hunter's place, took him and his bow and arrows in its mouth, and crossed the sea. It reached a distant mountain and placed the man on a high cliff. Soon, another snake came from the south, reaching the mountain, similar to the first snake but twice as large. The two snakes fought at the foot of the mountain, initially entwining their bodies, then biting each other with their mouths. The hunter knew the snake was seeking his help, so he passed on poisoned arrows to shoot it. The large snake had a problem with one eye. The hunter then shot at its eye, hitting it several times. After a while, the large snake died and fell to the ground. The smaller snake's head and tail were broken, and it carried several dou of large pearls and green stones, returning the hunter to his original place.

**Shoulder-Born** Once upon a time, there was a scholar who, while traveling, encountered a small snake and took it in. After several months, the snake grew larger, and the scholar often carried it on his shoulder, naming it "Shoulder-Born." Later, the snake became too large to carry, so the scholar released it into a large marsh east of Fan County. More than forty years later, the snake had grown to the size of a boat and was known as a divine python. Anyone who ventured into the marsh was surely devoured by the snake. The scholar, now old and frail, passed by the edge of this marsh. People told him, "There is a big snake in the marsh that eats people; you should not go there." It was a very cold winter, and the scholar thought that snakes hide in winter, so this couldn't be true. He decided to pass by the marsh and walked more than twenty miles. Suddenly, a snake chased him. The scholar still recognized its shape and color and called out to it from a distance, "Aren't you my Shoulder-Born?" The snake lowered its head and, after a long time, left. The scholar returned to Fan County, where the county magistrate asked him why he was not eaten by the snake. The magistrate considered it strange and put the scholar in prison, sentencing him to death. The scholar privately muttered, "Shoulder-Born, raising you ended up making me die, isn't that terrible?" That night, the snake attacked and turned the entire county into a lake, but only the prison was not destroyed, and the scholar was saved. At the end of the Tianbao era, Dugu Xian, whose uncle was the magistrate of Fan County, was boating in the lake on the third day of the third month with his family. Suddenly, their boat capsized, and his family almost died several times.

The people of Puzhou dug into the ground to make a well. The pit was deep, over a zhang, and they encountered a square stone but did not reach the spring. They wanted to remove the stone and dig again, but suddenly they fell into a deep pit. Snakes coiled like overturned boats, and the smaller ones were like ordinary snakes. Initially, they were very frightened, but after a while, they became accustomed. Hungry with nothing to eat, the snakes sucked in air, and they also imitated them, and they no longer felt hungry. Over months, they heard thunder. At the first sound, the snakes raised their heads, and soon they all moved. Soon they dispersed, with the larger ones going forward, one after another coming out and going back in. The person knew they would not harm him, so he went forward and hugged their necks. The snakes then went straight away, climbing up a white path, as if walking ten li. Ahead there was a beacon fire, and then they put the person down on the ground and left. The person went to ask the person who lit the beacon, who said it was Pingzhou.

The Wife of the Office Clerk of the Ministry of Revenue During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong in the Kaiyuan era, the wife of an office clerk in the Ministry of Revenue was beautiful but suffered from a demonic illness, which she could not understand. They had a fine horse that always received double rations of feed, yet it became increasingly thin and weak. The clerk asked a neighboring trapper about this. The trapper, who was also a diviner, laughed and said, "A horse that travels a hundred miles is still strong, but now it has traveled more than a thousand miles. How could it not become thin?" The clerk replied, "She never leaves the house, and there is no one else in the house. How could this happen?" The trapper said, "Every time you go on duty, your wife goes out at night, and you do not know. If you do not believe me, wait until you go on duty again and hide somewhere to observe. You will see for yourself." The clerk followed his advice and returned home at night, hiding somewhere else. One watch of the night, his wife put on her makeup, ordered the maid to saddle the horse, and mounted it at the steps. The maid rode a broomstick behind her, and they slowly rose into the air, disappearing from sight. The clerk was greatly frightened. The next day, he went to see the trapper and said in surprise, "The demon is confirmed. What should I do?" The trapper instructed him to wait one more night. That night, the clerk hid in a tent in the hall, and his wife soon returned. She asked the maid why there was a living person's breath. The clerk ordered the maid to use a broomstick and candle to light up the hall and corridors. Frightened, the clerk fled in a panic into a large jar in the hall.

After a short while, he rode a horse back, but just now the broom had already been burned, leaving nothing left to ride. His wife said, "Ride whatever is available, why bother with a broom!" The maid hastily climbed onto a large jar and followed him. The official was inside the jar, too afraid to move. After a short while, they arrived at a place, a clearing on the mountain top, with elaborate decorations and furnishings, and a very grand banquet. There were several groups of drinkers, each with a partner. They feasted and drank, being very intimate and close, and after several hours they dispersed. The woman mounted her horse, instructing the maid to ride the jar. The maid was startled and said, "There's someone in the jar!" The woman, drunk, ordered the maid to push the jar down the mountain. The maid, also drunk, pushed the official out. The official dared not speak, so he climbed onto the jar and left. The official did not see anyone until morning, but there were only remnants of smoke and ashes. He then found a winding path, rugged and difficult to traverse for dozens of miles, finally reaching the mountain pass. He asked where they were, and they said it was Langzhou, a thousand miles from the capital. He traveled and begged diligently, and after more than a month, he finally made it back home. His wife was surprised and asked, "Where have you been for so long?" The official gave a different answer. He went back to ask the man, seeking his advice. The man said, "The demon has already formed, wait until she leaves again, and you can quickly tie and capture it, burning it with fire." They heard a pleading voice from the air, and soon a gray crane fell into the fire and was burned to death. The woman's illness was cured.

○Lu Rong During the early Kaiyuan era, Lu Rong of Fanyang, while lying alone in bed due to illness, suddenly saw a large bird flying from afar. Soon, it landed on a tree in the courtyard, standing about four to five feet tall, resembling an owl with eyes as large as cups and a beak over a foot long. It descended onto the ground, climbed the steps, and entered the room, eventually climbing onto the bed. It lifted its wings, which had hands holding a small spear, intending to strike Lu Rong. Lu Rong cowered in fear, sweating profusely. Suddenly, another person entered from the back door and said to the bird, "This is a good person, be careful not to hurt him." The bird then flew away, and the person also left. Lu Rong's illness then permanently disappeared. ○Wang Xu At the end of the Tianbao era, Wang Xu, the Luoci of Taizhou, was dying from illness. A large bird flew into Wang Xu's room, walked to the bed, and brought its beak toward Wang Xu, saying, "Take, take." Wang Xu then died.

**○南海大鱼** Lingnan Military Governor He Luguang, a native of Zhu Ya, lived near the South Sea. He claimed to have personally witnessed three extraordinary events. The first involved two mountains in the sea, about six or seven hundred miles apart. On a clear day, they appeared green and close. In the late Yuan dynasty, a massive storm occurred in the sea, with rain of mud resembling foam. The sky and earth were dark for seven days. A large fish, carried by the current into the two mountains, found itself unable to move forward or backward. After some time, its gill hung on a cliff, and after seven days, the mountain split, allowing the fish to escape. The thunder was the fish's sound, the mud rain its spittle, and the darkness its breath. The second event involved an island in the sea, extending for thousands of miles. On the island, there were several objects resembling toads, the largest circling around four or five hundred miles, and the smallest a hundred miles. Every full moon night, they spewed white vapor, reaching the moon and competing with its light. The third event involved a mountain in the sea, circling around tens of miles. At the beginning of summer, a giant snake appeared, as tall as a hundred仞 mountain and several hundred miles long. In the late Yuan dynasty, the snake drank the sea water, causing the water level to drop for over ten days. It circled a mountain dozens of times before drinking water. After some time, it was swallowed by a large creature in the sea. Within half a day, the mountain split, and the snake and mountain were completely consumed. It is unknown what the creature that swallowed the snake and mountain was.

### Whale At the end of the Kaiyuan era, in Leizhou, there was a battle between a thunder god and a whale. The whale emerged from the water, and the thunder god, numbering in the dozens, flew up and down in the air, sometimes shooting fire, sometimes scolding and striking, until after seven days, the battle finally ended. People living by the sea went to watch, but they did not know which one was victorious; they only saw the sea water turn red. ### Carp In the middle of the Kaiyuan era, in Linhai, Taizhou, there was a battle between a large snake and a carp. The snake was as big as a house, circling the isolated island several times, extending its head toward the water; the carp was as big as a small mountain, its back and eyes all red, moving back and forth for several miles, making gestures to strike each other. The carp used its scales and back to touch the snake, while the snake used its mouth to bite the carp. They fought like this for three days, and the snake was finally killed by the carp.

In recent times, there was a Persian who often said that he had traveled to the country of Tianzhu, India, six or seven times. On his last journey, his ship was blown off course into the open sea, for an unknown distance. They arrived at an island where they saw a man dressed in grass leaves. Fearing him, they asked him about his story. The man said, "Once, I was with dozens of companions who were lost at sea. Only I was carried by the current to this place. Since then, I have survived by eating wild fruits and roots." The travelers felt pity for him and took him aboard the ship. The man then said that the mountains on the island were all filled with carnelian, agate, glass, and other precious stones, too numerous to count. The sailors all abandoned their own goods to take these treasures. Once the ship was full, the man ordered, "Sail quickly, for if the mountain god arrives, he will be envious and may take the treasures back." They then set sail with the wind and sailed about forty miles. From afar, they saw a red object on a peak that resembled a snake, which gradually grew larger. The man exclaimed, "This is the mountain god, who is envious of the treasures and is chasing us. What shall we do?" The sailors were all afraid. Soon, they saw two mountains rising from the sea, hundreds of feet high. The man was delighted and said, "These two mountains are actually the claws of a giant crab. The crab often fights with the mountain god, and the god usually loses, so he is very afraid of it. Now that its claws are out, we have nothing to fear." A large snake soon arrived at the crab's location, and they fought for a long time. The crab grabbed the snake's head, and the snake died in the water, with the crab's claws looking like mountains. The sailors thus managed to cross safely.

During the early Tang Dynasty, a scholar from the eastern capital of Luoyang found himself in a difficult situation due to insufficient funds for his journey. He decided to travel south to the Yangtze and Huai rivers, seeking help from acquaintances. After struggling and failing to find any assistance, he wandered in Yangzhou for a long time. There was a man named Xie Er who lived in the same inn. Feeling sorry for the scholar's misfortune, Xie Er always wanted to help him. He told the scholar, "Do not be sad. If you wish to return north, I will give you three hundred thousand coins." When they parted, Xie Er gave the scholar a letter and said, "My house is east of Wei Wang Pool. When you reach the pool, knock on the large willow tree. If anyone comes out, deliver the letter to them and they will give you the money." The scholar followed his instructions and knocked on the big tree. After a while, a young maid came out and asked what he wanted. He said, "Xie Er has sent a letter." Suddenly, he saw a grand house with red gates and white walls. The maid disappeared and then led him inside. Inside, he saw a strong and healthy old woman sitting in the main hall. She said to the scholar, "My son has asked me to pay you three hundred thousand coins for delivering his letter. I will not go against his wishes." When the scholar left, he found the three hundred thousand coins on the shore, all of which were official currency, though slightly worn.

Scholars suspected it to be a spirit or monster, not knowing where it had come from. They feared that using it might not be in accordance with natural law, so they reported the entire story to the authorities. The governor of Henan Province presented the matter, and everyone said, "There is a giant turtle's den in the pond of the Wei King, it must be that one." The emperor issued an order to shoot at it. They obtained dozens of Kunlun people, all armed with swords and spears, who dove into the den and captured dozens of giant turtles of various sizes. Finally, they captured one giant turtle as large as a bed, and the officials all killed it, obtaining thousands of items of money and silk. Five years later, the scholar was selected as a magistrate for the southern region and took up his post. Upon arriving at the East Store in Yangzhou, he suddenly saw Xie Er, who angrily said, "I did not treat you poorly, yet you betrayed me to this extent! My mother and family members all suffered misfortune, all due to your actions." After finishing speaking, he left. The scholar was greatly afraid and did not go to his post for over ten days. Under pressure from his companions, he finally set out. After traveling over a hundred miles, he encountered a wind, and the whole family was lost. People at the time said, "They believed it to be due to the harm caused by Xie Er."

○ A Fisherman from Jingzhou During the middle of the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, a fisherman from Jingzhou caught a blue fish. It was one zhang long, with scales adorned with five-colored round flowers, an extraordinary and beautiful appearance. The fisherman did not recognize it, finding it different from ordinary fish. He did not take it to the market but cooked and ate it himself. Finding it tasteless, he was quite puzzled. Five days later, suddenly, dozens of carriages and riders arrived at the fisherman's place. The fisherman, filled with fear, went out to bow. He heard someone in the carriage angrily say, "My prince went to pay homage to the Eastern Sea. Why did you kill him? I ordered the general to search for my prince, and you killed him again. I will make your body collapse and split, and you will suffer pain like my prince and the general." After he finished speaking, he scolded the fisherman, who fell to the ground, breaking out in a cold sweat. After a long time, he realized what had happened. His family helped him back home, and he then contracted leprosy. Within ten days, his body, mouth, nose, hands, and feet rotted, and his flesh separated. He died after several months.

Liu Yanhui During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Yanhui's father was the governor of Huzhou. A subordinate official found a turtle in the silver mine, which was one foot long, and presented it to the governor. All the officials congratulated him, saying, "Eating this turtle would grant a lifespan of 1,000 years." The governor thanked the official, saying he was not worthy of such a gift, and so he rode his own horse to return the turtle to the silver mine. Ten years later, the governor passed away. Liu Yanhui became an official in Fangzhou, taking his family with him. During his tenure, a flood occurred, submerging the entire area. The family was frightened and did not know what to do. Suddenly, a large turtle appeared and led the way. Liu Yanhui and his family discussed, saying, "The turtle is a divine being, and it is guiding us now." More than thirty people followed the turtle, and it led them through shallow waters for more than ten miles until they reached dry land, escaping the flood. The entire family was surprised and delighted, but they did not know why. That night, Liu Yanhui dreamed of the turtle, which said, "I was in the silver mine, and I received the kindness of the previous governor. Therefore, I am repaying the favor."

Fishing Man of Wu County During the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, a fisherman from Wu County often saw a large turtle on the Tiao River, with four feet each stepping on another turtle as it moved. The fisherman knew it was a sacred turtle and, carrying a stone, threw it at the turtle, hitting and catching it. After some time, he presented the turtle to the state official Pei. Pei summoned a turtle diviner, who said, "This is a turtle of the king, and it should not be used for divining small matters; whatever is divined by it will surely die." Pei was known for his arrogance. At the time, there was a magpie in his courtyard, and its young were still alive. Pei then ordered the diviner to use the turtle to divine the fate of the magpie's young. After several days, a strong wind damaged the magpie's nest, and all the baby magpies died. Soon after, Pei ordered the divination of his servant girl, asking whether she was pregnant with a boy or a girl. The divination said, "She will give birth to a boy." The boy was born, but soon died. Eventually, Pei offered this turtle to the imperial court.

○Qiu Jiafu Tang Dynasty's Qiu Jiafu was from Jingzhao Fu Ping, and his home was in Botaicun. He was going to Luoyang to take the imperial examinations. On his way out of the capital, he met a young man who looked like a nobleman, with a grand entourage and luxurious clothing and horses. Seeing Qiu Jiafu's joyful expression, the young man asked where he was going. Qiu Jiafu replied, "I am going to take the imperial examinations in the capital." The young man said, "I am also going east, and I am pleased to travel with you." Qiu Jiafu asked for his name, and he replied, "My surname is Bai." Qiu Jiafu secretly thought that there were no prominent Bai family members in the court, and he was somewhat suspicious. After a day, the young man said to Qiu Jiafu, "Your donkey is weak, and we cannot travel together." He then offered to have Qiu Jiafu ride in the back carriage. After several days, they arrived at the Temple of Huayue. The young man said to Qiu Jiafu, "I am not an ordinary person; the Heavenly Emperor has sent me to investigate all ghosts and spirits. Now, I must enter the temple to question them. Since you have a connection with me, and the situation has already been decided, can you enter the temple? After the task is done, I will bring you to the capital." Qiu Jiafu reluctantly followed the young man into the temple. He saw a dark, gloomy atmosphere with green curtains, and the setting was well-prepared. There was a desk, and the noble person sat at it. The noble person had Qiu Jiafu sit on a bed supported by bamboo.

Soon after, there was an order to summon the God of the Five Peaks, who arrived and bowed down. The high-ranking official repeatedly scolded him and then ordered his attendants to drag him out. He summoned all the gods of the Guanzhong region, called out their names, and inspected them. Finally, the deity of the Kunming Pool was called up the steps and spoken to, who suggested that Jiafu should stay a little distance away and not be involved in this matter. Jiafu went behind the hall and into the tent and heard a painful sound outside the curtain. He pried open the curtain and saw his wife hanging from a tree in the courtyard, confirmed she was dead, and became devastated. Shortly after, the high-ranking official summoned Jiafu back, noticed his distressed state, asked him why, and Jiafu answered truthfully. The official commanded a careful inspection again, and Jiafu's answer was correct. The official was shocked and said, "Your wife is as if she were my own, how could I not take care of her!" He then issued an order to summon the God of the Five Peaks. When the God of the Five Peaks arrived, he asked, "Why did you take Jiafu's wife from the village of Bouchai and subject her to torture?" The God of the Five Peaks initially did not know. A blue-clothed person, claiming to be a judge, stepped forward and answered, "This matter was summoned by the heavenly court, and now the document is being sent." The official ordered the document to be brought, sealed and stamped it, and it would be taken to the Heavenly Emperor. He had already informed the Heavenly Emperor and turned to the God of the Five Peaks, saying he could immediately release her.

He also told Jia Fu, "Originally I intended to go to the capital, but now it is not possible; you should quickly return to Fuping." Then, he bent his fingers to calculate the journey and said, "It will take four days to arrive, I fear it will be too late, so I will lend you a fine horse. If you think of me later, you can cleanse the room and burn incense, I will surely come." After saying this, he took his leave. After leaving, the divine servant also rode up on a horse, and Jia Fu mounted the horse and immediately arrived at his home, where the family was in a hurry and weeping. Jia Fu went straight in, removed the woman's face and clothing, and waited for her breath, and soon she revived. The whole family celebrated, and the village elders brought wine to congratulate, for several days. After four or five days, the real person returned on a donkey, accompanied by a servant, and the family did not distinguish between them. When they met inside and outside, they immediately merged, and it was then known that the earlier return was his spirit. After a year or more, Jia Fu again went to the capital for the imperial examination. He reached the temple of Mount Hua, encountered the wife of the magistrate of Dengzhou who had suddenly died, her cries were very sad, and he felt compassion for her. He personally went to see Cui, told him to stop crying, and promised to handle the matter, and Cui was very pleased. Jia Fu burned incense and cleaned the room, and mentally invoked the noble person. After a while, he arrived.

After the joyful conversation, they asked about the reason. "This is the work of the Yue deity," he said, "truly it can be kept, and I will deliver two hundred thousand for you. First, seek the money, then act." He then wrote nine talismans, saying: "First burn three talismans, if not cured, burn six more, and it will return." After speaking, he flew away. Jiafu informed Cui with the words of the deity, and Cui dared not disobey. Initially, they burned three talismans, but by morning, there was no cure. They then burned the rest, and soon the person revived. Cui asked his wife, "When I first entered the shop, I suddenly saw a cloud-mother carriage at the steps, with hundreds of strong soldiers, each holding weapons, lined up on both sides. I heard that the king's envoy came to welcome, and I hastily followed. The king saw and was pleased, just about to establish friendship, when suddenly three people came saying: 'The Taiyi God asks why you took the wife of a living person?' The deity was frightened and held a register, saying: 'Heaven has paired her as my wife, not taken her by force.' Yet still refused to send her away. In a moment, five or six great deities came with golden mallets to the king's court, the crowd scattered in terror, leaving only the deity standing under a tree. He begged for mercy for his life, and the king then led him back." Jiafu only then realized that the noble person was the Taiyi God. Afterward, he repeatedly thought and came, for Jiafu exchanged five or six officials, greatly benefiting from it.

A Tale of Liu Men Nu Emperor Gaozong was constructing the Daming Palace. When the Xuanzheng Hall was first completed, every night, dozens of riders were heard roaming around the hall, and all the guards inside the hall saw them. Their clothes and horses were very clean. This continued for more than ten days. Emperor Gaozong then ordered the diviner Liu Men Nu to inquire about the reason. Liu Men Nu replied, "I am the crown prince of King Wu of Chu." Liu Men Nu questioned him, "According to the *Book of Han*, King Wu of Chu conspired to rebel with the seven states, and the Han troops killed him, exterminating his clan and destroying his family. How can there be any surviving descendants?" The other replied, "When King Wu raised his army, he left me in Chang'an. After King Wu was killed, the emperor pitied me, spared my life, and raised me in the palace. Later, I died of illness and was buried here. The emperor pitied me and buried me with a pair of jade fish, which are now in the northeast corner of the main hall. The historians omitted this detail, so it is not recorded in the books." Liu Men Nu said, "Now the emperor is here. How dare you disturb the courtyard?" The other replied, "This was my old residence. Now that I am in the emperor's palace, my movements are quite restricted, and I am very unhappy. I request that I be reburied in a high and open beautiful place, which is truly what I hope for. Be careful not to take away my jade fish." Liu Men Nu reported this to the emperor, who ordered a reburial. When they unearthed the site, they indeed found an ancient tomb. The coffin was already rotten, but beside it were a pair of jade fish, exquisitely crafted. The emperor then ordered a new coffin and buried him outside the imperial garden with proper rites, and the jade fish accompanied him. From this point, the disturbances ceased.

Xue Jin, during the Kaiyuan era, served as the magistrate of Chang'an. He was in charge of managing the palace markets, alternating his duties between the eastern and western markets. One day, while at the eastern market, he saw a carriage. The woman inside had hands as white as snow, and Xue Jin admired her. He sent his attendants with a silver box, standing by the side of the carriage. The woman had her servant ask about the price, and said, "This is the property of the magistrate of Chang'an, Xue. If anyone asks, feel free to give it to him." The woman was very pleased and thanked him. Xue Jin subtly flirted with her, and she became very pleased, saying to him, "I am at Jin Guang Gate. You should visit me." Xue Jin sent his attendants to follow her to her home. The next day, as he passed by, he saw many people on horseback outside her house, hesitating and unable to pass through. Each person turned back, but Xue Jin ordered his attendants to announce his presence at the gate, and he sent his attendants with a card, inviting her to the outer hall. He was seated there, and she said, "Wait for me to get dressed." Xue Jin felt a chill and secretly suspected something was amiss. Soon, she led him into the inner chamber. The curtain was made of blue cloth, and he saw a dimly lit lamp from a distance, which seemed to move closer and then farther away. He suspected it was not a human, but having already sought her out, he decided to see her and then leave. He kept reciting the "Thousand-Armed Guanyin Mantra" in his heart. When he entered, he saw her sitting in the tent, with a silk cloth covering her head. Xue Jin tried to pull it off, and after some effort, it finally fell. He saw the woman's face, which was over a foot long, a deep blue color, and making a sound like a dog. Xue Jin suddenly fainted. His attendants arrived at his room, but they only saw a funeral hall, with Xue Jin inside, completely without gaps or openings. They quickly pushed the wall down, seeing that Xue Jin was dead, with a slight warmth in his chest. They moved him to a shop to rest, and after more than a month, he finally revived.

**Wēi Xiùzhuāng** During the Kaiyuan period, the governor of Huazhou, Wēi Xiùzhuāng, spent his leisure day on the city tower, gazing at the Yellow River. Suddenly, he saw a person in the tower who was about three chi tall, dressed in purple with a red cap, and formally introduced himself by giving his name and paying respects. Wēi Xiùzhuāng knew that this was not an ordinary human. He asked, "Who are you?" The spirit replied, "I am the spirit of the city protector." Wēi Xiùzhuāng then asked, "Why have you come?" The spirit answered, "The spirit of the Yellow River wants to destroy our city to clear the river path, but I refused. Five days after the agreed time, we will engage in a great battle by the riverbank. I am afraid that I cannot withstand the force alone, so I have come to seek your help. If you can provide two thousand men to hold bows and crossbows and assist with specific tasks, we will surely prevail. Your city is at stake; it is up to you to decide." Wēi Xiùzhuāng agreed, and the spirit then disappeared. On the appointed day, Wēi Xiùzhuāng led two thousand elite soldiers to the city walls. Suddenly, the sky became dark and gloomy. Soon after, a white vapor rose more than ten zhang high, and a blue vapor emerged from the tower, intertwining with the white vapor. Wēi Xiùzhuāng ordered the soldiers to shoot arrows and crossbows randomly at the white vapor. The shape of the vapor gradually diminished until it disappeared, leaving only the blue vapor, which wound like the shape of cloud peaks, returning to the hall. Initially, the Yellow River was close to the city, but afterward, it gradually receded, and to this day, it is five or six li away.

○阎庚 张仁亶，幼时贫乏，恒在东都北市寓居。有阎庚者，马牙荀子之子也，好善自喜，慕仁亶之德，恒窃父资，以给其衣食，亦累年矣。荀子每怒庚，云：“汝商贩之流，彼才学之士，于汝何有，而破产以奉？”仁亶闻其辞，谓庚曰：“坐我累君，今将适诣白鹿山，所劳相资，不敢忘也。”庚久为仁亶胥附之友，心不忍别，谓仁亶曰：“方愿志学，今欲皆行。”仁亶奇有志，许焉。庚乃私备驴马粮食同去。六日至陈留，宿逆旅，仁亶舍其内房。房外有床，久之，一客后至，坐于床所。仁亶见其视瞻非凡，谓庚自外持壶酒至。仁亶以酒先属客，客不敢受，固属之，因与合饮。酒酣欢甚，乃同房而宿。中夕，相问行李，客答曰：“吾非人，乃地曹耳。地府令主河北婚姻，绊男女脚。”仁视其衣装，见袋中细绳，方信焉。因求问己荣位年寿，鬼言亶年八十余，位极人臣。

Ren Dan asked about Geng again, and the ghost said, "Geng's fate is poor; he has no position or salary." Ren Dan asked how he could achieve this, and the ghost replied, "Perhaps if he ties up a good woman, and she is compatible with him, he should be able to get it. There is a village in the north of the Yellow River, more than a hundred li from White Deer Mountain, where there is an old woman named Wang, whose appearance is extremely noble. Recently, she has been tied up with someone else, but I will help you untie that and tie this, to make Yan Hou (a title). Just hurry up. When you arrive at that village, there will be heavy rain, and you will be wet. This will be the proof." Then the ghost bid farewell and left. Ren Dan and Geng traveled for six or seven days and arrived at the village. They encountered heavy rain, and their clothes became wet and dirty. They finally reached the west of the village and sought shelter at Wang's house. The door was closed for a long time before the host came out, apologizing to the guests, saying, "There was a little matter at home, so I was delayed; do not be surprised." Ren Dan asked the reason, and the host said, "I already have only one daughter, who was promised to marry the Zhang family in the west village. Today, they paid the dowry, but I did not intend to be single and poor. This is a sign of looking down on me, and I have already decided to cancel the marriage." Ren Dan and others looked at each other and smiled slightly. They stayed for several days, and the host was very happy. Ren Dan then said, "Yan Hou is my cousin, in his prime years, determined to learn, but not yet married." The host excused himself as a farmer, but he showed a joyful expression. Ren Dan firmly asked, and the host finally agreed, giving him a horse, a donkey, and other gifts as a token. After several days, the marriage was completed, and Yan Hou stayed with the Wang family, while Ren Dan went alone, and the host gave him gifts. Several years later, Ren Dan was promoted to the positions of Censor-in-Chief, Governor of Bingzhou, and Chief Censor in charge of political affairs. Later, Geng was repeatedly promoted and finally reached the position of a prefecture.

In the Eastern Capital, in the Thinking of Harmony District, there was a woman named Zhū Qīniáng, a singer and dancer. She had a close relationship with the Wáng family during the Yuan Dynasty period. One year, Wáng fell ill and died, and it had been half a year, but Zhū did not know. In the seventh month of that year, Wáng suddenly appeared at Zhū's place. After some time, as it grew dark, he said, "Can you come with me to the house in the Gentle Spring District?" Zhū wanted to agree, but her daughter, who was famous for singing and dancing, did not want her mother to go. She said, "It's good for the general to stay here, but if he returns, is there something to fear?" Reluctantly, Wáng rode back with Zhū on his horse. When they entered the courtyard, they were as happy as before. The next morning, the Wáng family sent a servant to collect the bedding from the funeral bed, and they found a woman inside. The servant quickly returned to report. The sons of the Wáng family were shocked and came to see. They asked what happened and learned that their deceased father had led them. They mourned deeply for a long time and then sent Zhū back home.

The Goddess of Mount Hua A scholar, preparing for the imperial examination in the capital, passed through the western pass and stayed at an inn in a small room. Soon, several servants of a nobleman announced, "The princess is coming to stay." They erected a tent around the inn and several other shops. The people were initially frightened and unable to move. Soon, the sound of the princess's carriage approached, and everyone got off. The people in the inn hid in their rooms, afraid to come out. The princess bathed in front of the door and ordered her maids to search the room, who said, "It is not suitable for someone to be here." When they saw the scholar, the maids scolded him loudly. The princess ordered him to be brought out and looked at him carefully, saying, "This scholar is quite intelligent and open-minded; it is not right to humiliate or belittle him. Just let him enter the room." After bathing, she summoned him, and they found each other very agreeable. She had her maids bathe and dress him in beautiful clothes, then set up a luxurious room with a red canopy and a bed of brocade, along with other furnishings and decorations for sleeping and play, all of which were extremely lavish and showed great courtesy. The next day, they returned to the capital together. The princess's residence was in Huaiyuan Lane, with hundreds of servants inside and out, her wealth and honor being unmatched at the time. The family called the scholar the prince's son-in-law, and his equipment, clothing, and vehicles were no different from those of the nobility. The scholar's parents lived in their old home, and the princess sent maids to inquire about their well-being and gave them a large sum of money and other valuable items. The scholar's family became very honored and prosperous due to the princess's generosity. They lived together for seven years and had two sons and one daughter.

The princess suddenly said she wanted to arrange a marriage for me. I was greatly surprised and found it strange to hear such words. She said, "I am originally not human; it is not appropriate for me to remain your wife for long. You should also have your own marriage, knowing that it is not a replacement for true love." After that, she also had another marriage, but our visits did not cease. Her family, because she would often stay away for several days without returning, sent someone to wait for her. They saw that I always entered the abandoned house, fearing that I might be bewitched by ghosts or spirits. One day, they got me drunk and then had a sorcerer write a talisman, which they placed in my clothes, covering my entire body. Later, I went back to the princess's house, and her family made me leave, stopping me from entering. I initially did not understand the reason, leaning against the door in sorrow. The princess soon came out and severely scolded me, saying, "You have always been a poor man, and I have elevated you to a position of wealth. This is not unkind to you, so why did you make my family write a talisman to interfere, as if I could not prevent them from killing you?" I looked at myself and realized there was a talisman, and I begged her to forgive me. The princess said, "I understand your feelings, but since the talisman has already been cast, I cannot stay." She called all her children and had them bid farewell to their father. I wept and choked with sobs. The princess ordered her attendants to pack quickly, and we left the city that very day. I asked about her residence and also requested her name and title. The princess said, "I am the third daughter of Mount Hua." After she finished speaking, she bid farewell and left, and I saw her no more.

During the reign of Wu Zetian, Diranjie served as the governor of Ningzhou. The governor's residence was known to be inherently ominous, having claimed the lives of more than ten previous governors. When Diranjie first arrived, the officials reported, "The governor's residence has been cursed, and many of our predecessors have met their end here, and it is in a state of disrepair, overgrown with thorns and in ruins, making it unfit for habitation. We request that you find another residence." Diranjie replied, "As the governor, I cannot abandon my official residence. Why should I seek another?" He ordered the house to be repaired and inhabited it without hesitation. Over several nights, strange and supernatural phenomena occurred, too numerous to recount. Angered by these occurrences, Diranjie addressed the spirits, "I am the governor, and this is my residence. You are the ones who are wrong, and I am the one who is right. Why do you not understand the proper order and instead use evil to oppose righteousness? I have no fear of you; your efforts are merely to change into countless forms." He continued, "If you truly wish to meet me, why not appear properly? If you are a spirit, how dare you interfere with me? I have no fear of you, and your transformations are futile. If you must see me, why not do so with propriety?"
