Shortly after, a figure appeared, dressed in formal attire, and said, "I was an official from a certain dynasty, buried beneath the tree on the west side of the courtyard. My remains have been disturbed by the roots of the tree, causing me great pain. In the past, several governors attempted to inform you, but they died before they could do so. Their spirits could not reach you, and thus they have remained here until now. If you truly wish to help, please reinter my remains. Why do you delay?" After speaking, the figure disappeared. The next day, Diranjie ordered the remains to be exhumed, and they were indeed found as described. He then performed a proper reburial, and the supernatural disturbances ceased.

○Li Guangyuan During the Kaiyuan period, Li Guangyuan served as the magistrate of Guantao. At that time, there was a severe drought, and Li Guangyuan wrote an extensive petition for drought relief. After completing the petition, he suddenly died. After his death, the county reported the petition to the prefecture, and the prefectural司马reviewed and dismissed the petition. The people resented this collectively, with some weeping bitterly, all saying, "If the magistrate had not died, how could this happen?" That night, Li Guangyuan suddenly arrived on a white horse at the drought petition office and told the people, "Even if I am dead, the drought relief will not fail. What kind of person is the司马, daring to obstruct this proposal?" He then went with the people to the司马's residence and said, "The magistrate wants to see you." The司马was greatly frightened and sent someone to apologize. Li Guangyuan scolded, "You are not a human. The drought relief is a matter for the people, not for life or death. It should be completed quickly. Otherwise, I will become a spirit of vengeance." After saying this, he bade farewell to the people and then left. That year, the drought relief was successful, and the people benefited from it.

Wang Jian, a young man recommended for the position of interpreter, entered the capital at the end of the Yuan dynasty. He arrived at the west of the imperial gate and rested under a locust tree. Hearing the sound of an imperial decree, he suddenly saw several horsemen who looked like middle envoys. They told him, "You have been announced as the true interpreter." They then took him away on their horses, and Wang Jian did not know who they were, so he hurriedly followed them. Soon, they arrived at the Temple of the God of Mount Hua, where they placed Wang Jian in a separate room and warned him, "Be careful not to look privately." They then went inside. Wang Jian sat alone and heard the sound of beating with rods and sticks. He cautiously peered forward and saw his wife tied to a tree by her neck and being beaten with rods. Wang Jian stood there sadly and mournfully. The middle envoy came out, saw his distress, and asked him the reason. Wang Jian tearfully and fully explained the matter. The envoy said, "Originally, we wanted to keep you, but since your wife is already dead, it is not reasonable to stay. If there is further delay, after your wife's death, it will not be possible to save her. You should quickly return and open the coffin; this will release your wife and save her." He then ordered his attendants, "Take a postal horse to send Wang the interpreter." Soon, a fox appeared, and Wang Jian had no choice but to ride it and gallop. It was as fast as the wind, and he reached his home in two days. The fox was his soul, and after his soul left, his body lost its voice. When his soul returned home, his family was weeping. Wang Jian ordered the coffin to be opened, and his wife was already alive. She said to Wang Jian, "How did you arrive?" The whole family was happy. After ten days, his body finally arrived. According to the news, "Wang Lang has returned, and he has been mute for more than ten days." The soul said, "Wang Lang has arrived." His family went out to meet him, and then his soul merged with his body.

Li Hào, the Minister of War during the Tang Dynasty, was a man of integrity and righteousness. At the beginning of the reign, a woman visited him, with an attractive appearance and graceful demeanor, and her speech and knowledge were the best of her time. Li Hào dared not accept her. Coincidentally, the Grand Master of Rites Jiang Jiao arrived, and Li Hào gave the woman to him. Jiang Jiao held a grand gathering of high officials, and the woman claimed to be skilled in physiognomy. She saw Zhang Yue and said, "This is the appearance of a prime minister." She then predicted the appearances of all the officials, and her predictions were always accurate. She told Jiang Jiao, "You have the appearance of a prime minister, but you will not live to a ripe old age." When the wine had run out, Jiang Jiao took her to a private room. She used flattery to please him and intended to engage in private relations. The officials took turns to look in. Li Hào was still in the room, and he went to see last. The woman let out a strange sound. Jiang Jiao was startled and fell to the ground. He lit a fire to see, and found white bones under the bed. At the time, people discussed how Li Hào's integrity and righteousness made even the ghosts and spirits fear him.

The Magistrate Li Ba of Qiyang was known for his harsh and cruel nature, showing no kindness to anyone he encountered. All clerks and officials under him suffered from his tyranny. However, he was self-righteous and proud of his own integrity, leaving his wife and children to suffer from poverty. After one term in office, he suddenly died. Upon his burial, there were no mourners at the funeral. His wife often hugged the coffin and wept bitterly, calling out, "What did Li Ba do in life that he leaves his wife and children to suffer such loneliness!" Several days later, the coffin suddenly spoke, saying, "Do not grieve, I will handle my own affairs." That evening, he instructed his family to set up a table and chairs in the hall. Li Ba appeared in spirit form and ordered them to summon the officials. The officials, who had always feared him, rushed to obey the order. Those who saw Li Ba were all terrified and trembling. He then summoned the deputy magistrate and the chief clerk and county constable. Upon their arrival, Li Ba scolded them angrily, saying, "You have no heart, why do you want to kill me? Could I not kill you if I wanted to?" After he finished speaking, they all fell down unconscious. The family members came to the courtyard and knelt down, pleading for their lives. Li Ba said, "Just provide the list of items, and there is no worry that they won't come back to life." The standard was five bundles of silk. As soon as the silk was provided, they came back to life. After they thanked him and left, he told the two officials, "I have always been kind to you, why do you also join the others in wanting to kill me? What good would that do? I will make your families' horses die as proof." In an instant, hundreds of horses collapsed and seemed to be dying. Then, two fine horses were brought in, and the horses returned to normal. He then told the officials, "Although I have always been clear and upright, I am now dead and have to thank you. Can you not even give a little?" The standard was again five pieces of silk. After it was done, he instructed a certain official to bring out a carriage, another to bring out a horse, and the clerks to make repairs. Those who disobeyed would surely die. After one watch of the night, they finally dispersed.

The arrangements for the following day were thoroughly settled, and the family then led the way. At each ritual site, they left offerings. After the offerings were made, they mounted their horses and departed. After traveling more than ten miles, they reached the outskirts of the city, and the figure disappeared. By nightfall, they stopped their horses, and the wife and children wanted to cry. The coffin spoke, saying, "I am here. You all are troubled, but there is no need to cry." The family of the deceased was in the capital, over a thousand miles from Qiyang. At each place they stopped, they were not allowed to cry. After traveling several hundred miles, he suddenly said to his son, "Tonight, do not sleep. Someone intends to steal the good horse, so prepare for it." The family, weary from the journey, did not follow the rules, and that night the horse was indeed stolen. The next morning, he said, "I instructed you to guard against thieves. Why did you sleep so greedily? Although the horse was lost, it will not be gone for long. Near the eastern shop, there is a road to the south. Follow this road for more than ten miles, and there is a dense forest. The horse is tied beneath the trees." They went to retrieve the horse as he said, and found it as described. Upon arriving in the capital, relatives and clan members heard of the strange events and came to mourn and comfort them. Morning and evening, they came to pay their respects, and the coffin responded to all of them, causing no one to remain calm. The gathering became noisy, and the family could not bear the disturbance. He suddenly said to his son, "The guests who come and go are only seeking to see me. You can set up the hall, and I wish to see my relatives." His son followed his instructions, and the people waited in the courtyard. After a long time, he said, "I am here." He ordered the curtains to be drawn. Suddenly, they saw him, with a head as big as a jar, red and bulging eyes, staring at the guests. None could remain standing, and they gradually left. He said to his son, "Human and divine paths are different. This house is not a place where I can stay for long. Quickly bury me in the wild." After he finished speaking, he was no longer seen, and his words came to an end.

#### Seasonal Guangchen In the Hexi region, there was a goddess known as the Lady of the Western Regions. When Seasonal Guangchen was young, he once traveled to the Hexi region and stayed at an inn. One day, while resting during the day, he dreamed of a cloud chariot descending from the sky, accompanied by dozens of attendants, who claimed to be two sisters of the goddess visiting him. Guangchen was initially very delighted, but upon waking and opening his eyes, he secretly saw them still present. Suspecting they were an evil spirit, he drew his sword from his waist and slashed at them. The goddess then scolded him, saying, "We have long wished to visit you, how could you have such a cruel heart!" She then left. Guangchen told the innkeeper about the incident, and the innkeeper said, "This is the Lady of the Western Regions." Guangchen then went himself to buy wine and meat for a sacrifice to atone for the past mistake, but the goddess remained unhappy. Therefore, Guangchen wrote a poem on the wall, but the ink did not form words. The next night, he dreamed the goddess came again, especially angry, saying, "I will ensure you never receive a fief or title in your lifetime." #### Zhang Shougui Zhang Shougui, the military governor of Youzhou, was young when he served as the main general of Hexi, guarding the Yumen Pass. His soldiers were all diligent, brave, and skilled in combat, and each time they went on reconnaissance, they often engaged in looting. A monk from the Western Regions traveled from the Western Capital to make over twenty loads of robes to return to the Great Land of India. He was accompanied by over twenty disciples. The scouts thought they were carrying silk and brocade, so they robbed them, killing all of them. When they reached the monk, they swung their swords and clubs but could not harm him. They felt very regretful and expressed their remorse in front of the monk, wailing and weeping. After a long time, the monk said, "These people in their past lives were all guilty of failing their commanders, so they are only wrongfully dead as the monk's ghosts."

However, you, as a garrison commander, hold a high salary and position, and will later become a military governor, a high-ranking official, and so on. These people cannot do anything to you! You can inform the garrison commander to accumulate good fortune. Afterward, in a few years, the garrison commander will have a minor misfortune, but there is also a way to avoid it. The messenger returned and informed Zhou Gui, who then kept the monk in his service for several years before letting him go. Later, Zhou Gui, accompanied by twenty-five of his followers, went to explore the enemy in the Iranshan Mountains. Suddenly, thousands of nomadic cavalry appeared. Zhou Gui was unable to resist, so he dismounted, removed his saddle, and showed a relaxed demeanor. As the enemy cavalry approached, Zhou Gui said to his attendants, "What shall we do? If it cannot be avoided, we must fight according to the situation." Suddenly, hundreds of cavalry with red flags rushed forward to fight. Zhou Gui followed, penetrating one side, and soon they all emerged, with the enemy daring not to pursue. The general under the red flag said to Zhou Gui, "I am Han's Li Guang, knowing you are in trouble, so I came to save you. In the future when you are wealthy and powerful, do not forget me." After speaking, he disappeared. Zhou Gui eventually became the military governor of Youzhou and a high-ranking official.

A Scholar from the Anyi Ward During the late reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, in the Anyi Ward of the Eastern Capital (Luoyang), there was a scholar who, one night, closed his door and organized his books. Suddenly, through a gap in the door, he saw a person's head appear. He shouted to ask who it was, and the figure replied, "I am a ghost, and I wish to visit you for a while." The ghost then invited the scholar to come out. The scholar followed the ghost outside, where the ghost drew a "十" (cross) on the ground and led him forward, exiting the ward and reaching the temple gate area. The scholar said, "If we encounter a monk or nun, we will not be able to pass." The ghost reassured him, saying, "Just follow me, and there will be no trouble." Soon, they arrived inside the Dingding Gate. The ghost carried the scholar through a gap in the door, and they proceeded to the Five Bridges. Along the way, they came across a tomb with a skylight emitting firelight. The ghost carried the scholar to the side of the skylight, where they saw a woman crying over an ill child, while her husband was dozing nearby. The ghost descended and covered the fire with his hand. The woman, frightened, shouted at her husband, "Our child is on the brink of death; how can you sleep so soundly? There is a malevolent presence covering the fire. You must get up and light the lamp." The husband got up and added more candles. The ghost then avoided the woman and suddenly took a cloth bag to carry the child, who was still able to move inside the bag. The ghost carried the child away. They returned to the skylight and descended to the ground, entering the Dingding Gate again. When they reached the scholar's home, the ghost thanked him, saying, "I obey orders from the underworld to take the child. The matter requires a living person as a companion, so I have troubled you. You should be forgiven." With that, the ghost departed. The scholar had initially followed the ghost, and wherever they stopped, he marked the ground with a "十" (cross). The next day, he led his brothers to verify the marks, and all the "十" (cross) symbols were confirmed. They then went to the house where the child was lost, and the story was the same.

Péi Shèng Dong Shiyuan said: "The magistrate of Yixing, Péi Shèng, was taking a nap during the day when suddenly a ghost led him away, causing his soul to leave his body. The ghost said it was escorting a child. Upon arriving at the child's home, the parents were lying with the child, and there were Buddhist rituals being performed. The ghost said, 'Because of the Buddha, the living person has arrived.' With a wave of its hand, the ghost caused the parents to fall asleep. The ghost then commanded Péi Shèng to carry the child out of the bed. As he carried the child, the child's throat made a sound, and the parents woke up in surprise. The ghost then led Péi Shèng out. Péi Shèng tried hard to invite the ghost back to his home, but the ghost pushed him back into his body, and he regained consciousness."

The Guest of Lixiang During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong in the Tang dynasty, there was a scholar whose family was poor. He went to beg in the region north of the Yellow River. Wherever he went, nobody responded to him, and he traveled around until he reached Lixiang. By that time, it was already late in the day, and the journey ahead still seemed far. Suddenly, he saw a grand house by the roadside. Planning to stay for the night, he went up to knock on the door. After a long while, a servant came out. The guest said, "It is getting late, and I cannot reach my destination. May I stay at your inn? Is that acceptable?" The servant replied, "Please allow me to inform the young master." He then went inside. Shortly after, the sound of dragging shoes was heard, and a handsome man dressed in formal attire came out. He had a graceful and distant demeanor, appearing noble and unique. He ordered the guest to be invited in, and they bowed to each other in greeting. The host said, "Are you not tired from your journey? I have a humble dwelling that is not worthy of your presence." The guest was secretly surprised by the host's unusual behavior and wanted to investigate further, so they went into the guesthouse together. The host was quite eloquent and could speak clearly about events from the time of Qi and Zhou, recounting them as if he had seen them with his own eyes. When asked for his name, he replied, "I am Xun Jihe of Yǐngchuān. My ancestors served in office and settled here." He ordered wine and delicacies to be set out, which were all very refined but not very flavorful. After a short while, he had a bed prepared in the guesthouse and invited the guest in, ordering a maid to attend to him for the night. When the maid became friendly, the guest asked, "What is the young master's current position?" The host replied, "I am currently serving as the chief clerk of the Princess of He. Please be careful not to mention it." Soon after, the guest heard sounds of shouting and pain from outside, so he secretly peered through the window.

One evening, the guest saw the host seated on a trundle bed, with lamps and candles arranged around. In front of him stood a disheveled and nude figure, surrounded by birds pecking at his eyes, causing blood to flow onto the ground. The host's face turned red with anger and he said, "Do you dare to harm me again?" The guest asked, "Who is this person?" The host replied, "Why must you know more about this?" The guest persisted in his questioning, and the host said, "He was the magistrate of Liyang, who enjoyed hunting and often chased animals, trespassing on my walls. For this reason, he was punished." The guest secretly remembered this. The next morning, when he looked back, he found a large tomb. He asked someone nearby, and they said it was the tomb of Master Xun. When the guest arrived in Liyang, the magistrate indeed excused himself with an eye disease. The guest said, "I can treat it." The magistrate was pleased and invited the guest inside, explaining the situation to him. The magistrate said, "That is true." He then secretly ordered the village chief to gather several ten thousand bundles of firewood and pile them up beside the wall. One day, the magistrate led his officials and set fire to the pile, burning the tomb and changing its location. The magistrate's eyes immediately recovered. He generously thanked the guest but did not tell him the truth. Later, when the guest returned to the place, he saw a person with a burned and rotten face, dressed in rags, squatting among thorns and brambles. The guest approached directly, but did not recognize him. The person asked, "Do you remember staying here before?" The guest was surprised and asked, "Why are you here?" The person replied, "I was suffering from the magistrate before, but I also knew it was not your original intention. My fate has run out." The guest felt very ashamed and regretful, and set up a simple offering. He burned the person's old clothes and gave them to him. The ghost happily accepted and then left.

A Man of the Ministry of Rites, Li Jiongxiao, had always been close friends with the monk Ling Zhen of Qingchansi. Several years after Li Jiongxiao's death, Ling Zhen suddenly saw two officials who brought a warrant to summon him. They hurriedly urged him to take the road, and he died suddenly. He arrived at a place that resembled an official office, and shortly after, he was invited to pay respects. A man in red clothing with a silver seal greeted him, and Ling Zhen suspected that he was not meant to die. The man in red said, "It was a mistake to summon you, Master. You should return." He ordered the officials to send Ling Zhen back. Ling Zhen wanted to take the old road, but the officials said, "This road cannot be taken; you must take the northern road instead." So, he took the northern road. The road was desolate, and Ling Zhen was quite unhappy. After traveling for dozens of miles, he arrived at a beautiful city. The gatekeeper greeted him and said, "It is convenient to see the general." They led him in. He saw a man in purple clothing sitting in the hall, who looked very much like Li Gong.

谓曰：“贞公那得远来？”灵贞乃知正是。因延升阶，叙及平旧。临别握手，曰：“欲与阇梨论及家事，所不忍言。”遂忽见泪下，灵贞固请之，乃曰：“弟子血祀将绝，无复奈何！可报季友等，四时享奠，勤致丰洁。兼为写《法华经》一部，是所望也。”即挥涕诀。灵贞遂苏，具以所见告。诸子及季友，素有至性焉，为设斋及写经。唯斋损独怒曰：“妖僧妄诞，欲诬玷先灵耳。”其后竟与权梁山等谋反伏诛，兄弟流窜，竟无种嗣矣。 He said, "How could Master Zhen have come from afar?" Ling Zhen then realized the truth. He invited him to ascend the steps and they discussed past events. As they were about to part, he took his hand and said, "I wish to discuss family matters with you, but I cannot bear to speak of them." Suddenly, tears welled up in his eyes. Ling Zhen firmly asked him, and he replied, "My ancestral sacrifices are about to cease, and there is nothing I can do! You should inform Ji You and others to offer regular sacrifices, diligently providing rich and pure offerings. Also, write a copy of the *Lotus Sutra*, that is my hope." He then wiped away his tears and bid farewell. Ling Zhen then awoke and recounted what he had seen. Ji You and the others, who had always been sincere, set up a Buddhist ritual and copied scriptures. Only Zhai Sun was angry, saying, "The false monk is spreading deceit, trying to defame our ancestors." Later, he conspired with Quan Liangshan and others to rebel and was executed, his brothers exiled, and the family line ended. ○琅邪人 A man from Langya passed through Rencheng and stayed overnight outside the city walls. The host was very happy to see him and prepared a variety of fruits. He took out a small knife with a rhinoceros horn handle from his pocket, intending to cut a pear, when the host suddenly changed color and died abruptly. He realized that the host was a ghost from a tomb and was very afraid, but he also used the knife to protect himself. He looked and saw a hole beside the tomb, illuminated by the sun, and inside he saw a coffin that had rotted away, with a fruit tray made of leaves. He crawled out and asked the people around, but no one recognized this tomb.

During the Tianbao era, Pei Hui from Hedong was the nephew of a county magistrate in Henan. Once, he walked alone by the side of an estate. On his way, he saw a woman with a strikingly beautiful appearance, gazing with a radiant and captivating demeanor. After a while, Pei Hui asked, "Why are you walking alone?" She replied, "Just now, the maidservants had some small transactions and were delayed in coming back, so I went out to wait for them." Pei Hui, known for his talent and wit, flirted with her using elegant and refined language. Initially, she did not change her expression but engaged in several rounds of toasting and conversation. They eventually arrived at her home, where he was invited to enter. The room was grand and magnificent. Upon entering, he heard an old maid servant angrily say, "Why did you let someone else come? Is there such a thing in the world of propriety?" The woman explained that there were distinguished guests at the door, and many family members came to inquire. Soon, the old maid servant emerged, saw Pei Hui bowing and apologizing, and noted his actions were deeply imbued with the manners of a gentleman. Shortly after, lamps were lit and curtains were set up, and he was invited to sit down. Several attendants, each with a beautiful appearance, moved and behaved very gracefully, with fragrant and pleasant scents filling the air. Soon, a young lady was ordered to come out and said, "Why should you, Sir Pei, avoid us?" The woman came out and did not go back in. Pei Hui alone was happy to stay, but just then, he felt his stomach was full and got up to go to the toilet. The ancient sword he held could ward off evil. After using the toilet, he took the paper wrapping the sword and suddenly saw the sword's light shining brightly. He grasped it and tried to turn back, but no longer saw the room, people, or objects. Looking around, he found himself on a lonely grave surrounded by thorny bushes. So he shouted loudly, and his family recognized him and held candles to search for him. More than a hundred steps away from the estate, he stared blankly and unable to speak, and after a long time, he finally realized what had happened.

During the Tianbao period, Li Tao of Longxi was residing temporarily in Xinzheng. He often slept in his room, and while sleeping, someone shook him. Li Tao was startled awake and saw a maid dressed in a skirt. She had a beautiful appearance. Li Tao asked, "Why suddenly came here?" The maid replied, "Lady Zheng wants to visit you." Soon after, a fragrant aroma filled the air, and a beautiful woman emerged from the northwest corner of the wall, came to the bed, and bowed twice. Li Tao knew it was a ghost and did not speak at first. The woman felt ashamed and withdrew, and the maid scolded several times, saying, "Country bumpkin, do you treat people like this? Making my lady feel immense shame!" Li Tao was pleased by her beauty and also felt surprised. He then lied and said, "Where is Lady Zheng? I have not seen her yet. Can you call her again?" The maid replied that she would come, and also said, "Lady Zheng values your old connection and will come again. Suddenly returning as before, you can be hospitable." When she arrived, Li Tao got up from the bed to show respect, invited her to sit beside him, and soon they were close. Lady Zheng's beauty was unparalleled, and Li Tao greatly enjoyed her. They stayed together for over ten days. Li Tao's mother personally looked in and repeatedly sent people to call Li Tao.

Tao feared that his resolve would be hindered and also never left. His wife said, "The master has summoned you; why not go? Are you not going to get me in trouble?" Tao then went to his mother, who wept and told Tao, "You carry on the family line; how could you have a ghostly wife?" Tao replied, "I will change." From then on, he stayed on, not leaving for half a year. Later, Tao participated in the selection process and went to the capital city, leaving his wife in the house. Afterward, Tao fell seriously ill, and the ghostly wife was in the room, telling her maid, "Master Li is gravely ill now; what shall we do about it? We should go to visit and inquire." They reached Tongguan Pass, where they were blocked by the ghostly gatekeeper and could not pass for several days. Coincidentally, Tao's elder brother also went for the selection, entered the pass, and the ghost was able to follow through. That night, they arrived at Tao's place, met with joy. Tao asked how she came to be there, and she said, "I saw you were gravely ill, so I came to check on you." She mixed the medicine she usually carried and gave it to Tao to drink, and Tao's illness soon recovered. That year, he was selected as the magistrate of Lintan and arrived at the residence with his wife and others. A few days later, when he was about to take office, the ghost refused to go. When asked why, she said, "Our karmic connection has ended; we cannot leave again." They parted with sorrow, and from then on, they were separated forever.

The Daughter of the Family Lu in Changzhou The magistrate of Changzhou County, Lu, was a poor man. On the third day of the third lunar month, all the family members went to the Tiger Hill Temple. The daughter, aged fifteen or sixteen, could not go because she had no clothes, so she stayed home with a maid. After the parents left, the daughter, feeling sad, threw herself into a well and died. The parents were deeply moved by this and wept for several days, then temporarily buried her in Changzhou County. About a year later, a man named Lu visited his grandmother. His grandmother's house was near the temporary burial place, and he passed by the burial site. A young maid followed him and said, "The lady wishes to meet you briefly." The man had no choice but to follow her to her home. The house was small and modest, and the lady was dressed elegantly, with a beautiful and gentle appearance. She asked, "Are you not a commoner from Changzhou? I am the daughter of the magistrate Lu, but I am not a human; I am a ghost. I wish to ask you to convey a message to my father: the man Li Baishi from Lin Dun has proposed marriage. I am a virgin, and it is morally difficult for me to marry myself. Please tell my father, and if he agrees, I will convey the message to you." The man stayed in the burial place. A short while later, the official of the district passed by the burial site and saw a piece of clothing and a belt sticking out. He looked inside and saw a woman. He reported this to the magistrate, who went there himself and had the wall broken to retrieve the man, placing him in the main hall. After a few days, the man could speak and asked how he had come to be there. The man replied with the words of the lady, and the magistrate sighed. He then sent someone to check on Li Baishi from Lin Dun, and indeed, he was still alive and well. Initially, the magistrate did not believe it, but after a few more days, the man fell ill and died after a few more days. The whole family was filled with sorrow and regret, and they eventually performed a posthumous marriage for the daughter with Li Baishi.

### Yang Zhun Yang Zhun was a man from Songcheng, a member of a distinguished family. One day, he went out into the countryside and saw a woman whose beauty was exceptionally striking. She tempted him, and they engaged in sexual intercourse in the open. After more than a month, she would come to his room and again tried to take him away, but he refused. Suddenly, he suffered unbearable heart pain and said, "If it's absolutely necessary, I will follow you wherever you go, but why go to such trouble to take care of me?" His illness then healed. He then followed the woman for more than ten miles to her house. The courtyard and buildings were clearly defined, but the doors and windows were small. The woman prepared food for Yang Zhun, and every time he ate, he finished the entire bowl. He found it strange, but he did not know it was a ghost. Later, he realized this. Every time Yang Zhun left, she would close the door and lie motionless on the bed, remaining that way for six or seven days before coming back to life. This happened for two or three years. Yang's brother told him, "As a son, you should continue the family line. Why suddenly marry a ghost?" Yang felt ashamed and fearful, left home, and wore the black robe of a Buddhist monk. The ghost then no longer came. Later, Yang returned to his former attire, was selected as a county magistrate, and married a relative's daughter. A year later, while handling official documents in the hall, he suddenly saw the woman enter through the door, looking very angry. Yang was terrified and went down the steps to beg for his life. The woman said, "It's time to let you die." She fiercely attacked him, and Yang then fell ill and died.

○ Wang Yi In Linru County, there was an official water channel store. About half a mile north of the store, Wang Yi of Li's estate, on his way to a market, passed by the gate of the estate. From a distance, he saw a young woman who appeared to be around fifteen or sixteen years old. She awaited him with joy, and he sent a servant girl to convey a message. Wang Yi wandered under the shade of a locust tree until dusk, then visited the estate to request lodging. The host welcomed him warmly and provided a generous meal. After midnight, a servant girl came and said, "The night is not yet over. We should wait with a light." The young woman arrived shortly after, and they engaged in intimate conversation. After the matter was completed, the young woman suddenly fell ill. Wang Yi said, "We were strangers before, but I am fortunate to have been invited. Now that we have shared this moment, our bond is significant. What is there to be unsatisfied with?" The young woman replied, "I did not hold back, but just as I was leaving the door closed, I climbed over the wall, and at the corner of the wall, there was an iron rake. Its teeth pierced my foot, causing excruciating pain that ran through to my heart, unbearable." She then showed him her foot. After finishing her words, she bade farewell and returned, saying, "I should be dead by now. If you have feelings for me, when you return, visit me to comfort my spirit." Later, Wang Yi returned to his hometown after obtaining an official position. On his way, he heard that the young woman had died. Privately, with the servant girl, he brought wine and food to the burial place to offer sacrifices and burst into tears. Soon, he saw the young woman come out from the burial place, and she fell to the ground and died. The servant girl saw Wang Yi's spirit enter the burial place with the young woman, and the two families arranged a marriage in the afterlife.

Wei Li** Wei Li, during the Tianbao era (742-756) of the Tang Dynasty, was the magistrate of Xin'an County. He had a young daughter around ten years old. On his way to take office, he traveled to Yangzhou. The girl wished to buy a lacquered back golden flower mirror. Wei Li said, "My journey to take office was arduous; how could I get such an item? I will wait until I take office and then buy it for you." A year later, the girl died, and Wei Li no longer remembered past events. At the end of his term, he carried the coffin back north. Docking at the riverside in Yangzhou, the girl, accompanied by a maid, held money to buy a mirror. Travelers saw her face was very beautiful, and she looked like a daughter of a wealthy family. They competed to offer to sell her a mirror. A young man, fair-skinned and charming, gave her a lacquered back golden flower mirror, over one foot in diameter. Another person said, "There is a mirror better than this, only costs three thousand." The young man then reduced the price by two thousand coins.

A woman lingered, her passion and spirit intertwined, and soon after, she departed. The young man had intentions to indulge in her, so he sent someone to follow her to her residence. Shortly after, they arrived at a shop, where they only obtained three sheets of yellow paper. The young man took the paper to the boat of Li, saying, "Just now, a young lady came here with money to buy a mirror, entered this boat, and now it has become paper money." Li replied, "I have only one daughter, who has been dead for several years. What was the appearance of the one you saw?" The young man described her attire, color, and appearance in detail. Li's husband and wife wept, and the daughter indeed matched the description. They then led the young man into the boat to search, but initially found nothing. Li's mother cut nine sheets of yellow paper and placed them on the table beside the coffin. Upon checking, they discovered that three sheets were missing. Everyone was surprised, so they opened the coffin again and found the mirror inside. All were filled with grief and lamentation. The young man said, "The money no longer matters." He explained his original intention and, as a gesture, gave ten thousand coins to hold a memorial service for the girl. ○ Hejian Liu Biejia Liu Biejia of Hejian often said, "Without women in the world, how could one find satisfaction." Later, he came to the Tonghua Gate of Western Capital and saw a woman of beautiful appearance in a carriage, whom he found charming. He followed her to her residence, which was located behind Zisheng Temple. The woman lingered for several nights, and both of them enjoyed themselves. Liu Biejia did not feel anything unusual. However, during the middle of the night, it was extremely cold, and despite the thick bedding, his body still felt cold, which made him secretly wonder. The next day, just before dawn, he suddenly found that the woman and her house had disappeared, and he himself was lying under several layers of fallen leaves in a desolate garden. As a result, he contracted a chronic illness.

Wang Xuanzhi, a man from Gaomi, was young and had a beautiful appearance. He served as the magistrate of Qichun and, upon completing his term, returned to his hometown, where he lived west of the city gate. One evening, as he lingered outside his gate, he saw a woman coming from the west, about to enter the city. She was exceptionally beautiful, around eighteen or nineteen years old. The next day, when he went out again, he saw her once more. This happened several times, and she always came at dusk. Wang playfully asked her, "Where do you live? Why do you come here at dusk?" The woman smiled and replied, "My home is near the southern hill, and I have to come to the city for some matters." Wang tried to court her, and she responded gladly. He then invited her to stay overnight, and they became very close. The next morning, she left, but she returned every few nights, eventually coming every night. Wang's affection for her grew stronger, and he asked her, "Since your home is so close, would you allow me to visit?" She answered, "My home is very small and poorly furnished, not suitable for entertaining guests. Moreover, I live with my deceased brother's daughter, and it might raise suspicions." Wang believed her and his affection for her became even more intense. She was particularly skilled in needlework, and all of Wang's clothes were made by her. Everyone who saw her work admired and praised her. There was also a beautiful servant girl who often followed her. Even during the day, the servant girl did not leave her side. Wang asked, "Is your sister-in-law perhaps looking forward to something?" The woman replied, "Why should I forcefully involve myself in other family matters?" This continued for a year. One night, she suddenly came, looking very unhappy, and she only cried.

The king asked her, saying, "I have been excessively favored and received with kindness, yet you are now leaving me, what can I do?" She then wept so intensely that her sobs could not be stopped. The king was startled and asked the reason. The woman said, "Are you going to make it difficult for me? I was originally the daughter of a former magistrate of Gaomi, married to a man surnamed Ren. My husband treated me coldly, and my parents pitied me, calling me back. Later, I fell ill and died, and was buried here. Now my family is coming to fetch my body, and I will leave tomorrow." The king, having been fond of her, no longer harbored any reservations or jealousy, and thus he was deeply saddened. He asked, "Will you be able to arrive tomorrow by what time?" She replied, "By evening." They spent the night talking without sleeping. The next morning, at the time of their parting, the woman left a golden cup and a pair of jade rings as a gift. The king responded with an embroidered robe. They held hands, wiping away tears, and parted ways. The next day, at the appointed time, the king went to the southern hill to see her. Indeed, there were family members there to fetch her body. When the coffin was opened, the woman's appearance remained unchanged, her makeup still intact. Inside the coffin was a box of embroidered clothing, but the golden cup and jade rings she had given were missing. The family only then realized something was amiss. The king then approached and explained the situation, showing them the golden cup and jade rings. They held them in their hands and wept bitterly, asking, "Who was your brother's daughter?" They replied, "She was the second daughter of our second son, who died at the age of ten and was also buried beside her. The wooden figure is also in the coffin." The woman's appearance was exactly like that of the attendant, and the king then stood before the coffin, wept bitterly, and bid farewell. Those around him were all deeply moved. Afterwards, he became distracted and fell ill, recovering after several days. However, every time he thought of her, he could not eat or sleep.

○ Zhu Ao Zhu Ao, the Deputy Governor of Hangzhou, once lived in seclusion on the Shaoshi Mountain in Henan Province. In the early days of the Tianbao era, Li Shu, the County Magistrate of Yangzhi, was at Yue Temple and sent a rider to invite Zhu Ao. Riding his horse swiftly, Zhu Ao galloped ahead, with his followers trailing behind. They gradually approached the foot of the Xiaoyi Temple. It was extremely hot weather, and Zhu Ao saw a young woman in a green robe, around fifteen or sixteen years old, with a very beautiful appearance. Zhu Ao thought she might be a servant or slave, and was also surprised that she was carrying a warm garment during the hot summer. He rode his horse to ask her, but the woman smiled without speaking and walked into the temple. Zhu Ao dismounted, but found no one inside. He then watched a painting on the wall. He saw the green-robed woman again, the same one he had encountered on the road. He sighed for a long time and told the story to Li Shu and others at the temple, who were particularly amazed by it. That night, after he went to sleep, he dreamed that the woman came to him. Holding the blanket, he felt delighted, and his vital energy overflowed, and this happened repeatedly over several nights. The Daoist priest Wu Yun of Mount Song wrote a talisman to ward her off, but it did not work. Wu Yun also tried to control her with Daoist techniques, but it did not work either. On another day, Zhu Ao stayed in the room of Daoist priest Cheng.

When the law is followed with purity, the gods will not come. After passing the imperial examinations in Henan Province, Ao Hou traveled to Weinan County with the magistrate Chen Cha Wei to visit the Taoist priest Cheng Gu Shen. The priest prepared dishes of yams and fresh sesame, without relying on lotus flowers, and they lingered in pleasant conversation until dusk before returning. About five miles from the Shaoshi Mountains, they suddenly encountered dark clouds surging up from the Songshan Mountains, accompanied by lightning. In a moment, darkness fell, and heavy rain poured down. Ao Hou, Chen Cha Wei, and one attendant lay down under a tree near a deep ravine. After a while, a strange light appeared, different from the sun and moon. Suddenly, in the light, there were pine forests everywhere. They saw several heavenly maidens holding a dance platform, spanning several miles, set up on the pine trees. Dozens of heavenly maidens, looking like celestial beings, danced on the platform, accompanied by various deities, including Guanyin. After two dances, it seemed like half a day had passed. Then, a few people, resembling clowns or jesters, rolled up the dance platform and left, and the sky and earth turned dark again, and no one was seen. Ao Hou and others, clinging to each other, finally reached their house at midnight.

○裴虬** The pavilion of Lu Quxia, a recluse from Suzhou, was once the residence of Dai Yong, a Song Dynasty official. At the end of the Tianbao era, Pei Qiu from Hedong often stayed at this pavilion as a guest and suddenly died. After some time, he came to his senses and recounted what happened: At first, a man came and said, "Lord Dai has summoned you." Pei Qiu asked, "Who is Lord Dai?" The man replied, "Do you know Dai Yong, the Attendant of the Imperial Palace?" Pei Qiu said, "I do." The man continued, "The one calling you now is him." When Pei Qiu arrived, he saw Dai Yong, who asked to marry his daughter to Pei Qiu. Pei Qiu said, "I am already married." Dai Yong responded, "Humans and gods follow different paths, why should you worry?" Pei Qiu replied, "I am already married into a family with official status, it would not be appropriate to be your son-in-law." After some time, there were exchanges of words. Dai Yong realized that Pei Qiu could not be persuaded and released him. Pei Qiu survived. **○赵佐** Zhao Zuo, at the end of the Tianbao era, was appointed as a student at the Four Gates Academy of the Imperial College. He often fell ill in his sleep and vaguely, two officials in yellow robes restrained him. They arrived at the west side of the Viewing Wind Pavilion in the Hot Springs Palace, where there was another office building, and the officials led him in. He first saw a person who looked like a king, and Zhao Zuo bowed before him. The king asked Zhao Zuo, "Do you recognize me?" Zhao Zuo replied that he did not. The king said, "Have you heard of Emperor Qin Shi Huang? I am he. You built many palaces near my home, and every time you played music and dancing, it was extremely luxurious. Truly, you were a beautiful king. So I also built a tower to enjoy the entertainment." He then asked many questions about human affairs. He also asked Zhao Zuo, "Humanity will soon experience great turmoil, you should find a way to avoid trouble, and do not stay in the capital for long." After speaking, he had someone send Zhao Zuo back.

The Official from Qi Prefecture An official from the Qi Prefecture once came to the capital for official business and stayed at a lodging place called Xingdouli. Suddenly, he saw two people and one headless person come and say that the king's order summoned him. The official recognized them as ghosts and asked, "What duties do you have in the underworld?" They replied, "We are in charge of handling matters." The official said, "Fortunately, we have similar tastes and interests. Can you help me? If the matter is resolved, I will offer ten thousand sheets of paper money." The ghosts agreed, saying, "After five days, if you do not come again, it means the matter is resolved. You can burn the money at Tiantmen Street." After five days, the official burned the paper money and moved to a new residence in Chongrenli. Later, when the matter in the capital was resolved, he returned west to the Qi Prefecture. On the way, he passed through a place called Apricot Tree Inn and met the same two people again. They asked, "Where did you come from? We recently visited the old place and found that the matter you were handling has been resolved. We are troubled to offer the money at a low price, but the authorities have already issued a permanent exemption document. Unless the time of death arrives, there will be no more illness."

The Wang Family of Junyi The Wang family of Junyi were scholars. When the mother of one of them was buried, her son-in-law, Pei, got drunk and entered the tomb, lying behind the coffin. The family did not know this and sealed the tomb. After several days, Pei was not seen, and his family accused the Wang family of killing him, leading to a lawsuit. The Wang family had no such thing; the entire family was worried, fearing he might be in the tomb on the day of burial. They opened the tomb and found him, barely alive. They fed him porridge, and after several days, he recovered. It is said that on the night of the burial, the wine was just starting to wear off, and he had no way to get out. He glanced around furtively and saw many people, a hall made of cypress wood, and a very beautiful house. The Wang family's deceased ancestors of all ages gathered. The ghosts were very surprised to see Pei, and one ghost asked, "Why don't you kill him?" The wife's mother said, "My daughter is young and depends on this, how can you want to kill him?" After a bitter struggle, he was spared. They saw a long table with delicious food, and music and singing filled the air. Soon, they heard someone say, "Call Pei." Pei was afraid and did not dare to get up, and he heard the maids singing in a circle, the lyrics of which were, "The cypress hall is newly built, the music is endless, come back and go around Pei." One maid named Nonghua burned his nose with a paper candle, causing a wound, and the pain was unbearable, so he got up and bowed to everyone. The ghosts repeatedly ordered Pei to sing and dance. When he was hungry, he asked for food, and the wife's mother said, "The food of the ghosts is not good," and ordered someone to bring food from the bottle. For several nights, the servants and maids were all spirit objects, and they no longer had their original forms.

During the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, Zhang Qiu Jiao served as the Military Governor of Jian Nan. After several years, he went to court. In Shu, there was a man named Zhang Yecha who appeared like a madman but whose predictions often came true. As Zhang Qiu Jiao was about to leave, he called Zhang Yecha and asked him, "If I stay in Shu, will I have an endless life? If I must go to court, will I not see good fortune?" Zhang Qiu Jiao was initially very frightened but after some time, he said, "How could such things be possible?" He then set out. Upon reaching Hanzhou, he entered the inn and fell off his horse, dying. Only his heart was slightly warm. The Governor of Pengzhou, Li Xian, ordered the Magistrate of Luoyang, Ma, to send medicine and wine, along with care and attention. Luoyang is fifty li from Hanzhou. Upon receiving the order, Ma immediately set out and arrived at the inn in Hanzhou, where he went to Zhang Qiu Jiao's place and suddenly collapsed and died. Later, Zhang Qiu Jiao revived and said that in the underworld, he met with the Magistrate of Luoyang. The Magistrate of Luoyang also died and went to his home.

The family was astonished and said, "Just now you received an order, why did you return so hastily?" Without speaking, he looked up to heaven and sighed deeply. His wife asked again, "Where are the attendants and why don't you hold the tablet?" Ma did not speak, but quickly waved them away. He then wept and said, "I have already taken the place of the Great Envoy Zhang Qiu to die. Just now in the underworld, I had a difficult discussion. In the underworld, everything is decided by others, and there is nothing to be done about it. Thinking to myself that I have only been in office for a short time, a distant guest who is lonely and weak, so I returned to take leave." He raised his voice and wept bitterly, then said to his wife, "Do not grieve, I have taken his place to die, and he will also receive deep sympathy. Do not worry, you will be able to return home. But now in such haste, death and life are forever separated, and this is my only regret." He finished speaking and then disappeared. The children initially were still in doubt, but soon they saw the body being carried back on a bed. The next day, he returned to Chengdu, gave the Ma family five million coins as a gift, and also ordered Pengzhou to give five million coins as a gift, and returned his salary and rank for four years.

During the late Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, when An Lushan rebelled, Li Shuji from Zhao County fled southward with his wife through Wu Pass to Xiangyang. On the way, his wife and two sons died. Li Shuji wandered in Jingchu for a long time. After An Lushan occupied Chang'an, his wife's aunt, a widow, could not escape and remained in the city, enduring great hardship. She employed a servant girl named Luo Nü to gather firewood outside the city. From afar, Luo Nü saw a calf running very fast, followed by a man in purple clothes on horseback. A woman in the carriage repeatedly called out to Luo Nü. As they drew closer, the woman asked, "Do you recognize me?" Luo Nü, surprised, replied, "Where has Master Li gone? Why are you traveling alone?" The woman then wept and said, "We traveled to Xiangyang, where Master Li and my two sons were all killed by the rebels. Because of hunger, I carried my young children and married this man following the carriage." She then met her aunt with Luo Nü, asked where her sisters were, and the aunt said they were nearby. The woman said, "This journey was so sudden, we cannot wait." They stayed for about half a day.

During a time of famine, the wife's mother prepared some food, which was coarse and tasteless. The wife and children took steamed rice and other delicacies from the carriage, called their husband and the mother-in-law to eat, and after the meal, they set off. At the moment of departure, she said, "Given the hardships here, it would be fitting to leave something behind. The bags had already been loaded ahead, but now there is only one and a half bolts of silk in the carriage. Leave it to be used for clothing, though I deeply regret the small amount." During the reign of Emperor Suzong, when the two capitals were recaptured, her mother-in-law and her son traveled together to Yangzhou. After more than a month, Uncle Zhi also arrived. They met and wept, again lamenting her, who died in childbirth while away from home, and their young children died one after another. After speaking, they wept again. At first, the mother-in-law felt ashamed because her niece had been taken by bandits. When she saw Uncle Zhi's deep emotion, she explained the situation. She said, "The skirt she wore was made from this silk." Uncle Zhi sighed and said nothing. Wu County's Zhu Ao once met a military officer among the bandits in Chenliu, who claimed he had captured Li Zhi's wife.

The Magistrate of Xin Fan County When the magistrate of Xin Fan County's wife passed away, he ordered his daughter to make mourning clothes. Among the mourners, there was a woman who was exceptionally beautiful and graceful. The magistrate was pleased with her and kept her with him, showing her great affection. After several months, one day she suddenly became sad and weak, her words choked with emotion. The magistrate was surprised and asked her why. She replied, "My husband is coming, and I am about to be sent far away, which is why I am so sad." The magistrate said, "I am here; who can do anything to me? Just take care of yourself and do not worry." A few days later, she requested to leave, but he tried to stop her, which she refused. She left him a silver wine cup as a parting gift and told him, "I am very much in love with you; please remember me by this cup." The magistrate gave her ten bolts of silk, and after she left, he constantly thought of her. He never let go of the silver cup and would place it on the table whenever he went to the office. The county magistrate (now in a different position) had returned to his hometown after being dismissed from his post, and his wife's spirit coffin was still in Xin Fan, so he came from afar to move it. He presented a card to visit the magistrate, who treated him very generously. The county magistrate saw the silver cup and looked at it several times in secret. The magistrate asked him why, and he replied, "This is an object from my deceased wife's coffin; I do not know how it ended up here." The magistrate sighed for a long time and then explained the whole story, including the woman's appearance, her words, and the events of leaving the cup and giving the silk. The county magistrate was furious all day, and later he opened the coffin and found the woman lying inside, holding the silk. He was very angry and burned her with piled-up wood.

Grass and Xiao Pin, a native of Qiantang, Hangzhou, was attending a drinking party when he suddenly died. After about an hour, he came back to life. He said that he first saw a person coming to call him, and he thought it was someone from the county office. He went out to see him, and was immediately taken away. They reached the city gate, and there were several officials in a boat. The one who caught him ordered Pin to pull the boat. Pin said, "I am a descendant, never having pulled a boat before." He was then beaten. Reluctantly, he had to pull it with all his might. They reached Yitingle Bridge, which was about eight or nine miles away. The ghost no longer guarded him, and he managed to escape.

○ Chang Yi During the Tang Dynasty, Chang Yi of Jiankang, courtesy name Shutong, was well-versed in the classics and possessed refined literary and artistic talents. He had an upright and honest nature, taking pride in his family's profession. Living near Qingxi Stream, he often sat alone during the day. One day, a young boy in a yellow robe delivered a letter to the pavilion, saying, "Scholar Zhu from Wu Prefecture wishes to be heard by you." Chang Yi had never met this person before and was greatly surprised. Upon opening the letter, it read: "Scholar Zhu Jun from Wu Prefecture pays respects to the esteemed Scholar Chang." The letter was written in a style that seemed otherworldly, mentioning that the writer's home was near Xigang Hill, wishing to be good neighbors and hoping to serve Chang Yi. At the end of the letter was a poem: "I hereby present: In life, I roamed the city; after death, I was left to decay in the wilderness. Since leaving the mortal world, I no longer know the seasons of autumn and spring. For a long time, cattle and sheep have grazed here; the cypress trees have almost become firewood. I have parted from the pleasures of carriages and horses, content to follow the foxes and rabbits. Where does the fresh breeze come from? May a gentleman be my neighbor. Your great fame and virtue are bright and shining, eagerly awaiting a distinguished guest. A thousand years, what are day and night? One room can move the spirit. Tall trees are in sight, the main road is easy to follow. If the high door has no barrier, toward and fold the dragon's ford." The paper and ink were old and worn. Chang Yi was deeply moved by the letter, marveling at its profound meaning for a long time.

Yi then wrote a reply, sincere and earnest, and set a specific date for a meeting. After he left, he instructed someone to follow and observe. They went to the west of his house, about one li away, and entered an ancient tomb. On the agreed date, Yi prepared wine and fruit. In a short while, they heard knocking on the door. They saw the little boy say, "Mr. Zhu has come to pay respects." Yi put on his belt and went out to welcome. The scholar wore a brimmed hat, a light cotton robe, and dragged his shoes, appearing to be about fifty years old. He was graceful and composed, elegantly refined. They exchanged greetings and expressions of concern. The scholar said, "I was in the Liang Dynasty, selected as a top scholar from my province. Due to the many difficulties in the country, I lost my desire for office and secluded myself to pursue my ideals. At the end of the Yongding era of Chen, I passed away here. I have been in the underworld for a long time, often admiring your character and style. The path between the living and the dead is cut off, so I ceased to attend to visitors. Fortunately, through this good opportunity, a great gentleman does not look down on me, and I can express my accumulated feelings. What could be more joyful than this!" Yi replied, "I am humble and inferior, not expecting that the realm of the spirits was so close. I have been absent for a long time, fortunately receiving your special favor, and my joy and gratitude are great." They then sat down, ate fruit, and drank wine. They asked about events during the Liang and Chen dynasties, which were clearly and distinctly described. He claimed to be a descendant of Zhu Yi and spoke of Zhu Yi's service to Emperor Wu, noting that he enjoyed unparalleled favor.

The emperor possessed a screen woven with gold threads, a dust whisk with coral and jade handles, a seven-jewel basin from Linyi, and a pillow carved from agarwood, all of which he cherished deeply. He often concluded his lectures in the Cloud-Rising Hall and would bestow all these treasures upon someone named Yi. When Prince Zhaoming died, a thick white fog filled the area; at his burial, four pairs of black cranes circled the tomb, lingered, mourned, and only departed after the burial was complete. Emperor Yuan lost sight in one eye and deeply feared this condition. When he was stationed as governor of Jingzhou in Xiangdong, the king once had a scholar expound on the *Analects* and, upon reaching the passage "When meeting a blind person, one must change color," he did not hide his true feelings. The emperor flew into a rage and had the scholar poisoned to death. He also once defeated the northern barbarians, personally beheading a subordinate general. When Yu Jin attacked Jiangling, the emperor was killed, and the executioner was his own son. When Shen Yue's mother was honored as the Grand Lady of Jianchang, the emperor sent a Gentleman Attendant to read out the imperial decree and present her with the seals and ribbons. Hundreds of officials, starting from the position of Cishi He Jingrong, came to the door to pay their respects and congratulate her. Since the Song and Liang dynasties, no noblewoman had ever received such an honor. When young, Yu Jianwu served under Master Tao and learned many arts. Once, during a hot summer, he gathered guests and exhaled heavily into the air, which turned into snow. He also made all objects float in mid-air. Emperor Jianwen ordered the construction of Fenglin Temple in Xiangyang. When the wooden pillars for the pagoda were not yet ready, a river official found a camphor tree in the river that matched perfectly with the other pillars.

The emperor was extremely filial. While residing near the coffin of Consort Ding, he wept incessantly, lying in pain with ulcers breaking out, and his face was covered in sores. When Hou Jing captured Tai City, the city was cut off from water and grain. Emperor Wu had ordered porridge brought in, but there was no rice in the palace. They obtained four sheng from a sack in the palace storehouse, and after eating it all, he died of starvation, unable to get more. Hou Jing made the captured Liang people wear long yokes, placed their heads in them, and ordered soldiers to shoot them with three arrows each, killing them indiscriminately, even high-ranking officials were not spared. After Chen Wu killed Wang Sengbian, it rained for over a hundred days. It is also said that Chen Wu was poor when he was young and worked as a hired servant to support himself. He often stole fish from the pond of the wealthy Ba family in Changcheng, and when caught, he was tied to a pole, suffering greatly. After ascending the throne, he exterminated the Ba family. These are all omissions by the historians; there are many such stories, too numerous to recount. Later, they often met, talked, feasted, and composed poetry together, forming a very intimate friendship. For any good or bad events in his family, he would always inform him. When the man was very ill, the scholar said to him, "The God of Fate has summoned you to be an assistant magistrate. I will also participate in the inspection. This position is very important and difficult to find, unmatched in its honor and power in the afterlife. All humans will eventually die, and even if you try to prolong your life for a few more years, how is it better than being here? You should not refuse!" The man happily accepted, did not take any medicine, and died a few days later.

**Zhang Shouyi** During the reign of Emperor Suzong (Danyuan), there was a man named Zhang Shouyi, who held the position of Deputy Chief Justice of the Dali Temple. He was known for his benevolence and leniency, and he often acquitted wrongfully convicted prisoners, thus saving many lives. One day, early in the morning, an elderly man with white hair, leaning on a staff, approached him, bowed deeply, and thanked him. Zhang Shouyi asked why he was thanking him and asked his attendants to step aside. The old man replied, "I am not a living person but the father of a prisoner whom you have set free. I am humble and lowly, and I have no way to repay your kindness. If you have any requests that I might be able to fulfill, please let me know, and I will do my best." Zhang Shouyi responded, "Your son is innocent, and it was not by bending the law that I released him. I am honored to hold such a high position, and I am well-provided for. Please do not trouble yourself." He comforted the old man repeatedly and sent him away. The old man said, "For now, please go, but if there is anything I cannot fulfill, please meet me again." And then he disappeared. Soon after, there was an imperial decree granting a public feast, and the city was filled with people enjoying the celebration. Zhang Shouyi, while observing the festivities, saw a woman from a scholarly family, whose beauty was breathtaking, and he found her very attractive. However, she was strictly guarded, and he had no way to approach her. He then called out to the old man who had appeared before, asking, "Can you help me obtain her?" As soon as he finished speaking, the old man appeared and said, "It is an easy matter, but it can only last for seven days." Zhang Shouyi replied, "That is enough."

Shouyi said, "Is it not possible that changes are confusing us?" The ghost replied, "Why are you so doubtful, noble one! I substituted something else for her body." So, he set up a quiet place and set up curtains. Soon, he suddenly arrived. After a long time, she woke up, startled, and asked, "Where am I?" Only Shouyi and the ghost were by her side. The ghost lied and said, "This is a heavenly messenger." They became intimate, and their affection was very deep. After seven days, the ghost said to the girl, "Heaven and earth are different, and our joy is still shallow. How can we part so soon?" He then wept and covered her eyes, sending her back. Later, Shouyi secretly observed the girl's family and found that the girl had suddenly fallen ill and did not recognize anyone, waking up after seven days. After ten years, Shouyi encountered the ghost again. The ghost said, "The heavenly court has summoned me, and I must depart. Here is a pill that can transform miscellaneous bones into good bone knives. Keep it precious, and use it if you are in need." He then sighed and departed. The pill was about the size of a chicken egg. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Shouyi upheld the law with fairness and leniency, but was framed by cruel officials and exiled to the southern regions. In dire financial straits, he used the pill to transform bones, and indeed, it worked. He then obtained sustenance through the use of the magical bone, and eventually died.

○ Li Ying Li Ying, the magistrate of Shouchang, had a thirteenth co-clan sister who was unmarried. During the early Zhide era, she followed her brothers south across the river, where she died and was buried in Haiyan, Wu. Her elder brother Min had a villa in Jiyuan, and he had a widowed sister living about ten miles away. Due to the chaos of the An Lushan Rebellion, she could not escape south. In the middle of the Shangyuan era, she suddenly saw her sister return and asked her why. Her sister said, "I was captured by the rebels." Her words were reasonable, and the family did not question her. Because of the chaos, she feared they would not be safe together, so she hastily arranged to marry a man from a nearby village. After four or five years, she had one son. She was very intelligent and capable, understanding everything. She often stayed in a room at her brother's house, coming and going safely. The land of her brother's house was often occupied by others, and they all sued in court to reclaim it. During the middle of the Yongtai era, when the country was relatively stable after the rebellion, Min and his brothers traveled from Jiangdong to the capital to participate in the selection process for government positions. After completing their affairs, they returned to the villa. They were about to travel hundreds of miles, and her sister was at the villa. Suddenly, she told the maid, "My brothers will arrive in a few days, I need to stay temporarily at the Zhang family's." She also bid farewell to her sister-in-law, who asked why, and she said, "I have been having frequent dreams about it." The maid accompanied her halfway and sent her back. After walking several steps, she turned back and did not see her again, and the maid found it very strange. Two days later, the Zhang family reported that she had died. Her aunt and nephew were just finishing their tears when her brothers arrived, and they then held a funeral for the Zhang family's sister. Min said, "She died in the middle of the Shangyuan era, was buried in Haiyan, how could she be here? I fear it might be a ghost." He then went to the Zhang family's house to inspect. Lifting the blanket, he did not see the body, and upon examining her clothes and mirrors, they were all the items she had when she was buried. Her son also died soon after.

宇文觌 韩彻，乾元年间担任陇州吴山的县令。他平素与进士宇文觌、辛稷等人交好，他们一同跟随韩彻来到吴山读书，并且韩彻答应提供秋赋的资助。吴山县令的职位被称为凶阙，前任县令大多在此去世。县衙内有一棵大树，宇文觌和辛稷等人认为这棵树被精魅所凭依，私下里与典正商议，打算在韩彻不在的时候砍伐掉它，约定在某一天行动。后来他们又告诉了韩彻，韩彻对他们说：“命运由天决定，过错不在树上，你们不要这样做。”他们的计划因此作罢。几天后，宇文觌和辛稷在树下行走时，发现了一个洞穴，洞穴周围非常湿润，里面有一股青色的气体，升腾而起化为云雾。他们等到韩彻回房休息后，便命令县里的百姓挖掘这个洞穴，挖了几尺深，发现了一个坟墓，坟墓中有腐烂的棺木，还有一些牙齿、头发以及小腿骨和胯骨。远处西北方向有一物，众人认为是怪异之物，于是花了五千钱请了两个人去取它。起初，他们用绳子将人吊下去，点燃了一束画烛，两人背着刀沿着绳索下去查看，发现了一个食瓶。瓶中装有水，水面上漂浮着林檎和缒夹等物品，这些东西流出地面后，全都像烟一样消失了。韩彻回来后，命令佐史收集骨头和头发，用新棺材装殓，将遗骸葬于野外。

A minor official stole money and hid it in a small book or container, possibly as a metaphor for hiding evidence. Upon returning home, he was in a hurry and felt like he was about to die, likely due to guilt or fear. His family reported the matter to the magistrate, who ordered a soothsayer to investigate. The soothsayer, in a trance-like state, claimed to communicate with spirits and said: "I am the general Qibie E from the Jin dynasty, who died in battle and was buried in this county. My tomb is near a horse stable, which is constantly filled with manure, causing distress. I want my tomb to be moved. I have tried to communicate before, but I was often interrupted by the presence of other spirits, which has caused great suffering in the afterlife, making it impossible to communicate effectively. Now, your kindness extends to the underworld, as you are willing to buy a coffin with your salary, which is greatly appreciated. However, the officials are cruel and have stored my bones in a small book or container, causing severe pain and necessitating revenge." The magistrate apologized several times and explained his position: "My leadership was unclear, leading to such deceit. I will buy a coffin and send clothes, hoping for a small pardon." The soothsayer then said: "You will soon be released. The plot was devised by Yuwen Seven and Xin Four. The spirits will remember them. Xin will soon be promoted, bringing glory to himself. However, Yuwen has a short life and no position, even if he gets a degree, he will not receive a salary. He will face many hardships and cannot be saved from three deaths, and if he becomes an official, even the spirits cannot save him." After finishing, the soothsayer left. The official was released, and the proper burial could proceed.

觌's family lived in Qishan. After some time, 銳 suddenly spoke from the air, saying, "The wife of the seventh son is gravely ill at the village. I have just gone to rescue her, and she is now slightly recovered. Soon, people from the village will come to report that there is no need to fear. If you return, your wife will be fine afterward, but be careful not to eat horse meat." Shortly after, messengers arrived, and everything was as he had said. When 觌 entered the house, his wife also recovered. Coincidentally, a guest's horse foal died, and the guest offered cooked intestines and meat to 觌. 觌 forgot the warning and ate it, suffering from dry cholera and fainting several times. Suddenly, he heard 銳 say, "Why did you eat horse meat? You broke the promise. The horse is an enemy from a past life. If I am not here, you will not be buried alive, and even if I am here, there will be no trouble." He then ordered his servants to take a pen and write a prescription. After taking the medicine, he recovered. Later, 觌 returned to Wushan. Coincidentally, local bandits in Qizhou wanted to usurp a false title and appointed hundreds of officials. 觌 had a reputation and was appointed as a secretary of the imperial secretariat. The bandits were soon killed by imperial soldiers, and 觌 and more than seventy others were imprisoned in the state prison, awaiting orders. 銳 came to the place where 觌's wife was and said, "The seventh son has committed a crime. I greatly pleaded in the underworld for him, but he needs three thousand strings of cash." The wife replied, "Our poor family really cannot afford it." 銳 said, "The underworld uses paper money from the human world." The wife said, "We will do our best to provide the paper money."

After burning, he returned to the prison and said to Di, "I just received three thousand strings of coins from the lady, and I have interceded for you. The matter is also resolved. When the Military Governor Liu arrives, you will be released, and you will eat well and be without worry." Soon after, the imperial decree appointed Liu Yan as the military governor of Longzhou. Liu Yan, upon his arrival, presented his report and said, "I have disgraced the name of a noble man, and I have not yet met him. Those who were arrested on suspicion of rebellion are all in prison. When I arrive at the state, I will release them all." The emperor approved his petition. When Liu Yan arrived at the state, he presented his report and summoned all the prisoners, announcing their release. Di, having been appointed by the rebels, felt ashamed and returned home. More than half a year later, Lü Chongben became the military governor of Hedong and sought scholars for the office of secretary. Many people in the court spoke highly of Di. Lü Chongben then petitioned to appoint Di as a Left Guard Officer and Secretary of Hedong, and the imperial decree granted him a set of clothes. Lü Chongben then gave him a hundred bolts of silk. When the imperial decree arrived, Di was very happy, accepted the decree, put on a green robe, and bowed westward. Suddenly, his servant fell to the ground, speaking in the voice of a spirit, and said, "Take great care, for I will not come again." Di replied, "If I return the official position now, what should I do?" The spirit replied, "You have already accepted the position, how can you return it now! Take great care, for I will not come again." Four days later, Di fell ill and died. Initially, a female shaman saw a spirit, who was dressed very grandly, with hair as red as fire, looking like the modern-day Kumo Xi people.

○ Péi Sheng Péi Sheng of Hedong. From a young age, he loved to play the zither. At one time, there was a zither teacher skilled in composing new melodies. Péi Sheng’s sister wished to learn, but it was difficult for her to receive instruction in person. So Péi Sheng went to study, and then taught his sister in turn, and thus gained a reputation for his skill. After some time, Péi Sheng traveled to Jiangxi and Xiang provinces, and died in the southern region of Chu. While his mother and sister were at home, Péi Sheng suddenly left alone to return. His family was surprised and asked him why, and he said, “My belongings and servants are following behind, and they will arrive at dusk.” After the joy and celebration, he then requested to play the zither. He had his sister play a piece, and when she made some mistakes, he corrected them all. He corrected over ten pieces in succession, and then he did not appear again. Soon after, a funeral carriage arrived. ○ Li Shi A woman named Li Shi from the district of Laoting in the capital. One day, she was sitting in her home hall when she suddenly saw the ghost of her deceased sister-in-law, who was wearing white mourning clothes and a cloth headwrap, coming directly to chase her. Li Shi ran around the bed to avoid her, but the ghost did not stop chasing. She then ran out of the house and fled swiftly, but in the rugged terrain, no one dared to intervene or rescue her. A soldier from the northern gate struck her with a horsewhip, and she immediately disappeared. Only the cloth headwrap remained, falling to the ground. Beneath it, they found a skull.

○韦璜 The wife of Zhou Hun, the county magistrate of Lucheng, was named Wei Huan. She was beautiful and clever. She always agreed with her sister-in-law and younger sister to say: "If one of us dies first, we will report on matters of the afterlife to each other." After marrying into the Zhou family, she had two daughters and died in the seventh year of the Qianyuan era. More than a month later, she suddenly appeared to the family, speaking through a servant, and said: "As promised, I have come to report. I have already seen the King of Hell and my relatives." The family asked, "Did you see the boiling oil and sword trees?" She replied, "Who am I to see such things?" Later, she spoke again through the servant: "The God of Mount Tai is marrying off his daughter. He knows I can dress and comb, so he called me. After the wedding is over, I will come back." The next day, the servant spoke again: "I went to Mount Tai, and the God of Mount Tai is marrying off his daughter. The ceremony is very grand. He asked me to prepare the makeup for the bride. I brought some rouge and powder for the girls." She then opened her hand, and there was some extremely red rouge, which was not different from the powder used by people. She also said, "The God of Mount Tai's family throws very large coins during the wedding ceremony. Even forty ghosts cannot lift one. I also brought some." Coins then fell from the air, and they were as large as bowls. She continued, "The God of Mount Tai knows I am good at dyeing red, so he ordered me to dye. Although I agreed, I did not personally handle it. I am usually just a servant, so I just followed his instructions."

The magistrate ordered me to take the maid, but now I have no choice but to take her away and send her back tomorrow.” The woman said, “Our whole household depends on this maid, how can you take her away?” Wei replied, “I only need her for two days. If it goes past two days, you should strike the stone chime and call out to her. When the stone chime sounds once, all spirits will hear it.” The maid suddenly ran out of breath and did not return after two days. The women struck the stone chime, and after a short while, the maid spoke from the air, saying, “I finished dyeing in the morning and had already sent the maid back. Why hasn’t she arrived? She must have gotten lost.” Soon, the maid returned, alive, with her hands suddenly turning deep red. She also composed several five-character poems for her sister, sister-in-law, and husband. The poem she sent to her sister-in-law said: “Longevity and brevity each have their own share, superficiality is also not true. Heartbroken in the underworld, deep sorrow is hard to fully express. Gloomy white poplar wind, evening makes people sad.
