Another poem sent to her husband, titled “Guest at the Underworld, Wei Huang,” said: “We cannot stay together forever, youth fades like the flowers of Shun. Our past life is now forever gone, the underworld is now our home.” Another poem said: “If I had known that separation would deeply wound the heart, I would regret having loved so deeply from the beginning. Though we have long departed to the dark and silent underworld, we still seek each other again in daytime.” She also composed a poem for her sister-in-law, with the preface saying: “My sister-in-law suspected me, and I left this poem: A loyal heart used up for mutual understanding, considering the future and preventing the past only confirms suspicion. Official documents can explain details, but even holy peachwood charms cannot do anything.” This is what I heard from those who knew her.

○范俶 范俶者，广德初，于苏州开酒肆。日晚，有妇人従门过，色态甚异。俶留宿，妇人初不辞让，乃秉烛，以发覆面，向暗而坐。其夜与申宴私之好。未明求去，云失梳子，觅不得。临别之际，啮俶臂而去。及晓，于床前得一纸梳，心甚恶之。因而体痛红肿，六七日死矣。 ○范俶 范俶在广德初年，在苏州开设了一家酒肆。一天傍晚，有一个妇人从门前经过，她的神色和姿态非常奇异。范俶留她住宿，妇人起初并不推辞，于是点上蜡烛，用头发遮住脸，面向黑暗而坐。当晚，范俶与她共度了私密的时光。天未亮时，妇人请求离去，说她丢失了一把梳子，找不到。在分别之际，妇人咬了范俶的手臂就离开了。等到天亮，范俶在床前发现了一把纸梳，心中非常厌恶。因此，他的身体疼痛并红肿起来，六七天后去世了。 ○李浣 河中少尹李浣，于广德二年去世。初七那天，家人为他设斋完毕，突然在中门见到李浣独自骑马从门进入。仆人们再次行礼，扶李浣下马，让他坐在西廊。他的儿子们前来拜见，哭泣着，李浣说：“生死是命，何必悲伤？这只会打扰亡者的安宁。”他详细嘱咐了家事许久。李浣先娶了项妃的妹妹，生了四个儿子。项妃去世后，他又娶了河南窦滔的女儿，她容貌美丽，特别受李浣宠爱。窦滔的女儿害怕不敢出来，李浣派人叫她。她提前对李浣说：“虽然生死不同，但至于感情，从未改变。你为什么害怕不出来呢？每次在地下，听到你的哭声，都会感到深深的悲伤。我也担心我的寿命不长，与你分别不会超过两年。夫妻情深，如今一起同行，难道不是一件快乐的事吗？人生终将有死，不必在人间多活一两年。”

What do you think?” Sin remained silent at first. Washing said, “If you wish not to follow, it is too late now. Tomorrow, I will send a carriage to come and fetch you. Please do not refuse.” He then called the maids and said to the four of them, “You have always served Lady [name], and you should also accompany her.” He took his wife’s clothes, separated them by hand, divided them into several bags, and gave them to the four maids, saying, “Tomorrow, you can bring these with you when you come with Lady [name].” He also told his sons, “I married your mother first, but we do not meet in the afterlife. It is not appropriate to bury your mother with me; you can bury her with the Sin family. If you disobey my words, the spirits will punish you.” After saying this, he left. The servants saw Washing ride away swiftly, turning from east to west, and they saw him no more. The next day, the carriage arrived at the door, but others did not see it; only the four maids saw it. They then dressed Sin, took the selected clothes, and said goodbye to the family. They then fell to the ground and died.

○萧审 萧审，工部尚书萧旻之子。永泰年间，担任长洲县令。他性格贪婪暴戾，但也有一定的治理能力，因此邑中百姓对他既畏惧又忌惮。萧审在长洲任职三年，前后贪污受贿无度。四年五月，守门人看见三十多个穿紫衣的人骑马从门外进来。迎接询问缘由，这些骑马的人起初不说话，直接进入厅堂。厅内正在处理文书的人都看到了。守门人进去告诉萧审说：“刚才有三十个穿紫衣的将军直接进来，没有通报。”萧审问：“这些人在哪里，怎么没见到？”守门人出来到厅堂。不久，看见骑马的人从里面出来，用白衫蒙住萧审，让他步行。守门人又报告了奇怪的事情，萧审只是看着不说话。众官吏把他送到门口，他便消失了。不久听到里面有人哭泣，大家这才明白萧审已经去世。七天后，他的弟弟萧宇去祭拜墓地，突然倒地说话，像是萧审的鬼魂，责备萧宇没有处理好家事，说了数十上百句话，又说：“安胡这个人，带走了我的两百石米和八十匹绢，进行非法牟利。现在我死了，这个家伙背叛了我，已经逃走了。明天吃饭的时候，帮我找他。”萧宇回到家，将此事告诉嫂子，婢女那天也说了一些类似的话。萧宇详细地告诉了刺史常元甫，常元甫命令衙役等候捉拿，果然抓到了安胡，米和绢都在他那里。起初，萧审还说：“米是我的钱，绢是非法所得，可以分给穷人。”萧宇最终把这些绢分给了穷人。

Shang Shun Shang Shun, from Danyang, married Zhang Chang's daughter from Wu Prefecture. Zhang Chang was the Jingzhao Shao Yin and was buried east of the Chan River, ten miles from his secondary residence. Shang Shun gathered in Chang'an for a long time. Eventually, Zhang's household sent a servant into the city to meet Shang Shun. As dusk fell, Shang Shun went with the servant. The servant drank secretly and became extremely drunk, and they lost each other. Shang Shun did not realize that the city gates had closed and could do nothing about it. He continued alone, and as the night grew darker and the rain and snow fell, he realized that his donkey was very slow and he was lost, not knowing where to go. He followed the donkey's path, and after traveling several miles, he did not see any villages or estates. He entered a dense thicket, shivering from the extreme cold. Soon, he reached a stream, and on the southern bank, he saw lights. Shang Shun was relieved and approached. He knocked on the door of a small thatched hut with a thatched fence, and a few steps down, the door was opened. Shang Shun asked, "A traveler is lost and suffering from the cold. May I temporarily stay for the night?" The person inside replied, "It is so dark and the rain and snow are so heavy. Who are you, and your place is so humble, it cannot accommodate a guest." They firmly refused him.

Shang Lang then asked how far Zhang Yin Zhuang was from there, and the person replied, "It is about four to five li to the southwest." Shun, thinking the road was near and could be reached, left the ravine and traveled more than ten li to the southwest, but did not reach the village. The rain and snow grew heavier, and Shun, realizing he would surely die, thought, "Since it is impossible to go further, where should I go?" He then tied his donkey to a mulberry tree and sat leaning against a tree. After a while, he saw an object that looked like a candle lantern, shining several zhang, moving directly toward him and stopping about a chi in front of him. Shun was at first very frightened, but soon asked, "Is this not the spiritual guidance of Master Zhang?" He then knelt forward and said, "If you are a venerable elder, please show me the way back." Seeing a small path in the light, Shun rode his donkey following it. As he approached, the light moved, always remaining about a chi ahead. After traveling six or seven li, he saw someone holding a light coming to meet him, and the lantern light then disappeared. When the light arrived, it was the servant guarding the Zhang family tomb. Shun asked how he knew he was coming, and the servant replied, "I just heard Master call out loudly, 'Shang Lang is coming from the east,' and hurried to meet him. I did this repeatedly, and so I knew." Thus, he stayed in the servant’s hut and left the next morning.

In the seventh year of the Dali era, Liu Yan, the Transport Commissioner and Minister of Personnel, was stationed in the Ministry. Li Zai, a Judicial Censor and Acting Inspector of Imperial Censorate, was appointed as the Military Governor of Fujian. Li Zai established his headquarters in Pucheng, Jianzhou. Pucheng is 700 li from Jianzhou, still a relatively cool place. Li Zai feared the tropical diseases and disliked his official duties, and after half a year, he died. The next day, he revived as if nothing had happened. His family brought him food, and he ate it as usual. He told his family, "I have already died, and now I have temporarily returned to complete my official business." He then pursued those who had not finished their tasks, instructed them in everything, and handed over the responsibilities. Later, he wrote a report to bid farewell to the Minister of Personnel and also wrote a will to arrange family affairs. His wife, surnamed Cui, had predeceased him, and he had only one concubine. He said to her, "When I died, I met my deceased wife in the afterlife. I told her about you, and she was very angry, intending to do something harmful to you. What should I do? She is coming today, so you should not stay here for too long." After speaking, he divided the wealth among them and sent the officials to send her back north. The concubine got off the boat, and the officials had some business and did not immediately set out. Li Zai also knew this, called the officials, gave them five strokes with a stick, and ordered them to leave quickly. After completing the matter and eating, he died again.

Gao Li, the mentor of Cui Shiguang, was sitting under a mulberry tree in front of his estate during the summer, watching people threshing grain. He saw a man riding a horse come from the east, approach him, and bow twice, saying, "Please treat the horse's foot." Gao Li replied, "I am not a horse doctor, how can I treat the horse?" The man smiled and said, "Just glue it and it will be fine." Gao Li was initially puzzled by his words, so the man explained, "I am not a human, I am a ghost. This horse is a wooden horse. Just use glue to stick it, and it will help your journey." Gao Li then took some glue, boiled it until it was soft, and went out to the place of the horse. When he arrived, he saw the transformation and realized it was a wooden horse with an ailment in its front foot. He applied the glue to fix it and returned the glue to his house. When he went out again, he saw the man already at the horse's side. The horse was very swift. The man returned to thank Gao Li and then mounted the horse and left.

○Zhu Zi Qin Zhu Zi Qin, from Wu County, died in the year Bao Ying. In the third year of the Dali era, his daughter, a nun named a certain Yi, was sent to the market to buy flatbread for a vegetarian meal. She saw Zhu Zi Qin with a group of twenty attendants, looking as if he was on official business. Seeing the servant weeping, he asked, "Is your nun well? Where is she going?" The servant replied, "She has ordered the purchase of flatbread for the meal." Zhu Zi Qin said, "I have flatbread here." He then ordered his attendants to give her thirty flatbreads and convey his greetings. The servant returned to the temple and told the nun, who wept and refused to eat. The flatbreads were eaten by the others. After about ten days, the servant went to the market again and saw Zhu Zi Qin, who greeted her as before. He then said to the servant, "Your nun does not understand the natural cycle of life and death. Why should she weep? The flatbread I sent was also not eaten. Now you can take thirty flatbreads and make sure they are eaten." The servant returned, but the nun still refused to eat.

Ten days later, the maid met self-admonition again at the market. After greeting, she told the maid, "In the severe cold of winter, I heard that you still haven't put on your fur coat. Now I am attaching two bolts of silk, to make warm clothes for the monk." The maid obeyed the command and carried it back, giving the silk to the nun. The nun used one bolt to make pants and kept one bolt for storage. Ten or more days later, the maid met self-admonition again and said, "There are dozens of guests. You can take the two bolts of silk and have the monk prepare a meal in the room for lunch. The next day at noon, I will come there." The maid returned. The nun sold the silk to buy various delicacies and waited for the next day. At noon, the maid fell into a deep sleep for a long time. The spirit spoke and said the guests had arrived. The maid got up and served the food. The meal was just finished, and the spirit spoke again, saying, "The monk, please stay well. I and the guests have eaten to our fill. Now I will go." The maid sent self-admonition out the door. After a long time, she realized. Since then, she has not been seen again.

○ Luo Yuanze In the past, Luo Yuanze from Liyang once traveled by boat to Guangling. On the way, it rained, and a man asked to be carried along. Luo Yuanze took him on board. Observing that he seemed like an elder, Luo treated him very generously. The man had no other belongings, only a book case containing a scroll, which Luo found unusual. That night, they slept together. In the morning, they arrived at a village, and the man asked, "Please get off the shore temporarily; I will return shortly. You may anchor the boat and wait for me, but be careful not to open the book in my case." Luo agreed, and the man got off. Soon after, Luo heard cries from the village, realizing something was amiss. He secretly looked at the book, which said, "On a certain day, I will come to a certain village to take a certain person." The village name was indeed correct, and Luo's name was listed below the other person's. Luo was very afraid. The ghost returned and scolded, "Why did you look at my book case?" Luo then stepped forward to confess and begged for mercy.

The ghost, moved with pity, said, "Have you ever wronged someone?" Yuanze pondered deeply and replied, "In my life, I have only wronged someone by taking ten acres of land from a fellow county resident named Zhang Mingtong, which led to his unemployment and his death." The ghost said, "This person is now complaining to me." Yuanze wept and said, "My parents are old and rely solely on me. Please show me mercy." After a long pause, the ghost said, "I remember your kindness and generosity. I will now leave. You should return home quickly, and for the next three years, do not leave the house. After that, your life can be extended by ten years." The ghost immediately left in a boat. Yuanze returned home. After some time, his father sent him to the fields to harvest rice, but Yuanze firmly refused. The father became angry and said, "A farmer should be self-reliant. You want to take it easy and sleep soundly, and you are making up nonsense?" The father was going to beat him. Yuanze had no choice but to comply and left the house, where he immediately saw the ghost again, who was now in a state of suffering and said, "I have come to you for this reason and cannot protect myself. Now that we have met again, I cannot let you go." Yuanze said, "Leave me and my parents." The ghost agreed. Yuanze told his father everything. After finishing speaking, Yuanze suddenly died. His father was in pain and hated him, and after a month, his father also died.

Chapter: Li Yuanping Li Yuanping, the son of Li Baocheng, the governor of Muzhou, was a guest at the Dongyang Temple reading books in the fifth year of the Dali era. After more than a year, at the end of the day, suddenly a beautiful woman wearing a red silk skirt and blouse appeared, accompanied by a maid dressed in blue. She entered the room where Li Yuanping was staying. Li Yuanping was delighted and went towards her, asking her where she was going and her name. The maid angrily said, "We have never met before, and you suddenly approach me, which is not what I expected from a nobleman!" Li Yuanping did not respond initially but only requested to meet her. After a while, the woman came out from the room. They met with joy, as if they had known each other for a long time. They talked happily for a long time. The woman then said, "I am here to see you, to discuss matters of the past. I am no longer human, so you have nothing to fear." Li Yuanping, already captivated by her, had no doubts or reservations.

The man said to the woman, "Go ahead and speak; I have nothing to fear." The woman replied, "My father, who passed away, once served as the governor of Jiangzhou. In a previous life, you were a gatekeeper at the governor's mansion, always serving there. Though born into poverty, your demeanor was pleasing. Due to our karmic connection, we secretly had a relationship. You lived only a hundred days before dying of cholera, and I dared not cry, feeling twice as much sorrow as usual. I have always recited the 'Thousand-Armed and Thousand-Eyed Bodhisattva Mantra,' and I wish that in our next lives, we will both be reborn into noble families and marry again. I marked your left thigh with red ink as a sign. Please look and see if there is still a mark; if there is, my words are true." Yuanping examined himself as she said, and became more convinced, so he decided to stay with her for the night. After some time, their emotional connection deepened, and they were very happy together. As dawn approached, she suddenly said to Yuanping, "My time to be reborn has come, and I cannot stay much longer. I feel deeply regretful." She spoke with tears, saying, "In my next life, my father will be a county magistrate, and when I am sixteen, I will become a high-ranking official. At that time, our marriage will be appropriate. Until then, please do not marry. However, fate has already determined our relationship, and even if you wish to marry, it will not be possible." After speaking, she bid farewell and departed.

Zhou Jichuan, from Ruyang, had a villa west of Yangzhou. He and his several brothers were all scholars. One night, after they had finished teaching, it was around the third watch, and they were about to sleep. Suddenly, they heard a continuous "clicking" sound outside the window. Zhou Jichuan looked out the window and saw a small child made of white bones. The child was moving in all directions in the courtyard, starting with crossed hands and then waving arms. The "clicking" sound was the grinding of bone joints. Zhou Jichuan called his brothers to observe it for a long time. His brother Ju Chuan shouted at it. With one shout, the child jumped onto the step; with two shouts, it entered the door; with three shouts, it wanted to get on the bed. Ju Chuan's shouting became more intense. The child said, "Mother, give me milk." Ju Chuan hit it with his hand, and it fell to the ground; when he lifted his hand, it was back on the bed. Its agility was like that of a monkey. The family heard it and suspected something unusual, so they brought weapons. The child again said, "Mother, give me milk."

In the family, someone struck him with a stick, and he was hit in the middle. The child's body parts scattered like stars and reassembled several times. He said, "Mother is nursing me." The family put him in a cloth bag and took him out, but he still sought milk from afar. They threw him outside the city walls several miles away into a dry well. The next night, he returned, holding a cloth bag, and jumped around happily. The family caught him again, put him in the cloth bag as before, tied a rope around it, attached a large stone, and threw him into the river. He tried to escape, but inside the bag, he said, "It's just like last night's guest." On subsequent days, he returned, carrying the bag in one hand and a broken rope in the other, running and playing as before. The family prepared a large hollow log, similar to a drum, and placed the child inside. They covered both ends with large iron sheets and nailed them shut. Then, they locked it with an iron lock, attached a large stone, and threw it into the great river. He tried to escape again, but said, "Thank you for sending me a coffin." From then on, he never returned. It was the 17th year of Emperor Dezong's reign (AD 799).

○ Du Wan Du Wan, the Magistrate of Lingnan, was about to take up his post when his wife died from poisonous malaria after several days. It was the height of summer, and there was no proper burial. He temporarily wrapped her body in reed mats and buried her beside a steep cliff. Upon arriving at his post, he was occupied with official duties and did not rebury her. When he returned north, he finally reached the cliff and intended to retrieve his wife's remains. Upon examining the burial site, only the reed mats remained. He lamented that she had been taken so deeply and felt deep sorrow for a long time. There happened to be a path up the cliff, and he tried to follow it, walking over a hundred steps to reach a cave. His wife was naked, her face distorted and frightening, no longer recognizable. She was holding a child in her arms, and another child beside her, looking like a demon. She woke up with a start, unable to speak, and drew on the ground with her hands, writing: "I have recently been reborn and was captured by a yaksha. These two children are my own." She wrote with tears. After a while, she could also speak, saying, "You must leave quickly. If the yaksha comes, he will surely kill you." He asked, "Can you leave?" She replied, "Yes, I can." She got up and took the child, following him to the boat, and they set sail immediately. The yaksha came and took the older child to the shore, calling out angrily at the boat and showing the child to him. As the boat sailed further away, he tore the child into dozens of pieces and then left. The child in her hand looked like a demon and understood human language. During the Dali period, the mother and children were still alive. ○ Zheng Qiying ○ Zheng Qiying Zheng Qiying, the Magistrate of Zheng, was a man of great virtue and learning. He was known for his fairness and integrity in governance. One day, he encountered a dispute between two villagers over a piece of land. The villagers were adamant in their claims, and the matter had escalated to the point where it threatened to disrupt the peace of the village. Zheng Qiying listened carefully to both sides, asking detailed questions and examining the evidence. He then made a decision that was both fair and just, resolving the dispute to the satisfaction of both parties. The villagers were grateful for his wisdom and fairness, and they praised him as a true servant of the people. Zheng Qiying's reputation for justice and integrity spread far and wide, and he became a model for other officials to follow.

Zhen Qiying, during the Kaiyuan era, served as a Minister of Personnel and an Inspector-General of Henan. As he was returning home, he passed through Huazhou. Suddenly, he saw five people dressed in garments of the five cardinal colors, who paid two bows at the hall. Zhen Qiying asked them why they had come, and they replied, "We are the spirits of the five internal organs." Zhen Qiying asked, "If the spirits should reside within the body, why do you appear before me?" They answered, "We guard the vital energy, and when it is exhausted, we must disperse." Zhen Qiying said, "If this is true, am I going to die?" They replied, "Yes." Zhen Qiying hastily sought to extend his life by a short period, wishing to prepare formal documents and arrange his affairs after death. The spirits said, "You can do it when you return to the back hall." Zhen Qiying prepared wine and food, and they all bowed and accepted it. After preparing the documents, he bathed, put on new clothes, lay down against the western wall, and died at the appointed time. ○ Liu Shaoyou Liu Shaoyou was skilled in divination and famous in the capital city. During the Tianbao era, a guest brought a piece of fine silk to Liu Shaoyou. Liu invited him in and asked the reason for his visit. The guest replied, "I wish to know my lifespan." Liu Shaoyou cast the hexagrams, and upon completion, he sighed sadly, saying, "Your hexagram is not auspicious; you will die by the end of today." The guest was deeply saddened and lamented for a long time. He then asked for water, and the family brought water, but saw two Liu Shaoyous, unsure which one was the guest. Liu Shaoyou pointed to the spirit as the guest, instructing the family to give the water to the guest, who then left. The child escorted him out the door, and after a few steps, the guest disappeared. Soon, a mournful cry was heard in the air. Returning, Liu Shaoyou asked, "Do you recognize this person, young master?" The child explained the previous events. Liu Shaoyou then realized that the guest was a spirit. He quickly checked the silk, and it turned out to be just a single sheet of fine silk. He sighed, "The spirit has left me; I am going to die." He died at dusk.

○ Liu Shaoyou Liu Shaoyou was skilled in divination and famous in the capital. During the Tianbao era, a guest came to Liu Shaoyou carrying a piece of silk. He was invited in and asked about the reason for his visit, to which he replied, "I wish to know my fate." Liu Shaoyou performed the divination and, upon completion, sighed sadly and said, "Your divination is not auspicious; you should die by dusk today." The man lamented for a long time. He then requested some water, and the family brought water. They saw two Liu Shaoyous and did not know which one was the guest. Liu Shaoyou pointed out the spirit as the guest and ordered the water to be given to it. The guest then took leave. The child escorted the guest to the door, and after a few steps, the spirit disappeared. Soon, a crying sound was heard in the air, very sorrowful. They returned to ask Liu Shaoyou, "Do you recognize this person?" He recounted the previous events. Liu Shaoyou then realized that the guest was a spirit. He quickly ordered someone to check the silk, and it turned out to be just a piece of silk. He sighed and said, "The spirit has left me; I will die." He died at dusk. ○ A Woman from Luoyang During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, a woman from Luoyang was troubled by a demon. Various practitioners tried to cure her but failed. Her son sought out Daoist Ye Fashan and requested a spell to drive it away. Ye Fashan said, "This is a celestial demon. It has committed sins in heaven and has been punished by the emperor, temporarily in the human world. However, its punishment is complete, and it will soon leave on its own; there's no need to send it away." The woman thought it was a way to explain the situation and sought his help. Ye Fashan said, "I am willing to go." He then brought the woman deep into Yangzhai Mountain. At the foot of a steep mountain, there was a pool of water. He performed a banishment ritual at the edge of the pool. After a while, he saw a head of hair in the water, as large as three rooms, slowly emerging. When it reached the eyes, they flashed like lightning. In a moment, clouds and mist gathered around, and then it disappeared.

○ Chao Liangzhen Chao Liangzhen was renowned for his discernment, possessed a stern and resolute nature, and was unafraid of ghosts. Every year, he would dig up the *Tai Sui* site and build a house there. Later, he suddenly found a piece of flesh larger than a human skull. Chao Liangzhen whipped it hundreds of times and sent it to the main thoroughfare. That night, he sent people to listen secretly to it. After the third watch of the night, a group of chariots and riders arrived at the place of the flesh and asked the *Tai Sui*, "Why did you suffer this humiliation and not retaliate?" The *Tai Sui* replied, "He is currently in his prime; what can I do about it?" The next day, it was gone from its place. ○ The Li Family At the end of the Shangyuan era, another family of the Li clan did not believe in the *Tai Sui*, and dug it up, finding a piece of flesh. It was said that "whoever obtains the *Tai Sui* should whip it hundreds of times and thereby avoid calamity." The Li family whipped it ninety times, and suddenly it rose into the air, then vanished. Of the seventy-two members of the Li family, nearly all perished, leaving only the young *Kuai Gong* alive. Fearing their family would be wiped out, the Li brothers, at night, ordered their servants to all dress as ghosts, seized the young *Kuai*, and hid him. Only this child survived, and later succeeded in being enfeoffed as *Kuai Gong*. ○ Another Account In a certain state of □zhou, there was a man who also dug up the *Tai Sui*, which was as large as a square, in appearance like a red mushroom, and had thousands of eyes. His family did not recognize it. They moved it to the main road and asked knowledgeable people about it. A foreign monk was startled and said, "This is the *Tai Sui*; it should be buried immediately." The man quickly returned it to its original place. After a year, nearly all the people in the household died.

○ Zhang Yin Zhang Yin of Fanyang once traveled south of the ruins of the ancient city of Luoyang. It was already dusk, and he wished to seek lodging at an old friend's house. As he passed through a narrow road, his horse suddenly became startled and looked back, standing on tiptoe and refusing to move. Zhang Yin suspected something strange ahead and looked at the tombstones beside the road. At the top of a large stone column, there was an object that seemed like a silk lantern, growing larger like a bridge pillar, gradually expanding to the size of several measures. When it reached the ground, it flew like a shooting star, making a sound like thunder, scattering the birds roosting in the forest. After traveling over a hundred steps, it fell into a household. Zhang Yin secretly remembered this and continued on his way. More than a month later, he passed by that household again, and found no one missing, neither young nor old. He then asked the neighbors, who said, "Her wife was disrespectful to her mother-in-law, and after her mother-in-law died, she suffered this calamity." ○ Yan Fengxiang Yan Fengxiang of Pingyang was fairly well-versed in the Six Arts and gathered students to teach them. One night, while he was at home with his wife, they suddenly heard a muffled sound outside, thinking it was a thief. They stepped out in slippers to look and saw an object, white and about a zhang long, in the courtyard. They quickly shut the door. Gradually, they heard footsteps climbing the stairs, and someone called out to Fengxiang, "It's not late yet, why have you closed the door?" Fengxiang remained silent and did not answer, lighting the lamp to guard himself. Soon, a face appeared in the door crack, like a monkey, and burst in. It called out to its companions, hundreds of them, all entering through the cracks, each about two chi long, wearing leopard-skin short trousers, puffing out their lips and glaring, with very ugly appearances. Some climbed the walls, some were in the beams and rafters, jumping around behind, seemingly about to attack.

Feng Xiang found himself surrounded only by a pillow and his wife’s pipa. He threw these objects, and those that hit the spirits caused them to disappear, until dawn when they were finally gone. Thus, he was able to escape. He often saw, in a hazy state, a formally dressed man in the room, standing at the four walls, who said, “I am the envoy of Pingyang Yao Ping.” He called upon shamans and priests to perform rituals and prayers, but they could not dispel the spirits. So, he took refuge in a temple, where he saw a large face under the Buddha’s couch. He stared at it intently. Trying to escape to another place, he went out the door and saw a group of ghosts playing in the alley, rushing towards Feng Xiang, who could not get away. Having no way out, his illness worsened. He then invited many monks to set up a ritual feast, built an altar, and chanted spells. He also invited Daoist priests of the Six Directions to perform talismanic spells and curses, and the ghosts gradually departed. After several days, Feng Xiang dreamed of a man in red clothes and black headgear, floating in the air, who said, “I am returning your soul.” He then threw objects at Feng Xiang, some resembling women’s hair and others resembling red clothing, several dozen in total, which Feng Xiang accepted. The next day, he recovered.

**○韦训** Wei Xun, a scholar from the capital region of the Tang dynasty, was reading the *Diamond Sutra* in his home study on a day off. Suddenly, he saw a woman in a red skirt, three meters tall, jump over the wall and enter. She grabbed his teacher by the hair and dragged him to the ground. Then she also grabbed Wei Xun. Wei Xun held the *Diamond Sutra* in front of him to shield himself and managed to escape. His teacher was dragged into a house, and people called out to him, and he managed to escape. The ghost entered a pile of feces. His teacher’s entire body was stained blue, and his tongue protruded about a foot. The family helped him back to the study, and he gradually recovered. They then dug up the pile of feces and found a broken ceramic figurine of a new bride, which they burned at a major intersection. The ghostly occurrences ceased. **○卢赞善** Lu Zansan, a person from the Tang dynasty, had a ceramic figurine of a concubine. After several years, his wife jokingly said to him, "Be my concubine." From then on, Lu Zansan felt disoriented and often saw a woman lying in the bed. Over time, he suspected that the ceramic figurine was causing trouble and sent it to a temple for worship. A young boy who cleaned the temple noticed a woman in the hall and asked where she came from. She said she was Lu Zansan’s concubine, who was being jealous of by his wife, and she had been brought there. Later, when Lu Zansan’s family arrived, they mentioned seeing the concubine. Lu Zansan investigated the matter thoroughly and found that the woman’s appearance matched the ceramic figurine. He ordered it to be smashed. Blood oozed from the figurine’s heart, as large as an egg.

○Su Pi's Daughter During the Tianbao era, Su Pi, a man from Wugong, served as the magistrate of Chuqiu. His daughter married into the Li family. The Li family had a maid whom they favored greatly, and because of this, the relationship between the maid and Su Pi's daughter was not close. The maid sought out a practitioner of the yǎn gǔ (魇蛊) method and cast a spell, burying a talisman in the manure of the Li family's house. She also bound seven colorful human figures, each about a foot long, and hid them in a niche in the east wall, covering them with mud. No one knew about this. After several years, the Li family and the maid died one after another, and Su Pi's daughter was left a widow. Four or five years later, the spell took effect, and the colorful human figures began to appear inside the house. Su Pi's daughter fell ill and fainted. The Li maid had already died, but no one knew the cause. After a year, they repeatedly sought out sorcerers to perform spells and incantations, but they could not stop the spell. Later, when the figures reappeared, they led a group of dozens of people to catch one of the figures. They found that it had eyes, eyebrows, and a full body, and it moved constantly in their hands. They cut it with a knife, and blood flowed to the ground. They then piled wood and burned it. The other figures' followers came to the burning site and cried out, some floating in the air and others on the ground. After the fire was extinguished, the smell of burning filled the house. The next day, everyone wore white clothes and cried for several days without stopping. Six months later, they caught six more figures and burned them all, except for one that escaped. They chased it, and it suddenly disappeared into the manure. Su Pi's daughter led over a hundred people to dig through the manure, reaching a depth of seven or eight feet, and found a peachwood charm. The red inscriptions on the charm were clearly legible, stating: "Li Yun's maid cast a spell on Su Pi's daughter, creating seven human figures, hidden in the earth niche on the east wall. The spell will be completed in nine years." They followed the instructions and broke the wall, finding another figure. From then on, Su Pi's daughter was no longer affected by the spell.

Jiang Wei Yue was not afraid of ghosts and gods, and he often slept alone by the window. One day, he heard human voices outside. Jiang Wei Yue said, "If you are a wronged spirit, please enter to meet. If you are just a wandering spirit, there is no need to disturb me." Suddenly, the door was pushed open, and the spirits wanted to climb onto his bed. Seeing that he was not afraid, they stood still by the wall, and there were seven of them. When Jiang Wei Yue asked them what they wanted, they stood still without answering. Jiang Wei Yue hit them with a pillow, and they all rushed out of the door. They then ran and disappeared into the courtyard. The next day, digging up, he found seven broken wheel spokes, and the strange occurrences ceased. His brother often suffered from a serious illness, and Jiang Wei Yue personally looked after him. Deep into the night, he saw three female spirits again, at his brother's bedside. He scolded them away three times, and the spirits all fell to the ground. After a while, they left, and his brother recovered.

○ Wei Liang During the reign of Emperor Suzong (乾元 period), the magistrate of Jiangning County, Wei Liang, suddenly saw a small ghost in front of his hall. The ghost covered its face with its lower lip and came to the lamp. It left and then returned, and someone was sent to chase it, but it sank into the steps. The next morning, they dug where it had sunk and found an old door panel, about a foot long, with its head in the shape of a curled lotus. ○ Wang Huanyan Prince Wang Huanyan, when young, was unrestrained and had great principles, not paying attention to minor details. He often traveled with guests as a knight-errant, drinking in a remote marsh. As dusk fell, the guests dispersed, and Wang, along with a few others, got very drunk and lay down in the marsh. After the second watch, suddenly there appeared something over ten feet long and as thick as ten hands, holding spears and halberds, glaring and shouting loudly, coming straight towards Wang and the others. Everyone crouched and did not move, but Wang, who had courage and strength, then leaped up and shouted, with clenched fists moving forward. The creature then turned and fled. It encountered a large willow tree, and Wang broke off a branch with his hand, held it, and struck it. The sound was crackling, like striking an empty object. Several times, the creature crawled away, and Wang pursued it more urgently, and it entered an ancient tomb. By morning, he went to look, and it turned out to be a broken mask of a ritual figure.

A Man Named Cai Si Cai Si of Yingyang was a scholar skilled in literature and poetry. In the early years of the Tianbao era, he lived in Junyi, a district of Chenliu. While composing or reciting poetry, there was always a ghost who came to sit on his bed, sometimes asking about the meaning of his verses and sometimes praising his poetry. Cai Si asked, "What kind of ghost are you, why have you come to visit me?" The ghost replied, "I am surnamed Wang, the greatest, and I have come to admire your talent and virtue." Initially, Cai Si was very frightened, but later he became familiar with the ghost. Whenever the ghost came, he would call him "Wang Da" and refer to Cai Si as "Cai Shi," and they would talk and laugh happily. One of Cai Si's old friends had a young servant who saw the ghost. Cai Si tried to show the servant, but the servant trembled with fear. When asked about the ghost's appearance, the servant described it as a large ghost, about ten feet tall, with several smaller ghosts following behind. Later, Cai Si built a small wooden house in the southwest corner of his home and planted various fruit trees around it. When the ghost arrived, Cai Si said to him, "Humans and spirits are different in nature, as you know. Yesterday I built this small house with you, and it should be a comfortable place for you." The ghost was very pleased and thanked Cai Si. From then on, after their conversations and laughter, the ghost would enter the house to rest, and this became a regular occurrence. After some time, the ghost told Cai Si, "I want to marry off my daughter, and I need to borrow your house temporarily." Cai Si refused, saying, "My elderly parents are still alive. If they are affected by the ghost's presence, they will not be at ease. You should find another place instead."

The ghost said, "In the grand hall of the noble lady, simply close it, and it will definitely not enter. I will borrow it for seven days." P had no choice but to lend it. After seven days, it returned and stayed, and everything was safe and without any other issues. A few days later, the ghost said, "Set up a vegetarian meal." Relying on P, it asked to borrow dishes and tents, and P said, "Initially, I don't know anyone else, only my own things." Then it asked where to set up the meal, and the ghost said, "Close to the north of Fan Tower. When the moon is at its zenith, it is the time for the underground meal." P asked, "When the time comes, can we go to see it?" The ghost replied, "Wherever you want." P, fearing the ghost, had the entire family recite the *Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Mantra*. As long as the family remained clean, the ghost would not come; if they served impure or bloody food, the ghost would definitely appear. To reach the ritual site, the family all recited the mantra carefully, dressed in new clean clothes, and went to Fan Tower under the moonlight. They saw a tent full of monks and disciples, and the family all recited the mantra, approaching and confronting it. They saw the ghosts panic and scatter, knowing that they feared people, and then advanced further. Once there, they suddenly dispersed. The leader of the ghosts, with more than ten followers, headed north, and P followed them. About five or six li away, they arrived at a cemetery forest, and then disappeared. P remembered the place and returned. The next day, with family, they went to see it and found it was an abandoned tomb, with dozens of ritual objects inside. The one at the center was the largest, with a 'king' character on its forehead. P said, "Is this the king of the ghosts?" They burned it with fire, and the ghosts were then eliminated.

Li Hua, a low-ranking official in the Tang dynasty's Ministry of Personnel, was young when he and five or six of his peers studied in a mountain villa in Jiyuan. After half a year, an elderly man with white beard and eyebrows appeared, always holding a wrapped stone as big as a fist. Every day until evening, he would ride on the wall of the courtyard and sit, throwing the stone at Li Hua and others in front of and behind the window. For several months, the residents were troubled by this. There was a neighboring general named Qin, skilled in archery and well-known. Li Hua went to visit him himself and explained the situation in detail. Qin cheerfully held a bow and went to the mountain to wait for him. When evening came, the elderly man returned, continuing to throw the stone. Qin then shot an arrow through the gap, and one shot hit it immediately. Looking at it, it was a wooden ritual object.

During recent times, there was a man traveling in the suburbs of a merchant town. At first, he was accompanied by another person. After several days, the ghost suddenly said to the man, "I am a spirit, rebelling against the ancestral objects in my home, engaging in battle day and night. I wish to use a single statement to stabilize the chaos, what should I do?" The man replied, "If it can be accomplished, I have no fear." As evening approached, they came upon a large tomb on the left side of the road. The ghost pointed to the tomb and said, "This is my own tomb. You should shout loudly in front of the tomb, 'With an imperial edict, cut down the gold and silver tribes.' That will be enough." After the ghost finished speaking, it entered the tomb. The man then proclaimed the edict. Soon, he heard the sound of cutting and execution. After a while, the ghost emerged from the tomb, holding several figures of gold and silver horses, all with their heads cut off. The ghost said to the man, "With these, you will have enough fortune for a lifetime, to repay my kindness." The man went to Chang'an, where he was reported to the officials by those responsible for investigations. The local officials said, "These are ancient artifacts, likely obtained by breaking into a tomb." The man answered truthfully. The officials reported the matter to the governor, who then submitted a report. Officials were sent to open the tomb, and they found hundreds of figures of gold and silver horses, all with their heads cut off.

A woman from Donglai died and was buried. She reached the underworld but was unjustly captured and returned. Two officials were ordered to escort her back. The ghost took her to the tomb, where she was alive but had no way to escape. The ghost was troubled by this and asked the woman, "Among your family, who misses you the most?" The woman replied, "Only my youngest paternal uncle." One ghost said, "I can make him come and rob the grave. If he sees you alive, you will be allowed to live." The woman said, "My uncle is kind-hearted and has never done anything wrong. How could he open my tomb?" The ghost said, "I will change his heart." The ghost left to guard her, while the other ghost went to inform the uncle. Soon, the uncle and the robbers decided to open the coffin. The woman suddenly rose from the coffin. The uncle was shocked and asked her what had happened. She told him everything. The uncle felt great shame and regret. The robbers wanted to kill her, but the uncle wept and pleaded, and they spared her. He carried her back home.

○ Zheng Hui Zheng Hui of Xingyang lived in Weinan. From a young age, he was known for his strength. At the end of the Tianbao era of the Tang dynasty, An Lushan rebelled, and bandits and thieves rose up like bees in all places. Many people gathered in the prefectures and counties. Zheng Hui relied on his strength and still lived in his village, with many relatives and family members depending on him. Zheng Hui always rode a horse, spying on bandits from all directions, doing so for several months. Later, he suddenly did not return for five days, causing his family to worry and grieve. However, due to the fear of banditry, no one dared to search for him. Suddenly, a voice spoke from a tree in his family's courtyard, calling out, "Ah Ni," which was the nickname of Zheng Hui's wife's wet nurse. The family was terrified and hid. The voice continued, "Ah Ni, do you not recognize me? Before, I went to scout the bandits and encountered them. Being outnumbered, I was killed. Because my fate had not yet come to an end, I frequently appealed to the underworld officials, and now I have been granted a second chance at life. My body lies in a ditch by the roadside, five li north of this village. You can bring fire and clothes to retrieve it." The family followed the instructions and found his body in the ditch, but his head was missing. They heard the voice again, saying, "The head has moved north by over a hundred steps, buried under a mulberry tree. When you return to the house, use the inner bark of a gourd tree to make a thread and bind it together. I will not return, so make sure to do it properly." After speaking, the ghost howled and disappeared. When the family returned home, they followed the instructions to bind the head to the body, and Zheng Hui's body gradually warmed up. After several days, he was able to see, and they constantly fed him rice gruel. After a hundred days, he was back to normal.

Wang Mu, a general from Taiyuan, served during the early period of the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Suzong. He was a commander under Lu Min. At Nanyang, he suffered a defeat, and his troops fled in disarray. Wang Mu had a robust appearance, and his horse was exceptionally large. The enemy cavalry pursued him in great numbers. One of the enemy soldiers stabbed Wang Mu in the neck from behind with a sword, causing him to die and fall to the ground. All of his ribs were broken, but only his throat remained connected. Initially, he was unaware that he had died, and it took an hour for him to realize. His head was on his navel, and he began to feel sorrow. Soon, he felt food leaking from his mouth. He then used his hands to lift his head back to his neck, but it fell again, and he fainted once more. After a long time, he woke up. After straightening his neck, he tied his hair on both sides and was able to sit up. His mind was still confused, and he did not know how to save himself. The horse he rode did not leave him. When Wang Mu got up, the horse also got up and came to stand in front of him. Wang Mu helped himself to stand, and his left arm's hair came loose, causing his head to fall into his chest. He did not wake up until the night. After tying his hair back, he thought to himself, "The horse must lie down for me to mount it." The horse suddenly lay down in front of Wang Mu, and he was able to mount it. The horse also got up and carried Wang Mu southeast. His hands were on his cheeks as the horse traveled forty miles. Several dozen scattered soldiers under his command also sought out Wang Mu on the side and helped him into a village house. The village was forty miles from the enemy territory, and the soldiers were filled with fear and anxiety. They then carried him back to the Wu army. The army city was soon surrounded by the enemy. Wang Mu recovered in the city and took over two hundred days to recover. There was a lump of flesh around his neck like a finger, and his head was slightly tilted. Min considered Wang Mu a distinguished family member and he served the king's cause. He was appointed as the magistrate of Nanyang, and soon was appointed as the magistrate of Ye. After a year, he was promoted to the magistrate of Linru. After completing his term, he was appointed as the magistrate of Zhaoyang, and he died in office.

汤氏子者，其父为乐平尉。令李氏，陇西望族，素轻易，恒以吴人狎侮。尉甚不平。轻为令所猥辱，如是者已数四。尉不能堪，某与其兄，诣令纷争。令格骂，叱左右曳下，将加捶楚。某怀中有剑，直前刺令，中胸不深，后数日死。令家人亦击某系狱。州断刑，令辜内死，当决杀。将入市，无悴容，有善相者云：“少年有五品相，必当不死，若死，吾不相人矣。”施刑之人，加之以绳，决毕气绝，牵曳就狱，至夕乃苏。狱卒白官，官云：“此手杀人，义无活理。”令卒以绳缢之。其夕三更，复苏，卒又缢之。及明复苏。狱官以白刺史，举州叹异。而限法不可，呼其父令自毙之。双于州门，对众缢绝。刺史哀其终始，命家收之。及将归第，复活。因葬空棺，养之暗室，久之无恙。乾元中，为全椒令卒。 The son of the Tang clan, whose father served as the magistrate of LePing, encountered the magistrate Li, a prominent family from LongXi who treated him with contempt, often mocking him as a person from Wu. The magistrate became extremely angry. He was repeatedly humiliated by the magistrate, which happened several times. Unable to bear it any longer, he and his brother went to argue with the magistrate. The magistrate cursed at him and ordered his attendants to drag him down, intending to beat him severely. The son had a sword in his chest and stabbed the magistrate directly in the chest, not deeply, and the magistrate died several days later. The magistrate's family also beat him and imprisoned him. The prefecture judged the case and decided that the magistrate's death was within the bounds of guilt, and he should be sentenced to death. He was about to be executed in the market, but he showed no signs of distress. A good fortune-teller said, "This young man has the appearance of a fifth-rank official, and he must not die; if he does, I will no longer look at people." The executioner tied him with a rope, completed the execution, and he lost consciousness. He was dragged back to the prison and revived that night. The prison guard reported to the official, who said, "This hand killed someone, and there is no reason to spare him." The executioner strangled him with a rope. That night, at the third watch, he revived, and the executioner strangled him again. By morning, he revived again. The prison official reported to the prefect, and the entire prefecture was amazed. However, legal restrictions could not be changed, and the official ordered his father to commit suicide. Both were strangled to death at the prefecture gate in front of the crowd. The prefect pitied his entire ordeal and ordered his family to take him. When he was about to return home, he revived. He was buried in an empty coffin and kept in a dark room, and for a long time, he was fine. During the Qianyuan era, he became the magistrate of Quanjiao and died.

### Li Qiangyou Li Qiangyou was the son of Yu Shi Rubi, a high-ranking official. During the late Tianbao period, Li Qiangyou served as the county magistrate of Yan County. On his first few days in office, a butcher he had known since childhood came to visit him and bowed twice. When Li Qiangyou asked why, the butcher replied, "I suddenly fell ill and died. I was led through the underworld by officials and passed Mount Tai, where I saw a young man serving as a main clerk. I went to him to plead my case. I was not supposed to die and was released to return to life. That is why I came to thank you." The young man he referred to was Li Qiangyou himself. Upon hearing this, Li Qiangyou was deeply moved and said, "To die as a main clerk in the underworld, what else could one worry about!" He then asked the butcher about his duties. The butcher explained, "There are two main clerks in Mount Tai, and their roles are similar to those of judges in the human world. They have many attendants, and most matters concerning ghosts and spirits pass through their hands." A few days later, one of Li Qiangyou's close relatives died but was revived and reported that they had been taken to Mount Tai. There, he learned that there were two main clerks: one surnamed Li, who was Li Qiangyou, and one surnamed Wang, under whose jurisdiction the other person had died.

If one were to discuss separately, the year is not yet over. Suddenly, the magistrate summoned Wang Zhubu, who went out and returned soon, saying that the official had set up a feast and required ten thousand lacquerware items. He told someone, "Your family has this item; you can borrow it for use. Please take it quickly, and it will be returned after the matter is resolved." This person came to see Strong Friend and said, "I was asked to lend the lacquerware, but truly have no strength to do so." Strong Friend pleaded with Wang Hou, but after a long time, no decision was made. Then he heard the magistrate call Li Zhubu, who went out and returned, telling the close officials, "The official was angry that Wang Zhubu did not handle the matter well and instead ordered him to find the lacquerware. This matter is urgent; there is no time to explain. It should be taken quickly." The person had no choice but to bring the strength to take it, and the sound of selection and review was heard by all family members. After the matter was completed, Strong Friend led the person past the magistrate, and thus they were released. After recovering, he told Strong Friend about it. Strong Friend is strict with the officials, and the officials are very afraid, often staying outside after office hours. Suddenly, the magistrate came out, and none failed to show respect. Some secretly looked and saw Strong Friend wearing a hat, followed by more than a hundred people, and could not recognize him; all were surprised and astonished. Like this for more than ten days, and then Strong Friend died.

○Wei Guangji Wei Guangji died suddenly during the Shangyuan period. He claimed that he first saw an official holding a summons, saying: "King Yama has summoned me to be a judge in the underworld." He had already arrived at the gates but had not yet seen the king. Soon, the Governor of Quzhou, Wei Huangchang, also arrived. Guangji greeted him. Huangchang and Guangji were cousins, and he asked: "Why have you come here?" Guangji replied: "I followed the king's summons to be a judge." Huangchang laughed and said: "I have already been appointed to that position, so you should be able to leave." He ordered Guangji to sit. After a while, he ordered the attendants to prepare food. Soon, the food arrived, and the dishes were filled with human noses, fingers, and other parts. He told Guangji: "This is food in the underworld. Since you wish to return, you should not eat it." He then sent Guangji back. When Guangji revived, he recounted the story, and Huangchang was still alive. A few days later, Huangchang died suddenly. That same year, Lu Yan was the Military Governor of Zhe Dong, and a fortune-teller told him: "What is said in the underworld is that the king has summoned you to be a judge. Quickly perform good deeds, and perhaps you will be spared." Yan was very fearful and began to create Buddhist scriptures and images. After several days, the fortune-teller said: "You have been spared, but now the king has taken Wei Huangchang of Quzhou, and the decree has been issued." Yan sent someone to Xin'an to quickly report the news. Ten days later, Huangchang finally died.

An Official from Xizhou Who Died and Revived An official from Xizhou died and came back to life several days later, recounting his experience. He said that the King of Hell, Yama, summoned him to serve as a minor official, a dianshi, in charge of records. He told Yama that he was not familiar with the cases. Yama then ordered him to nominate someone he knew, and he recommended a fellow official. Yama issued a summons to fetch the person. Yama asked the official, "Since your fate has not yet run out, I will now let you return." He then asked his attendants, "Did this person commit any sins while alive?" The attendants replied, "This person once killed a dog and a snake." Yama said, "The dog deserves to die, but why did he unjustly kill the snake? The law demands a different death for the one who killed the snake." He ordered the official to turn around and applied a ladle of hot needle juice to his back. After enduring the punishment, he was sent back. The official was then approached by a bureaucrat who demanded a hundred thousand coins. The official replied, "I have always been poor; how could I possibly afford it?" The bureaucrat then asked for fifty thousand coins, and the official again said he had none. The bureaucrat said, "Your family has an infinite amount of foreign currency; why claim to be poor?" The official answered, "The foreign currency was not obtained through my own efforts." The bureaucrat said, "If you can take it, why not?" He led the official to his home to retrieve the money. The official's wife was lying on a trundle bed, and his son was sitting on a pile of money. The official could not immediately take the money. He went into the courtyard, and a dog barked at him. The official kicked the dog, and it ran away. He then saw his wife preparing food for a seven-day memorial service, and he shouted at her with all his might, but she did not hear him at all. Angered, he pulled her collar, and she fell to the ground. After some time, people outside urged him. When he went out, his son was still sitting on the money, and the official punched his side, causing the boy to become unconscious. He then took fifty thousand coins and gave them to the bureaucrat, and was released, returning to life. When he revived, his son was still sick and had not yet recovered. Later, when the official settled his accounts, he ultimately lost fifty thousand coins.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, a吏部侍郎 named Lu Congyuan's father, who had never practiced Buddhism, experienced an extraordinary event. At the beginning of the reign, one of the candidates for an official position suddenly died. Because his life span had not yet ended, he was allowed to return to life by the officials in the underworld. After regaining consciousness, he met an elderly man wearing shackles. The old man told him, "You have been allowed to return. My son, Congyuan, is currently serving in the Ministry of Personnel. If your selection process is not yet complete, you should see him and convey this message: I have never practiced Buddhism, and now I am suffering various punishments, enduring extreme suffering. Please quickly make scriptures and images to save me." The candidate who was revived then informed the铨司马 about the encounter. Lu Congyuan, upon hearing this, wept and took leave to write the scriptures and images. Once the task was completed, he went to the candidate to thank him and said, "I have already been reborn in the human world. Please tell my son." After saying this, the old man disappeared.

Cui Mingda Cui Mingda, whose nickname was Hanshi, was from Dongwucheng, Qinghe. His grandfather, Yuanjiang, was a Vice Minister of Personnel and Governor of Hangzhou. His father, Tingyu, was a General of the Gold Guards and Governor of Jizhou. Mingda, when young, took monastic vows at the Great Peace Temple in the Western Capital and studied under Master Li Shè. He was proficient in the *Nirvana Sutra* and was considered the leading monk of his time. One day, after the evening meal, he slept in his room during the day. Upon waking, he found himself outside the eaves of his room. Returning to his room, he felt as if he was still outside. This happened several times, and he felt very disturbed by it. Soon after, he saw two officials with ox-heads, carrying dead bodies and roasting them outside his room. The foul smell filled the air. He asked them why they were doing this, and they replied, "We intend to summon you." Mingda said, "Just don't let it smell bad, I'm not afraid to go." The officials then pulled out his soul from his head and led him out of the city. Along the way, he met many people. Mingda tried to tell others about his experience, but he could not. Once they reached the west of the city, the path became narrow and small. Soon, he lost sight of the two officials. A red rope tied to a small bone led him along, and he felt a strong connection to it. After walking several miles, the bone disappeared. Mingda felt sad and walked alone, eventually reaching another city.

The city wall was in ruins, and Mingda saw hundreds of people repairing it with foreign iron. He passed silently, without asking any questions. After traveling several miles, he arrived at another city. Before the city, he saw dozens of officials and laborers mixing mortar to repair a small room. A red-robed official shouted at Mingda, asking him questions. Soon, the official ordered the laborers to push Mingda into the room and block it with mortar. Mingda shouted in protest, but the official said, "I just wanted to test you, no harm done." Shortly after, an inner message from the king was conveyed, summoning Mingda as a teacher. Mingda followed into the main hall, where he saw a distinguished young man, perhaps around twenty years old, with officials dressed in red and purple standing on the steps, numbering in the thousands. As Mingda walked into the courtyard, he secretly thought, "The king has summoned me, but he did not descend from the steps." Suddenly, he saw the king standing below the steps, with his hands clasped in reverence. The king said to Mingda, "Deeply valuing the merits of the world, I have heard that you are well-versed in the *Nirvana Sutra*, so I have sent to invite you to deliver a lecture to extend your life." Mingda thought, "If I am to give a lecture, but there is no platform, how can I explain it?" He then saw the platform for the lecture set up under the western corridor. The king instructed Mingda to sit on the main seat and begin the lecture, and they set up a seat under the pagoda.

The king knelt, and Mingda explained a single line. The king said, "I understand." Mingda descended from his seat and approached. The king ordered his attendants, "Escort Master Mingda back." Before parting, he told Mingda, "You can recite all the scriptures." After leaving, Mingda suddenly saw a group of dozens of horsemen on the road, who said they were Cui Shangshu. When they arrived, it turned out to be his ancestor Yuan Zhuang. Yuan Zhuang saw Mingda and was displeased. Mingda spoke loudly, saying, "I am a man, Uncle, don't you recognize me?" Yuan Zhuang led him to the hall. He first asked about Lan Tian Manor, then about Ting Yu. Mingda answered truthfully. Yuan Zhuang said, "Since my death, I have had duties and never returned home; I did not know if they were alive or dead." Soon after, an official brought a case to Yuan Zhuang. Mingda secretly saw his name on the register, which stated, "A monk from Taiping Temple, ranked fifth in Songshan." After it was finished, Yuan Zhuang asked Mingda if he had seen it, and Mingda declined to see it. He then ordered two officials to escort Mingda to the judge and two others to send him back home. The judge saw him but did not show much respect.

The guests around him said, "This is the legitimate grandson of the Secretary of State; how can we treat him as an ordinary guest?" The magistrate then ordered two clerks to escort Mingda. He said, "These people who bring offerings, there are about five or six each year, and they can be rewarded with small gifts." When they went out, each clerk asked for five hundred coins. The clerks said, "When you get home, you should conveniently buy some tools, and we will leave after receiving the money." When they arrived at the room, they saw two old maids with their hair loose crying, and the disciples sighed. Ming did not recognize the body, but only saw a large pit. The clerks pushed Mingda into the pit, and he came back to life. He was still dazed, unable to speak, but kept raising his hands. The people around him said, "You need a thousand strings of paper money." Mingda nodded. After the paper money was burned, Mingda saw two people each holding money and leave, and from then on, he recovered. At first, when Mingda arrived at the king's gate, he saw several clerks carrying an old woman to his residence, saying she was from Lingyan in Hu County. When they entered, the king angrily said, "What kind of old servant is this, holding the precepts of a Bodhisattva, and yet so unclean!" He ordered her to be released and to clean herself. When they came out, the old woman followed Mingda for about a hundred steps, and then they parted ways. Mingda recovered from his illness and went to Lingyan, where he saw the old woman again, as if they were old acquaintances.

During the Tianbao era, the military officer Fei Ziyu, stationed in Jianwei, was sleeping in his official residence one night when two officials suddenly appeared at his bedside. Startled, Fei Ziyu asked who they were, and they replied, "The Great King summons you." Fei Ziyu said, "I am an official of the state, not subject to the kingdom, so how can I be summoned?" The officials replied, "Yama." Fei Ziyu was greatly frightened and called for someone to prepare a horse, but no one responded. In a hurry, he followed the officials. They arrived at a city where there were thousands of people inside and outside the gates. Fei Ziyu was reciting the Diamond Sutra, which he had been doing constantly. He also thought deeply, "If I meet a bodhisattva, I will plead my case." Soon, the king ordered him to be brought in. Fei Ziyu bowed twice and was very joyful. Suddenly, a monk descended from the clouds, and Fei Ziyu approached to pay his respects. He then loudly declared his desire to see Bodhisattva Dizang. The king said, "Fei Ziyu, this is he." Fei Ziyu bowed before the bodhisattva, and the bodhisattva asked, "How do you know me?" He then told the king, "This man has recited the Diamond Sutra all his life, and his lifespan is not yet complete, so he should be sent away." The king looked at Fei Ziyu, suddenly angry, and asked for his name. Fei Ziyu replied, "I am Fei Ziyu, a military officer of Jiachou." The king said, "Jianwei county, how is that Jiachou?"

You should die, as you are being punished by the Bodhisattva through hardship. Release you now. Zi Yu bowed twice and left. The Bodhisattva said, "Return home, do not eat meat again, and you will gain eternal life." The Bodhisattva led Zi Yu to bow before the sacred image. The sacred image was a bronze Buddha, whose head, face, and hands moved. The Bodhisattva bowed, and his limbs fully extended. Zi Yu also bowed. After bowing, Zi Yu asked, "Why are there so many people outside?" The Bodhisattva replied, "These people are unclear about their sins and merits, having been ghosts for several hundred years, unable to remember their past lives." Zi Yu returned home and was revived. Three years later, he ate meat again and died, being summoned for judgment. The Bodhisattva saw him and became angry, saying, "I never told you to eat meat, why did you break your promise?" Zi Yu was reborn and then stopped eating meat. Initially, Zi Yu had taken three wives, all of whom claimed to be summoned and came to see him. Zi Yu asked, "How did you come?" His wives said, "Just ignore us." The younger wife said, "You treated me poorly, so I came out of resentment. Why don't you return the money I used?" Zi Yu said, "Money is easy to get." The wife said, "Did you use my copper coins and now want to return paper money?" Zi Yu said, "A husband using his wife's money has no obligation to return it." The wife had no response and left slowly.

Mai Xian of Qiantang always made virtuous deeds his profession, enjoyed holding and reciting Buddhist scriptures, and also performed the Seven Births' Blessing Ceremony. The neighbors called him a lay practitioner. During the Tianbao era, he suddenly fell ill and died but came back to life. He recounted, "After I died, I was led by someone and, along with ten or more others, appeared before Yama, the King of Hell. Yama asked, 'What virtuous deeds did you perform in your life?' I replied, 'I only held scriptures and recited the Buddha's name.' Yama said, 'This virtue you can practice; your fate in the afterlife cannot be empty.' He then checked Mai Xian's records and said with joy, 'You were not supposed to die yet; now I will let you return to life. You should uphold your original virtue.' I bowed twice. Since no one came to accompany me, I was kept in the office. Yama then questioned further and next came to Bao Zhi, the head of Qiantang's local administration. He asked, 'Why did you take money from Li Ping, the barber, without reporting it to your subordinate household?' Bao Zhi replied, 'I am the head of the village and often stay in the county office, returning late at night and leaving early in the morning; I truly did not know. I beg to summon my son to ask.' Yama ordered a summons for Bao Zhi's son. Soon, an envoy arrived and sent Bao Zhi back. I then came back to life and recounted the story. At that time, his son was very much unharmed. Everyone tested it. After five or six days, Bao Zhi's son indeed fell ill and died two days later."

Hui Jing, a native of Juxu, began his official career as a magistrate in Wucheng. During the reign of Governor Li Rong of Caozhou, Hui Jing was tasked with overseeing the capture of bandits. One of the bandits had a brother who was a monk and had stopped stealing. Hui Jing investigated the case and showed leniency toward the monk. The governor criticized Hui Jing for being too lenient and took over the investigation himself. The monk confessed under questioning. The governor ordered Hui Jing to execute the monk. In the second year of the Zaitai era, during the summer of the sixth month, Hui Jing suddenly fell ill and died. After the temporary burial had been completed, he was to be married posthumously to his deceased sister-in-law, so the burial was not yet finalized. After twelve days, Hui Jing revived, moaning inside the coffin. His younger brothers and nephews were frightened and fled, but his mother alone ordered the coffin to be opened with an axe. She placed her mouth near his, and she could feel a slight warmth. After a while, his eyes opened. His flesh was all rotten, and they slowly fed him cow's milk. After he recovered, he said that upon his initial death, he passed through the offices of the afterlife, where the guards and flags were very solemn. He was brought before an official who asked Hui Jing why he had killed the monk. The monk stood before him, and they debated the matter. The monk could not defend himself, and the official told Hui Jing, "You have no further business here, go back." The official's attendants said, "The flesh has already decayed." The official ordered medicine to be fetched, wrapped in paper, and said, "Give back his old flesh." After taking the medicine, they reached the gate and heard crying. Hui Jing was afraid and did not want to enter. The messenger forced him to go. At the door, the messenger scattered the medicine in the coffin and pulled Hui Jing's arm, pushing him back into the coffin. He fell unconscious. After he revived, his flesh was completely rotten, but after a month, he was as good as new. When he first returned to the house, the dogs, horses, chickens, and geese all cried out, suggesting that he had seen something significant.

○杨再思 In the first year of the Shenlong era, the Minister of the Central Secretariat, Yang Zaisi, died. On the same day, the official in charge of the imperial kitchen also died, and both were led by officials of the underworld to the place of judgment. The judge asked Yang Zaisi, "What sins did you commit in life that you have so many charges against you? How can you atone for them?" Yang Zaisi replied, "I really did no wrong." The judge then ordered the ledger to be brought. Soon, a yellow-robed official arrived with the ledger and recited Yang Zaisi's sins: "In the first year of the Ruyi era, Merkhet invaded the states of Ying and Tan, and the state sent too few troops to rescue, unable to match the enemy. Someone submitted a petition for advice, but Yang Zaisi disregarded the advice and sent troops anyway, resulting in a defeat by Merkhet, with more than a thousand people killed. In the first year of the Dazhu era, locusts in Hebei caused a disaster, and the people were starving. As a chancellor, Yang Zaisi failed to open the granaries to provide relief, and the people are still displaced, with more than twenty thousand people dying of starvation. In managing the yin and yang, Yang Zaisi's laws and policies were unjust, causing harm to the harmony of the heavens and earth, thus causing a great flood in three counties of Henan, drowning thousands of people." Such items totaled six or seven, and they were shown to Yang Zaisi. Yang Zaisi knelt twice and confessed guilt. Suddenly, there was a hand as big as a bed, with hair that was frightening, which grabbed Yang Zaisi, causing blood to flow between the fingers, and flew away into the air. The judge then asked the official in charge of the imperial kitchen, "Why did you end up here?" The official in charge replied, "I want to ask the person." It was said, "There is no fault, should be released back." The official in charge of the imperial kitchen survived and often told people about the incident. Emperor Zhongzong heard about it and summoned him to ask, and he answered in detail and truthfully. Emperor Zhongzong ordered that his deeds be recorded in the hall of the Secretariat.

During the late reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Wang Cheng, the county magistrate of Jintan, was tasked with leading the household tax in the capital and was at the Left Treasury to make the payment. Suddenly, an envoy arrived at the treasury and said, "The king has summoned the magistrate." Wang Cheng hastily followed him, leaving the city and traveling more than ten miles to reach an administrative office. Upon entering, he heard the voice of Cui Xi Yi, the former left attendant. Wang Cheng had known Cui Xi Yi for thirty years. He asked the gatekeeper for information and learned the reason, then requested an introduction. The gatekeeper went inside to report, and Cui Xi Yi asked, "Where is this person?" He immediately ordered him to be called in. They met and were surprised. Cui Xi Yi said to Wang Cheng, "Do you know if this is the underworld?" Wang Cheng then realized he had died and felt sad for a long time. Cui Xi Yi asked again, "Have you seen Cui Han?" Cui Han is Cui Xi Yi's son. Wang Cheng replied, "Since entering the city, I have been busy with the treasury office and had no time to visit the house." Cui Xi Yi laughed and said, "You are truly a superficial person, regarding life and death lightly." He then asked Wang Cheng about the reason for his presence, and Wang Cheng explained, "I was just now in the treasury, following the envoy here, and did not understand the reason."

After a while, an announcement was made that the king had arrived. Cui ordered the message to be conveyed to the king, saying: "Jin Tan Wang Cheng is a friend or relative of the king, and he should not have died; the matter is resolved. Please release him quickly, as it is hot, and I fear that the place where he is held might deteriorate." The king invited someone in and spoke to them, saying: "You were previously a county magistrate who accepted bribes and implicated others." The person saw the magistrate wearing shackles and sitting under a tree in the courtyard. They asked, "If I did not initially agree with the actions, why was I falsely accused?" The magistrate explained, "I suffered punishment but did so to save others in an emergency." The king said, "If you are not involved, you should be released." The person left and went to bid farewell to Xi Yi. Xi Yi said, "You have been returned safely, which is good." He then conveyed a message to Cui Han, advising him not to act unjustly in his official capacity, as he will face consequences later and that taking money will shorten his life. He also recommended that on the first and fifteenth day of each lunar month, a bottle of clear water should be sent to a temple, placed on the Buddha hall, to gain great blessings. The person asked, "What kind of merit is this?" Xi Yi replied, "This is a matter of the afterlife, and you should not inquire further, but know that it brings blessings." After finishing speaking, he sent the person away, and they survived.

A Tale of Wei Yanzhi Wei Yanzhi, Deputy Governor of Muzhou, completed his term of office and temporarily resided in Jiaxing, Suzhou. In the eighth year of the Dali era (767 CE), he fell ill with dysentery and was sleeping alone in the hall during the summer. Suddenly, he saw two officials who said, "The superior has ordered you to come." Wei asked, "Who is the superior?" The officials replied, "We have been commanded to pursue you, but we know nothing else." Suspicious of ghosts or demons, Wei got up and wanted to leave. The officials approached and held his sleeve, saying, "We must take you with us; where do you want to go now?" Wei's spirit followed along, and they left the city. On the journey, they did not see any ponds or marshes, only a land route. After traveling several miles, they arrived at a place with an administrative building. The officials led Wei to the chief official, who instructed him to bring him to the magistrate. The officials then led Wei to the magistrate, who, dressed in official robes and holding a ceremonial tablet, descended the steps with great respect and seriousness. He said to Wei, "Someone has a complaint against you, and you must answer the charges." The clerk then led Wei to a room for questioning. The room was in front of the magistrate's hall, and the hall resembled a county magistrate's hall, with two rows of houses and rooms between them. In front of the room was a slanted lattice, and inside, people were sitting on wooden beds. The clerk instructed Wei to sit on a wooden bed for questioning. Soon, the clerk led six or seven prisoners, some shackled, some bound, and some with their heads uncovered, to Wei's room. The clerk said, "The charge against you is that you took money from Governor Wei, and now the underworld is settling the score."

The official asked, "Who are you accusing?" The prisoner replied, "He is Wei Bing, the司马 (a high-ranking military or administrative official), but I do not know him." The official then congratulated Wei Bing, saying, "You have been given a second chance at life, which is very good news." The official then led the prisoner to the judge's office and reported everything in detail. The judge also congratulated Wei Bing, and ordered the prisoner to be returned. The official then reported to the ambassador, who ordered Wei Bing to be released. The official then led the prisoner to the ambassador's hall, where the ambassador had already returned inside. The official conveyed the order to release Wei Bing, and sent someone to arrest Wei Bing. Soon after, a green-robed clerk arrived with a case, shouting at the arresting officer, "Why did you arrest the wrong person?" Both were punished with sixty strokes, and blood covered the ground. The official then ordered the prisoner to be sent back. The prisoner said, "I want to know about my future official position." The clerk replied, "How could you know that?" The prisoner strongly requested, and the clerk opened the register. The prisoner's name was listed, but it was a blank piece of paper with no words, and thus the prisoner was released. After walking more than a hundred steps, the prisoner saw a clerk arresting Zheng Jingke, the magistrate of Qingliu County, who was the prisoner's maternal uncle. The prisoner asked, "Why are you here?" Zheng Jingke replied, "I was accused by someone." Zheng Jingke also asked the prisoner, "Why are you here?"
